Defense Sec. Pete Hegseth and General Dan Caine hold briefing on the U.S.-Iran war — 3/31/2026

CNBC Television(3.4M) ·36:29 ·31.6K views · 2026-03-31
Pete Hegseth (Secretary of Defense) Dan Caine (Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff)
> "What I witnessed was motivation.
TL;DR: One month into Operation Epic Fury, the U.S. has struck 11,000 targets and effectively neutralized the Iranian Navy to compel a peace deal; the Pentagon reports high troop morale and a shift toward "dynamic" strikes as Iran’s command structure and industrial base...

TL;DR: One month into Operation Epic Fury, the U.S. has struck 11,000 targets and effectively neutralized the Iranian Navy to compel a peace deal; the Pentagon reports high troop morale and a shift toward "dynamic" strikes as Iran’s command structure and industrial base collapse.

Operational Progress and Tactical Evolution

  • Strike Volume: The Joint Force has struck over 11,000 targets in the first 30 days of the conflict.
  • Naval Dominance: General Caine confirmed the destruction of more than 150 Iranian ships, including the entirety of their Jamaran-class frigates.
  • Dynamic Targeting: Within the last 24 hours, the U.S. conducted 200 "dynamic strikes"—engagements where pilots receive real-time target updates (such as mobile launchers or troop formations) while already airborne.
  • Air Superiority: Increased control of the skies has allowed the transition to overland B-52 missions.
  • Degraded Morale: Intelligence reports indicate widespread desertions and personnel shortages within the Iranian military following the destruction of command bunkers and infrastructure.

Strategic Objectives and Presidential Mandate

  • The Nuclear "Red Line": Secretary Hegseth reaffirmed President Trump’s primary objective: Iran will never be permitted to possess a nuclear weapon.
  • Economic Consequences: Following President Trump’s recent communications, the U.S. is prepared to "obliterate" Iran’s electric plants, oil wells, and Kharg Island if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened immediately.
  • Diplomatic Pressure: Negotiations are currently active and "gaining strength," involving officials such as Jared Kushner and Marco Rubio. Hegseth noted that the military’s role is to "negotiate with bombs" to compel Iran to accept U.S. terms.
  • Unpredictability: Regarding "boots on the ground," the Pentagon is intentionally refusing to foreclose any options, maintaining strategic ambiguity to keep Iranian leadership off-balance.

Assessment of the "Arsenal of Freedom"

  • Industrial Base Revitalization: The briefing emphasized the rapid rebuilding of the U.S. defense industrial base, termed the "Arsenal of Freedom," which is operating at its highest capacity since World War II.
  • Critical Infrastructure: General Caine praised the national assembly lines, noting that the precision of American machinists and technicians is the "core of American combat power."
  • Iranian Attrition: Conversely, Iran’s defense industrial base is described as nearly destroyed, with negligible ability to reconstitute lost weaponry or technology.

Force Readiness and International Relations

  • Troop Morale: Following a weekend visit to CENTCOM, Hegseth reported an "urgency to finish the job" among troops, contrasting the current high-tempo mission with the "rotational" mindset of the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan.
  • Allied Criticism: Hegseth voiced frustration with traditional allies, specifically mentioning the Royal Navy and NATO. He suggested that countries relying on the Strait of Hormuz must "step up" and defend the waterway rather than relying solely on U.S. "heavy lifting."
  • Countering Adversaries: The U.S. is monitoring arms and intelligence support for Iran from Russia, China, and North Korea, with Hegseth stating the U.S. is "addressing and confronting" these influences where necessary.

Notable Quotes

"What I witnessed was motivation. It was sheer mission focus. It was the American warrior unleashed. It was the kind of warfighting American spirit that comes with a clear mission against a determined enemy." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of Defense
"Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that this new regime... is in a better place if they make that deal. And so we'll continue... in the meantime, we'll negotiate with bombs." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of Defense
"To our adversaries out there, I remind you to beware of the American workforce... The distance from that factory floor and that assembly line to the front line is incredibly short." — General Dan Caine, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff
"You can't just have flags, you have to have formations. You can't just have a few ships, you have to have enough to effect change. Those things matter in a dangerous world." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of Defense
Well, over the weekend, I had the opportunity to visit our troops fighting in Operation Epic Fury. We were in the ground, or on the ground, excuse me, in CENTCOM on Saturday for about half the day. For reasons of operational security, so those troops are not targeted, the places and bases will not be named. Suffice it to say, the trip was an honor. I had a chance to bear witness, and I witnessed the best of America. I witnessed warriors, a brotherhood of men and women warriors all. Active duty, Guard, and Reserve, united in their love for each other, their shared purpose, and their commitment to the mission. I witnessed sheer competence. I watched a Private First Class confidently calling out an enemy missile trajectory to a room full of officers. Everyone focused, the room was locked in. Two enemy missiles successfully shot down. I spoke to Air Force and Navy pilots on the flight line, who every day both deliver bombs deep into Iran but also shoot down drones defending their base. Many had just returned from the skies of Iran and Tehran. I put on a headset and spoke to one crew in the cockpit. Locked and loaded, each and every day. I witnessed ingenuity, American ingenuity. I met the young Army officer who figured out how to neutralize maneuvering enemy missiles, saving countless lives. His commander confirmed that the whoops and cheers erupted in the Tactical Operation Center when his new approach was first successful. I met the Air Force intel analyst who refines target packages faster than the enemy can adapt. I actually gave him my card and told him to keep me posted on the ground truth. I did the same with his boss, a Colonel with a heart the size of Texas and a beautiful deployment mustache to match. I witnessed lethality. I met a junior Airman, as the sun was going down and a chill was setting on the tarmac, who when asked what they needed, simply looked up at me with a sly smile on her face and said, "More bombs, sir. And bigger bombs." We will happily oblige her. I met the Army targeting team who found and sunk the pride and joy of the Iranian Navy. Their fighting position was plastered with images of sunken enemy ships. And I witnessed urgency. Right when we landed, another C-17 landed just minutes after us. And within 30 seconds of the aircraft coming to a full stop, a team on the ground pulled up and the cargo was being uploaded. Wartime speed. To a man and to a woman, on the ground, in the air, on the flight line, and in the TOC, I heard, "We want everything faster. Higher op tempo. Wartime speed." The feeling was the exact opposite of the rotational units year after year in the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan that we're so familiar with. In those wars, it was always about the next rotation, never knowing when the mission would end or exactly what the mission was, year after year. Not with Epic Fury. I witnessed urgency to finish the job, urgency to achieve mission success. Not looking at the next rotation. Only moving as fast as possible to win. I got a chance to troop the line, to witness firsthand what we already know is true. I spoke to all ranks and all services, none of whom knew we were coming. It was not rehearsed or scripted; sometimes we just wandered. What I witnessed was motivation. It was sheer mission focus. It was the American warrior unleashed. It was the kind of warfighting American spirit that comes with a clear mission against a determined enemy. A crew chief we flew with summed it up nicely. He said, "It's been a busy few weeks, sir. Tough stuff. But I'm so honored to be called up. This fight is long overdue. We need to address it for our kids. We cannot pass the buck. Please thank the President from us." I heard that time and time again. I asked each young American, "What do you need?" And nobody said better equipment. Nobody said more comfortable living conditions. Nobody said send me home. Well, of course, eventually we want all those things, they do too. But what those Americans said to me—young and old, officer and NCO, male and female, black and white—was, "Let's finish the mission. Get us even more bombs, bigger bombs, more targets. Let us finish this." In fact, Admiral Cooper noted this morning that the three Air Force captains shot down by Kuwaiti friendly fire early in the fight weeks ago, they never left the theater. All dropped bombs over Tehran last night. These men and women live the Iranian threat every day. Incoming missiles and drones, and know what a world looks like, what the world would look like if Iran had the most dangerous weapon in the world: a nuclear weapon. As President Trump has said time and time again for years and in this administration, Iran cannot have a nuclear bomb, and they won't. These troops, they want to finish this fight for their kids and their grandkids. This is about history. This is about legacy. Success matters. And because of this President and these Americans, we are closer than ever before to winning. President Trump is doing what no other President had the guts to do. Previous Presidents were all talk. He's all action. On the battlefield, because of the latitude the President has given us, American firepower is only increasing. Iran's is decreasing. We have more and more options and they have less. Just one month in, only one month, we set the terms. The upcoming days will be decisive. Iran knows that, and there's almost nothing they can militarily do about it. Yes, they will still shoot some missiles, but we will shoot them down. Of note, the last 24 hours saw the lowest number of enemy missiles and drones fired by Iran. They will go underground, but we will find them. We recently destroyed another one of their command bunkers. Leaders were forced to flee. No water, no power, no oxygen, no command and control. Their faith in their caves is diminishing. The latest intel is clear out of CENTCOM: our strikes are damaging the morale of the Iranian military, leading to widespread desertions, key personnel shortages, and causing frustrations amongst senior leaders. Just last night, we had 200 dynamic strikes alone. A dynamic strike is a strike where a pilot leaves and during their flight they get a new target set based on real-time intel given to them. A new launcher, a new location, a new troop formation. A dynamic target is one that changes while you're in the air because of improved intelligence. There were 200 dynamic strikes alone, in addition to the pre-planned targets. The video the President posted last night of Esfahan showed an ammo depot struck by US bombers. You see, you don't get to see many of those videos because, as a reminder, Iran has still shut off the internet to 99.9% of its population. But if Iran is wise, they will cut a deal. President Trump doesn't bluff and he doesn't back down. You can ask Khamenei about that. The new Iranian regime should know that by now. This new regime, because regime change has occurred, should be wiser than the last. President Trump will make a deal; he is willing. And the terms of the deal are known to them. If Iran is not willing, then the United States War Department will continue with even more intensity. Standing here this morning in this briefing room, in my mind's eye, I'm actually looking out at the groups I met this weekend. The pilots, the logisticians, the intel analysts, the targeters, the sustainers, the flight crews, the air defenders, the base security, those maintainers who we walked up to at sunset with the chill in the air on the flight line. May God watch over all of them, each day and each night. May His almighty and eternal arms of providence stretch over them and protect them and bring them peace. In the name of Jesus Christ, and amen. Mr. Chairman, over to you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, and good morning, everyone, and thank you again for being here. As operations continue, I remain deeply grateful for the service and determination of 2.8 million members of our joint force, each of whom are serving something greater than themselves. Every day those deployed, and in many cases those at home who are deploying forward in bomber pulses and others, put themselves in harm's way, and we owe them a deep debt of gratitude. I remain especially grateful for our fallen, who gave the ultimate sacrifice. We'll never forget their valor and their determination to do something greater than themselves, and each day we continue to earn what they've given to us. This morning, I want to share that yesterday the joint force had the honor of participating in the burial of Colonel Clarence Emil "Bud" Anderson, who passed away in May of 2024 at the ripe old age of 102. He was laid to rest in Arlington Cemetery yesterday morning alongside his wife of 70 years, Eleanor, who he married on February 23rd, 1945. She passed away in 2015 at the age of 92. Bud yesterday, a legend to our Air Force and fighter pilots everywhere, was honored with a combination flyby of F-35s and a four-ship of P-51 Mustangs. He was one of the most prolific flying aces of World War II and the highest-scoring P-51 ace with 16 and a quarter kills in his squadron and in his group. And he served from 1942 to 1972, and his last combat tour was flying F-104 Thunderchiefs over North Vietnam. For anyone that had the chance to meet Colonel Anderson, you knew what an incredibly special man he was. And that's true for each and every one of our World War II vets, who become fewer and fewer with each passing day. They are the Greatest Generation and give us the gift of an incredible example as we execute Operation Epic Fury today. And Colonel Anderson, we we we mourn for your loss and remember that smile on the right side as you went out to do our nation's business. I'd like to now share an operational update. Our joint force continues to focus on our military objectives as we systematically continue to degrade and destroy Iran's ability to project power and threaten stability beyond its borders. First, the joint force continues to destroy Iran's ballistic missile and UAS capabilities. We remain focused on interdicting and destroying the logistical supply chains that feed these programs. And this remains a truly joint effort, prosecuted around the clock from air, land, sea, space, and cyberspace. Long-range bombers from US Strategic Command coordinating with tactical fighter aircraft from our joint force launched from bases around the region and the continental United States, while simultaneously Navy fighters from the sea and sailors continue to project power from the sea while Army and Marine artillery units continue to execute long-range precision fires deep into enemy territory against high-value targets. Meanwhile, on the defense side, our Army and air defenders and aviators, as the Secretary talked about, remain vigilant, forming a shield to protect our forces and our partners, intercepting missiles and drones. Together we continue to deliver precision strikes against key manufacturing nodes, component storage sites, and research facilities deep within Iranian territory. And over the past 30 days we've struck more than 11,000 targets. Given the increase in air superiority, we've successfully started to conduct the first overland B-52 missions, which allow us, as we've said before, to continue to get on top of the enemy and, as the Secretary talked about, switch towards more and more dynamic targets, servicing mobile targets around the battle space. We've continued to do the work against Iran's missile, drone, and naval production facilities, and we continue the multi-domain pressure that we've talked about. Second, on the Navy front, we continue to assert dominance over the Iranian Navy. We remain focused on targeting their mine-laying capability, their naval assets, and we've now, as I mentioned briefly last time, started to work attack helicopters and other close air support assets into the naval domain. CENTCOM continues to identify and work against naval depots and storage areas, and we've taken out again more than 150 ships, including all Jamaran-class frigates inside their Navy. Third, we continue to prosecute our campaign against their defense industrial base at scale. This includes factories, warehouses, nuclear weapons research and development labs, and the associated infrastructure required for Iran to reconstitute its combat capability. I'd like to continue my theme of highlighting the incredible contributions of Americans to our joint force. Today I want to talk a little bit about a different front line, a line that doesn't have bunkers or guard posts, but is just as critical to our nation's security: our national assembly lines. Today I want to recognize a group of Americans who live at the beginning of our nation's combat power: the Americans who actually make our weapons, both inside our defense industrial base, but even more broadly inside our national industrial base. In every military option, we could not and cannot do our jobs without the men and women across our country who show up every day. Around the clock, to a factory floor, a workshop, or a laboratory, they build the weapons and capabilities we need to project American combat power at the time and place of our choosing. These great Americans, and I've had a chance to spend some time with them when I was in the private sector, are the core of American combat power. They're the machinists running high-tech CNC machines, cutting raw blocks of metal into incredibly precise parts. They're the assembly workers painstakingly taking a kit of parts and turning that into a complex guidance system or precision munition or a rocket motor or building a jet or submarine. Or they're the quality assurance technicians who ensure that when a warfighter pulls a trigger, the weapon works every time. This can be and is tough and gritty work. It's not a quiet office and a desk with paper, and there's nothing wrong with that, but this is exactly the way this group of Americans likes it. I know this, I've seen it myself, and I remain deeply honored by that gift. It's often loud and dangerous work, demanding, requiring absolute focus for hours at a time with deep commitment to get it right every single time. It's hands-on work where one uncaught mistake or deviation can put an American's life at risk. A single misplaced wire, a microscopic flaw in a weld, or an incorrectly calibrated sensor could mean the difference between mission success or mission failure. The difference is measured in the lives of our sons and daughters who we put around the field and on the field of battle. We rely on and trust in these great Americans, and it's not just their manufacturing skill, it's their innovative minds and their entrepreneurial spirit. From those who build exquisite capabilities down to the mom-and-pop machine shops all over this great country, they live at the beginning of and the core of America's combat power. They are constantly adapting, constantly improving, and constantly learning. And there are examples of this throughout history and current days to include building things like the F-117 stealth fighter, the B-2 stealth bomber, making our combat capability undetectable over the enemy to today's B-21 and F-47. It's work like in the shipyards of the East Coast and Northeast side of the United States that go out and build America's nuclear submarines that allow us to patrol around the world at the time and place of our choosing. These innovators, these workers, these incredible Americans don't get the same glory as a fighter pilot returning to a carrier deck at night or an artilleryman sending rounds down range. And yet they show up every single day. And without them, we could not do the work that we are tasked to do. 24/7 they build the tools that we need to do our business. The skill, the commitment, the patriotism, the dedication poured into every piece of combat capability and hardware is seen and felt out at the edge of the force. As the Secretary talked about those young bomb builders out in the desert that he had the chance to see, you can see it, you can feel it, and it's real. And we're deeply grateful. So to the American workforce out there, both inside our defense industrial base and in our national industrial base, thank you on behalf of the joint force. We carry the weapons that you build, we rely on the systems that you create, and the distance from that factory floor and that assembly line to the front line is incredibly short. Thank you, keep it up. And to our adversaries out there, I remind you to beware of the American workforce. We continue to press forward in our military objectives. The Secretary and I remain deeply humbled by the spirit, tenacity, commitment, and grit of our 2.8 million member strong joint force. I want to, as always, thank the force and their families and, as always, remember our fallen. We carry them and their names every day. Sir, I'll turn it back over to you. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Two things: he didn't note it, but in memory of Colonel Bud Anderson, the Chairman himself flew his F-16 to honor him recently. And thank you for highlighting the defense industrial base, the Arsenal of Freedom. It's worth noting no one has rebuilt that defense industrial base faster and with more purpose than President Trump in his first term and now. We're rebuilding the Arsenal of Freedom; our defense industrial base is more vibrant today than it's been since World War II and growing rapidly. Contrast that with Iran, whose defense industrial base is nearly completely destroyed. Their ability to reconstitute the weapons of war they're using now that they're hiding in underground facilities and attriting over time, their ability to rebuild that is negligible. And I think that contrast is an enduring legacy of this as well. Right here. Thank you for your time. We've had some signaling that the US will retake control of the Strait of Hormuz. At that point, what military posture will be imposed to guarantee safe passage for our allies, deter our enemies, and demonstrate US dominance in the region? And also, can you speak to how much America's adversaries—Russia, China, North Korea—are supporting Iran's war machine at this point with arms and intelligence, and what we are militarily doing to punish the enemy coalition? Appreciate both questions. On the Strait of Hormuz, there are many more vessels flowing through today than there were, as the President has arranged. The President's been clear to Iran: open it for business or we have options. And we certainly do. And when you look at what the Chairman laid out with the Navy, with the Navy industrial base, with coastal cruise missiles, with UAVs, with counter-mine capabilities, we've been focused from the beginning on attriting and defeating those capabilities and limiting their options. There's lots we're doing as well, some of which is known, some of which is not known, to set the conditions. And I think the President was clear this morning in his Truth that there are countries around the world who ought be prepared to step up on this critical waterway as well. It's not just the United States Navy. Last time I checked, there was supposed to be a big bad Royal Navy that could be prepared to do things like that as well. So he's pointing out this is an international waterway that we use less than most, in fact dramatically less than most. So the world ought pay attention and be prepared to stand up. President Trump's been willing to do the heavy lifting on behalf of the free world to address this threat of Iran. It's not just our problem set going forward, even though we have done the lion's share of preparation to ensure that that Strait will be open, which is an outcome the President's been very clear on. As far as Russia and China, we know exactly what they're doing—what they are or are not doing. We don't have to air publicly what all of that is, but where necessary we're addressing it, we're mitigating it, and or we're confronting it head on. I don't know if you have anything you want to add. No, sir, I think you covered it. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. David Muir, The America's Voice. And I witnessed that enthusiasm on the lines in our submarine builders and iron works. It's unbelievable. But I just wanted to ask you, what's the status of the delivery of any hardened bunkers for additional protection for our troops? And without getting too specific, what kind of measures are we taking to protect some of the larger and more strategic aircraft like the Sentry and others? Well, it's a great question. I'll say what I witnessed where I went was a completely locked-in discipline of bunker use and bunker improvement. From the beginning, as we stated very clearly, the first thing we did was set up a defense and make sure our defensive capabilities were maxed out before any of this even started. That included fortifications as much as possible. But it also included dispersal. If all of our people are in one place, you could imagine why that's a big problem. So dispersing is part of that defeat. Alongside that dispersal is more and more bunkers. And I can tell you talking to base commanders, talking to our allies in Israel, talking to others, rapidly fielding that and then improving those positions is a theater priority, no doubt. As are the air defenses and the layered air defenses. It's not just Patriots and THAADS; it's fighters in defensive caps, it's other kinetic defeat systems, it's electronic warfare. So the defense of our troops and our assets is maxed. I will say on some of those other assets you talked about—air wings, airframes—there's some things adversaries are doing to provide info and intel that they shouldn't. We're aware of it, and ultimately we move things around and don't—one of the biggest principles you learn in the military is to not set patterns, predictable patterns. And so commanders are working hard to adjust in real-time with those systems and make sure they're in the right places and not easily targetable. Jillian. Thanks, Mr. Secretary. The US and Iran appear right now publicly to disagree about whether there are these negotiations ongoing. What impact does that have on military strategy? Does it have any bearing at all? And General, the President wrote yesterday that the US must destroy Iran's electricity generating plants and oil wells. Those are his words, before ending this operation. Is there a way to do either of those things without like seriously jeopardizing or seriously harming civilians? So on the talks, I can tell you having been with Steve and Jared and the Vice President and Marco and many others yesterday, they are very real. They are ongoing, they're active, and I think gaining strength. And we appreciate that. As I said in my remarks, we would much prefer to get a deal. If Iran was willing to relinquish material they have and ambitions they have, open this... great. That's the goal. We don't want to have to do more militarily than we have to. But I didn't mean it flippantly when I said in the meantime we'll negotiate with bombs. Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that this new regime, this regime in charge, is in a better place if they make that deal. And so we'll continue; we're working hand in hand, but the primary effort is a deal. We want that deal to be accomplished if at all possible. If not, then we're prepared to continue. Yeah, thanks for the question. We're always thinking about those considerations and develop options to be able to mitigate those risks pursuant to the normal practices that we do in the military. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. I know you slightly addressed this already, but just on the Strait of Hormuz, is opening the Strait an essential objective to Operation Epic Fury, or is that the job of those other countries? And then secondly, without asking you to comment on things that you can't talk about, what is your message to Americans who love the President and strongly believe in him but are very worried about this notion of boots on the ground? Well, first on the Strait of Hormuz, our core objectives from this podium from day one from me, from the Chairman, from the President, from the Vice President, from Secretary Rubio and others have been clear: defense, missile production and missile programs, so their entire missile program; defense industrial base and production ability to build; and Navy and power projection. So those have been—and then of course wrapping it all is Iran's never going to have a nuclear weapon. Those have been very clear. Defeating the Navy is a core part of ensuring they can't project that kind of power. But ultimately, I think the President's Truth this morning lays that out very well. This Strait of Hormuz issue, which we've set the conditions for success and we will make sure Iran knows that very clearly, is not just a United States of America problem set. We've been willing to lead, President Trump's led the entire time, but it's not just us. So ultimately, I think other countries should pay attention when the President speaks. He's proven that when he speaks, he means something, and he's pointing out, you know, you might might want to start learning how to fight for yourself. Something some of us have been saying for quite some time. You can't just have flags, you have to have formations. You can't just have a few ships, you have enough to effect change. Those things matter in a dangerous world with ascendant adversaries. That's why the President—that's why the Chairman's talking about our industrial base. That's why we're launching the Arsenal of Freedom tour to revive our defense industrial base. President Trump is doing it in a way that nobody else has. As far as President Trump and boots on the ground, I don't understand why the base, which they have already, they understand, wouldn't have faith in his ability to execute on this. Look at his track record of pursuing peace through strength, America First outcomes. And what he's simply saying, and it's exactly true, and I've said from this podium too, we're not going to foreclose any option. You can't fight and win a war if you tell your adversary what you are willing to do or what you are not willing to do, to include boots on the ground. Our adversary right now thinks there are 15 different ways we could come at them with boots on the ground. And guess what, there are. So if we needed to, we could execute those options on behalf of the President of the United States and this department. Or maybe we don't have to use them at all. Maybe negotiations work, or maybe there's a different approach. But the point is to be unpredictable in that. Certainly not let anybody know what you're willing to do or not do. But if anybody has internalized the lessons of Iraq and Afghanistan, as the first one, President Trump, to call them out for what they are, he's not going to repeat those lessons. And I think I've been very clear about that from the podium. One last question. Thank you, Secretary Hegseth. Question for you and then a question for General Cane. You said we're a month into the Operation Epic Fury. How long until the objectives are achieved and is there a scenario where a deal is struck before the objectives have been achieved? And then for General Cane, there's been lots of media coverage that suggests a ground invasion is imminent. What other purposes might the soldiers and the Marines who have been deployed over to the Middle East serve in this conflict? Well, just like the previous question, it's sort of military 101. Don't tell your enemy what you're willing to do or not do, and don't tell your enemy when you're willing to stop. Especially an enemy that likes to hide in bunkers and try to hoard their missiles and hope you'll wait out. So that's not a question I'm going to answer or that the President has said definitively. We have our own goals and guidance and things were military objectives that we're moving toward and things that we look at. And as he's articulated, you know, he's said four to six weeks, six to eight weeks, three weeks—it could be any any particular number. But we would never reveal precisely what it is because our goal is to finish those objectives. And we're well on our way, and the Chairman and I look at this every single day. It will be the President's determination and the President's determination alone when those objectives are complete. It will be when it serves the interest of the American people to cut that deal, to make sure that Iran doesn't have a nuclear capability and ultimately that our objectives or our interests are advanced. I don't know if you want to add anything. No, just to answer your question, Reagan, you know the range of military options that those forces can offer are extensive, not just limited to what you mentioned in terms of forces on the ground. And I wouldn't want to take away the President's decision space, but there are a multitude of things. Not the least of which is Iran should note that they're out there and that they are a pressure point. So they should carefully consider, I think at the diplomatic level—not my job as the Chairman but at the diplomatic level—to consider what's in front of them. Mr. Secretary, thank you for the question. Do you still believe, you've said previously, that the Iranian leadership would surrender? Do you still believe that? And is the United States still committed to NATO's collective defense? And to General Chairman Cane, could you give us a sense of your thinking about the legality of striking desalination plants if the President were to order that? I will just say that any mission that ends on our terms, you want to call it a surrender, call it a defeat, call it what you want, we remain committed to it. A conflict that ends on our terms and the President's terms. There's no doubt about that. And as far as NATO's concerned, that's a decision that'll be left to the President, but I'll just say a lot has been laid bare. A lot has been shown to the world about what our allies would be willing to do for the United States of America when we undertake an effort of this scope on behalf of the free world. These are missiles that don't even range the United States of America; they range allies and others. And yet when we ask for additional assistance or simple access, basing, and overflight, we get questions or roadblocks or hesitations. And the President's pointing out you don't have much of an alliance if you have countries that are not willing to stand with you when you need them. He's simply pointing that out. And ultimately it'll be his decision of what that looks like after this is complete. I'll just—I won't talk about any particular target, this really builds on Jillian's question too that I don't think I completely answered. But you know the joint force is the most professional force in the world, and we have numerous processes and systems to carefully consider the whole range of considerations from civilian risk to legal considerations with any target. And as targets come before us, we run them through the same process that we always do and always strike lawful targets in accordance with the normal procedures that we use. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Thank you, I appreciate it. On Sunday, President Trump said that Iran's first regime was all destroyed and the second one is mostly dead, and the third regime that we're on right now has been much more reasonable in negotiations. Do you agree with that assessment and why? I would defer to the negotiating team there. They're the ones talking to him, but they're getting a back-and-forth on terms, which is a productive development. We stand right there next to our negotiating team, always willing and prepared to put them in an even better position. Thank you very much, thank you. Thank you.

LIVE: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Gen. Dan Caine hold press conference

MS NOW(9.7M) ·37:40 ·27.0K views · 2026-03-31
Pete Hegseth (Defense Secretary) Dan Caine (General)
Operation Epic Fury has reached a decisive phase, with over 11,000 targets struck in 30 days and the systematic dismantling of Iran’s defense industrial base.
TL;DR Operation Epic Fury has reached a decisive phase, with over 11,000 targets struck in 30 days and the systematic dismantling of Iran’s defense industrial base. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Gen. Dan Caine report a collapsing Iranian military morale and a strategic...

TL;DR

Operation Epic Fury has reached a decisive phase, with over 11,000 targets struck in 30 days and the systematic dismantling of Iran’s defense industrial base. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Gen. Dan Caine report a collapsing Iranian military morale and a strategic shift toward "negotiating with bombs" to ensure Iran never acquires nuclear weapons.

Operational Status and Battlefield Momentum

  • Scale of Destruction: General Caine confirmed that the joint force has struck more than 11,000 targets in the first month of operations, including 150+ Iranian ships and all Jamaran-class frigates.
  • Dynamic Targeting: Last night alone, the U.S. executed 200 "dynamic strikes"—real-time targeting of mobile launchers and troop formations based on immediate intelligence rather than pre-planned coordinates.
  • Degrading Capabilities:
      • Air Superiority: The U.S. has begun overland B-52 missions, indicating total control of the skies.
      • Enemy Morale: Secretary Hegseth reported widespread desertions and key personnel shortages within the Iranian military as command bunkers are neutralized.
      • Fire Volume: The last 24 hours saw the lowest number of enemy missile and drone launches since the conflict began.
  • The "Arsenal of Freedom": The U.S. defense industrial base is operating at its most vibrant levels since WWII, contrasting sharply with Iran’s defense industry, which Hegseth described as "nearly completely destroyed" and unable to reconstitute.

Strategic Objectives and "Negotiation by Fire"

  • Nuclear Non-Negotiable: The primary objective remains the permanent prevention of an Iranian nuclear weapon.
  • Diplomatic Pressure: Secretary Hegseth confirmed that negotiations are active and "gaining strength." He described the current Iranian regime (the "third regime") as more reasonable in back-and-forth terms.
  • The Military Mandate: Hegseth characterized the current strategy as "negotiating with bombs," using military intensity to compel Iran to accept U.S. terms regarding its nuclear program and regional interference.
  • Strait of Hormuz: President Trump has signaled a demand for the Strait to be "open for business." Hegseth emphasized that while the U.S. has set the conditions for this, it is an international waterway, and allies (specifically mentioning the UK's Royal Navy) are expected to "step up" rather than relying solely on the U.S.

Troop Readiness and Logistics

  • Wartime Speed: Hegseth reported a distinct shift in troop culture compared to the Iraq/Afghanistan eras, noting an "urgency to finish the job" rather than a focus on the next rotation.
  • Technological Ingenuity:
      • Junior personnel are actively developing new tactics to neutralize maneuvering missiles.
      • Intelligence analysts are refining target packages faster than the enemy can adapt.
  • Force Protection: The Department has prioritized "maxed out" defensive capabilities, including layered air defenses (Patriots, THAAD, electronic warfare) and a "discipline of bunker use" to mitigate enemy retaliation.

Alliance Relations and "Boots on the Ground"

  • Allied Friction: Secretary Hegseth expressed frustration with certain NATO allies, noting that while Iranian missiles range those allies and not the U.S. mainland, some countries have still created "roadblocks" regarding basing and overflight access.
  • Strategic Ambiguity: When asked about a ground invasion, Hegseth refused to rule out "boots on the ground," arguing that a commander should never tell the enemy what they are *unwilling* to do.
      • He emphasized that while 15 different ground options exist, the administration intends to avoid the mistakes of long-term occupations seen in previous decades.

Notable Quotes

"I met a junior airman... who, when asked what they needed, she simply looked up at me with a sly smile on her face and said, 'More bombs, sir. And bigger bombs.' We will happily oblige her." — Secretary Pete Hegseth
"Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that this new regime... is in a better place if they make that deal. In the meantime, we'll negotiate with bombs." — Secretary Pete Hegseth
"To our adversaries out there, I remind you to beware of the American workforce... the distance from that factory floor and that assembly line to the front line is incredibly short." — Gen. Dan Caine
"You can't just have flags, you have to have formations. You can't just have a few ships, you have to have enough to effect change." — Secretary Pete Hegseth (referring to international allies)
Well, over the weekend, I had the opportunity to visit our troops fighting in Operation Epic Fury. We were on the ground, excuse me, in CENTCOM on Saturday for about half the day. For reasons of operational security, so those troops are not targeted, the places and bases will not be named. Suffice it to say, the trip was an honor. I had a chance to bear witness, and I witnessed the best of America. I witnessed warriors, a brotherhood of men and women—warriors all. Active duty, Guard, and Reserve, they are united in their love for each other, their shared purpose, and their commitment to the mission. I witnessed sheer competency. I watched a Private First Class confidently calling out an enemy missile trajectory to a room full of officers. Everyone was focused. The room was locked in. Two enemy missiles were successfully shot down. I spoke to Air Force and Navy pilots on the flight line who every day both deliver bombs deep into Iran but also shoot down drones defending their base. Many had just returned from the skies of Iran and Tehran. I put on a headset and spoke to one crew in the cockpit, locked and loaded each and every day. I witnessed ingenuity—American ingenuity. I met the young Army officer who figured out how to neutralize maneuvering enemy missiles, saving countless lives. His commander confirmed that whoops and cheers erupted in the Tactical Operations Center when his new approach was first successful. I met the Air Force intel analyst who refines target packages faster than the enemy can adapt. I actually gave him my card and told him to keep me posted on the ground truth. I did the same with his boss, a Colonel with a heart the size of Texas and a beautiful deployment mustache to match. I witnessed lethality. I met a junior airman as the sun was going down and a chill was setting on the tarmac. When asked what she needed, she simply looked up at me with a sly smile on her face and said, "More bombs, sir. And bigger bombs." We will happily oblige her. I met the Army targeting team who found and sunk the pride and joy of the Iranian navy. Their fighting position was plastered with images of sunken enemy ships. I also witnessed urgency. Right when we landed, another C-17 landed just minutes after us. Within 30 seconds of the aircraft coming to a full stop, a team on the ground pulled up and the cargo was being uploaded. That is wartime speed. To a man and to a woman—on the ground, in the air, on the flight line, and in the TOC—I heard, "We want everything faster. Higher op tempo. Wartime speed." The feeling was the exact opposite of the rotational units year after year in the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan that were so familiar. In those wars, it was always about the next rotation, never knowing when the mission would end or exactly what the mission was year after year. Not with Epic Fury. I witnessed urgency to finish the job and urgency to achieve mission success. They are not looking at the next rotation; they are only moving as fast as possible to win. I got a chance to troop the line to witness firsthand what we already know is true. I spoke to all ranks and all services, none of whom knew we were coming. It was not rehearsed or scripted. Sometimes we just wandered. What I witnessed was motivation. It was sheer mission focus. It was the American warrior unleashed. It was the kind of warfighting American spirit that comes with a clear mission against a determined enemy. A crew chief we flew with summed it up nicely. He said, "It's been a busy few weeks, sir. Tough stuff. But I'm so honored to be called up. This fight is long overdue. We need to address it for our kids. We cannot pass the buck. Please thank the President from us." I heard that time and time again. I asked each young American, "What do you need?" and nobody said better equipment. Nobody said more comfortable living conditions. Nobody said "send me home." Well, of course, eventually we want all those things, and they do too, but what those Americans said to me—young and old, officer and NCO, male and female, black and white—was "let's finish the mission." They said, "Get us even more bombs, bigger bombs, more targets, let us finish this." In fact, Admiral Cooper noted this morning that the three Air Force captains shot down by Kuwaiti friendly fire early in the fight weeks ago never left the theater. All three dropped bombs over Tehran last night. These men and women live the Iranian threat every day—incoming missiles and drones. They know what a world would look like if Iran had the most dangerous weapons in the world, a nuclear weapon. As President Trump has said time and time again for years, Iran cannot have a nuclear bomb, and they won't. These troops want to finish this fight for their kids and their grandkids. This is about history. This is about legacy. Success matters. Because of this President and these Americans, we are closer than ever before to winning. President Trump is doing what no other president had the guts to do. Previous presidents were all talk. He's all action. On the battlefield, because of the latitude the President has given us, American firepower is only increasing. Iran's is decreasing. We have more and more options, and they have less. Just one month in, only one month, we set the terms. The upcoming days will be decisive. Iran knows that, and there's almost nothing they can militarily do about it. Yes, they will still shoot some missiles, but we will shoot them down. Of note, the last 24 hours saw the lowest number of enemy missiles and drones fired by Iran. They will go underground, but we will find them. We recently destroyed another one of their command bunkers, and their leaders were forced to flee. There was no water, no power, no oxygen, and no command and control. Their faith in their caves is diminishing. The latest intel is clear out of CENTCOM: our strikes are damaging the morale of the Iranian military, leading to widespread desertions and key personnel shortages. It is causing frustrations amongst senior leaders. Just last night, we had 200 dynamic strikes alone. A dynamic strike is a strike where a pilot leaves and, during their flight, they get a new target set based on real-time intel given to them—a new launcher, a new location, a new troop formation. A dynamic target is one that changes while you're in the air because of improved intelligence. There were 200 dynamic strikes alone in addition to the pre-planned targets. The video the President posted last night of Isfahan showed an ammo depot struck by US bombers. You see, you don't get to see many of those videos because, as a reminder, Iran has still shut off the internet to 99.9% of its population. But if Iran is wise, they will cut a deal. President Trump doesn't bluff, and he does not back down. You can ask Khamenei about that. The new Iranian regime should know that by now. This new regime, because regime change has occurred, should be wiser than the last. President Trump will make a deal; he is willing, and the terms of the deal are known to them. If Iran is not willing, then the United States War Department will continue with even more intensity. Standing here this morning in this briefing room, in my mind's eye, I'm actually looking out at the groups I met this weekend: the pilots, the logisticians, the intel analysts, the targeters, the sustainers, the flight crews, the air defenders, the base security, and those maintainers that we walked up to at sunset. May God watch over all of them each day and each night. May His almighty and eternal arms of providence stretch over them and protect them and bring them peace in the name of Jesus Christ. Amen. Mr. Chairman, over to you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, and good morning everyone. Thank you again for being here. As operations continue, I remain deeply grateful for the service and determination of the 2.8 million members of our joint force, each of whom is serving something greater than themselves. Every day those deployed, and in many cases those at home who are deploying forward in bomber pulses and others, put themselves in harm's way. We owe them a deep debt of gratitude. I remain especially grateful for our fallen who gave the ultimate sacrifice. We'll never forget their valor and their determination to do something greater than themselves. Each day we continue to earn what they've given to us. This morning, I want to share that yesterday the joint force had the honor of participating in the burial of Colonel Clarence "Bud" Anderson. He passed away in May of 2024 at the ripe old age of 102. He was laid to rest in Arlington Cemetery yesterday morning alongside his wife of 70 years, Eleanor, whom he married on February 23rd, 1945. She passed away in 2015 at the age of 92. Yesterday, a legend to our Air Force and fighter pilots everywhere was honored with a combination flyby of F-35s and a four-ship of P-51 Mustangs. He was one of the most prolific flying aces of World War II and the highest-scoring P-51 ace with 16 and a quarter kills. He served from 1942 to 1972, and his last combat tour was flying F-105 Thunderchiefs over North Vietnam. For anyone that had the chance to meet Colonel Anderson, you knew what an incredibly special man he was. That's true for each and every one of our World War II vets. They become fewer and fewer with each passing day. They are the Greatest Generation and give us the gift of an incredible example as we execute Operation Epic Fury today. Colonel Anderson, we mourn for your loss and remember that smile on the right side as you went out to do our nation's business. I'd like to now share an operational update. Our joint force continues to focus on our military objectives as we systematically continue to degrade and destroy Iran's ability to project power and threaten stability beyond its borders. First, the joint force continues to destroy Iran's ballistic missile and UAS capabilities. We remain focused on interdicting and destroying the logistical and supply chains that feed these programs. This remains a truly joint effort prosecuted around the clock from air, land, sea, space, and cyberspace. Long-range bombers from US Strategic Command are coordinating with tactical fighter aircraft from our joint force. These are launched from bases around the region and the continental United States. Simultaneously, Navy fighters and sailors continue to project power from the sea. Army and Marine artillery units continue to execute long-range precision fires deep into enemy territory against high-value targets. Meanwhile, on the defense side, our Army and air defenders and aviators remain vigilant, forming a shield to protect our forces and our partners. Together we continue to deliver precision strikes against key manufacturing nodes, component storage sites, and research facilities deep within Iranian territory. Over the past 30 days, we struck more than 11,000 targets. Given the increase in air superiority, we've successfully started to conduct the first over-land B-52 missions. This allows us to continue to get on top of the enemy and switch towards more and more dynamic targets, servicing mobile targets around the battlespace. We've continued to do the work against Iran's missile, drone, and naval production facilities. Second, on the Navy front, we continue to assert dominance over the Iranian navy. We remain focused on targeting their mine-laying capability and their naval assets. We've now started to work attack helicopters and other close air support assets into the naval domain. CENTCOM continues to identify and work against naval depots and storage areas. We've taken out, again, more than 150 ships, including all Jamaran-class frigates inside their navy. Third, we continue to prosecute our campaign against their defense industrial base at scale. This includes factories, warehouses, nuclear weapons research and development labs, and the associated infrastructure required for Iran to reconstitute its combat capability. I'd like to continue my theme of highlighting the incredible contributions of Americans to our joint force. Today I want to talk a little bit about a different front line, a line that doesn't have bunkers or guard posts but is just as critical to our nation's security: our national assembly lines. Today I want to recognize a group of Americans who live at the beginning of our nation's combat power. These are the Americans who actually make our weapons, both inside our defense industrial base but even more broadly inside our national industrial base. We could not do our jobs without the men and women across our country who show up every day around the clock to a factory floor. These great Americans are the core of American combat power. They're the machinists running high-tech CNC machines cutting raw blocks of metal into incredibly precise parts. They're the assembly workers painstakingly turning a kit of parts into a complex guidance system or precision munition. They are building a jet or submarine, or they're quality assurance technicians who ensure that when a warfighter pulls a trigger, the weapon works every time. This can be and is tough and gritty work. It's not a quiet office and a desk with paper. This is exactly the way this group of Americans likes it. I know this; I've seen it myself. It's often loud and dangerous work, demanding absolute focus for hours at a time with deep commitment to get it right every single time. It's hands-on work where one mistake can put an American's life at risk. A single misplaced wire or a microscopic flaw in a weld could mean the difference between mission success or mission failure. The difference is measured in the lives of our sons and daughters who we put on the field of battle. We rely on and trust in these great Americans. It's not just their manufacturing skill; it's their innovative minds and their entrepreneurial spirit. From those who build exquisite capabilities down to the mom-and-pop machine shops all over this great country, they live at the core of America's combat capability. There are examples of this throughout history, from building things like the F-117 Stealth Fighter and the B-2 Stealth Bomber to today's B-21 and F-47. It's work like in the shipyards of the East Coast that build America's nuclear submarines that allow us to patrol around the world. These innovators and workers don't get the same glory as a fighter pilot returning to a carrier deck at night, and yet they show up every day. Without them, we could not do the work that we are tasked to do. 24/7, they build the tools that we need to do our business. The skill and patriotism poured into every piece of hardware is seen and felt out at the edge of the force. You can see it, you can feel it, and it's real. So to the American workforce out there, thank you on behalf of the joint force. We carry the weapons that you build. The distance from that factory floor and that assembly line to the front line is incredibly short. Thank you, keep it up. And to our adversaries out there, I remind you to beware of the American workforce. We continue to press forward in our military objectives. The Secretary and I remain deeply humbled by the spirit, tenacity, and grit of our joint force. I want to, as always, thank the force and their families, and as always, remember our fallen. We carry them and their names every day. Sir, I'll turn it back over to you. Absolutely. Well, thank you, Mr. Chairman. Two things. He didn't note it, but in memory of Colonel Bud Anderson, the Chairman himself flew his F-16 to honor him recently. And thank you for highlighting the defense industrial base, the Arsenal of Freedom. It's worth noting no one has rebuilt that defense industrial base faster and with more purpose than President Trump in his first term, and now we're rebuilding it again. Our defense industrial base is more vibrant today than it's been since World War II and growing rapidly. Contrast that with Iran, whose defense industrial base is nearly completely destroyed. Their ability to rebuild the weapons of war they're using now is negligible. I think that contrast is an enduring legacy of this as well. Right here. Thank you for your time. We've had some signaling that the US will retake control of the Strait of Hormuz. At that point, what military posture will be imposed to guarantee safe passage for our allies, deter our enemies, and demonstrate US dominance in the region? And also, can you speak to how much America's adversaries—Russia, China, North Korea—how much they're supporting Iran's war machine at this point with arms and intelligence, and what we are militarily doing to punish the enemy coalition? Appreciate both questions. On the Strait of Hormuz, there are many more vessels flowing through today than there were, as the President has arranged. The President's been clear to Iran: open it for business or we have options, and we certainly do. When you look at what the Chairman laid out with the Navy and counter-mine capabilities, we've been focused from the beginning on attriting and defeating those capabilities. There's lots we're doing as well, some of which is known, some of which is not known, to set the conditions. I think the President was clear this morning that there are countries around the world who ought to be prepared to step up on this critical waterway as well. It's not just the United States Navy. Last time I checked, there was supposed to be a big bad Royal Navy that could be prepared to do things like that. So he's pointing out this is an international waterway that we use less than most, in fact, dramatically less than most. The world ought to pay attention and be prepared to stand up. President Trump's been willing to do the heavy lifting to address this threat of Iran. It's not just our problem set going forward, even though we have done the lion's share of preparation to ensure that that Strait will be open. As far as Russia and China, we know exactly what they're doing. We don't have to air publicly what all of that is, but where necessary, we're addressing it or confronting it head-on. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. David Zeig, The America's Voice. I witnessed that enthusiasm on the lines with our submarine builders; it's unbelievable. I just wanted to ask you, what's the status of the delivery of any hardened bunkers for additional protection for our troops? And without getting too specific, what kind of measures are we taking to protect some of the larger and more strategic aircraft like the Sentry and others? Well, it's a great question. I'll say what I witnessed was a completely locked-in discipline of bunker use and improvement. The first thing we did was set up a defense and make sure our defensive capabilities were maxed out before any of this even started. That included fortifications as much as possible, but it also included dispersal. If all of our people are in one place, you can imagine why that's a big problem. Dispersing is part of that defeat. Alongside that dispersal is more and more bunkers. Talking to base commanders and allies in Israel, rapidly fielding and improving those positions is a theater priority, no doubt. As are the air defenses. It's not just Patriots and THAADs; it's fighters in defensive CAPs, it's other kinetic defeat systems, it's electronic warfare. The defense of our troops and our assets is max. On some of those other assets like airframes, there's some things adversaries are doing to provide intel that they shouldn't. We're aware of it, and ultimately we move things around. One of the biggest principles is to not set predictable patterns. Thanks, Mr. Secretary. The US and Iran appear right now publicly to disagree about whether there are these negotiations ongoing. What impact does that have on military strategy? And, General, the President wrote yesterday that the US might destroy Iran's electricity generating plants and oil wells. Is there a way to do either of those things without seriously jeopardizing or seriously harming civilians? So on the talks, I can tell you having been with Steve and Jared and the Vice President and many others yesterday, they are very real. They are ongoing. They are active and I think gaining strength. We would much prefer to get a deal. If Iran was willing to relinquish material and ambitions they have, and open the Strait—great, that's the goal. We don't want to have to do more militarily than we have to. But I didn't mean it flippantly when I said in the meantime we'll negotiate with bombs. Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that they are in a better place if they make that deal. So we'll continue—we're working hand-in-hand, but the primary effort is a deal. We want that deal to be accomplished if at all possible. Thanks for the question. We're always thinking about those considerations and develop options to be able to mitigate those risks pursuant to the normal practices that we do in the military. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Is opening the Strait an essential objective to Operation Epic Fury, or is that the job of those other countries? And what is your message to Americans who love the President but are very worried about this notion of boots on the ground? Well, first on the Strait of Hormuz, our core objectives have been clear from day one. Defense, missile programs, the defense industrial base, and power projection. Wrapping it all is the fact that Iran is never going to have a nuclear weapon. Defeating the navy is a core part of ensuring they can't project that kind of power. This Strait of Hormuz issue is not just a United States of America problem set. We've been willing to lead, but it's not just us. Other countries should pay attention when the President speaks. He's pointing out, you know, you might want to start learning how to fight for yourself. You can't just have flags; you have to have formations. You can't just have a few ships; you have to have enough to effect change. That's why we're launching the Arsenal of Freedom tour. As far as boots on the ground, I don't understand why people wouldn't have faith in his ability to execute on this. Look at his track record of pursuing peace through strength. He's simply saying we're not going to foreclose any option. You can't fight and win a war if you tell your adversary what you are willing to do or what you are not willing to do. Our adversary right now thinks there are 15 different ways we could come at them with boots on the ground. And guess what? There are. If we needed to, we could execute those options. Or maybe we don't have to use them at all. But the point is to be unpredictable. If anybody has internalized the lessons of Iraq and Afghanistan, it is President Trump, and he's not going to repeat those lessons. Thank you, Secretary Hegseth. You said we're a month into Operation Epic Fury. How long until the objectives are achieved and is there a scenario where a deal is struck before the objectives have been achieved? And for General Caine, what other purposes might the soldiers and Marines serve? Well, it's military 101. Don't tell your enemy what you're willing to do and don't tell your enemy when you're willing to stop, especially an enemy that likes to hide in bunkers. So that's not a question I'm going to answer. We have our own goals and military objectives that we're moving toward. It will be the President's determination alone when those objectives are complete and when it serves the interest of the American people to cut that deal. Reagan, the range of military options those forces can offer are extensive, not just limited to forces on the ground. I wouldn't want to take away the President's decision space, but there are a multitude of things. Iran should note that they are out there as a pressure point. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Does the US assess that Al-Qaeda's current Amir, Saif al-Adel, is still in Iran? And Mr. Chairman, Iranian daily missile barrages have dropped dramatically, but they're not at zero. How do we get that closer to zero? I won't speak to a particular target. I'll just say that Al-Qaeda remains our enemy, obviously, and there are a lot of people on our target list in Iran. If they were to be harboring Al-Qaeda, they would certainly fit that list. The mobile targets that we continue to service, such as the 200 hits that were done last night, are one component of continuing to lower those numbers. The second impact we're having is the strikes against their defense industrial base at scale, which will not allow them to reload. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Do you still believe that the Iranian leadership would surrender? And is the United States still committed to NATO's collective defense? And Chairman Caine, what are your thoughts on the legality of striking desalination plants? I will just say that any mission that ends on our terms—you want to call it a surrender, call it a defeat—we remain committed to a conflict that ends on the President's terms. As far as NATO's concerned, that's a decision that'll be left to the President. I'll just say a lot has been laid bare about what our allies would be willing to do for the United States when we undertake an effort of this scope. These are missiles that range allies and others, and yet when we ask for simple access or basing, we get roadblocks. The President's pointing out you don't have much of an alliance if countries are not willing to stand with you. Ultimately, it'll be his decision of what that looks like. I won't talk about any particular target. The joint force is the most professional force in the world, and we have numerous processes to carefully consider everything from civilian risk to legal considerations. We always strike lawful targets in accordance with normal procedures. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. On Sunday, President Trump said that Iran's first regime was all destroyed and the second one is mostly dead. He said the third regime has been much more reasonable. Do you agree with that assessment and why? I would defer to the negotiating team there. They're the ones talking to them, but they're getting a back-and-forth on terms, which is a productive development. We stand right there next to our negotiating team, always prepared to put them in an even better position. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you, sir.

(Tim Carney) Finding Place in an Era of Loneliness: Keynote Lunch

Pepperdine School of Public Policy(24.4K) ·49:14 ·2 views · 2026-03-31
Loneliness has become a public policy crisis driven by the collapse of "little platoons"—the local institutions like churches, civic groups, and neighborhoods that provide social capital.
TL;DR Loneliness has become a public policy crisis driven by the collapse of "little platoons"—the local institutions like churches, civic groups, and neighborhoods that provide social capital. Reversing this epidemic requires moving beyond hyper-individualism toward a culture...

TL;DR

Loneliness has become a public policy crisis driven by the collapse of "little platoons"—the local institutions like churches, civic groups, and neighborhoods that provide social capital. Reversing this epidemic requires moving beyond hyper-individualism toward a culture of communal interdependence and local service.

The Geography of Social Cohesion

  • The "Orange City" Model: Speaker Tim Carney highlights Orange City, Iowa, as a pinnacle of social cohesion. Despite being politically conservative, residents "live left" by providing robust community support and social safety nets without government intervention.
  • The Institutional Foundation: High-cohesion areas are defined by "hub-and-spoke" relationships where residents are bonded through shared institutions (churches, schools, local businesses) rather than just bilateral friendships.
  • The Fayette County Decline: Carney contrasts successful communities with Fayette County, Pennsylvania, where the disappearance of steel mills was followed by the closure of coffee shops and churches.
      • The Safety Net Collapse: Without these institutions, residents lack a social "insurance policy" to help during personal crises, leading to a sense that the American Dream is dead.

The "Two Sides" of Isolation: Collectivism and Atomism

  • The Tocqueville Warning: Carney argues that collectivism (state control) and atomism (individual isolation) are not opposites but reinforcing tendencies. As horizontal bonds between neighbors weaken, the vertical bond between the individual and the state strengthens.
  • "The Life of Julia": Carney critiques the 2012 Obama campaign infographic as the ultimate expression of atomization, depicting an individual who relies solely on federal programs (Head Start, loans, Medicare) from birth to death without any depicted community, family, or religious support.
  • The Post-Pandemic Impact: COVID-19 lockdowns accelerated loneliness by prioritizing "quinoa delivery" over human interaction. Carney notes that social media and remote services have taught Gen Z to navigate life without ever dealing with another human being.

The Crisis of Family and Fertility

  • The Marriage Gap: The collapse of community is most acute in the working class, where marriage rates for women without college degrees have plummeted from 85% in 1960 to below 50% today.
  • The Baby Bust: U.S. fertility has dropped from 2.1 in 2007 to below 1.6 today. Carney attributes this not just to economic factors but to the loss of the "village" necessary to raise children.
  • The "Worship of Autonomy": Both the political Left and Right have embraced a "hyper-individualism" that views human beings as "free-floating atomized bundles of rights" rather than communal creatures.
      • On the Left: This manifests as a rejection of family responsibility and local community input in schools.
      • On the Right: This manifests as "Giga Based" isolationism—distrusting all strangers and seeking to escape civilization entirely.

Policy Implications and Solutions

  • The Institutional Enemy: Carney asserts that the true enemy of a centralizing state is not the "rugged individual" but the "institution of civil society" (churches, private schools, local clubs) because these groups claim a loyalty that competes with the state.
  • Localism as Patriotism: True patriotism is experienced at the local level (e.g., a town square July 4th celebration). Because America is too large for national cohesion, the "epidemic of loneliness" must be solved through a hundred thousand small, local solutions.
  • Service Over Dialogue: Carney suggests that the best way to bridge polarization and loneliness is not through discussing ideas, but through shared service—such as coaching Little League or volunteering for church cleanups—where people of different backgrounds work toward a common goal.

Notable Quotes

"Collectivism and atomism are not opposite ends of the political spectrum but rather two sides of one coin. They are closely related tendencies and they often coexist and reinforce one another, each making the other possible." — Tim Carney (quoting Yuval Levin/Tocqueville)
"The enemy of the state is not the rugged individual, but is the institution of civil society." — Tim Carney
"[The spirit of democracy] not only makes each man forget his ancestors, but it hides his descendants from him and separates him from his contemporaries. It constantly leads him back toward himself alone and threatens to finally confine him wholly in the solitude of his own heart." — Alexis de Tocqueville (quoted by Carney)
"We really our authentic self can only be found by going off to a lake... but really, what we are is social, communal, familial creatures, and I think that we widely reject that." — Tim Carney
Welcome to the School of Public Policy. I'm Pete Peterson, the very grateful Dean of the Graduate School. And, I'm sure all of you are asking a, a similar question: what does a school of public policy care about a topic like loneliness? There are really two reasons why we're here. One is that, as a policy program from our founding back in 1997—with folks like James Q. Wilson, Michael Novak, Jack Kemp, and Kevin Starr deeply involved in our founding—we've always understood that there is both a quantitative aspect of public policy but also a human part of public policy. We are dealing with the public part of public policy, one that can be shaped and determined by cultures and histories in different places and certainly in different times. And in that, we care about very human problems. And if it sounds like if you're here, you have a sense that this discussion around a topic of loneliness is one that, even leading up to COVID, but certainly afterward, is one that has come under great scrutiny and discussion. So much so that our past Surgeon General called this an epidemic, quantifying different health-related aspects to increasing levels of loneliness. In that, policymakers have increasingly become engaged in trying to respond to this issue. Some of you may have followed the story, even beyond the shores of the United States, that in the United Kingdom, they actually created, as the Brits are wont to do, a Ministry of Loneliness. I think that's a crazy idea. But suffice it to say, and I know this from working with local government officials—and we'll hear from some of them in our last panel this afternoon—that this really is an issue that is both multi-faceted, multi-disciplinary, and multi-sector. One that is being engaged both by academia across disciplines but also by governments and non-profit organizations that have this sense, again, I think increasingly understood following COVID, that we are dealing with some chronic issues that in one way, shape, or form fall under this umbrella. This is not the first time that the School of Public Policy has engaged on this set of issues. In fact, some of our speakers here today, including our keynoter, have spoken here on a number of occasions on the issues related to loneliness. In that, it will be a topic that we will continue to engage in. So it's natural, in a certain sense, that the School of Public Policy would be discussing this, even though I would argue there's probably not another policy school in America that cares much about this topic, much less explores it. But it takes more than that. It takes people who are willing to come alongside you to fund and support topics that might not get a lot of coverage in other sectors of academia. You see here on your agenda that this event is made possible through a grant from our friends at Fieldstead and Company. And with that, I wanted to thank Howard and Roberta Ahmanson, who are here and funding this event today. Thank you to you both. For a time here, we had a long-time faculty member, Ted McAllister, and on two occasions, we had a visiting faculty member here, Bill McClay. And when they would come together to design events, a couple of which were on the topics of place and loneliness, I would always describe them as Bill and Ted's Excellent Adventure. And, in many ways, the relationship that the School of Public Policy has had with Fieldstead, and particularly with you, Howard, has been a series of excellent adventures. And I have no doubt that this is going to be another one. It takes people who are willing to think more broadly about topics and areas of exploration that may not get the attention that they deserve. And, over a series of events and a series of years, I've just been so grateful for your—not just support financially, but also your thought leadership and partnership and friendship in thinking through topics and events like this. You see on your agenda, we are going to kick off with a keynote from my friend, Tim Carney. You will also see at your tables a printed out PowerPoint presentation that Tim is going to be working through. So just to make sure that you have those on hand. We will then go into our first panel on Loneliness, Place, and the American Experience. That will be followed by the Social Science of Loneliness, and then we will move to the third panel looking at the American response to loneliness through non-profits and local government organizations. We will then conclude our afternoon together with a conversation between me and Grace Olmstead, the author of the book, "Uprooted." You know, it surprised both of us, Grace, when we were talking about this event and the possibility of you coming, that this week actually marks the fifth anniversary of that book and the publication of it. So both "Alienated America"—which I remember speaking about this book, Tim, well before it was published—but also with "Uprooted," I'm just so delighted that the School of Public Policy plays a role in connecting great minds like this, both to our students and our community here. So let's get started. Tim Carney is a senior fellow at the American Enterprise Institute. He is also the author of a really foundational book that helped, I think, millions of Americans better understand this issue and also its related impacts both culturally and politically. Please join me in welcoming Tim Carney. Thank you, Pete, for having me here. The first time Pete had me here, yes, it was before the book came out. In fact, I wrote a couple chap-, or I rewrote a couple chapters for the book in this building. He said, "Can you come out? You'd only have to stay one or two nights." I said, "Could I stay four or five?" You see, I, my wife and I, we have, we have six kids and, it's, it's the greatest blessing I've ever had, and getting away from them is the greatest blessing for me as an author. Pray for my wife Katie, who is home with five of those six kids right now. The oldest is in college, we didn't like offload one of them. I'm going to talk about the book and the slideshow, but in the end, what I'm hoping to do, it's sort of like a baseball leadoff batter has certain jobs. You want to see a lot of pitches, you want to, ideally get on base for the bigger hitters later.
