I'm going to try to throw out some ideas that I've been thinking about that touch on loneliness from a higher level and hopefully that can be some of the fodder for the day. But I'll start with the story about the question of whether the American Dream is dead, because that was a huge thing that we totally separately—a lot of the people here and around the country—saw something last decade. We said, "Wait a second, a lot of people think the American Dream is dead, and this might not just be an economic story. Something's going on." So it's a story about faith, it's a story about family. My book came out—I was writing it right around the 2016 election, so to some extent, it's a book about Donald Trump and how he became the President of the United States. Don't worry, I won't talk about politics too much. I'm a political reporter, though, and so my story begins in Iowa. And I'm an Irish Catholic, so if you turn the page, you will see my story begins in a bar. Joe's Place in Iowa City. You like the sort of very Iowa colors on that. I think it's on Iowa Ave in Iowa City in Iowa. And I was talking to a couple that worked at the university there, and the one woman told me that she was from Orange City, Iowa. Now, I had been covering the caucuses for decades, and I didn't know about Orange City. So I asked her, again, Irish Catholic, we sometimes, if you know your history, can get triggered by the color orange. So I, I said, "What's orange about it?" And she said, "Well, we're Dutch." Now, I'm from New York, and so in New York, you're like, you're Irish, you're Italian, our streets are named after Dutch people, but I think the rest of the country doesn't really have ethnicity, right? They're all—I said, "How, how Dutch are you really?" And she said, "Let me put it this way: we used to march in our wooden clogs past Windmill Square for the annual Tulip Festival." And she went on, and it was like a send-up of Dutch towns. I think I've got a picture of Windmill Square there and then the parade. So I went out to Orange City, and I went, I covered a Jeb Bush event. Remember that? He ran for president. I covered a Jeb Bush event, and it was at Dordt College, a Christian college. And the first person I talked to was a grandmother named Wilhelmina, who was bragging that she was named after the Queen of the Netherlands through both World Wars. And then every other student I talked to there, again, they were proud of their Dutchness. If they weren't from there, they were from Pella, Iowa, you know, and they were super conservative and they didn't like Trump. And, I did talk to one fairly liberal guy, the husband of a professor, and he said, "Well, the thing about people out here is they may vote right, but they live left." And I thought, "Ooh, maybe I've found something. Is this some like den of pot-smoking swingers in the middle of Iowa?" But no, he said, "What I mean is that, you know, people will, they, they care about the planet and they look out for their neighbors, but they do it without the government." And I thought it was a little annoying that caring about the planet and looking out for your neighbors was called living left, but I set that aside to say, "I understand what he means. There is really strong social cohesion in Sioux County, Orange City, Iowa." And why is that? And then, well, and so then I went out to Holland, Michigan around the time of the Michigan primary and found the same sort of thing. In fact, politically, they were very similar. Donald Trump's single worst county in the Iowa caucuses was Sioux County. Donald Trump—and I've got the map of Michigan there, the red part are the counties that Trump won, the orange, the yellowish ones are the ones that Cruz won—and I asked Cruz, I think right after the Michigan primary, I said, "I've noticed that you win all the counties with high Dutch populations. Why is it?" And he says, "Well, Tim, you know, you've met the Dutch people. They're really nice. Donald, Donald is not so nice. They want to vote for someone nice." And I'm thinking, "Like Ted Cruz? Really?" So anyway, some people, another theory was that the Dutch didn't like Trump because he had appropriated the color orange. I rejected that theory as well. So I went out to Oostburg, Wisconsin, which is Dutch for, like, Eastburg or something. And there I saw what I had missed in these previous places. What was going on. Why these Dutch places that voted for Ted Cruz—it didn't have so much to do with Ted Cruz, it was that they were conservative and they rejected Trump's line that the American Dream was dead. So why was the American Dream alive in these places? Well, I sat at the diner at Judy's Place, the name of the diner. I sat at the counter and in come all the families from the—I'll forget the names of the churches, but it's like Bethel Orthodox gets let out. And then the families come out about an hour later from the Christian Reformed Church. And then the Reformed Church of America, which again, to an East Coast Catholic, I did not know the distinctions between them. They have even different colored hymnals, so it was very offensive that I had confused them. And then the fourth Christian Reformed Church, like Dutch Calvinist church, gets let out, and the diner is now totally swarming. And I realized that it was the churches, that the people who had come over in the 1800s from the Netherlands hadn't just planted churches, they had planted incredibly tight-knit communities. One of the guys there, who was not terribly religious, complained to me. He showed up to his kid's public school concert. This is a guy who at the diner always has the grease under his fingernails from fixing the farmers' tractors, and he shows up at the Christmas concert and he's there like two minutes before it starts and there's nowhere to sit. So this was his complaint about Oostburg. And he looks over and he sees a private school, Christian school family, big family taking up six seats. He's like, "Dave, what are you, what are you doing? You don't even have a kid at the school." And Dave says, "Well, we had to come see our kids sing." Our kids, meaning the children of Oostburg. And that was actually the title of a subsequent book by Robert Putnam after "Bowling Alone." I've got other slides in here about Silver Spring, Maryland and the Jewish communities there, but then also Chevy Chase, the upper-middle-class neighborhoods. Oh, well, Chevy Chase is not upper-middle-class, it's rich. It's the richest—the village of Chevy Chase is the richest part of the DC area, which unfortunately is kind of the richest part of America. I don't think that's a good sign for the Republic. And so that also, same political thing. They voted for like Kasich or Rubio, all those really rich tight-knit spots. And I have conservative friends who assume that like the rich people all live behind gated homes and they hire their servants to take the kids to Little League and there's no community connection, and that's just not true. I mean, upper-middle-class Democrat voters in the United States finish school, get a job, get married, raise kids, stay married, and probably coach Little League—the success sequence. I added the coach Little League; that's not the way most people define it. And so these two places that in different ways rejected Donald Trump in 2016, they sound really different: Christian conservative middle-class Oostburg, Orange City; upper-middle-class wealthy left-of-center, very college-educated Chevy Chase. I have on here Grosse Pointe. They're not two different kinds of places, they're one kind of place. These are places with institutions of civil society that bring people together. Whether it's Judy's Place and the Christian Reformed Church or the local country club, and that's what that's a picture of in Chevy Chase, but it's also the professional networks. Chevy Chase actually has like a local neighborhood bar. If you read, Brett Kavanaugh used to go there. He likes beer. And people belong to things. And it's so easy when I came here and I spoke to the students, I said, "I'm talking about, I'm trying to describe to you a fish what water is like." If you end up as a college kid in America and if you're in this room, you know that you've been totally immersed in institutions of civil society—things you belong to, things that you without thinking say "we." You know, with my kid's school, I say about the lacrosse team—and I would never touch a lacrosse stick in my life—I say, "Did you hear we beat Gonzaga yesterday?" because my kid's school is "we." Growing up in New York, our hometown, Pelham, 12,000 people, it was a "we." And so we belong to fewer and fewer of those things. There's pictures of when my daughter was in the hospital, and I want to move on quickly, so I'll just say: my kids remember when their youngest sibling was in the hospital for a week because they said that was the best week of eating we ever had. And a lot of you know what I'm talking about. The freezer was packed with the meals that people brought because my wife and I were stuck at Children's Hospital all week. And they would, my friends would sneak in like chocolate for my wife and—well, they were allowed to bring in the chocolate. They, they would sneak beer in for me and one of the nurses was like, "Who are all these people constantly bringing you things?" I said, "Oh, that's a, that's a guy I coach Little League with. That's a guy from my parish. This guy's my colleague at AEI, that's my editor at the Examiner." I realized this sort of swarm of friends bringing us stuff was not a swarm because it wasn't just a bunch of bilateral relationships. This was a hub-and-spoke thing. Every time I described the friend, there was an institution that bound us together: the book club, the parish, the employer, the Little League team, etc. And so that's what civil society does. It makes it so that your freezer is overflowing if something happens to you. It's an insurance policy that you don't think about. You don't think, "Oh, it's good that I belong to all these things because that way if something goes wrong"—no, you pour. That's why civil capital, social capital, a term I don't use that often, it's apt. You gain capital by pouring in your volunteer time and then you draw it out when you need it. And Raj Chetty found that the biggest causes of upward mobility were intact families and his various measures of—and Putnam's various measures of—social capital. So that is what makes people do better in life is belonging to things. In "Alienated America," I talked about my visit to Fayette County, Pennsylvania, which I picked because a study found that it had the biggest drop-off in church attendance for both Christians and—for both Protestant Christians and Catholics. And so I drove out to Fayette a little bit before the 2016 election and pulled over to the side of the road and on Yelp searched for the right kind of bar I wanted to go to. Now, the right kind of bar you want to go to should be on a state highway or a US route. This is if you want to talk to Trump voters, frankly. But it also, because a man has needs, it also should have good barbecue or good wings. And I find Smitty's in Uniontown, PA. Excellent reviews for the wings; the negative reviews are things like, "the bartender alternated between calling everybody honey and cursing at them." I was like, "Okay, this sounds like the kind of place I want to go." So I show up at Smitty's and I'm there, I was actually at the bar sort of researching some of the data and seeing how Fayette County, where Uniontown is, had all these bad numbers: high school dropouts, drug overdoses, yada yada. And I start talking to everybody. I'm writing about politics, but I'm also, I want to research their culture, and I ask about the economy, which is an easier way to start. And I get two conflicting stories: "the economy's horrible, you can't hire anybody," or "the economy's horrible, there are no jobs." It's like, "How can we have both of these?" And then one guy's like, "Well, no, there is an explanation. It's welfare." And they start telling all these welfare queen stories you hear, you know, "somebody used their food stamps to buy dog food," yada yada. And they're talking about how lazy everybody is, and I realize it's on a Tuesday. Now, I was in the bar at 2:00 on a Tuesday because it was literally my job. I don't know that that was true of anybody else, and so I asked Dave at the end of the bar, I said, "Dave, why, why are you in the bar at 2:00 on a Tuesday?" He said, "Well, my back," and he had all these back surgeries. And I said, "So all the jobs around here are manual labor, there's no like desk jobs?" He said, "Tim, I can't sit at a desk for half an hour." I said, "Dave, you've been sitting at the bar for 90 minutes." Yeah, Dave had been sitting at the bar for 90 minutes. But that was when Dave looked at me and he said, "Well, I'm numb today because my son died this morning." Bartender told me Dave hadn't had a drink in five years until that day. And so all this data that I knew about Fayette County, it was now looking me in the face. From my confines of Capitol Hill and Montgomery County, Maryland and Greenwich Village where I grew up, it was baffling to me why the American Dream seemed dead. And the numbers out there showed some problems, but until I looked it in the face, I didn't understand it. And the problem wasn't just that the steel mills went away. Steel mills went away 60 years ago. It was that the steel mills went away and then the coffee shop where the guys used to work went away and then the churches one by one closed down and then the marriages fell apart. Dave wasn't married to his son's mother anymore and there was no safety net. By safety net, I mean a social network of people who are there and institutions that are there to prop you up. Other consequences of the collapse of civil society is the collapse in marriage. I have a slide here that unfortunately is from 2016, but it shows it has three lines going down. The red line is college-educated women—are they married at age 40? It drops from 85% down to about 2/3. The blue line is women who didn't go to college—are they married at age 40? That also drops from 85% back in 1960 down to 50% in 2016. It's below 50% now. So the collapse in community is more acute—it's not absent in the upper-middle-class circles, but it's more acute in the working class—and the collapse in marriage is the most important consequence. Well, I would say the collapse in marriage and family formation. The baby bust—a lot of conservatives think that the complete collapse in the birth rate in the last 10 years has to do with just college-educated women, you know, being feminists who think "a woman needs a man like a fish needs a bicycle" or whatever. It's not true. It's actually, it's kind of two things. It's that people spend a lot of time in college, get married later, and can't have kids. But it's also working-class people aren't getting married and staying married. But it's also a million other things. And I always say a wise woman once put it well that "it takes a village to raise a child." And Hillary probably meant like the Department of Housing and Urban Development or whatever. But if you've raised kids, you know what it means. So that collapse—from 4.3 million babies born in 2007 to 3.6 born last year, from a fertility rate of 2.1 something down to below 1.6 in just 18 years, where now we have fewer children in America than we did last decade—and it's going down every single day. That is a consequence of the collapse of community. What else? So what causes this? So here I quote my boss at AEI, Yuval Levin, and he's sort of channeling Tocqueville. And I'm going to try to—Tocqueville is what I'm hoping to leave on the table for you guys here. "Collectivism and atomism are not opposite ends of the political spectrum but rather two sides of one coin. They are closely related tendencies and they often coexist and reinforce one another, each making the other possible." This cuts against the sort of dichotomy that I believed in when I was 18, that Ayn Rand laid out—that like the individual and the collective, that that was the grand struggle. And Mamdani, the mayor of New York, he said, "The warmth of collectivism will beat the frigidity of individualism." But like if that rallied right-wingers to individualism, then that just won a point for collectivism. Because the two are, again, they're not opposites, they're two sides of the same coin. And Tocqueville lays out how our stronger bond towards the central weakens our horizontal bonds with our friends. The enemy of the state is not the rugged individual, but is the institution of civil society. And a great demonstration of that was back during the 2012 election: "The Life of Julia." So I took some slides from this Barack Obama re-election thing. The whole idea of "The Life of Julia," if you turn over slide 19—I know the letters are tiny, but what happens is under President Obama, Julia gets like Head Start preschool, and under Romney, she would lose it all. So she's doing great. It's a little sad that she's the only one in her preschool there, but then at Head Start or the Race to the Top program under President Obama that of course evil Romney would take away. And then you see Julia in high school, where she was clearly the valedictorian because there's nobody else in her high school. Same thing in college. Number one in the class, probably goes on to, you know, edit the Law Review because there are no human beings, there's just the federal government. And then finally, another human appears. Under President Obama, Julia decides to have a child. Where did this happen? This is like the Virgin Birth, but instead of the Holy Spirit, it's the Department of Health and Human Services. Just the whole life is this individual connected with the state. So that was my argument in "Alienated America" and what's changed. I guess I started with Ted and Donald having made up, but of course the pandemic and the lockdowns that went along with it were absolutely an isolating thing. And there were people who reported positive things—who had more over-the-fence conversations with my over-the-fence neighbors in those days when my kids weren't in school than I had before or after. But it was terribly, terribly isolating for a lot of people. A lot of people who stopped going to church then never went back. And they lost touch with their neighbors. Just the months of those lockdowns made us realize how much human interaction mattered. Most people were able to get their paychecks through the Paycheck Protection Program and all sorts of things, but what they couldn't get was the human interaction that they needed and the things they belonged to. So this is why we lost our minds when the county—Montgomery County—tried to close our the non-public schools. They said the public schools aren't opening and the Catholic and Jewish and other schools are not allowed to open. And people said, "It's just a building, why are you so upset?" I said, "It's not a building. It's the center of our lives, our Catholic parochial school and then the independent boys' and girls' school. These were institutions around which we had built it." Lots of research afterwards has found that the loneliness increased after and because of the lockdowns. People say "the pandemic," I say "the lockdowns." And as Pete said, the Surgeon General put out the epidemic of loneliness and isolation afterwards. Social connection definitely decreased in all sorts of ways, including people didn't hang out with friends as much. There's a whole class of people who grew up thinking that commerce should happen without any human interaction. There was a famous tweet in 2022 or 2023 by this journalist who said, "I am going to murder the next delivery man who does not obey my 'just leave it on the front doorstep' command in DoorDash." Meaning, the delivery man just wanted to like make sure that she got her quinoa—or whatever it is, it's pronounced quinoa? Quinoa. I should not try to pronounce these words when I come to California. And she wants to murder this person because the social media, the lockdowns, all of that stuff have taught us to try to go through life. And you talk to a businessman who's like, "Well, I'm designing something for Gen Z." What they mean is, "I'm designing it to make it so that no human has to deal with another human being." Of all the places I get to go, one of the places I got to be was at the White House and the steps of the Capitol on January 6th, 2021. Walking from one to the other—or on my way to one—it was the Epiphany, and I remember these people were like begging for an epiphany. They said, "I just want to see. I don't want Trump to tell me anything, I want to see it," as if they could like see an election. But then as I was walking over, I decided I was going to ask everybody I could talk to a question that I knew no other reporter would ask. I said, "Where do you go to church?" Every single person I interviewed self-identified as a Christian and every single one of them said they did not go to church. "I do my own reading" was one of the things that was said. And "I do my own reading"—now again, I'm a Catholic, so obviously I have my biases against that sort of answer—but as a reporter, "I do my own research." Not that we don't read the Bible, we do, in small servings every Sunday. No, if you talk to somebody who says "I do my own research," there's a 50% chance that this is a person who thinks they're super independent-minded but just follows the rantings of some obscure conspiracy theorist. And if that's how American religion is changing, that's for the worse. Here I quote David, automechanic from Colorado: "I don't go to church but I'm religious. I do read the Bible, I do my own studies online and stuff. But really, since Trump came in, I really have felt a shift in the power I feel. It's definitely more positive than it was before." So then quick politics, same sort of thing happened in the 2024 Iowa caucuses. Trump did not have much of a challenge, but the more Dutch a county was, the more Mormon a county was, or the more educated a county was, the better Ron DeSantis or Nikki Haley did and the worse Trump did. Now, I think those are proxies for the stronger the community was, the less people voted for the guy who said and ran on "the American Dream is dead." Church attendance has been falling over the last eight years. Ryan Burge, who is an excellent religion scholar, says, "Iowa is culturally conservative non-practicing Christians at this point." That is what Burge said for why Trump was going to win the caucuses in 2024 after losing to Cruz in 2016. In 2008, over half of Republicans reported attending church at least once a month; in 2022, over half reported attending less than once a year. So most Republicans were at least monthly attendees; now most Republicans are less than annually. In general, church attendance has been falling since the lockdowns. As I said, people who stopped going haven't come back. Now I want to get into some of the ideas. My slides talk about the left, but I'm also going to talk about the right. I have a picture there of Nancy Pelosi taking the gavel surrounded by her grandkids. Now, if any of you guys know Chuck Schumer, a very weird thing about Chuck Schumer is that he's the biggest yenta of Congress. On his wall, he has pictures of all of his staffers whom he successfully got married off. This is something he has taken pride in, okay? And Joe Biden—Joe Biden's family is a mess, but you know what, he literally always takes a phone call if one of his grandkids calls. That's the old Democrats. Those are the people dying and fading away. The new ones, I show Kamala and her running mate Tim Walz and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez. AOC not interested in marriage for herself and warns that climate might be a good reason to not have children. Tim Walz, the running mate, he said the golden rule—now some of us have one golden rule, his was "mind your own business." "Mind your own darn business." Now, Tim Walz believed in high taxes, in high regulation, and he did all these lockdowns. But by "mind your own darn business," what he meant was the government will take care of your stuff and it's not up to you what we the government are doing or what your neighbors are doing at all. He proposed a ban, he passed a ban on book bans. What did that mean in practice? It meant parents and principals and teachers could have no say over what books were in the school library. Only officially certified librarians. So removing community input into schools. And that Democratic ticket won by two to one among unmarried women. And then I also have on slide 39, I have this freakout that happened in 2024 about The Atlantic. They ran an article about the fact that it's okay for kids to share bedrooms. And then somebody else—New Yorker maybe—ran an article about how the idea of "parentification," which started as this psychological idea of kids should not, like a girl without a father present or with an alcoholic father who has to take on motherly duties, how that can be harming. And then now parentification means giving your kids any responsibilities. And there's weirdly enough a freakout about sharing a bedroom or giving kids responsibilities. And then the loss of concern in the local. I wrote once about eight different city councils that passed stuff—resolutions—calling for a ceasefire in Gaza. That's not your job as a city council, but it's a lack of concern in the local. And then, of course, the closing argument for the Democrats in 2024 was "marriages are inherently abusive." Remember that "you don't have to tell your husband how you're voting" thing? The premise there is that in white marriages, if the man is a Republican, he is abusively forcing his wife to vote for Donald Trump. So that's the left. On the right, you have a libertarian right and sort of a Maga right where there's just as much individualism. I called up this old—there's a Twitter account I followed for a little bit with the lovely name Giga Based Dad. This is a play off of Giga Based Chad. You know, a Chad is like a super masculine handsome guy. And so I followed this because the first couple things were like a dad taking his six kids to church. I'm like, "That's me, I am Giga Based Dad." Or like, you know, strong tight-knit communities or maybe like—and then it gets into like the self-reliant farming. I'm like, "Okay, I don't really grow my own crops, but I get where this is going." And then I see more and more it was about escaping civilization and distrusting all strangers. Basically, the Giga Based Dad carries a gun in case any stranger comes up and talks to their family. A little weird, but then it posted this quote, a popular meme: "Can you remember who you were before the world told you who to be?" I was like, "That's some weird like Rousseau channeled through 17-year-old girl stuff. How is that Giga Based?" And the premise is that our authentic self can only be found by, you know, going off to a lake like Rousseau, Thoreau, or whatever. But our authentic self is a pre-social creature. Really, what we are is social, communal, familial creatures, and I think that we widely reject that. And again, it's not just the left. That account also quoted Elon Musk. Now, I put that in quotes because this is not in fact an Elon Musk quote, but this account thought it was. The quote is: "Don't ever attach yourself to a person, a place, a company, an organization, or a project. Attach yourself only to a mission, a calling, a purpose." You kind of understand where they're going, but that's not the problem with our society—that people are too attached to their neighbors, people, men are too attached to their wives, or people are too attached to their family. We have the opposite problem today. It actually was a line from a liberal black feminist author and activist. So that Musk and this person could be confused was telling. Tocqueville points us to this. He predicted—I think I've said to you before, I think Alexis de Tocqueville was a time traveler. He predicted that our egalitarian instincts and our democratic makeup would lead us to this point of losing what was best and most remarkable about us, which was our little platoons. And I think that when you look at how we have the sort of the rise of post-liberalism in the last decade here in the United States, its critique of liberalism is, again, channeling Tocqueville: that because we value our equality so much, we don't like the idea of depending on our neighbor to be the one to feed us if something goes wrong. Because we're so democratic, we need to level everything and make everything—because there's something undemocratic about a little platoon, right? Like if you join a club, I don't get to vote on anything that club does if I'm not in it. I'm not allowed in it if you don't want me into it. There's something undemocratic about institutions of civil society. So you can argue that the epidemic of loneliness is a fruit of sort of that liberalism in that sense of—not leftism—but you take all these good things: pluralism, equality, democratic republic. If that escapes what it's supposed to be, which is kind of a system of government, and becomes a way of life, and it becomes an anthropology, well, that wears away at all our social bonds. Cuz liberalism dissolves lasting bonds between people. If we treat all of our life the way we treat the free market—in other words, if I like my auto mechanic, but if he all of a sudden doubled his prices, I would leave him. I don't do that to my parish. If there's a homily I don't like, I don't do that to, you know, my wife. It's not like every day we get up and we revise whether we're going to stay married. So when the logic of the market creeps into our social arrangements, when the logic of democracy creeps into our relationships, it replaces our relationships with transactions and it tears us from one another. Now I happen to—and Tocqueville said: the spirit of democracy, when it spills out of government and into the way we live our lives daily, it not only makes each man forget his ancestors, but it hides his descendants from him and separates him from his contemporaries. It constantly leads him back toward himself alone and threatens to finally confine him wholly in the solitude of his own heart. And when I read that again, I was like: being confined in the solitude of your own heart. I had an image of somebody staring at Instagram and the algorithm feeding them and their whole thought process just turning back in on themselves and away from their ancestors, their descendants, and their neighbors. And so again, I think liberal democracy—I always say the best thing to be is a Catholic in a Protestant liberal democracy. But I think, and I'm a 98% capitalist when it comes to the market, but those things have crept into our life in a way that Tocqueville predicted. A hyper-individualism has taken over. Now sometimes we as a conservative say this—didn't ISI used to be called like the Intercollegiate Society of Individualists? Because we were fighting against communism, right? There are times and places where we need more individualism. I think right now is not that time. I think right now we have too much hyper-individualism. It's our anthropology. We think of ourselves—I have a liberal friend making fun of his hyper-individualist friends who said: "they all reject marriage because they want us all to be free-floating atomized bundles of rights that only bump into each other in consensual exchanges." And I think that that idea is present across the political spectrum, and I think it is unthinkingly dominant in that way. And I think that we need to actively constantly ask ourselves what we're talking about, what our policies are, what we're doing. Are we restoring the proper understanding of the individual as familial, as communal, as interdependent, or are we strengthening the hyper-individualism that dominates our day? Thank you, I have time for a couple questions, right? Thank you. Yes, the first question. "There is one grandchild that President Biden doesn't take calls." Yes, yes. Oh, hold on, there's a mic coming and I think since we're recording this, let's wait for that. "Thanks, so, I was in ISI when it was the Intercollegiate Society of Individualists, then the Intercollegiate Studies Institute. But my question is simply that the thesis of ISI then was not that there wouldn't be a de Tocqueville kind of community, but that it required individuals who then would come together in a community. So, my question really is, is your quarrel not so much with rugged individualists or individualists as it is with a society where people are so detached via the internet and AI and so on, and with the growing government that they lose that ability to have a kind of community cohesion?" Yeah, I mean, I think I'd say I always just think of Aristotle's idea of virtue—that it's a mean, right? That we need a certain amount of individualism and that America has always been individualistic. Once I was in Europe and a cabinet minister said: Europe historically has these two types of people, the people who follow orders, who do what they're told, who are reliable; and the people who go out on their own, and that second group of people all got on a boat and went to America. Like, that's always been part of who we are, but again, as Tocqueville will tell you, that individualism was always tempered because it was in the context of belonging to something. And belonging to something, you can't just say, "Oh, well I choose to belong to this church so it's still individualist." No, they still have some sway over you. They still get you—a parish will get you to do something that you wouldn't have done on your own. I coached kindergarten girls' basketball at one point. I tried to get out of it because I said I was too busy writing a book and they said, "What's the book about?" "It's a y—" Like that. So I don't think it solves the problem to say, "well, people individually freely choose these institutions," but I would say that, again, I'm to some extent I'm trying to pull on the end of the rope and say that we needed lots of individualism and right now we have way too much. One more question. Yes. And again, a mic is coming. "So what is the solution to building more communities? Is it bringing people together as far as we all are American, you know, like patriotism?" So patriotism's a funny one. I think American patriotism has always manifested itself on the local level. Just think about your July 4th. Everybody can remember July 4th and nobody in this room remembers the same July 4th as anybody else. Cuz yours was, you know, in the town square outside—in New York it was always some country club would put it there and so you would stand right outside the fence of the country club. Every—so patriotism is, you know, when we get to play hockey against Canada, then we're all kind of doing the same thing. But America's too big of a country. It's not even close to the city-state of the Greeks or even anything that can be that cohesive of an idea. The answer has to be on the local level. I always tell students there's no one solution, there's a hundred thousand small solutions, and so you should try to be that. But the reason that there's a shuttered church on the front of "Alienated America" is because I think the solution has to be things that bring us together and point us to a higher good. So it's like, can we get a great awakening? I wouldn't know how to bring one of those about, but that would be part of it. Yes. "It strikes me as not so much individualism as it is autonomy. That we can exist on our own. Individualism—I mean, I'm quirky and crazy, I'm married to somebody who is too, and we got married. We just celebrated 40 years, which is a surprise to both of us. But I mean, I grew up, my father was an individual, there's no question about it, but he took care of the alcoholic man who lived across our alley and bought him groceries at the end of the month because he knew he'd run out of money. He was a deacon in the Baptist church. But he also was Earl Green and everybody knew that he took these vacations, 'what's wrong with him?' We went out west. So I think it's something other than individuality or individualism, maybe the ism part, but it's this striving for autonomy. That we are separate, autonomous, and we don't need God and we don't need each other." I'll take that as a friendly amendment. I think that's exactly right. It's a worship of autonomy. One more question. Yes. "Thank you so much for your talk. So, it's very interesting to be here. I am that liberal 40-year-old person that you're talking about, and I think I would say sometimes, I think loneliness is created by polarization and not really understanding. And so I think one thing that could really help bridge gaps is really trying to see how similar we are, and how that exists everywhere. And I think a lot of times there's some misconception about equality and equity. And so sometimes, it's really more of a communication issue and not having these type of spaces that are so valuable. So very, very interesting to be here. I really appreciated your talk." It was more of a comment than a question. Well, thank you, and thank you for being here, and I'll finish with one related comment before stepping off. I think the best way to bring people together is actually not this—not explicitly to talk about ideas—but is in service. And I always think about my old parish in Maryland, which I read a lot about in "Alienated America," how everybody was supposed to sign up and volunteer for everything. And there was this big fall festival and I would sign up for cleanup. And there's always one person in charge of cleanup, and two years in a row it was this woman who was an immigrant from Eritrea, and the cleanup guys were me, a lobbyist, and a former member of Congress. And so what we had was this team where we're all like, "Alright, Monica, what do we do?" And then she was, you know—so and, first of all, a lobbyist, if you know half of what I've written, there couldn't be a bigger difference than between a federal lobbyist and me. But also you had the gender differences, you had the racial differences, you had the national origin differences. And a lot of—that parish you also had ideological differences. And what made it work was not us necessarily getting together and sharing a ton of ideas, but coaching Little League together and getting in the trenches and being like, "this one kid, he bawls every time anything goes wrong, how can we help this kid?" Me and a very liberal guy working together on that in the dugout—that's the sort of thing that I think is going to do more to bridge it. Anyway, thank you guys very much. I look forward to this day.

Defense Secretary and Joint Chiefs Chair News Conference

C-SPAN(1.8M) ·37:00 ·10.7K views · 2026-03-31
Pete Hegseth (Defense Secretary) Dan Caine (Joint Chiefs of Staff Chair)
One month into Operation Epic Fury, U.S.
TL;DR One month into Operation Epic Fury, U.S. forces have struck over 11,000 targets, severely degrading Iran’s military morale and industrial capacity while the administration pursues a diplomatic deal backed by "wartime speed" kinetic pressure. Targeting Success: The joint...

TL;DR

One month into Operation Epic Fury, U.S. forces have struck over 11,000 targets, severely degrading Iran’s military morale and industrial capacity while the administration pursues a diplomatic deal backed by "wartime speed" kinetic pressure.

Operational Milestones and Strategic Impact

  • Targeting Success: The joint force has struck more than 11,000 targets in the last 30 days, including 200 "dynamic strikes" (targets identified and hit in real-time) in a single night.
  • Degraded Enemy Morale: Intelligence indicates widespread desertions within the Iranian military and personnel shortages. Iranian leadership has been forced into underground bunkers that lack power, water, and oxygen.
  • Reduced Enemy Capability: Secretary Hegseth noted that the last 24 hours saw the lowest number of enemy missiles and drones fired by Iran since the conflict began.
  • Aerial Dominance: The U.S. has commenced overland B-52 missions, signaling a high level of air superiority that allows for persistent presence over Iranian territory.

Naval Dominance and the Strait of Hormuz

  • Maritime Attrition: The U.S. Navy has destroyed over 150 Iranian ships, including the entirety of their Jamaran-class frigates.
  • Strait of Hormuz: President Trump has demanded the waterway be opened for international business. Secretary Hegseth signaled that while the U.S. has prepared the conditions to keep the strait open, international allies (specifically mentioning the Royal Navy) are expected to "step up" and do their share of the heavy lifting.
  • Counter-Naval Tactics: Operations have expanded to include attack helicopters and close air support within the naval domain to target mine-laying capabilities and naval depots.

The "Arsenal of Freedom" and Industrial Strategy

  • Domestic Production: General Caine highlighted the "national assembly line" as the core of American combat power, praising machinists and technicians for maintaining the precision required for high-tech warfare.
  • Industrial Contrast: Hegseth noted that while the U.S. defense industrial base is at its most vibrant state since World War II, Iran’s industrial base is nearly completely destroyed, leaving them with negligible ability to reconstitute lost weapons.
  • Modernization: The briefing emphasized the rapid deployment and manufacturing of advanced platforms, including the B-21 and F-47, to maintain a technological edge.

Diplomacy and "Boots on the Ground"

  • Ongoing Negotiations: Diplomatic talks are "real, ongoing, and active." The administration’s primary goal is a deal that ensures Iran never acquires a nuclear weapon and relinquishes its regional ambitions.
  • Military Compulsion: Secretary Hegseth described the current strategy as "negotiating with bombs," using military intensity to compel the new Iranian regime to accept U.S. terms.
  • Strategic Ambiguity: Regarding the potential for "boots on the ground," the Department refuses to foreclose any options. Hegseth emphasized that unpredictability is a core military principle and that the U.S. has "15 different ways" to execute ground options if directed by the President.
  • Ally Friction: The administration expressed frustration with certain allies regarding access, basing, and overflight rights, suggesting that future alliance commitments may depend on their willingness to support U.S. operations.
"Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that this new regime... is in a better place if they make that deal. And so we'll continue... in the meantime, we'll negotiate with bombs."

— Secretary Pete Hegseth

"To our adversaries out there, I remind you to beware of the American workforce... The distance from that factory floor and that assembly line to the frontline is incredibly short."

— General Dan Caine

"Previous presidents were all talk. [President Trump] is all action. On the battlefield, because of the latitude the President has given us, American firepower is only increasing. Iran's, decreasing."

— Secretary Pete Hegseth

Over the weekend, I had the opportunity to visit our troops fighting in Operation Epic Fury. We were on the ground in CENTCOM on Saturday for about half the day. For reasons of operational security, so those troops are not targeted, the places and bases will not be named. Suffice it to say, the trip was an honor. I had a chance to bear witness, and I witnessed the best of America. I witnessed warriors, a brotherhood of men and women warriors, all active-duty, guard and reserve, united in their love for each other, their shared purpose, and their commitment to the mission. I witnessed sheer competency. I watched a Private First Class confidently calling out an enemy missile trajectory to a room full of officers. Everyone was focused, the room was locked in, and two enemy missiles were successfully shot down. I spoke to Air Force and Navy pilots on the flight line who every day both deliver bombs deep into Iran but also shoot down drones defending their base. Many had just returned from the skies of Iran and Tehran. I put on a headset and spoke to one crew in the cockpit, locked and loaded each and every day. I witnessed ingenuity, American ingenuity. I met the young Army officer who figured out how to neutralize maneuvering enemy missiles, saving countless lives. His commander confirmed that the whoops and cheers erupted in the Tactical Operations Center when his new approach was first successful. I met the Air Force Intel analyst who refines target packages faster than the enemy can adapt. I actually gave him my card and told him to keep me posted on the ground truth. I did the same with his boss, a Colonel with a heart the size of Texas and a beautiful deployment mustache to match. I witnessed lethality. I met a junior airman as the sun was going down and a chill was setting on the tarmac. When asked what they needed, she simply looked up at me with a sly smile on her face and said, "More bombs, sir, and bigger bombs." We will happily oblige her. I met the Army targeting team who found and sunk the pride and joy of the Iranian Navy, their fighting position plastered with images of sunken enemy ships. And I witnessed urgency. Right when we landed, another C-17 landed just minutes after us, and within 30 seconds of the aircraft coming to a full stop, a team on the ground pulled up and the cargo was being uploaded. Wartime speed. To a man and to a woman, on the ground, in the air, on the flight line, and in the TOC, I heard, "We want everything faster, higher op tempo, wartime speed." The feeling was the exact opposite of the rotational units year after year in the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan that we're so familiar with. In those wars, it was always about the next rotation, never knowing when the mission would end or exactly what the mission was, year after year. Not with Epic Fury. I witnessed urgency to finish the job, urgency to achieve mission success, not looking at the next rotation, only moving as fast as possible to win. I got a chance to troop the line, to witness firsthand what we already know is true. I spoke to all ranks and all services, none of whom knew we were coming. It was not rehearsed or scripted; sometimes we just wandered. What I witnessed was motivation. It was sheer mission focus. It was the American warrior unleashed. It was the kind of warfighting American spirit that comes with a clear mission against a determined enemy. A crew chief we flew with summed it up nicely. He said, "It's been a busy few weeks, sir, tough stuff, but I'm so honored to be called up. This fight is long overdue. We need to address it for our kids. We cannot pass the buck. Please thank the President from us." I heard that time and time again. I asked each young American, "What do you need?" and nobody said better equipment, nobody said more comfortable living conditions, nobody said "send me home." Well, of course eventually we want all those things, and they do too, but what those Americans said to me, young and old, officer and NCO, male and female, black and white, was "Let's finish the mission. Get us even more bombs, bigger bombs, more targets, let us finish this." In fact, Admiral Cooper noted this morning that the three Air Force captains shot down by Kuwaiti friendly fire early in the fight weeks ago, they never left the theater. All dropped bombs over Tehran last night. These men and women live the Iranian threat every day, incoming missiles and drones, and know what a world looks like, what the world would look like if Iran had the most dangerous weapon in the world, a nuclear weapon. As President Trump has said time and time again for years and in this administration, Iran cannot have a nuclear bomb and they won't. These troops, they want to finish this fight for their kids and their grandkids. This is about history; this is about legacy. Success matters and because of this President and these Americans, we are closer than ever before to winning. President Trump is doing what no other president had the guts to do. Previous presidents were all talk. He's all action. On the battlefield, because of the latitude the President has given us, American firepower is only increasing. Iran's is decreasing. We have more and more options and they have less. Just one month in, only one month, we set the terms. The upcoming days will be decisive. Iran knows that and there's almost nothing they can militarily do about it. Yes, they will still shoot some missiles, but we will shoot them down. Of note, the last 24 hours saw the lowest number of enemy missiles and drones fired by Iran. They will go underground, but we will find them. We recently destroyed another one of their command bunkers, leaders forced to flee, no water, no power, no oxygen, no command and control. Their faith in their caves is diminishing. The latest Intel is clear out of CENTCOM; our strikes are damaging the morale of the Iranian military leading to widespread desertions, key personnel shortages, and causing frustrations amongst senior leaders. Just last night we had 200 dynamic strikes alone. A dynamic strike is a strike where a pilot leaves and during their flight they get a new target set based on real-time intel given to them. A new launcher, a new location, a new troop formation. A dynamic target is one that changes while you're in the air because of improved intelligence. 200 dynamic strikes alone, in addition to the pre-planned targets. The video the President posted last night of Esfahan shows an ammo depot struck by US bombers. You see, you don't get to see many of those videos because as a reminder, Iran has still shut off the internet to 99.9% of its population. But if Iran is wise, they will cut a deal. President Trump doesn't bluff and he does not back down. You can ask Khamenei about that. The new Iranian regime should know that by now. This new regime, because regime change has occurred, should be wiser than the last. President Trump will make a deal, he is willing, and the terms of the deal are known to them. If Iran is not willing, then the United States War Department will continue with even more intensity. Standing here this morning in this briefing room, in my mind's eye, I'm actually looking out at the groups I met this weekend: the pilots, the logisticians, the intel analysts, the targeters, the sustainers, the flight crews, the air defenders, the base security, those maintainers who walked up at sunset with the chill in the air on the flight line. May God watch over all of them, each day and each night. May His almighty and eternal arms of providence stretch over them and protect them and bring them peace, in the name of Jesus Christ, and amen. Mr. Chairman, over to you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, and good morning, everyone, and thank you again for being here. As operations continue, I remain deeply grateful for the service and determination of 2.8 million members of our joint force, each of whom are serving something greater than themselves. Every day those deployed, and in many cases those at home who are deploying forward in bomber pulses and others, put themselves in harm's way and we owe them a deep debt of gratitude. I remain especially grateful for our fallen who gave the ultimate sacrifice. We'll never forget their valor and their determination to do something greater than themselves, and each day we continue to earn what they've given to us. This morning I want to share that yesterday the joint force had the honor of participating in the burial of Colonel Clarence Emil "Bud" Anderson, who passed away in May of 2024 at the ripe old age of 102. He was laid to rest in Arlington Cemetery yesterday morning alongside his wife of 70 years, Eleanor, who he married on February 23rd, 1945. She passed away in 2015 at the age of 92. Bud, yesterday a legend to our Air Force and fighter pilots everywhere, was honored with a combination flyby of F-35s and a four-ship of P-51 Mustangs. He was one of the most prolific flying aces of World War II and the highest-scoring P-51 ace with 16 and a quarter kills in his squadron and in his group. He served from 1942 to 1972 and his last combat tour was flying F-105 Thunderchiefs over North Vietnam. For anyone that had the chance to meet Colonel Anderson, you knew what an incredibly special man he was. And that's true for each and every one of our World War II vets who become fewer and fewer with each passing day. They are the greatest generation and give us the gift of an incredible example as we execute Operation Epic Fury today. And Colonel Anderson, we mourn for your loss and remember that smile on the right side as you went out to do our nation's business. I'd like to now share an operational update. Our joint force continues to focus on our military objectives as we systematically continue to degrade and destroy Iran's ability to project power and threaten stability beyond its borders. First, the joint force continues to destroy Iran's ballistic missile and UAS capabilities. We remain focused on interdicting and destroying the logistical and supply chains that feed these programs, and this remains a truly joint effort prosecuted around the clock from air, land, sea, space, and cyberspace. Long-range bombers from US Strategic Command coordinating with tactical fighter aircraft from our joint force launched from bases around the region and the continental United States, while simultaneously Navy fighters from the sea and sailors continue to project power from the sea, while Army and Marine artillery units continue to execute long-range precision fires deep into enemy territory against high-value targets. Meanwhile, on the defense side, our Army and Air defenders and aviators, as the Secretary talked about, remain vigilant forming a shield to protect our forces and our partners, intercepting missiles and drones. Together we continue to deliver precision strikes against key manufacturing nodes, component storage sites, research facilities deep within Iranian territory, and over the past 30 days we've struck more than 11,000 targets. Given the increase in air superiority, we've successfully started to conduct the first overland B-52 missions which allow us, as we've said before, to continue to get on top of the enemy and, as the Secretary talked about, switch towards more and more dynamic targets servicing mobile targets around the battlespace. We've continued to do the work against Iran's missile, drone, and naval production facilities and we continue the multi-domain pressure that we've talked about. Second, on the Navy front, we continue to assert dominance over the Iranian Navy. We remain focused on targeting their mine-laying capability, their naval assets, and we've now, as I mentioned briefly last time, started to work attack helicopters and other close air support assets into the naval domain. CENTCOM continues to identify and work against naval depots and storage areas, and we've taken out again more than 150 ships including all Jamaran-class frigates inside their Navy. Third, we continue to prosecute our campaign against their defense industrial base at scale. This includes factories, warehouses, nuclear weapons research and development labs, and the associated infrastructure required for Iran to reconstitute its combat capability. I'd like to continue my theme of highlighting the incredible contributions of Americans to our joint force. Today I want to talk a little bit about a different frontline, a line that doesn't have bunkers or guard posts but is just as critical to our nation's security: our national assembly lines. Today I want to recognize a group of Americans who live at the beginning of our nation's combat power: the Americans who actually make our weapons both inside our defense industrial base but even more broadly inside our national industrial base. In every military option, we could not and cannot do our jobs without the men and women across our country who show up every day around the clock to a factory floor, a workshop, a laboratory, who build the weapons and capabilities we need to project American combat power at the time and place of our choosing. These great Americans, and I've had a chance to spend some time with them when I was in the private sector, are the core of American combat power. They're the machinists running high-tech CNC machines cutting raw blocks of metal into incredibly precise parts. They're the assembly workers painstakingly taking a kit of parts and turning that into a complex guidance system or precision munition or a rocket motor or building a jet or submarine. Or they're the quality assurance technicians who ensure that when a warfighter pulls a trigger, the weapon works every time. This can be and is tough and gritty work. It's not a quiet office and a desk with paper, and there's nothing wrong with that, but this is exactly the way this group of Americans likes it. I know this, I've seen it myself and I remain deeply honored by that gift. It's often loud and dangerous work, demanding, and requires absolute focus for hours at a time with deep commitment to get it right every single time. It's hands-on work where one uncaught mistake or deviation can put an American's life at risk. A single misplaced wire, a microscopic flaw in a weld, or an incorrectly calibrated sensor could mean the difference between mission success or mission failure. The difference is measured in the lives of our sons and daughters who we put around the field on a field of battle. We rely on and trust in these great Americans, and it's not just their manufacturing skill; it's their innovative minds and their entrepreneurial spirit. From those who build exquisite capabilities down to the mom-and-pop machine shops all over this great country, they live at the beginning of and the core of America's combat capability, constantly adapting, constantly improving, constantly learning. And there are examples of this throughout history and current days. This includes building things like the F-117 Stealth fighter, the B-2 Stealth bomber, making our combat capability undetectable over the enemy, to today's B-21 and F-47. It's work like in the shipyards of the East Coast and northeast side of the United States that go out and build America's nuclear submarines that allow us to patrol around the world at the time and place of our choosing. These innovators, these workers, these incredible Americans don't get the same glory as a fighter pilot returning to a carrier deck at night or an artilleryman sending rounds downrange, and yet they show up every single day and without them we could not do the work that we are tasked to do. 24/7 they build the tools that we need to do our business. The skill, the commitment, the patriotism, and the dedication poured into every piece of combat capability and hardware is seen and felt out at the edge of the force, as the Secretary talked about those young bomb builders out in the desert that he had the chance to see. You can see it, you can feel it, and it's real and we're deeply grateful. So to the American workforce out there both inside our defense industrial base and in our national industrial base, thank you on behalf of the joint force. We carry the weapons that you build, we rely on the systems that you create, and the distance from that factory floor and that assembly line to the frontline is incredibly short. Thank you, keep it up. And to our adversaries out there, I remind you to beware of the American workforce. We continue to press forward in our military objectives. The Secretary and I remain deeply humbled by the spirit, tenacity, commitment and grit of our 2.8 million member strong joint force. I want to, as always, thank the force and their families and as always remember our fallen. We carry with them and their names every day. Sir, I'll turn it back over to you. Well, thank you, Mr. Chairman. Two things: he didn't note it, but in memory of Colonel Bud Anderson, the Chairman himself flew his F-16 to honor him recently. And thank you for highlighting the defense industrial base, the Arsenal of Freedom. It's worth noting no one has rebuilt that defense industrial base faster and with more purpose than President Trump in his first term, and now we're rebuilding the Arsenal of Freedom. Our defense industrial base is more vibrant today than it's been since World War II and growing rapidly. Contrast that with Iran, whose defense industrial base is nearly completely destroyed. Their ability to reconstitute the weapons of war they're using now—that they're hiding in underground facilities and attriting over time—their ability to rebuild that is negligible, and I think that contrast is an enduring legacy of this as well. Right here. Thank you for your time. We've had some signaling that the US will retake control of the Strait of Hormuz. At that point what military posture will be imposed to guarantee safe passage for our allies, deter our enemies, and demonstrate US dominance in the region? And also, can you speak to how much America's adversaries Russia, China, North Korea, how much they're supporting Iran's war machine at this point with arms and intelligence and what we are militarily doing to punish the enemy coalition? Appreciate both questions. On the Strait of Hormuz, there are many more vessels flowing through today than there were as the President has arranged. The President's been clear to Iran: open it for business or we have options, and we certainly do. And when you look at what the Chairman laid out with the Navy, with the Navy industrial base, with coastal cruise missiles, with UAVs, and with counter-mine capabilities, we've been focused from the beginning on attriting and defeating those capabilities and limiting their options. There's lots we're doing as well, some of which is known, some of which is not known, to set the conditions. And I think the President was clear this morning in his truth that there are countries around the world that ought to be prepared to step up on this critical waterway as well. It's not just the United States Navy. Last time I checked there was supposed to be a big bad Royal Navy that could be prepared to do things like that as well. So he's pointing out this is an international waterway that we use less than most, in fact dramatically less than most. So the world ought to pay attention and be prepared to stand up. President Trump's been willing to do the heavy lifting on behalf of the free world to address this threat of Iran. It's not just our problem set going forward, even though we have done the lion's share of preparation to ensure that that strait will be open, which is an outcome the President's been very clear on. As far as Russia and China, we know exactly what they're doing, what they are or are not doing. We don't have to air publicly what all of that is, but where necessary we're addressing it, we're mitigating it, and or we're confronting it head-on. I don't know if you have anything you want to add. No, sir, I think you covered it. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. David Zere, Real America's Voice. And I witnessed that enthusiasm on the lines and our submarine builders in the ironworks, it's unbelievable. But I just wanted to ask you, what's the status of the delivery of any hardened bunkers for additional protection for our troops, and without getting too specific, what kind of measures are we taking to protect some of the larger and more strategic aircraft like the Sentry and others? Well, it's a great question. I'll say what I witnessed where I went was a completely locked-in discipline of bunker use and bunker improvement. So from the beginning, as we stated very clearly, the first thing we did was set up a defense and make sure our defensive capabilities were maxed out before any of this even started. That included fortifications as much as possible, but it also included dispersal. If all of our people are in one place, you can imagine why that's a big problem. So dispersing is part of that defeat; alongside that dispersal is more and more bunkers. And I can tell you talking to base commanders, talking to our allies in Israel, and talking to others, rapidly fielding that and then improving those positions is a theater priority. No doubt as are the air defenses and the layered air defenses. It's not just Patriots and THAADS, it's fighters and defensive CAPS, it's other kinetic defeat systems, it's electronic warfare. So the defense of our troops and our assets is maxed. I will say on some of those other assets you talked about, air wings, airframes, there's some things adversaries are doing to provide info and intel that they shouldn't. We're aware of it and ultimately we move things around and don't—one of the biggest principles you learn in the military is to not set patterns, predictable patterns. And so commanders are working hard to adjust in real-time with those systems and make sure they're in the right places and not easily targetable. Thanks, Mr. Secretary. The US and Iran appear right now publicly to disagree about whether there are these negotiations ongoing. What impact does that have on military strategy? Does it have any bearing at all? And General, the President wrote yesterday that the US might destroy Iran's electricity-generating plants and oil wells. Those are his words before ending this operation. Is there a way to do either of those things without like seriously jeopardizing or seriously harming civilians? On the talks, I can tell you having been with Steve and Jared and the Vice President, Marco and many others yesterday, they are very real. They are ongoing, they're active, and I think gaining strength. And we appreciate that. As I said in my remarks, we would much prefer to get a deal. If Iran was willing to relinquish material they have and ambitions they have, open this strait—great, that's the goal. We don't want to have to do more militarily than we have to, but I didn't mean it flippantly when I said in the meantime we'll negotiate with bombs. Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that this new regime, this regime in charge, is in a better place if they make that deal. And so we'll continue; we're working hand in hand, but the primary effort is a deal. We want that deal to be accomplished if at all possible. If not, then we're prepared to continue. Thanks for the question. We're always thinking about those considerations and develop options to be able to mitigate those risks pursuant to the normal practices that we do in the military. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. I know you slightly addressed this already, but just on the Strait of Hormuz, is opening the strait an essential objective to Operation Epic Fury or is that the job of those other countries? And then secondly, without asking you to comment on things that you can't talk about, what is your message to Americans who love the President and strongly believe in him but are very worried about this notion of boots on the ground? Well, first on the Strait of Hormuz, our core objectives from this podium from day one—from me, from the Chairman, from the President, from the Vice President, from Secretary Rubio and others—have been clear: missile production and missile programs, so their entire missile program; defense industrial base and production ability to build; and Navy and power projection. So those have been—and then of course wrapping it all is Iran's never going to have a nuclear weapon. Those have been very clear. Defeating the Navy is a core part of ensuring they can't project that kind of power. But ultimately, I think the President's truth this morning lays that out very well that this Strait of Hormuz issue, which we've set the conditions for success and we will make sure Iran knows that very clearly, is not just a United States of America problem set. We've been willing to lead; President Trump's led the entire time, but it's not just us. So ultimately I think other countries should pay attention when the President speaks. He's proven that when he speaks he means something and he's pointing out you might want to start learning how to fight for yourself. It's something some of us have been saying for quite some time. You can't just have flags, you have to have formations. You can't just have a few ships, you have to have enough to affect change. Those things matter in a dangerous world with descendent adversaries. That's why the Chairman's talking about our industrial base; that's why we're launching the Arsenal of Freedom tour to revive our defense industrial base. President Trump is doing it in a way that nobody else has. As far as President Trump and boots on the ground, I don't understand why the base, which they have already, wouldn't have faith in his ability to execute on this. Look at his track record of pursuing peace through strength, America-first outcomes. What he's simply saying, and it's exactly true and I've said from this podium too, we're not going to foreclose any option. You can't fight and win a war if you tell your adversary what you are willing to do or what you are not willing to do, to include boots on the ground. Our adversary right now thinks there are 15 different ways we could come at them with boots on the ground. And guess what, there are. So if we needed to, we could execute those options on behalf of the President of the United States and this department. Or maybe we don't have to use them at all. Maybe negotiations work or maybe there's a different approach. But the point is to be unpredictable in that. Certainly, do not let anybody know what you're willing to do or not do. But if anybody has internalized the lessons of Iraq and Afghanistan, it is President Trump, the first one to call them out for what they are. He's not going to repeat those lessons. And I think I've been very clear about that from the podium. Thank you, Secretary Hegseth. A question for you and then a question for General Caine. You said we're a month into the Operation Epic Fury. How long until the objectives are achieved and is there a scenario where a deal is struck before the objectives have been achieved? And then for General Caine, there's been lots of media coverage that suggested a ground invasion is imminent. What other purposes might the soldiers and the Marines who have been deployed over to the Middle East serve in this conflict? Well, just like the previous question, it's sort of military 101. Don't tell your enemy what you're willing to do or not do, and don't tell your enemy when you're willing to stop, especially an enemy that likes to hide in bunkers and try to hoard their missiles and hope they'll wait you out. So that's not a question I'm going to answer or the President has said definitively. We have our own goals and guidance and military objectives that we're moving toward and things that we look at. And as he's articulated, he's said four to six weeks, six to eight weeks, three weeks—it could be any particular number, but we would never reveal precisely what it is because our goal is to finish those objectives. And we're well on our way and the Chairman and I look at this every single day. It will be the President's determination and the President's determination alone when those objectives are complete and when it serves the interest of the American people to cut that deal, to make sure that Iran doesn't have a nuclear capability and ultimately that our objectives and our interests are advanced. Just to answer your question, Reagan, the range of military options that those forces can offer are extensive, not just limited to what you mentioned in terms of forces on the ground. I wouldn't want to take away the President's decision space but there are a multitude of things. Not the least of which is Iran should note that they're out there and that they are a pressure point. So they should carefully consider, I think, at the diplomatic level—not my job as the Chairman—but at the diplomatic level, to consider what's in front of them. Mr. Secretary, the US government IC assessed for many years that Iran is protecting Al-Qaeda senior leaders including the current Emir, Saif al-Adel. Does the US assess that he's still in Iran, has he fled to Afghanistan, or is he on a target list? And Mr. Chairman, based on CENTCOM figures and open source, Iranian daily missile barrages and drone launches have indeed dropped dramatically since the start of the war, but they're not at zero and they seem to have maintained some level of low but stable numbers; perhaps they've dropped a little bit more in recent days. How do we get that closer to zero and what impact is Iran's degraded but still very real capability having in impacting current and future operations? I won't speak to a particular target; I'll just say that Al-Qaeda remains our enemy, obviously, and there are a lot of people on our target list in Iran. If they were to be harboring Al-Qaeda, they would certainly fit that list. Just quickly, the mobile targets that they have that we continue to service, as the Secretary talked about the 200 hits that were done last night, that's one component of continuing to lower that. The second and more long-term impact that we're having is the strikes against their defense industrial base at scale, which will not allow them to reload after that. So we do continue to see a trend down. They are still shooting and we do continue to exert significant pressure against both sides of that system. Mr. Secretary, thank you for the question. Do you still believe—you'd said previously that the Iranian leadership would surrender—do you still believe that? And is the United States still committed to NATO's collective defense? And to Chairman Caine, could you give us a sense of your thinking about the legality of striking desalinization plants if the President were to order that? I will just say that any mission that ends on our terms, you want to call it a surrender, call it a defeat, call it what you want, we remain committed to a conflict that ends on our terms and the President's terms. There's no doubt about that. And as far as NATO is concerned, that's a decision that will be left to the President. But I'll just say a lot has been laid bare, a lot has been shown to the world about what our allies would be willing to do for the United States of America when we undertake an effort of this scope on behalf of the free world. These are missiles that don't even range the United States of America; they range allies and others. And yet when we ask for additional assistance or simple access basing and overflight, we get questions or roadblocks or hesitations. And the President's pointing out you don't have much of an alliance if you have countries that are not willing to stand with you when you need them. He's simply pointing that out and ultimately it'll be his decision of what that looks like after this is complete. I'll just—I won't talk about any particular target. This really builds on Julian's question too that I don't think I completely answered. The joint force is the most professional force in the world and we have numerous processes and systems to carefully consider the whole range of considerations from civilian risk to legal considerations with any target. As targets come before us we run them through the same process that we always do and always strike lawful targets in accordance with the normal procedures that we use. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. On Sunday President Trump said that Iran's first regime was all destroyed and the second one is mostly dead. The third regime that we're on right now has been much more reasonable in negotiations. Do you agree with that assessment and why? I would defer to the negotiating team there. They're the ones talking to them but they're getting a back and forth on terms which is a productive development. We stand right there next to our negotiating team, always willing and prepared to put them in an even better position. Well, thank you very much everyone. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.

VP JD Vance in NC and what it means for NC-01

The News & Observer(141.0K) ·20:59 ·2 views · 2026-03-31
JD Vance (Vice President) Dawn Vaughan (Host and Capitol Bureau Chief, The News & Observer)
Vice President J.D.
TL;DR Vice President J.D. Vance’s visit to Rocky Mount underscores North Carolina's 1st Congressional District (NC-01) as a critical 2026 battleground, as the GOP attempts to flip the seat by labeling Democratic incumbent Don Davis a partisan loyalist despite his moderate voting...

TL;DR

Vice President J.D. Vance’s visit to Rocky Mount underscores North Carolina's 1st Congressional District (NC-01) as a critical 2026 battleground, as the GOP attempts to flip the seat by labeling Democratic incumbent Don Davis a partisan loyalist despite his moderate voting record.

Strategic Importance of NC-01 and Rocky Mount

  • Targeting the 1st District: Following redistricting, the 1st Congressional District has shifted from a swing district to one that "tinges red," making it a top priority for the GOP to expand its slim House majority.
  • Geographic Advantage: Rocky Mount is strategically located to reach multiple media markets and sits near the Virginia border, another key area for Republican gains.
  • Frequent Visitation: NC GOP Chairman Jason Simmons noted that voters should expect to see both President Trump and VP Vance "over and over" in North Carolina throughout this cycle.
  • A "Hometown" Attack: The choice of location allowed Republicans to challenge former Governor Roy Cooper (now a U.S. Senate candidate) in his home region of Nash County.

J.D. Vance’s Campaign Messaging

  • Economic Platform: The official messaging focused on "Lower Prices, Bigger Paychecks," aimed at one of the state's lower-income regions struggling with inflation at gas pumps and grocery stores.
  • The "Pelosi" Strategy: Vance repeatedly characterized Rep. Don Davis as a "mouthpiece" for Speaker Emerita Nancy Pelosi.
      • Fact Check: Reporter Danielle Battaglia noted this rhetoric is "off" for those following the district, as Davis frequently votes against his party and Pelosi no longer holds a leadership role in Congress.
  • Bipartisan Voting Record: Examples of Davis breaking party lines include voting with Republicans to reopen the government after a 43-day shutdown to protect military paychecks and constituent services.

Candidate Profiles: Davis vs. Buckhout

  • Shared Backgrounds: Both incumbent Don Davis and Republican challenger Laurie Buckhout are military veterans.
  • Laurie Buckhout (R): A former cybersecurity advisor for the Trump administration who worked closely with the President in the White House.
  • Don Davis (D): A second-term congressman and former "youngest mayor" of Snow Hill who has built a reputation for prioritizing community interests over party loyalty in a swing district.

The U.S. Senate Race and Federal Oversight

  • The Senate Campaign: Republican Michael Whatley, running for the seat vacated by Thom Tillis, appeared with Vance to attack opponent Roy Cooper on crime.
      • Crime Rhetoric: Whatley criticized Cooper over the release of a prisoner who later killed a Ukrainian refugee, though the release was part of a complex court-ordered COVID-19 pandemic agreement.
