On the talks, I can tell you having been with Steve and Jared and the Vice President and Marco and many others yesterday, they are very real. They are ongoing, they're active, and I think they are gaining strength. We appreciate that. As I said in my remarks, we would much prefer to get a deal. If Iran was willing to relinquish material they have and ambitions they have, and open the strait, great. That's the goal. We don't want to have to do more militarily than we have to. But I didn't mean it flippantly when I said in the meantime we'll negotiate with bombs. Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that this new regime, this regime in charge, is in a better place if they make that deal. So we'll continue. We're working hand-in-hand, but the primary effort is a deal. We want that deal to be accomplished if at all possible. If not, then we're prepared to continue. Secretary, what happened at Isfahan? Was that dynamic strike with drones? Thanks for the question. We're always thinking about those considerations and develop options to be able to mitigate those risks pursuant to the normal practices that we do in the military. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. I know you slightly addressed this already, but just on the Strait of Hormuz, is opening the strait an essential objective to Operation Epic Fury, or is that the job of those other countries? And then secondly, without asking you to comment on things that you can't talk about, what is your message to Americans who love the President and strongly believe in him, but are very worried about this notion of boots on the ground? Well first, on the Strait of Hormuz, our core objectives from this podium from day one have been clear. They involve defense missile production and missile programs, meaning their entire missile program. They involve the defense industrial base and production ability to build, and Navy and power projection. Those have been very clear. Defeating the Navy is a core part of ensuring they can't project that kind of power. But ultimately, I think the President's Truth this morning lays that out very well. This Strait of Hormuz issue, for which we've set the conditions for success, is not just a United States of America problem set. We've been willing to lead, and President Trump's led the entire time, but it's not just us. So ultimately, I think other countries should pay attention when the President speaks. He's proven that when he speaks he means something. He's pointing out, you know, you might want to start learning how to fight for yourself. It's something some of us have been saying for quite some time. You can't just have flags; you have to have formations. You can't just have a few ships; you have to have enough to effect change. Those things matter in a dangerous world with descendent adversaries. That's why the President and the Chairman are talking about our industrial base. That's why we're launching the Arsenal of Freedom tour to revive our defense industrial base. As far as President Trump and boots on the ground, I don't understand why the base wouldn't have faith in his ability to execute on this. Look at his track record of pursuing peace through strength and America First outcomes. What he's simply saying, and it's exactly true, is we're not going to foreclose any option. You can't fight and win a war if you tell your adversary what you are willing to do or what you are not willing to do, to include boots on the ground. Our adversary right now thinks there are 15 different ways we could come at them with boots on the ground. And guess what? There are. And so if we needed to, we could execute those options on behalf of the President of the United States and this department. Maybe we don't have to use them at all. Maybe negotiations work, or maybe there's a different approach. But the point is to be unpredictable in that, and certainly not let anybody know what you're willing to do or not do. If anybody has internalized the lessons of Iraq and Afghanistan, it is President Trump. He was the first one to call them out for what they are, and he's not going to repeat those lessons. I think I've been very clear about that from the podium. Thank you, Secretary Hegseth. Question for you and then a question for General Kane. You said we're a month into the Operation Epic Fury. How long until the objectives are achieved, and is there a scenario where a deal is struck before the objectives have been achieved? And for General Kane, there's been lots of media coverage that suggested a ground invasion is imminent. What other purposes might the soldiers and the Marines who have been deployed over to the Middle East serve in this conflict? Well, just like the previous question, it's sort of military 101. Don't tell your enemy what you're willing to do or not do, and don't tell your enemy when you're willing to stop. This is especially true with an enemy that likes to hide in bunkers and hope you'll wait it out. So, that's not a question I'm going to answer, or one the President has said definitively. We have our own goals and guidance and military objectives that we're moving toward. As he's articulated, it could be four to six weeks, six to eight weeks, or three weeks. It could be any particular number, but we would never reveal precisely what it is because our goal is to finish those objectives. And we're well on our way, and the Chairman and I look at this every single day. It will be the President's determination, and the President's determination alone, when those objectives are complete. No, just to answer your question, Reagan, the range of military options that those forces can offer are extensive. They are not just limited to what you mentioned in terms of forces on the ground. And I wouldn't want to take away the President's decision space, but there are a multitude of things. Not the least of which is that Iran should note that they're out there and that they are a pressure point. And so they should carefully consider what's in front of them at the diplomatic level. Mr. Secretary, do you still believe that the Iranian leadership would surrender? And is the United States still committed to NATO's collective defense? And to Chairman Kane, could you give us a sense of your thinking about the legality of striking desalination plants if the President were to order that? I will just say that any mission that ends on our terms—you want to call it a surrender, call it a defeat, call it what you want—we remain committed to it. We want a conflict that ends on our terms and the President's terms. There's no doubt about that. As far as NATO's concerned, that's a decision that will be left to the President, but I'll just say a lot has been laid bare. A lot has been shown to the world about what our allies would be willing to do for the United States of America when we undertake an effort of this scope. These are missiles that don't even range the United States of America. They range allies and others, and yet when we ask for additional assistance or simple access, basing, and overflight, we get questions or roadblocks or hesitations. The President's pointing out that you don't have much of an alliance if you have countries that are not willing to stand with you when you need them. He's simply pointing that out, and ultimately it'll be his decision of what that looks like after this is complete. I won't talk about any particular target. This really builds on Jillian's question too that I don't think I completely answered, but the joint force is the most professional force in the world. We have numerous processes and systems to carefully consider the whole range of considerations from civilian risk to legal considerations with any target. As targets come before us, we run them through the same process that we always do and always strike lawful targets in accordance with the normal procedures that we use. Chairman Kane, do you agree with "no quarter, no mercy" for enemy production as Hegseth has said? Thank you, Mr. Secretary. On Sunday, President Trump said that Iran's first regime was all destroyed and the second one is mostly dead, and that the third regime that we're on right now has been much more reasonable in negotiations. Do you agree with that assessment and why? I would defer to the negotiating team there. They're the ones talking to them, but they're getting a back and forth on terms which is a productive development. We stand right next to our negotiating team, always willing and prepared to put them in an even better position. All right. Thank you very much, sir. Appreciate it. Thank you.

Compliance at scale and why TAM is a distraction with Christina Cacioppo of Vanta

Stripe(94.6K) ·57:38 ·3.2K views · 2026-03-31
Christina Cacioppo (Founder and CEO, Vanta)
Vanta has scaled to over 15,000 customers by reframing compliance as a "painkiller" rather than a "vitamin," leveraging a real-time "unit test" model for security and now deploying AI to automate over 90% of security questionnaires.
TL;DR Vanta has scaled to over 15,000 customers by reframing compliance as a "painkiller" rather than a "vitamin," leveraging a real-time "unit test" model for security and now deploying AI to automate over 90% of security questionnaires. The Buying Moment: Christina...

TL;DR

Vanta has scaled to over 15,000 customers by reframing compliance as a "painkiller" rather than a "vitamin," leveraging a real-time "unit test" model for security and now deploying AI to automate over 90% of security questionnaires.

The Strategy: Compliance as a "Painkiller"

  • The Buying Moment: Christina Cacioppo identified that while startups rarely buy "security" early on, they are forced to buy "compliance" (like SOC 2) to close enterprise deals.
      • Vanta was founded on the hypothesis that starting a security company for startups requires starting as a compliance company.
  • Scale and Growth: The company currently serves over 15,000 customers, ranging from two-person startups to the Fortune 50. Growth has accelerated to over 60% annually in recent years.
  • Market Education: Vanta famously used a "101 billboard" campaign in San Francisco ("Compliance that doesn't SOC 2 much") to build a cultural association between the brand and the standard.
      • Marketing Lessons: Cacioppo initially doubted podcast advertising, but a $60,000 spend on *This Week in Startups* immediately yielded 34 sales, proving the efficacy of high-intent audio channels.

Product Architecture: "Unit Tests" for Security

  • Automated Monitoring: Cacioppo describes Vanta as a battery of unit tests for compliance rules.
      • The "Test" Model: Instead of static spreadsheets, Vanta connects to tools (GitHub, AWS) to monitor controls (e.g., "Is MFA enabled?" or "Is there a code reviewer?") in real-time.
  • Marketplace Positioning: For small startups, Vanta acts like "TurboTax," providing a guided path to audit readiness. For enterprise customers, it acts like "Datadog for compliance," providing observability and auto-remediation for existing programs.
  • Network Effects: Vanta utilizes data from over 30,000 completed audits to advise customers on what specific auditors want, creating a proprietary data moat that general AI models cannot replicate.

AI and the Future of Agentic Trust

  • Automating Questionnaires: Security questionnaires, previously a massive manual burden, are now heavily automated.
      • GitHub Case Study: GitHub currently uses Vanta to answer 92% of the security questionnaires they receive automatically.
  • Agent-Generated UI: Vanta is moving beyond standard dashboards to "on-demand software."
      • Bespoke Interfaces: Launching in Summer 2024, Vanta will use agents to generate task-specific UI on the fly (e.g., a specific reporting view or connection wizard) tailored to the user’s immediate compliance need.
  • Professional Evolution: AI is enabling the "Amelia Engineer"—a single technical owner who can manage security, IT, and compliance roles simultaneously, delaying the need for specialized GRC (Governance, Risk, and Compliance) teams.

Founder Philosophy: Why "TAM is Bullshit"

  • Market Creation vs. Sizing: Cacioppo argues that market sizing is a poor predictor of success because it only measures the present.
      • The 2018 Reality: In 2018, the "startup SOC 2 market" was essentially $0. Vanta created the market by reducing the cost and time friction required to get compliant.
  • Truth-Seeking: Influenced by her time at Union Square Ventures (USV), Cacioppo notes that successful founders are relentless truth-seekers.
      • Anti-Patterns: A common sign of failure is a founder who becomes delusional or sends investor updates filled with "a lot of words and no metrics."
  • USV Influence: Working with Fred Wilson and Brad Burnham taught her to prioritize the intellectual engagement of an idea over the "great man" theory of venture capital.
"One of the original founding hypotheses of the company is if you want to start a security company for startups, you should actually start a compliance company. Because your customers never ask you for security, but they do ask you for compliance." — Christina Cacioppo
"Market sizing is bullshit. The market size today is only a predictor of the market size today... If you looked at the SOC 2 market in 2018, my best estimate was there's $10 million spent globally. You would never start a startup on that." — Christina Cacioppo
"There is a totally a truth-seeking piece of it. Sometimes you can bend reality to your will, but often reality is reality and you gotta embrace it and figure out how to work around it... Gravity's gravity." — Christina Cacioppo
Christina Cacioppo founded Vanta in 2018 to solve a problem most founders didn't even know they had: compliance. Under her leadership, the company has defined the trust management category, growing to over 15,000 customers. Cheers. Cheers. Good to see you. Tell the Vanta story. We help companies start or build out their security programs and then get credit for all that work through an audit, through a security questionnaire, a trust center. But it's basically like: do all the work to improve your security and then go get credit for that with your customers. All the Vanta billboards I see use the word compliance rather than security. What's going on there? It is one of those where you're like vitamin and painkiller, I think, right? And I think compliance is—SOC 2 is a word that no one knows what the heck it means until they deeply know it and they want it this quarter. Exactly. Yeah. And so it's kind of like—and one of the original founding hypotheses of the company is if you want to start a security company for startups, you should actually start a compliance company. Oh. Because your customers never ask you for security, but they do ask you for compliance. So compliance is like the buying moment for startups. I see. Exactly. Exactly. And then when you're going through that, you have to implement a bunch of—do a bunch of best practices, maybe buy some tooling. But you don't—even if you want to, you don't do it before that moment because you're doing the thing the customer wants. And I guess at a later stage, the buyer would be split where security would be the CISO versus compliance would be the CFO, GC, something like that. I actually think Stripe is like a little—I don't know, in what I see, bit biased, but different in that usually compliance is—there's this unified GRC (Governance, Risk, and Compliance) function and that lives in the CISO org. Okay. And that will centralize internal audit, it'll centralize like enterprise risk. It's like you put those teams together. Third-party risk. Those teams are all together in the CISO org. So you're mostly selling to CISOs. Yes. Okay. How did you wake up one morning and decide you're passionate about starting a compliance company? When I was three years old, it was my first word. Yes. We joked in the early days, we'd like never be able to pull that story off. I don't know. I heard training businesses are good. I've heard—I've heard more absurd founding myths, so I think you could just go for it. Yeah, go. No, it's—the real story is twofold. So one, prior to Vanta, I worked at Dropbox. I worked on what at the time was a new product, Dropbox Paper. We were trying to take it to market and didn't take it to market as well as we could have for several reasons, one of which was—turned out at the time, all the Dropbox contracts had written into them like, "we're secure, we're compliant, we're pen tested, we're XYZ." Our new thing had none of those. And so it was like, in order to talk to someone with a Dropbox account, which was, you know, 100 million people or whatever it was, we had to go through this process. This was Dropbox 2015, so like height of its Silicon Valley power, and you know, year and a half, 10 engineers, no feature building, and all that. I like—that all happened. I was not smart enough to then be like, "Aha, startup idea." And instead, like this—like "Dropbox, what are you doing? This sounds bad." It was about a year and a half later, just talking to startups and founders about security, trying to figure out like, why—is there a company to be built here? How do you get more startups to care about security? How do you give them a way to do it? And kind of came across basically companies that either sort of did nothing for security but felt really badly about it sometimes—still did nothing. And then companies that had a lot of stuff in place. And the "a lot of stuff in place" was because they'd gotten a questionnaire, they'd gotten SOC 2 because of an enterprise customer. And that was kind of the like, "Oh, that thing." I remember that being crazy and onerous and sort of terrible. But also kind of if you do it, like, you know, that is like "Pass GO, collect $200." Huge benefit on the other side. Yeah, yeah. And it was kind of the combo of those two things. What you're describing, I think, is just so commonly the experience in founders who start companies. I mean, it was our experience with Stripe as well, where we had just run into the problem before. But it's funny, you often run into people in university who are excited about starting a startup. And that's generally—and you know, obviously there's lots of sexy stories of people who dropped out of college to start something. That's generally a bad time because you talk to university students and often their ideas for companies are pretty half-baked. It's like "Find My Friends", it's like a college textbook exchange app, you know? Where it's like the five apps, whatever. Whereas so frequently what happens is people go out and they build successful products in the world and they, you know, do Dropbox Paper or they get some experience. And it turns out there are huge markets available with problem spaces that most normal people have not heard of with things like SOC 2. But you have to kind of spend a while seeing how the value flows in the real world work to discover those big opportunities. Okay, so how do you feel when you go to YC now and you have these founders who've like dropped out and been like, "My passion is sales enablement"? But they actually kind of do know surprisingly much about it, kind of. I mean, if you truly managed to learn enough about sales enablement to be, you know—to be able to field a strong product there, then good on you. I just think you're more likely to discover those areas after, you know, five or ten years. What stage is the business at now? We have 15,000 customers. Our growth rate actually quickened the last couple of years and quarters and months, and so it's been about 60% annual plus for the last couple of years since that milestone. So a ballpark number there. A proper business. Mostly. And you go to market through—it's all sold. All sales. Yeah, yeah, which is one of the blessings and curses of Vanta. With what company sizes? All. So we do the like—we call them "the two founders on the couch." But it's like the two founders on the couch are building their thing and someone asks them for SOC 2 and they're working on it on, you know, Friday night, because like when else are you going to do the thing? All the way up to at least one member of the Fortune 50. I would have thought that compliance is very different for founders who have never even heard of it versus companies who have a lot of existing teams here with opinions and stuff built out. So how does that work? It kind of is. So downmarket, or downmarket, I kind of—we're not quite TurboTax, but I think that is the kind of experience a founder wants. Is like: "This is high stakes and I don't want to get it wrong and I don't really know, but just guide me through." And then so that's—that's more of the product experience. And then the output is like, here's a set of controls, but like security rules you follow that are monitored on an ongoing basis. And because of that, whenever you're—you're kind of constantly audit-ready. You always have everything in place. Great. And so that—that's sort of the experience a founder wants, but the output is still a security program that's monitored all the time. Upmarket—I'm looking at an engineer—it's like it's more Datadog for your compliance controls, right? You're like: "I have my program, I have my thing," but it's—it lives in a spreadsheet, it lives in a Jira, custom Jira, it lives in something like that. And I want real-time dashboards and visibility and like, you know, deviations and maybe auto-remediation. And like, I want that world. Okay, so there's almost like two layers to Vanta. There is what your controls should be and then how the controls are monitored and implemented. And kind of early-stage companies want both. Later-stage companies may want more of the latter. Exactly. Exactly. And then the tie-to-audit is like, great, well, you know, in some ways it's like if controls are always monitored, you just pass the logs to an auditor. It's more complicated than that, but that's—that's the base model. Okay, you're getting to a question I had which was like: compliance at some level is not a thing you can just buy, it's a thing you have to do. And so like if you actually talk about all these rules like SOC 2 in particular, but for example, a lot of compliance regimes have this notion of, you know, doer and approver being separate for some thing. And so it's like the, you know—the code review is the famous one. Exactly. The nuclear submarine where you know you have to have the two keys turn simultaneously to launch the PR, I guess, in this analogy. Exactly. And again, Vanta can't do that for you. You have to do it. And so what you do is one, for say a startup, you actually just let them know the complete list of things they actually need to do. And I presume there's some—maybe you can talk about there's some logic of only telling them the stuff that actually applies to them. Exactly. And then there's actually how do you enforce, say, separate doers and approvers in something like code review? Yeah, so for something—so this is where sort of the first thing we built, and we call it "test". So it's—Vanta is modeling after unit tests. You're like: turn each of these controls into a unit test. And so pull from version, you know, GitHub, GitLab, whatever, look at every pull request and check, you know, these fields or this thing or run some logic over it. And that is our test for the control. And so that—that was kind of again the first thing we built, were these tests. But tests are just ways to prove control. So you're just a test suite. You're the battery of unit tests for the compliance rules. Exactly. Why didn't you just say that? Why didn't your billboard say that? It's too niche outside of San Francisco. Oh, now I understand. Yeah, yeah, but I think for the 101 billboard... Oh, what's the controversy with your 101 billboard? Oh my goodness. How—how much do we want to do this? We had a great 101 billboard. I drive by it every day. Yeah, yeah. "Compliance that doesn't SOC 2 much." Arguably, you know, hundreds of millions of dollars in market cap attributed to that billboard. It's funny, that was just in the, you know, annuals of Vanta and startups. The person who came up with that billboard, very pleased with herself, as she should be. As she should have been. 100% right. Yeah, yeah. Her manager at the time was very skeptical of that billboard. Are we negging our users? Yeah, yeah. Is this okay? Are we too far over the line? Anyway, you can guess which one of those people is still at Vanta today. Not just because of that, but it was a good—it's kind of a cultural test. Exactly. Yeah, yeah. Anyway, so we had this billboard, it was great for many years. I used to joke that we've had it locked up for years. Turns out we didn't, and I'm an idiot. Oh, you forgot to renew it. Not even. I know you should have had a Vanta check for that. I know, it was like—it was like your domain and you just—something you're not supposed to—yeah. It was slightly better but still bad. The agency we worked with—one, we should have caught this—our contract was just written in crayon. And we got lots of people who asked about our billboard. We introduced them to lots of startups. You know, some of those startups were also buying with that agency. Wow. And so that startup you introduced them to went in and took your billboard. They didn't even do it on purpose. It was just the agency went to them and was like, "Oh we have this great inventory, would you like it?" And then we found out. Okay, hmm. That's rough. But people will learn about Vanta in other ways. They do learn. We do like to market. Yeah. Okay, and then going back to the other part of the question. So how does the layer work for, you know, the rulebook might be 1000 pages long, compiling that rulebook into the steps that are actually actionable for me? Because I am not a farm, and so all the farm parts of the rulebook don't apply to me. Yeah, okay. So the initial—some of the IP of Vanta. The initial version of it actually was—this is back when we were founders on a couch—was getting like as many SOC 2s as we could. So like Salesforce, Slack, AWS, right? Whatever. And actually opening them all and just comparing them. And trying to extract what was common and sort of doing it that way. So that was the first cut. What we do now is hopefully more advanced, but there's a bit of—now that we have probably 30,000 audits completed, we can just go back and be like, okay, for a company that looks like you, and for this auditor often, what sorts of controls are there? And so we have that input in. Then we can also layer in, both for a company in particular and in general, it's like, you get questionnaires. What are the themes in the questions you're being asked? We just launched a new commitments product that ingests contracts and scans the contracts, right? Like things that are contracted. And so you can then like both pull them out and say: "Hey, this should be a control." And, you know, God forbid something happens, but you're like: "What are my obligations to my customers?" And you can just have all that, you know, basically all that structured data. But one of the most important things is they just want to see progression over time and increased maturity over time. And you've probably had this at Stripe where you want to do some cool new tool that like had no security posture. And a contingent—let's go attack that baby—but like one part of it was like: "Oh, can you just walk this up over time and show me you're making progress." Yes, yes. Is SOC 2 the main Bible, books from which you read? Basically. I mean, it's funny, we don't break it out by framework anymore because it's all just—they're all just inputs into the system for what you need. Sure, but like ultimately you need to comply with some specific things, right? Yeah, yeah. But like yes, most customers will come to us for that first. Number two is ISO 27001, which is if you—part of—if you demand ISO 27001 European enterprises. Okay. And so if you're a European company selling to Europeans, you will start with that. If you're a European startup selling to Americans, you'll start with SOC 2. Australia? How aligned are they? I think our official mapping is like 60-ish, 65%. And the additional ISO stuff is often documentation. Okay, so just a great place for software. Sounds like Europe. Yeah, yeah. Exactly. Yeah, there's like less, you know, "Please implement these six more rules." Okay, so is it SOC 2 and its international equivalents is basically—that captures most of what you're doing. It is probably plurality, not majority. And so we see a lot of growth in—there's like this whole host, "thousand flowers bloom" of AI standards right now. Like that is a whole thing there. There are healthcare-specific things. Sure. There's the PCI piece, which I know you're very familiar with. There's that. And healthcare is this—which... There's HIPAA, which is US law. You can just declare yourself compliant with HIPAA. Like, you get to decide. The downside of doing that is if you do that and are breached, the fines are enormous. And so that's the check, there's some semi-market check there. Can you describe the policy goals that something like SOC 2 seems to accomplish? And you might say, oh it's simple, it's just, you know, security. But yeah, like as we know there's many different facets to that. And so it could be preventing information leaks or it could be preventing fraud against the customer, or it could be all these different things. And so if you were to stack rank, what is SOC 2 actually trying to accomplish at a policy level? I would say it is trying to ensure customer data is protected. I think that is what it is trying to do. And your—just to round out the point here—Java-JavaScript comparison is that, you know, Java was a very popular language before the, you know, emergence of web browsers with JavaScript. And so when they invented JavaScript, they wanted to kind of ride off the Java halo as an easy-to-do programming language, despite the fact Java and JavaScript share no connection at all. But it was just good branding. And what you're saying, is that a similar with SOC 2 here? Okay, so you're saying the primary goal is to ensure that the data that you are giving this company, your software provider, whatever, is adequately protected. Many companies have had humongous data breaches, you know. Equifax was a great—AT&T I believe, a few. Kind of all of them. Like, if you look, every big company has a SOC 2. Yeah, yeah, but there's a difference between kind of some data was leaked in some context versus like in the Equifax case, "Sorry, we lost all of your data." Exactly. Yeah, yeah. We didn't patch the database. Exactly. Which data did you lose? Like, all of it. Yeah. It's very hard to find that moment in the Equifax stock price chart. What's going on there? As in, we think society cares, society should care. It's valuable to not lose this data. And yet, it does not seem to impair what investors deem to be the terminal value of the company. Yes. What are investors betting on? They're betting on like: will anyone churn off of Equifax because this happens? And I think the cynical but correct take is no. Sometimes because you're like, you know, Equifax or Delta. You're not like "Am I going to stop—you know, like I'm not going to stop flying Delta." Especially 10 to 15 years into this, where you're like, "Oh, another one. I'll add an eighth credit monitoring service," right? Like... and I think there is a cynicism there that is probably correct. Yes. The other thing that feels like it's changing in this ecosystem is that the costs of having data breaches are going up because Europe in particular is getting very strict about notifications and sometimes fines around these breaches. How is that changing your world? We see more—so we also cover some of the data privacy standards, so your GDPR, your CCPA, there's a whole, again, alphabet soup of acronyms here. It goes honestly, we see demand for that that goes in waves. And it kind of tracks what you'd expect: it's higher in Europe. I mean Vanta as a product in general does kind of even better in Europe, and better than you would guess for an American—for a California company that doesn't have European roots. And I do think there's some cultural affinity and just seriousness there versus the easy critique of Americans in compliance is: "I'm just checking—you tell me where the bar is and I'll meet your bar, but it's a box-checking exercise." Exactly. Or it is just kind of culturally something that is more important. You can tell me where the bar is, I'll meet it, but also I have my own internal bar, which is more the European take. But we see like demand for say CCPA, which is the California privacy—California version of GDPR, quote-unquote, go in waves. And right now it is definitely—I mean all the American, you know, regulation is—is kind of at a total nadir, but it's—it's down right now. Well, it's down at a federal level. Is it also down at a state level? The kind of energy around these CCPA type things? Yes, it is. Even with California, and like it's not clear what California's going to do and it could go multiple ways, but I think national politics casts a larger shadow, even over like a state like California. Oh, that's interesting. Yeah. And then on the national side, the current administration is very into streamlining regulation through automation and AI. Yeah, yeah, but like that is the catchphrase that they deeply believe in and are driving. I would have thought that this kind of stuff is just too boring to be caught up in any reform initiative. Or will this be streamlined? I think there's—there's very hard-working folks in DC in especially—well kind of across the board, but in GSA, in the executive office trying to do this. And the primary lever they're using is FedRAMP. Yes. Yeah, yeah, I've heard of this. And which broadly I would think of as SOC 2 for the federal government, but basically a very onerous set of both controls and requirements and documentation in order to begin trying to think about selling to federal and often state and sometimes even local governments. And how do state and local governments also use FedRAMP as kind of their rule set? There's StateRAMPs, so there's like literally TexasRAMP. Do they exactly, yeah, yeah. And there is a part of GSA and one team in particular led by a guy called Pete Wasserman who is trying to modernize FedRAMP, but I would say make a like 2020s version of FedRAMP where the current version feels a bit more 90s. And it is unclear if he will get the traction to succeed, but he's fighting the good fight and he gets it. But even if they do that, I find it hard to imagine the society of accountants just copying the new FedRAMP block, stock, and barrel. I don't think they will. I think you're just going to have even more divergence between these things. Yeah. You're just like less control over overlap is the system. Yeah, I feel like your life is the XKCD of, you know, we have 15 standards and the answer is 16. Yes, yes. Yeah, yeah. That is also my answer when people are like, "Well, is Vanta going to make a standard? Could you make a better one?" And you're like: "No. Now you have 16." We have that posted on the office wall. Yeah, yeah. Because it is your life. Yes. But okay, going back to the effects of the European strictness. It doesn't show up in the form of kind of maybe American companies previously were looking to kind of check the SOC 2 box versus now they're like: "Okay it's really important I don't cross this actually quite strict European rule?" Right, right. Whereas I think now, and I think in the—it's funny we were starting Vanta—Vanta as what it is now today in spring of 2018, which is when GDPR was going into effect. And so it was kind of running around and being like, "Will you talk to me about compliance?" And everyone said yes, having this great talk, and then I'd show up and I'd be like, "So SOC 2." And they'd be like, "GDPR is the priority like, next please." And that energy is like mostly dissipated, especially in the United States. I think it's because it's like—the theory at the time was GDPR is written by lawyers at a very high level. You see—it's like not a spec you can hand to an engineer, like comically kind of bad as an engineering spec. But like: "It's fine, we will clarify that in court over the next 10 years." And now we're whatever seven, eight years in... hasn't really happened. Yes, yes. It's still is—is kind of hand-wavy for an engineer, at least to go implement as it ever was. And how does this work with agentic coding where the honest answer to the number of human reviewers of this code is zero? Yes. How should it work? Because right now it is like, well somebody needs to be like, "I did code"—well actually right now it's like agent, you know, writes code, human or agent puts up PR, maybe human or agent reviews it. And I think to a like naive SOC 2 auditor, you're like those seem like two user IDs had that conversation and so we can go forward. But it's more about having two throats to choke as opposed to, you know, "We read the code of this, you know, ATM software and guaranteed that you didn't introduce an infinite money glitch." Yeah. My interpretation from talking to folks is some of the impetus behind that or the primary impetus was insider threat. And like that's what you're preventing against, which maybe—maybe that's my like macro answer: just go through the SOC 2 controls and be like, "What are we trying to do here?" And be like: "Okay great, let's design for that." And that may or may not be how it's written today. That's a good question because on all the insider threat stuff, having two reviewers is kind of one way to do it. Does SOC 2 mandate exactly a lot of other insider threat stuff? Because presumably you should be logging a lot of activity, auditing a lot of activity. You know, there should be process that you have in place. No. And I think this is where actually the—you get to the like technical standard made by folks who often aren't as in their depths in engineering, let's say. Right. And so the controls for like there are a bunch of logging and monitoring controls that are suggested. One thing maybe also mentioned: unlike PCI, SOC 2 doesn't have a prescribed control list. So PCI is kind of different in that it's like: "you must do X, like you must buy this tool whether or not it is useful to you." Which, you know, I'm sure you have your own story about that. Yes. SOC 2 is like: "you must log useful events and have a system to look at them." I see. But it is up to you to decide what the heck that means. Which sometimes it's helpful. I think for a startup that's never done this, it is unhelpful because it sort of opens up a—you know, a maze in a way that's just not great. And that's where being prescriptive like... is part of I think the—and Vanta's initial product-market fit I think is actually largely due to that in a way that—that was not the plan. But I think it's like figuring out how to take that high-level guidance, bring it down in some places in a way that actually makes sense. Christina and her team at Vanta are helping their users automate compliance, which for many companies is the thing standing between them and being able to sell to enterprises. We're very familiar with this category of product at Stripe where you have a complex web of rules that businesses need to be able to comply with so they can move on to actually improving their products. Just take tax compliance and our product, Stripe Tax. As you start selling in more states and more countries, you discover there's thousands of rules you need to follow. For example, did you know Chicago actually has a lease tax which applies to SaaS companies too, since you're leasing out software? Stripe Tax is built to automate all of this. With one integration, it knows what you're selling, when and where you have to collect transactional taxes, and how to register and file on your behalf. So if you want to sell globally without becoming an expert in tax rules, check out Stripe Tax. You know, the—the kind of joking reference that everyone makes is they talk about kind of competition from Claude Code for software products is, yeah, you know, you're not going to just vibe code your X in—in a weekend. But obviously something like SOC 2 is actually the kind of thing that LLMs or kind of coding agents are good at working with. Because there's just so much training data out there and it's a, you know, codified set of rules. And so how is AI helping with what you're doing? And kind of what is your plan for—you're describing some of the scale economies you have in having seen other customers, and I'm curious just kind of what the defenses are against—a customer could in theory say: "Hey Claude, give me the plan for our SOC 2 compliance, make no mistakes," you know? That is a thing you can contemplate. Right. And I think like there's—you know, so you can do the—sort of the very defensive thing, but—and then give you the actual—and the very defensive thing is like right, but like this is a place where you don't want to get stuff wrong. Spending much time on it does not make your beer taste better, right? Like is this really the place—even if you really want to vibe code a bunch of stuff, is this really what you want to vibe code? Whatever. Let's ignore them all. I think where the LLMs are excellent and a little dangerous in built-versus-buy, but then we just need to build better experiences on top of this in our—is like: "Hey Claude, I'm going to give you a mess of data. You go make sense of it to me and like get me ready," right? I'm just going to like give you a bunch of AWS screenshots or API calls. I'm going to give you all my policy documentation. Like, go. I'm going to give you my existing Jira workflow. Go turn it into a thing. And so you know you can go do that today. We are building—this is our like onboarding flow, or it will be our onboarding flow, which is: "Oh, you have an existing program that's already running. Give us all the stuff. We will go map it into the Vanta world." And then in a like Claude world an LLM, it's like: "Okay cool, now you get a—I don't know, files in a folder structure that you then, you know, box-share that over to EY and like call that your audit." Fine. You can—you can do that. In a Vanta world, the outcome is now hopefully we have your program mapped and it is observable and monitored and alerted. And so you have continuous control monitoring, you get your dashboard, you always know what is in place and what is not. And yes, you can go, you know, send a share link to your auditor here too, and they can log in and see everything. And so we sort of think about it as they have lowered the initial audit prep in a way inside-outside Vanta, or like if they're not inside Vanta, what are we—what are we kind of doing? So building that. But the continuous monitoring piece, that you're not going to get out of at least an LLM chat. You've got to go vibe code that whole system. Okay, so you're saying that everyone just wants—like no one enjoys spending time on SOC 2. Everyone wants to have been SOC 2 compliant as of yesterday. And so you're saying part of the advantage in this new landscape is you can just take a whole bunch of unstructured stuff and just empty it into the Vanta hopper and Vanta will make sense of it. And that—it'll get widgets out that, you know, that people want. And I presume part of the defensibility comes from the fact that preference amongst practitioners, in this case the auditors that are reviewing your SOC 2 materials, is a very strong effect. And both QuickBooks and Xero to some extent really grew off accountants becoming familiar with those pieces of software. And companies could have opinions about what they were using, but those were generally overridden by the opinions of the auditors. And so... We have a version of that. It's not—I don't think it's as strong of an effect yet, at least. But even again, we've seen 20,000 audits and—and thousands for particular firms, and so you're like—and control—you know, so you're like: we now do AI evidence evals. So it's like: "Oh, you're going to provide this piece of evidence. We can just tell you: is it going to work for this auditor? Did you upload a cat picture? Did you upload a screenshot without a timestamp on it and you're going to get told: put the timestamp back on?" You know, just like that feedback loop we already have and we've thought about doing things for auditors as well with that. But yeah, it—it sort of moves in the direction of like an AI internal audit, at least. It feels like the data you have of anonymized prior audits is an incredibly powerful network effect that cannot be replicated. Because again it doesn't exist on the public internet. Like the AIs don't have it available to them because it's just private data. And just like, you know, Stripe is an advantage because we have all the fraud data, we know what like a normal buying pattern looks like versus not, and so we can offer the best anti-fraud performance just because we're working with a larger data set than other people. Similarly, people going through an audit: you can tell them that this will work and this won't. This is our Radar. Yeah, yeah. Exactly. In a way that just you cannot do even if you're—you decide to buy it yourself. Yeah. It was a big deal. Yeah. It was kind of cool. Where else have you seen that be useful? In relationships between a software vendor and buyer, right? And so we—so Vanta core, we think of ourselves as broadly and most known for serving software vendors, but people who make software and want to sell it into the world, right? And you're like: "Do you security work? Is it secure?" Grand. Okay. Then we have this third-party risk product. But it's basically I think of: like you're an organization—maybe it's tech, maybe it's not tech—you are buying software and you're going to go put a bunch of your customers' data in it. You want that software to be secure, because if not you have to turn around and tell your customers like: "I lost your data, but it was actually our email provider—but you don't care about our email provider, you think it's me." And I've sent it—anyway, right? Like no one wants to send that email. So there's a whole world of like third-party risk or vendor reviews, and we built a product for those folks. But is there kind of a compliance versus security tension here as you're doing this stuff? We haven't seen as much. What we have seen is—so the person buying software, you know, they might work at a tech company and be quite savvy and up to date on these threats. They might work—one of our customers is, you know, a hotel chain, literally a hotel chain, right? And so not—and they certainly don't get compliance themselves because they don't build software, right? But they buy it. Fine. And so what we generally see is some companies will come in with their set of questions they want to ask. And you know, "Maybe I will read your SOC 2, maybe I will not, but I really want to ask you questions one through ten." Some companies don't have that and again there's some part of the Vanta value proposition is like, well we'll prescriptively guide you. And so we have a product principle just around reasonable defaults, and it's like: can we make the reasonable default questionnaire in this case something that leans into security versus compliance, or versus, you know, "Do you have a policy to X?" And you're like: "Can you just ask them if they X if you care?" And so that's a place where we've tried to, on the margin, nudge—nudge the buyer questions toward more security knowing that will change the like economic incentive of the vendor. One of the big debates people are having right now is how AI productivity gains show up. And I feel like you could have an opinion on this because we have filled out a lot of security questionnaires at Stripe. And I think we'd be very happy if the machines could take over from here. We really don't—we filled out enough. Exactly. But so one case you could make is the machines are getting quite good, they can understand what Stripe, you know, is and isn't and can do and can't do. And so every time we get a security questionnaire, AI can fill it out. The counter argument you could say is maybe Jevons Paradox will show up and they'll be even more exhaustive and elaborate and custom, you know, security questionnaires, and so, you know, the total amount will increase. But so just how do you see AI productivity showing up here and the effect? So the questionnaire is actually a great example because on the questionnaire side, we tried to build this product in 2018, actually before SOC 2, because it just easy—it seemed easier actually, but the language models were not good enough. And then we tried again in like early '21, and like BERT came out and you're like, "Oh is there a model?" It like was not good enough. And now it is good enough. So to that actually, GitHub gets 92% of all of the questionnaires they receive answered through Vanta. And so you're not—not at 100, but you're like it's GitHub, they have AI tools like—they have Copilot, it's a lot. And so we are absolutely seeing this. Like the models are definitely good enough to do questionnaires. Sorry, people ask GitHub to fill out a security questionnaire. Most of them fill it out. Exactly. Using GitHub and now they can mostly turn around and return those security questionnaires. Exactly. With 92% filled out just entirely automatically. Exactly. And we have—it's kind of human, but just it's review and approve, right? And then the confidence scores on like prioritizing even for the reviewer, it's like you probably want to look at this section if you want but you kind of don't have to, whereas like: will you really look at these ten? Yeah. And so like all of that work, like our product does that. That's cool. Okay, so where do you think it goes broadly? I think that so much of the work of a compliance team is again keeping things in sync, keeping—and like different sorts of text in sync, right? Adding on new compliance regimes, which is just adding controls. And then you—but really you truly want to—you want to map the, you know, new ones to the old ones and figure out where the duplicates are. That's actually a huge part classically of the work of a compliance team. And so I think there are so many opportunities for LLMs and agentic workflows in Vanta's business. And we've, you know, we're—we probably have a couple dozen of them. And if I think about a roadmap, you know, knock on all the things, like, we'll have hundreds by the end of the year. But it's just—and it's kind of what we've been doing is breaking down what folks do, right? And so you're like, okay, there's the questionnaire piece. If you send out a questionnaire, someone has to read it on the other side. And then you have to think about it and figure out, you know, where does it work, where does it doesn't. Oh, I have this new policy update. I need to put this thing in a policy. I need to—we're going to start doing, I don't know, ISO 42001, which is a new AI standard. And so how do I map that in? I need to rerun a risk assessment. I'm going to change my like risk score. Anyway, all of these things, all of these tasks, are all just like workflows that you can have an AI do, run an eval against with like subject matter experts and then help climb until they're quite good. And so it feels like, you know, you can reason about the number of people in a profession especially at a certain stage of company changing. Like if you think back to ancient times, I don't know the year 2000, if you had a 10-person company with, you know, 10 Gateway 2000 beige workstations... Yeah, yeah. They probably would have had an IT person. And that IT person would have had servers in the closet. Yeah, exactly. Servers in the closet. They had a Microsoft Access database. They had to do software updates, you know, for all the machines. Occasionally like lint and stuff would get stuck in the mouse ball and they'd have to take it out and clean it. Exactly. Or they'd pull it out of a hat. Exactly. So IT was a real job. Now, I don't think a 10-person company really has an IT person because the hardware is super reliable, you just buy a new version every now and then. Everything's in the cloud so there's no porting data over. You just use Google Workspace for everything and it works really nicely. And so IT still exists as a profession, there's lots of interesting things, but, you know, Stripe has a bunch of IT people. You don't need a bunch of IT people at a 10-person company. You just have to mail laptops to how many countries in the world, which is like actually kind of hard. Yeah, totally. Yeah, we have some IT challenges. But—but again, we're 10,000 people. And again, it naively feels like you will have a similar effect with compliance as we had with IT, where the profession very much stays around, it actually gets more skilled rather than less. Like: I think the stuff we do in IT is harder than kind of the basic IT that a 10-person company would have done. I think it's basically true. Yes. So one—one model we've thought about with Vanta even like pre-AI is we will delay the point at which you have to bring on a full-time security compliance person or like a kind of consultant who's spending meaningful time. But you know in the past if you're an enterprise company, maybe you did that at 50, 100. And it's like: can we actually push that further out? Because what we see is then like an engineering leader or someone in the engineering org can manage more of this. Because they kind of have the mental models and they're usually systems thinkers and they can, you know—and they're responsible for it so they can kind of change the stuff. Exactly. Yeah, yeah. And so you're like have this—what persona? We call them the "Amelia Engineers". But like you have the Amelia Engineers just like going further here. And then you bring on—and then you can bring on a like unified security and compliance person versus like, oh you have your security person and your IT person and your compliance person. But it's a little bit of like the what we're seeing in the like engineer-PM-designer collapse, you have the like security-compliance-IT collapse into one role. You keep them unified for longer. Exactly. If you can give them good tools, right, they can do that. Okay fine. And then again pre-AI, but then over time that team starts to grow and you have a GRC team and you have a CISO and all this. What we're talking about now, and we haven't seen yet, but if I like had to future-cast and guess, is we're going to see actually those GRC teams collapse a bit more into these single threaded owners. Because if you think about a GRC team today, there's maybe one person answering questionnaires, one person just reviewing new software vendors, right? And you look at those and you're like: "Okay, I think you can mostly agent—agent the work and then have someone oversee it with 20% of your time." But like okay great, it's collapsed two into 40%. Right? And you just kind of—and you're like: okay, you have some person who's like the—who was responsible for bothering the engineers to get evidence for them, you know, when they—for the audit or like to get the control in place. Because they don't own the control but they own the program so they have to go to the engineer and be like, "Hello, I noticed you have a new database that is not encrypted and like will you please encrypt it?" Right? And you're like: you can just have software go nag that person. Anyway, it collapses and so I do think we will see smaller GRC teams managing agents. But actually in the future... yeah. And then they are doing more "I'm not doing the security reviews, I'm like thinking about the findings and overall managing this like risk portfolio, this like vendor risk portfolio," versus being like: "Oh this vendor doesn't have this thing and I need to go get it from them." Yeah, I think what you're saying is there's a strategy component to how should we be doing things and then there's an hourly labor component to compliance, which is like, "Oh we did ten times as many sales, we need like ten times as many bodies on the security reviews." And you're saying that AI will eat up a lot of the hourly labor part of compliance and leave people doing the strategy work. Yes. Yeah. I—I do think that. What changes are coming down the pike in the world of compliance? I think there—to the XKCD, there's lots of folks both trying to make new compliance standards, but it's a little bit of like: what's—what's the difference with the 22nd one? From a Vanta perspective, we've sort of taken a like: "We will support them all because we have built a machine where it is easy to add a new one in." But obviously you only want to support ones that customers actually want to comply with, so you're not... Well, yeah, but like kind of what we do, actually, we used to spend a bunch of time debating which ones those would be and it was honestly so frustrating. Exactly. Now you're just like build the machine that just logs them in. And so the debate and the documentation you would write... I also need some painted ones. You want to support this painted standard? Sure. Whatever. Yeah. Exactly. We did that with compliance standards and integrations because it was just like the—the prioritization debates were just too intense. Yes. And you're like: "We can take all of that debate time." Anyway, so anyway, there's a bunch of those. I what—I'd bet on any of them. If you really like pressed to me, I would say ISO 42001, just because it's the European one and... I don't know that ISO. You got to—you got to catch me up on this new ISO. Yeah. You're 42001, it's a good one. It's a good one, add to my recommended bedtime reading. So ISO—you you know this, but like European standards body and it is their version of what you—what one should care about with AI. It ends up being pretty data privacy focused and pretty high level. Those are the counters. The pros are that European enterprises are the ones that care the most about AI and this is where they would turn and so it's the thing that has the most market traction so far. But again, none of these are like breakout. None of them have product-market fit. And none of them are regulatory. Like they are all... correct. They're all you opt-in to... correct. Exactly. It is like this market has like kind of roughly agreed you might need this thing. So there's that. I think the—okay. I'm kind of proud of this. The—like trust centers. Are you familiar with the trust centers? They are the security status pages. Oh, sure. Yeah, yeah. Like trust.blahblahblah. trust.vanta.com. I didn't know they're called "trust centers", it's just like a status page. Yeah, but like very—for your security posture. So you get the like, you know, green bars sort of or green traffic lights or yellow traffic lights, but it's for your controls. I see. Because they all just say the same thing. Like a status page is like red, amber, green, whereas hopefully the trust center always says "We're real compliant, boss." Yeah, yeah. Exactly. And so there's like a version of that. And so if nothing else, what they actually are, they're ticket deflection for the GRC team. I see. You know, because like one, your sales team sends them out and you're like: "Doesn't it look good?" And then if you have any questions, you know: "Here you go." But it's the pre-filled questionnaire. It's a pre-filled—yes, exactly. It's like: "Here is the binder of information. Please read it and if you have questions for me thereafter, I am here." Does that work? It does, actually. And I think part of it is the just show of strength. Yeah, yeah. And the show of like: "I'm on top of it." Yep. And then there's the like: "Yeah, yeah, read things first and then if you want to ask me, go for it." Has outbound selling gotten harder now that everyone is has a million AI bots spamming everyone? I think it—what I have heard is phone calls work. In a way that I—I kind of wouldn't expect. For now. Around until a year from now. But like emails... bleh, yeah, yeah. A million AI bots. and I mean like how many ChatGPT-written emails do you get, you know, in your inbox every day? Yeah. But outbound phone calls are currently working. Got it. Yes. But again, it's only a matter of time and I think, you know, then you're just back to like, oh events, right? Especially like small but curated events and yeah. A topic we talk about sometimes here is on-demand software. Patrick's taken to saying that software should be like pizza, you know, delivered fresh, piping hot. But why are you using software that someone coded five years ago, rather than just the computer deciding what to render to you at that moment? Is that coming to Vanta? It is. It's something we're—we're playing with internally, but like really excited about is having an agent that maybe is guiding you through the process or doing something in the near—need—needs the user to render an opinion or make a connection or, you know, just do something. And you're like: can the agent just generate UI specific for that task so the user completes it? And then move on. And you get this like bespoke, agent-generated, hand-generated UI just for that task that does happen. But are you talking about—because like maybe people have a little bit of a experience with agentic UI where, you know, an AI chat interface is like people's first experience. And you know, maybe there's like three options you can choose, for example. That's like kind of an agentic UI. But you're talking about a full UI? Or like maybe you have that agentic chat bar on, you know, half of the page or a third of the page and then the other two-thirds would be a SaaS app you could imagine: a data table with a view and columns and, you know, rather than just like customizing it, you're like: "No, no, no, I just want you to do this thing and I will take over that right-side canvas of the page, generate the UI for the thing," or generate the report—I think reporting is another great use here. And what step of the process would this be in? Would this be like you have 14 things you need to fix to get to... Yeah, so we thought about in two ways: so kind of in the like you're—you're setting it up and you're—you're going through. And actually reporting is another, I think, great case. It's like no one wants more, you know, knobs and whistles on their reporting tool. And also no one really wants to learn—learn SQL. You just want like: "I want a report for this, go generate it." Yeah, yeah. Like: "Not quite right, take this out." That's cool. So when will we be seeing generated UI in Vanta? This summer. Wow, okay. What has worked well from a go-to-market perspective for you guys? In a way that we don't—we have tried to—but brand spend, honestly. The billboards, we do all the stuff people do of like, you know, zip code tracking and all of that. Gong call mentions, so recorded sales—like mentions of the word "billboard" on recorded sales calls, and then you can track... To measure the billboard uplift? Exactly. Then you track those deals through to closed-won and like that's great. And you're ultimately doing a geo split? You're looking at the, yeah, yeah, locations where you had a billboard versus not and seeing... Exactly. And then just like does the prospect say the word "billboard"? I see. On a call at some point. anyway, so some of that. Podcasts have been—podcast advertising has been exceedingly effective for us. It's funny because we start—we started doing it in late 2020 and our first salesperson, Eric, who's still at the company, he really wanted to advertise I think on "This Week in Startups". And I thought it was silly because my model is like the only, you know, companies that advertise on podcasts are like founders who want to hear about themselves, like this is just nonsense. Or mattress companies. Exactly, or mattress companies but like we are neither, right? Like does everybody really need a SOC 2? Anyway. And so that folly came to me and was like, "I want to spend $60,000 on this ad." And my deal with him was like, "Fine, but you gotta sell four more Vantas because a Vanta basically costs $15,000." And the next month he sold like 34 more Vantas because of the podcast ads, and that was one where you're like, "Well I know nothing, you should keep going." I call this, by the way, I think there's a real—there's the "founder negative value added time" where, you know, founders have these incredibly strong views that are wrong, but it's—it's like really hard to over—I mean it's good that you let him go and do it, because sometimes, you know, I think some people would have said, "No, we're not doing that, it's silly," and would have taken many more years to learn the—no the deal was he had to sell four extras. Yeah, yeah, I like that. I feel like I've heard you on the "Acquired" podcast. We do. We advertise on "Acquired". We do "Invest Like the Best". Yeah. Yeah, I like those. And I think in the early days... so this was helpful and then deeply unhelpful... but in the early days before we had competitors, we tried to basically make this call-response of like someone says "SOC 2", someone says "Vanta". And this really close association... Oh you wanted to like own the term SOC 2, basically. Basically. Which worked really well until we had competitors who were like, "Well we do a SOC 2, but we're, you know, Vanta but cheaper, but worse but better." And then you're like, "Oh that got," you know, like now we're all pointing at a thing we don't own. Yep. And like that's bad. And so there was a—yeah that's like kind of a—then there was a great reframe on that one, but... What did you learn working with Fred Wilson? USV is a very special place in lots of ways, and I think USV is fundamentally about ideas. More so than other venture firms? Yes. I think most venture firms are sort of "great man, great person" firms. They're about the person, that this person will like do the thing. I have no idea what this is, but I like the cut of his jib. Exactly, yes. And I think USV is, you know, in a—in a—this is too black and white, but is like basically the opposite. whatever person can walk in, but like if it is an idea that is interesting and compelling and intellectually engaging and networked, that is like classic USV. And that maps back to some great people, I don't mean that, but it's just first thing—first, second, and third thing is the idea. And so like really pressing on that... so there's that piece. that I think was like very important. I think the second part is market sizing is bullshit. you know you can—be as academic or whatever strategy-ish as you want about it, and like the market size today is only a predictor of the market size today. And I think I like deeply learned that because if you like—just if you looked at the SOC 2 market in 2018, my best estimate was there's $10 million spent globally, and you would never start a startup on that. But the theory of Vanta was like, well if we can make this thing easier to get and like take down the cost—dollars but really time—more people will get them. And like that ended up being deeply true. But yes, that was not a market, especially for startups. The market for startups getting SOC 2 in 2018 was zero dollars. Truly zero. Yes, yes. Okay, so Vanta is an example of the kind of company that being too TAM-brained you would not—you would not come up with it. And now it's like "Oh, but of course everyone gets it." And you're like "Right, but like 2017..." Again, when did Stripe get a SOC 2? Probably reasonably early on because it's so core. Like it's not a small part of your stack. But—but definitely before 2017. It's a very interesting framing on USV where—I feel like you can see this a little bit in, you know, Fred's blog and stuff where it's clear, yeah, exactly attraction to ideas and a prepared mind for, you know, when something like crypto comes along, you're—you're ready to just try. Yes. And is that across the firm or is that Fred in particular? It's Fred and Brad for sure. Brad is the undersung Fred partner. I mean they started the firm together so... tell me about the Fred and Brad relationship. Yeah, yeah. Brad Burnham is a venture capitalist, mostly retired now, but like ex-also excellent, incredible track record. He and Fred started Union Square Ventures in I think 2002, first fund was '04, took them two years to raise that fund. If you go look up USV '04 vintage, like, god we all should have invested in that, you know. but it was the two of them and then Albert came on as the venture partner I think in '06 and is now also a founder as a partner. Going real deep here, sorry. but it was the two of them and there is just... yin-yang is not the right frame, but like complementarity? Yeah, like and so many of the ideas of the firm were back and forth by them. And and then Fred was excellent at articulating those ideas in a way the rest of the world could understand which he did on AVC. but I think one of the underappreciated things is like how much back and forth there—there kind of was there in the creation there. Yes. And like that—that pairing is I think probably should be in the annuals of like, you know, the Kohl-Dor pairing, you know, maybe like Leone-Moritz, like these venture pairings where you had two people who could play off one of another and they were just like that. Like I think Brad and Fred had that for like a decade and a half. What's the difference in person? I guess like say Doug and Mike Moritz at Sequoia are very different people and again I think that's part of how it works. Yeah. Nothing Fred and Brad are as different as this—sure but like yeah, Brad is cerebral, philosophical, academic, like so interesting to talk to and you have this wonderful conversation and you'll be like, "Are there any ties to the business world in this?" You know, but like—but truly these—and then like one thing Fred could do was like go back and forth with him and be like, "Oh, freemium!" You know, and then like run with freemium, right? But it—it wasn't just I'm going to market this—it was the back and forth and then the communication out. Wait, did Fred coin the term freemium? He did. Huh. In a like a blog post in like 2000, I don't know, eight, nine, something like that. Got it. Yeah, yeah. Right, like doesn't that feel like it was just like always a term? Yeah, exactly, it just was what it was called. It's like 1952, didn't they talk about freemium? Yeah, it's like when you learn one of those things like did you know the "saying the quiet part out loud", that term comes from The Simpsons? In what ways are you a different CEO coming from your experience as an investor? I wouldn't have done it, is the real answer. that's a good start. Yes, sure. I mean I was really lucky in approximately nine million ways with them. One of the ways was for two years I just like met 15 founders a week for two years straight. And I think whatever model I had of like what a founder is or does was like, yeah that exists, but like look at all the ways one can do it. Yeah, and there's like some common ones, some more successful than others, but just like there's a lot of ways to do this thing and I think that exposure was super helpful for me because you got to see people who I felt more affinity or similarity to in whatever dimension like also do it. And it was just—it was kind of the role model thing but not like one person just, you know, you meet a thousand of them and you can pick out the pieces. Having all that training data, what patterns do you think you see in people who went on to be successful? Or maybe conversely, what anti-patterns did you see in the people who... Oh, I think there is a like—someone said this better from me than me but like there is a totally a truth-seeking piece of it or just—sometimes you can bend reality to your will but often like reality is reality and you gotta like embrace it and figure out how to work around it. Like reality sometimes is an immovable object. And I think there was a delusion. Yes. I've noticed that. To the unsuccessful founders. Yeah, yeah, the like, "Oh no, but I can change this," and you're like, "I—that one I don't, you know, gravity's gravity kind of." Yes, yes. Yeah, the—the version of this I talked about with Des Traynor is I feel like investor updates with a lot of words and no metrics... oh yeah, those are bad... are those—those are bad. And actually like no investor update is fine, like you didn't—you didn't have to send me anything anyway. No, it's either very good or very bad. metrics is fine, but like a lot of words and no metrics is almost a sure sign of failure. Because again I think it gets at that delusion/failure to truth-seek tendency. Right. what else? There are some famous Etsy and Kickstarter, a bunch of these companies of this era stories where I think I developed—yeah this is true—this huge appreciation for product-market fit. That sounds so dumb, but kind of now it's the like if you think you have it you don't framing, but you're just—if you're asking whether you have it you don't, yes. And so Etsy, great example. You're like co-founder CEO spent 80% of his time for kind of years like making people desks because they had this lovely cultural thing where when you joined you would get a like homemade bespoke desk because they sold homemade bespoke things. So it was a thing that Yancy would make people a desk? Rob—I think Rob Kalin at Etsy he would make people a desk. Okay, or sorry I'm getting confused between Kickstarter and Etsy. This is an Etsy version. And you're just like now if you're like 80% of a CEO's time is making desks and the business is on fire. See Amazon had it figured out where you had to make your own desk. I think that's a much more scalable—Rob made the desks. But you know you're just like—I mean it's kind of a funny story but you're like the business was fine. Yeah, yeah. Exactly. And so there are just these things that have like their own physical immovable objects and you can be making desks for people or the... it doesn't matter. Yeah, yeah. Exactly. And like if you don't have that, you know, it's not that we should all—I mean maybe we should all go make desks, I don't know. How did—would you—would you spend time making desks at this stage? I think woodworking is very—I—I don't do it, but I did it as a kid and it was satisfying, so yeah. Does Vanta expand from here beyond security? Do you start helping people comply with everything else? Just do you continue taking over the world until all the world runs on Vanta? What's fun? Definitely taking over the world, making desks along the way. No. I think right now we do think about especially in this world where in theory code has become much cheaper. Actually so two things: so one is like can we add different pillars or verticals? And so there's a whole lot in security especially for a small business or a mid-market business. I think enterprise is a different ballgame there, but there are things there. And then when we think about it, we really think about parts of the CISO organization versus for the most part other parts of an organization. But we would think about enterprise risk or internal audit. Financial audit is adjacent and interesting. What can you do in internal audit or financial audit? So internal audit is sort of easier for us given what we've built in a way is like we have all of this and you know currently we're packaging material and sending it to the auditor. But you can imagine packaging it and sending it to a internal... yes, and it's the same thing, it's a controls platform, right? Decide what it is that you should do and then validate that you are doing it. Prove that you are doing it. Exactly. Financial audit is the system is similar, it's a different set of integrations and data. And so it's thinking through, okay, what is at what is the right point to start building out those ERP integrations, the payment integrations, all of that to get that sort of data to parcel this in. Exciting. Well, Christina, thank you. Thank you.

The delayed Trump-Xi summit, Iran, and the US-China relationship

Brookings Institution(164.0K) ·32:57 ·158 views · 2026-03-31
Donald Trump (President of the United States) Xi Jinping (President of China)
> "The postponement was purely due to logistics, it was not a pressure tactic on Beijing...
TL;DR: The postponement of the Trump-Xi summit to May 14–15 due to the U.S.-Iran conflict provides Beijing with strategic "time" to build leverage, as Washington appears increasingly distracted and burdened by Middle East operations and upcoming midterm elections. Rescheduled...

TL;DR: The postponement of the Trump-Xi summit to May 14–15 due to the U.S.-Iran conflict provides Beijing with strategic "time" to build leverage, as Washington appears increasingly distracted and burdened by Middle East operations and upcoming midterm elections.

Strategic Context of the Summit Postponement

  • Rescheduled Timeline: Originally slated for March 31–April 2, the summit was delayed by President Trump to mid-May. While the administration officially cites "logistics," the President initially tied the delay to seeking Chinese assistance in stabilizing the Strait of Hormuz.
  • Fragile Stability: Experts describe the current relationship as a "big building on a weak foundation." Both sides are prioritizing stability over competition in the near term, though this stability is viewed as precarious.
  • Beijing’s Reaction: While frustrated by the "unorthodox" notification of the delay via media, Chinese leaders view the extension as an opportunity to play for time.
      • Strategic Reprieve: The delay may prevent the U.S. from advancing competitive measures in the immediate run-up to the meeting.
      • Leverage Building: Beijing believes its leverage increases as the U.S. approaches midterm elections and faces internal economic pressures.

The Impact of the Iran War on U.S.-China Dynamics

  • U.S. Strategic Distraction: The war in Iran is seen as a "war of choice" that leaves the U.S. strategically distracted, internally strained, and diplomatically isolated from allies who refuse to engage in the Strait of Hormuz.
  • Net Benefits for China:
      • Indo-Pacific Vacuum: Military resources are being diverted from the Indo-Pacific to the Middle East, potentially weakening the U.S. regional deterrent.
      • Defense Resource Depletion: High munitions usage in Iran depletes U.S. stockpiles that rely on Chinese rare earth inputs, strengthening China’s "rare earth card."
  • Risks for China: Beijing remains concerned about disruptions to global trade and the potential for a spike in oil prices, as China receives a majority of its oil through the Strait.

Taiwan and the "Principles" Trap

  • Rhetorical Pressure: President Xi is expected to push for a shift in U.S. declaratory policy regarding Taiwan.
      • The Language Shift: China wants the U.S. to move from "not supporting" Taiwan independence to "opposing" it.
      • Strategic Goal: Beijing seeks to have the U.S. endorse Chinese-framed principles like "win-win cooperation" and "mutual respect," which implicitly require the U.S. to respect China’s "core interests" in Taiwan.
  • Regional Consequences: Any shift in U.S. rhetoric on Chinese soil would cause significant "ripple effects" across the U.S. alliance network in the Indo-Pacific.
