Competition and Industrial Policy: Who Has "Time"?

  • Technological Sovereignty: China is moving rapidly to build a fully domestic semiconductor industry to eliminate vulnerability to U.S. export controls and sanctions.
  • U.S. Vulnerabilities: Despite knowing about rare earth dependencies for 16 years, U.S. progress in reducing this vulnerability has been slow.
      • Structural Deficit: Unlike the Chinese or Japanese systems, the U.S. is not institutionally built to conduct long-term industrial policy outside of the Department of Defense.
  • Military Modernization: Far from being deterred by U.S. operations in Iran, the PLA is likely using the conflict as a "gym" to study U.S. precision-guided munition tactics, similar to their response to the First Persian Gulf War.

Signposts to Watch

  • The "Putin Factor": A potential visit by President Putin to Beijing in the first half of the year could trigger a reaction from the Trump administration.
  • PLA Personnel Purges: Over 100 generals have recently been dismissed. Experts are watching to see if this "operatic turmoil" begins to infect China's policymaking process as Xi prepares for a likely fourth term in 2027.
  • Treatment of Allies: Watch for Beijing to increase pressure on U.S. partners (e.g., Japan) if they perceive the U.S. is too distracted by the Middle East to offer meaningful pushback.
"The postponement was purely due to logistics, it was not a pressure tactic on Beijing... both are signaling that the postponed summit shouldn't be read as a major setback... it's really about logistics." — Patty Kim
"I've always had this view that stability is kind of a vacuous term... it's one of the most dynamic relationships in the world... it always seems very fragile or precarious." — Jon Czin
"China's trying to do what I don't think any other country has done before, which is to build a fully domestic semiconductor industry... China is playing the long game here." — Kyle Chan
"I don't envy the person who has to explain to President Trump why he can't say that he opposes as opposed to does not support Taiwan independence." — Ryan Hass
Hello, you're listening to The Beijing Brief from the Thornton China Center at the Brookings Institution. This is part of the Brookings Podcast Network. My name's Ryan Hass; I'm the Director of the China Center. And I'm Jon Czin, a Fellow in the China Center, and this is our first episode of a new podcast. We're talking to our colleagues about the US-China relationship on the heels of President Trump's postponed visit to Beijing. And hi, I'm Patty Kim. I'm a Fellow with the China Center where I work on Chinese foreign policy, US-China relations, and the politics and security of East Asia. And I'm Kyle Chan, also a Fellow with the China Center. I focus on China's technology and industrial policy, from AI robotics to biotech and manufacturing. This podcast is part of our effort to try to bring some of our work here at the China Center and across Brookings to a wider audience. We have a lot of great work going on within our China Center. We have a strong roster of experts across our non-resident senior fellows and across the Brookings Institution. And we recognize, as much as it may pain us sometimes, not everybody has the time to read all our finely crafted posts, but you do have time, we hope, to listen to us and dial into a conversation that really goes beyond the headlines to understand some of the deeper issues that are in play, both in the US-China relationship and within China. Thank you, Jon. President Trump was scheduled to travel to Beijing from March 31st to April 2nd for a summit with Xi Jinping, which would have been the first presidential visit in nearly a decade to China. But on March 16th, President Trump announced that he was asking to delay the visit by roughly a month because of the war in Iran. The announcement came just as the United States and Chinese trade negotiators were finishing two days of intensive talks in Paris to help set up the summit. Just a brief note: at the time of this episode's taping, the summit had not been rescheduled; it had just been postponed. We've just learned that the summit will be rescheduled for May 14th and 15th. Now back to the episode. And so to get us into this conversation, I'd like to ask the team to situate what is happening in the United States-China relationship today, and where is the relationship heading? Patty, at the most basic level, in your view, why did President Trump decide to delay his meeting with President Xi? Well, Ryan, in the President's own words, he said that because of the ongoing war in Iran, he felt the need to stay in Washington rather than travel to Beijing. And what's notable is that in an interview with the Financial Times, which first broke the news of a potential delay, it seemed like Trump was tying his trip to Beijing to a commitment by China to help out in the Strait of Hormuz—to help stabilize the Strait of Hormuz. And he specifically said that he'd like to know whether the Chinese would step up, and he implied that they should because they get a majority of their oil from the Straits. Now, since those initial comments, the administration has really gone out of its way to walk back those comments. So we heard from Scott Bessent in Paris and others who emphasized that the postponement was purely due to logistics; it was not a pressure tactic on Beijing. At the same time, we've heard US officials emphasize that the trade talks went really well, that they were constructive, and that the bilateral relationship is still on track. And interestingly, the Chinese response has been pretty measured too. I mean, they've also repeated those words that the bilateral relationship is progressing, that leader-to-leader engagement is important, and that they are in communication about whenever the summit is rescheduled for. So I think the takeaway here is that Washington and Beijing are both signaling that the postponed summit shouldn't be read as a major setback to the bilateral relationship and that it's really about logistics. Do you all buy that explanation? I mean, I think there's of course some validity to that with a war going on in the background, right? That makes sense that you would want to postpone. I've heard some people say that Beijing wasn't necessarily interested in hosting President Trump, too, while a major conflict is underway and that that could be potentially awkward. That's not my sense, though, from what I've seen in Chinese sources. It seems like that if the trip were going to go ahead, they would have been happy to welcome him. My sense is probably big picture this will end up being a blip for the bilateral relationship. But I think for the moment, I would be surprised if behind closed doors people in Beijing who were involved in orchestrating this visit were not frustrated. And there was plenty of reporting already that they were frustrated by the lack of detailed staff preparations going into this. And so I suspect that that's probably bubbling just below the surface. And I think there's also an open question, too: if this is just about logistics, where are we going to be in the Iran war—this is a huge separate question—five, six weeks from now, as the President has said? Or do we really think that operations are going to be wrapped up and this will have a bow on it? That seems unlikely as a non-Middle East expert at this point. Ryan, what are your thoughts having been involved in so many of these kind of engagements before? Well, I think that you and Patty have covered it well. The only thing that I would add to it is that I've heard that the Chinese were deeply frustrated to learn through the media of President Trump's plans to change the timing of his trip, which is very unorthodox and sort of reflects the manner by which the President relates to and approaches President Xi and China. Kyle, did you have anything you wanted to add? Overall, it's very interesting to see that both Washington and Beijing are pulled to each other kind of like magnets right now. They both have strong reasons to want this summit to happen. I think from the US side, President Trump wants a stable relationship with China, wants to be seen as making progress, especially on the trade front, and most importantly doesn't want any issues related to China to derail his many other activities foreign and domestic. On the Chinese side, I think they would also be keen to signal to the world, to their own domestic audiences, that the two, in their view, world superpowers are nearly at the same level and are at least enjoying a moment of stability in the near term. That's really interesting to hear you say that because whenever this meeting does happen, it really puts Beijing in the position of being a relative bright spot in Trump's foreign policy, right? He's got a lot of metaphorical and actual fires to put out right now and Beijing doesn't seem like it's one of them at the moment, right? So there are some ways in which, you know, I think this really ends up playing to Beijing's advantage. And I think we were having this conversation beforehand too. I mean, as frustrated as Beijing might be, I wonder if one of the other advantages that they get is that they do seem like they are playing for time. And pushing this visit off, while they may be annoyed about it right now, it does kind of further build in that backstopping, right? I think give the administration real pause to advance any kind of competitive measures over the next five or six weeks or whatever the timelines end up being in the run-up to this meeting whenever it happens. You know Jon, to put just a finer point on it, and if we were doing this podcast as a drinking game, I think stability would be the word that would require a shot of everyone. And I think by the end of the podcast people would be pretty tipsy, which is quite a contrast from where we were a year ago or two years ago at this time where competition would have been the watchword or the big word in the word bubble. Yeah, it's really interesting you say that too because I mean, I've always had this view that stability is kind of a vacuous term, right? Especially in the context of the US-China relationship. It's not really a stable relationship. I think as we all know and we all appreciate in our day-to-day work, it's one of the most dynamic relationships in the world. There's a lot going on, it's very multifaceted, and so when you're talking about stability, it always seems very fragile or precarious or just a lack of an obvious crisis or irritant in the moment. And I actually think that very much applies right now in this moment. And I've seen some Chinese commentators use this phrase, right, that it's a large building on a flimsy edifice, something along those lines. I'm butchering the translation, but have you guys seen this as well? Yeah. Big building on a weak foundation. Yes. Thank you. So I want to ask you, Jon and Kyle, going back to sort of how Beijing is reading this moment. Clearly, Jon as you mentioned, even after the war broke out, Beijing was not going to cancel this summit. They realized that in order to manage the US-China relationship, you really need to keep Trump happy. And he was talking about this trip for months and he still is, and so they weren't going to pull it. But having said that, they did have reservations about giving Trump sort of the grand reception that he was probably looking for at a moment when the United States is at war with one of China's strategic partners, and in a war that the Chinese have opposed. And second, even before the military strikes, there were a lot of concerns that not enough working-level engagement had happened to really set up the logistics or to set up concrete deliverables for this summit. And so I'm just curious to hear from you both, maybe starting with you Kyle, on sort of how you think Beijing is reading the postponement or the broader situation. Do they see this as a setback, maybe an American power play, or do they see this as a relief for something else? I think it's probably a mix of relief and anxiety. On the one hand, I think that they had been hoping that the US would prepare a bit more, that the talks would be more substantive, and they could put some real issues on the table. Maybe that wasn't how the US was going to approach it, but maybe that was the hope from the Chinese side. So to see the timing get pushed back a bit, maybe it would offer the US some extra time to get ready for these discussions. At the same time, though, there is maybe a tinge of anxiety here where the question is: it got pushed back once, can it get pushed back again? How much further can this slide? And will an issue like the Iran war end up overtaking and kind of pushing out what China sees as its key priority, which is the US-China relationship? It's interesting, I have a slightly different take on it. I think it depends on what you think Beijing wants from these engagements. And my theory of this has been for a while is that they mostly want time, right? There may be other things that they want or they try to get, maybe additional US investment, rollback of some of our competitive measures, but I think mostly they're playing for time. And I feel like my theory of this has been that Beijing already thinks that it has a lot of leverage over Washington after last year and what they did with the rare earths, and they think that that leverage is actually going to increase as we approach the midterm elections. And I think we've all heard this, even before the current spike in oil prices, there was also kind of a mirror image of many assessments of China's economy in the US. They can see the frailties in our own economy, right? They can see the K-shaped economic growth, the problems in the labor market, right? And they think that those two dynamics, the political calendar and our economic frailties, they thought they were going to compound anyway in the run-up to the midterm elections and that that would give them more leverage. So if the theory is that Beijing wants more time and they think they're going to have more leverage over time, I could see them getting real benefit from the fact that this is delayed. And my thought was too, especially because the administration said that they want to do a series of engagements over the course of this year, that could actually diminish Beijing's incentive to have a big deal in this meeting. I mean, this is often the case with Chinese statecraft, right? The meeting is the objective, but I think that was going to be especially true for this meeting. So I know there was grumbling and grumpiness about not having more to offer, but I think we all agree that there were going to be pretty low expectations in terms of this meeting, right? Patty, I want to ask you one question, especially because you've written a book on this now, about China's partnership with Iran. I know there's a school of thought in Washington that said that, you know, this is somehow an indirect move to compete with China. This is great power competition through another vector, and that the moves against Venezuela and Iran in particular are designed to box in China. Somebody who's really looked at this closely: do you buy that and what do you think of that argument? I think some people could spin it that way, but I don't really see it that way. I don't really know how squeezing Venezuela or Iran is really giving us an upper hand, giving the United States an upper hand vis-à-vis China. And of course there's been interesting conversations in China as well about, you know, how are they going to manage these situations because the United States is taking out their partners. They're not really doing much beyond sort of rhetorical positioning where they say, you know, we are for sovereignty and territorial integrity, we are against military action, and, you know, the US should return to the negotiating table to hammer out conflicts. But you don't really see the Chinese stepping up to assist their partners and I'm sure there's questions about should we be doing something more. But the Chinese way of operating for decades has been: let's just have very flexible strategic partnerships. We're not going to be offering security guarantees or nuclear umbrellas or anything like that. When we're working with partners, we can dial down or dial up the relationship as needed. And so, I think for them it's probably an interesting moment. It'll be interesting to see when Trump goes to Beijing how Xi will talk about these conflicts and whether he'll be asking the US to pay attention to Chinese interests or what conversations might come out on those topics. And when the two leaders meet to discuss these issues, it will be a rather fresh conversation because thus far in the preparation for the President's trip, the only channel that has been activated has been the economic channel. The absence of a strategic dialogue on a sustained basis while the rest of the world is literally blowing up has been a somewhat extraordinary event, which leads me to ask all three of you: do you find from China's perspective the situation in Iran right now, is it net positive, net negative or neutral for Chinese interests? So, I can point out a couple positives and negatives from the Chinese point of view. Some positives are that it distracts the US away from the Indo-Pacific and away from China. It focuses the US back again on the Middle East. Another potential positive for China is also that, remember, their rare earths card was and still is their most powerful piece of leverage. And right now, the US defense industrial base is starting to deplete munitions that rely on these rare earth inputs. So, that is something where, in a sense, the US is now kind of strengthening that card that China has. On the flip side, though, there are some risks from Beijing's point of view with this conflict dragging on. One is that even though it has taken many steps to build up a stockpile of oil and other goods that would pass through the Strait of Hormuz, there are concerns about what would happen to those supplies should this drag on. And then more broadly, there could be knock-on consequences in terms of disruptions in global trade and the global economy, which China depends on quite a lot. I completely agree. I think there are definitely risks for China. But I think the war essentially puts the United States on weaker footing vis-à-vis China. I mean, right now Washington is looking really strategically distracted, internally strained. The Trump administration has launched a war of choice against Iran with very unclear objectives and unclear endgames. The US is looking isolated. All of our allies have basically said: no, we're not going to help out in the Strait of Hormuz. At the same time, as you mentioned, the oil markets are under pressure, gas prices are rising, there's risks to the broader global economy. Domestically, the war is extremely unpopular. And Trump's polling is weak with the midterm elections coming. And so this doesn't put the US in a strong negotiating position vis-à-vis Beijing. And I think this gives them leverage because they understand that President Trump, when he goes to China, will need some wins. He'll want the trip to yield big numbers, whether it's on Chinese purchase agreements on agricultural goods or Boeing planes, US chips or what have you. And so the question is: what is Beijing going to ask for in return? If you look back in history, really Chinese leaders tend to press the hardest when they sense an advantage. So if you look back to the 1970s, when Beijing engaged with the Nixon administration, they saw an administration that had campaigned on sort of winding down the conflict in Vietnam and it was looking for an exit. The administration saw Beijing engagement with Beijing as a strategic card to press the Vietnamese. And so the Chinese realized, hey, we have some leverage here. And it was at this point that they tried to push the United States to basically recognize their position on Taiwan. They didn't achieve all of their objectives, but they were able to push the United States in that direction. So that within that decade, the US had recognized Beijing as the official government of China and we had abrogated our official ties with Taiwan and wound down the defense relationship that we have. And so you can imagine this is the sort of opportunity that Beijing may see on the table. Now, I don't know if this is necessarily what the Chinese are going to be pushing for exactly in April or May whenever the summit happens. But they're going to certainly be laying the groundwork for this. And some of the clues we've heard is that the Chinese want the United States to embrace principles that Xi Jinping has long championed for the bilateral relationship. So these are win-win cooperation, peaceful coexistence, and mutual respect. And of course, baked into these principles, at least from the Chinese perspective, is that the US recognize China's core interests, especially when it comes to Taiwan. And so they're going to want to lay the groundwork so that in future summits, in future engagements with Trump or other presidents, they could say, hey, you endorsed these principles and so now you need to live up to it and respect our claims on Taiwan. So that's something to be watching for, I think. That's why I think getting drawn into a negotiation over principles is a trap that the United States has to avoid. What do you think about Patty's point on the Taiwan issue? I get asked this question a lot, right? Is Taiwan going to be on the menu if they're not at the table in this summit? And is there going to be some kind of grand bargain, or is there going to be a push for a rhetorical change in US policy? Ryan, I know this is something you've thought a lot about in particular. Well, at a minimum, I expect that President Xi will make a great effort to impress upon President Trump how sensitive of an issue this is and where his concerns lie as it relates to Taiwan. When the two leaders meet, when they meet in United States, the United States sort of controls the atmosphere, the environment, and to a certain extent the agenda. When they meet on the margins of multilateral events like the G20 or APEC, it's usually a fairly quick meeting with consecutive interpretation. Each side can only get through a few issues at a time. When they meet in China, China controls the environment and that's when you get the full extended version of explanation of China's concerns and interests as it relates to Taiwan. And so I expect that at a minimum President Xi will go through in intricate detail what he wants to see and not see as it comes to Taiwan. The question that is unresolved is whether President Xi will push aggressively for President Trump to say that he opposes Taiwan independence as opposed to does not support Taiwan independence, which would align more closely with China's preferences and goals, or whether China pushes for President Trump to say that he supports eventual peaceful unification of the Taiwan Strait, which again would align more closely with China's goals and preferences. The countervailing force on this is that President Trump changes his mind very often. His words are very fluid, and the Chinese recognize this, and so it would have, I think, less currency coming from President Trump than it would a more traditional president. And it also could invite a certain degree of backlash, making Taiwan a more prominent issue in the run-up to midterm elections in the United States and eventually presidential elections in 2028. And so the Chinese will have to sort of weigh that against their long-held desire to get a US president on Chinese soil to parrot their preferences as it relates to Taiwan. Yeah, I think it's a very fair point. I mean, that distinction between opposing Taiwan independence and not supporting it—I mean, it's pretty subtle stuff for most people, right? And you can imagine how it would be hard to explain that to your average principal or just about anybody, like what is the real difference and what's really at stake here. I think we appreciate, right, that this would have a broader significance and a broader impact across the region if there were a shift in US rhetorical policy, but I think for most people it seems like pretty abstract. Yeah, I don't envy the person who has to explain to President Trump why he can't say that he opposes as opposed to does not support Taiwan independence. But in the most basic terms, I think why it matters so much is that "does not support" is a principled position, "oppose" implies an active effort to challenge something, and does the United States want to put itself in a oppositional position to a potential outcome for Taiwan in the future? And one thing I would just note is that, you know, the region is going to be watching this very closely. Is there a change in US declaratory policy or even off-the-cuff remarks by President Trump? And it could, you know, have ripple effects, especially given sort of the tensions that are already existing in the US alliance network in the Indo-Pacific region. And I think if allies start to think that they could be on the negotiating table between the United States and China, that is a bad place to be for the US. And so there're going to be reverberations even though these seem like rhetorical, nuanced discussions that have little relevance. Does anyone want to make a case that the awesome military capacity on display around Iran will have a deterring effect upon Chinese adventurism? So my theory about it is that in some ways they are deterred, right? This is part of why they have not made a move on Taiwan, but in terms of building up their own military capacity, I think they seem very focused on it. And the purges in the PLA, which is something I've been following very closely, they get a lot of questions about this. I think from my view, I think this shows how serious Xi is about getting the PLA oriented and prepared for this mission. The fact that he's willing to get rid of, you know, people that have ties to his father, people like Zhang Youxia, the PLA's top officer, I think it shows that he's willing to make deep incisions into his own political network to make sure they get this right and that he is actually very focused on this. I actually worry about this that, you know, as remarkable operationally and tactically as the US operations have been this year, both in Venezuela and in the early phases of the operations in Iran, that it's actually going to have an invigorating effect on the PLA over the longer term, right? Like, they've already gone back to the gym and gotten pretty bulked up and I think this has given them some new interesting exercises to contemplate. And it's kind of analogous to what happened to the PLA after the First Persian Gulf War. They saw what the US was able to do with precision-guided munitions and I think that really fed into a lot of their thinking about how to build out this already quite dramatic military modernization program. So I worry that, you know, again, this goes back to the conversation we were having before. There's this school of thought that this is going to get inside Beijing's head. I think the question is: well, yes, it's going to get inside their head, but what is going to be the outcome going to be? And I could see it being one that leaves the US in a weaker position over the medium- to longer-term rather than a stronger one. Especially because, yes, they're seeing the upsides, but as you were saying, Kyle, there's also the depletion of our munitions, right? Like, we're actively moving forces out of the Indo-Pacific theater, right? So there are potentially medium- to long-term upsides for them and even short-term upsides for them. Yeah, and I would also just add the sort of reaction on the military side might mirror that of China's reaction on the economic and technology side, where feeling exposed to, say, US export controls and sanctions has really spurred China's own efforts to shore up its vulnerabilities, to, you know, zero in on some of the areas where it feels that it needs to patch up its weaknesses and double down on this industrial policy to become more resilient to US pressure and to become a more powerful actor on the global stage. So I think one of the really interesting things that's going on in this moment is that both sides see this as a tactical reprieve, maybe a strategic one, right? They recognize that they need to shore up their vulnerabilities, both the Chinese side and the US side after what happened with China's export controls on rare earths last year. And I think one of the interesting things that I've noticed is that both sides seem to really think that time is on their side. You see that reflected in Beijing's rollout of the Five-Year Plan just a couple of weeks ago, that they think time is on their side and this next five-year period is going to be really crucial for them. But I think there's a little bit of that in the zeitgeist in Washington here too, especially in the administration, that time is on our side, we understand that we have this vulnerability and we just need to play it out. I think this raises then the really interesting analytical question of, okay, objectively, who has time on their side? Kyle, somebody who follows a lot of these issues closely, what's your thought on that? Yeah, so from the Chinese side, they've not only identified their areas of weakness and have long known about them, but they are moving very fast to try to make progress in areas like semiconductors, aircraft engines, a lot of the key inputs that go into the broader semiconductor industry. China's trying to do what I don't think any other country has done before, which is to build a fully domestic semiconductor industry, including all the equipment that goes into it. This is very difficult. It takes years, maybe even decades, but I think China is playing the long game here when it comes to technology, when it comes to building up its industrial strength over time. And you can ask the question about who will get there faster? Will we fix our rare earths vulnerabilities faster? And to some extent we can harness certain advantages that we have that China just doesn't have—most notably, working with our allies and working with a broader network of countries who also share many of the same concerns and worries. But then it does come down to the political question, that of political will. Can the US stay focused in the way that China has on our issues and trying to make progress on something like rare earths? Or is it something where we're going to go back and forth and not really address the problem and it will still be hanging over our heads? Yeah, it's important to note that's kind of been the default setting, right? Like, we've known about this vulnerability for now 16 years, right? Since they did this to the Japanese in 2010. And I'm very struck by a piece the New York Times did on this a few months ago where they said, after 15 years of diligently focusing on this, the Japanese were able to reduce their dependency, I think from 90% to 70%. And so Beijing has the advantage from my perspective of time. And the other thing that's on my mind too is that, you know, when you look at the Japanese system and especially the Chinese system, they are built to conduct industrial policy, and we are by definition not. So I do think, when you're thinking about the competition and who has time on their side in terms of remediating these vulnerabilities that the other side has, just institutionally they are built for this in a way that our Commerce Department, despite the good work that they do or others in the US government—it's not even clear to me who would be the locus for that in the US government. Like, how would we manage that? Like, the Defense Department part of it is kind of the most obvious part because they have such a close lash-up with industry. But if you're talking about some of these civilian supply chain vulnerabilities, it's not clear to me who owns that. You'd need appropriations from Congress, which don't seem to be necessarily materializing at this point. So many people in the US seem to think that time is on our side, but I worry that if you look at it objectively, that it's not, and I find that very worrisome going forward. Well, guys, we've put a lot on the table. I think that this conversation has helped situate how Iran plays into the US-China relationship as well as what we should be thinking about as the United States-China relationship goes forward. But before we close, I do want to ask each of you: what are a few of the signposts that you will be watching in the coming weeks and months to have an indication of the direction of travel of US-China relationship going forward? Patty, maybe we'll start with you and then work over to Kyle and Jon. Sure. So what I would say is that while there's a lot of attention on this delayed summit—when is it going to happen, what's going to happen there—I'm going to be personally watching for what happens afterwards. And so less so on sort of the concrete deliverables that I think many of us could predict, like purchasing agreements, maybe investment, that would be sort of a new area that would be interesting. There's been talk about a potential board of investment and board of trade that the Trump administration has floated, so it'll be interesting to watch those deliverables. But what I will be looking at is how does the Chinese side articulate their demands, how do they describe the trajectory of the bilateral relationship, how do they frame the outcomes from the summit? Because this is going to tell us how Beijing is laying the steps to shape the terms of engagement going forward from this spring summit as the two sides look toward additional meetings, whether it's this year or beyond. Yeah, just building off of what Patty said, I think Beijing usually does not surprise people. They usually telegraph things pretty far in advance, and I think some of the asks that they might put on the table, they might signal pretty clearly from early on. And some things to look out for are what we've talked about so far: Taiwan, trade and investment, potentially export controls might be another topic. So, we'll see how the messaging is done by Beijing leading up to the summit. Yeah, I think from my perspective, I'm going to be more focused in the coming months on what Beijing does rather than what it says. I have very low expectations for what Beijing wants out of this and I think this is a dynamic across administrations where the US often wants a number of things from China, whether it's on the economic front or on the foreign policy front. And I think, you know, not to be glib about it, I think Beijing a lot of times just wants us to stay out of their way, for the most part. So I don't expect too many surprises from it. Part of the puzzle here is given where we're at in the bilateral relationship, what does Beijing do with that, right? Given that you have this surprisingly resilient stability. What I'm looking for is how they treat our allies and partners in the region in this moment, since Trump has put such an emphasis on the bilateral relationship and the relationship with Xi Jinping. Because I think we could start to see even over the course of last year as the administration was focused on mollifying Xi after the post-liberation day walk down. They were pushing pretty hard on some of our allies across the Indo-Pacific. And I think we kind of hit the high-water mark for that with what the Chinese did to Japan right after the meeting between the two leaders in Busan where they rolled out export controls again against the Japanese, and again without much pushback from the Trump administration. So I wonder if even if this meeting is delayed, if Beijing sees the kind of good vibes that we have in the relationship now as signaling that they have an opportunity to push our allies and partners in the region even harder in the coming year, especially if the US is distracted and primarily focused on the Middle East. Are there any domestic or elite political issues in China that you think we all should be following closely in the coming period? So big picture for domestic politics right now, I think the key motif so far in Xi's third term has been policy continuity. We've gotten a giant dose of that in the Five-Year Plan just a couple of weeks ago, that they think time is on their side and this next five-year period is going to be really crucial for them. But I think there's a little bit of that in the zeitgeist in Washington here too, especially in the administration, that time is on our side, we understand that we have this vulnerability and we just need to play it out. I think this raises then the really interesting analytical question of, okay, objectively, who has time on their side? Kyle, somebody who follows a lot of these issues closely, what's your thought on that? Yeah, so from the Chinese side, they've not only identified their areas of weakness and have long known about them, but they are moving very fast to try to make progress in areas like semiconductors, aircraft engines, a lot of the key inputs that go into the broader semiconductor industry. China's trying to do what I don't think any other country has done before, which is to build a fully domestic semiconductor industry, including all the equipment that goes into it. This is very difficult. It takes years, maybe even decades, but I think China is playing the long game here when it comes to technology, when it comes to building up its industrial strength over time. And you can ask the question about who will get there faster? Will we fix our rare earths vulnerabilities faster? And to some extent we can harness certain advantages that we have that China just doesn't have. Most notably, working with our allies and working with a broader network of countries who also share many of the same concerns and worries. But then it does come down to the political question, that of political will. Can the US stay focused in the way that China has on our issues and trying to make progress on something like rare earths? Or is it something where we're going to go back and forth and not really address the problem and it will still be hanging over our heads? Yeah, it's important to note that's kind of been the default setting, right? Like, we've known about this vulnerability for now 16 years, right? Since they did this to the Japanese in 2010. And I'm very struck by a piece the New York Times did on this a few months ago where they said, after 15 years of diligently focusing on this, the Japanese were able to reduce their dependency, I think from 90% to 70%. And so Beijing has the advantage from my perspective of time. And the other thing that's on my mind too is that, you know, when you look at the Japanese system and especially the Chinese system, they are built to conduct industrial policy, and we are by definition not. So I do think, when you're thinking about the competition and who has time on their side in terms of remediating these vulnerabilities that the other side has, just institutionally they are built for this in a way that our Commerce Department, despite the good work that they do or others in the US government—it’s not even clear to me who would be the locus for that in the US government. Like, how would we manage that? Like, the Defense Department part of it is kind of the most obvious part because they have such a close lash-up with industry. But if you're talking about some of these civilian supply chain vulnerabilities, it's not clear to me who owns that. You'd need appropriations from Congress, which don't seem to be necessarily materializing at this point. So many people in the US seem to think that time is on our side, but I worry that if you look at it objectively, that it's not, and I find that very worrisome going forward. Well, guys, we've put a lot on the table. I think that this conversation has helped situate how Iran plays into the US-China relationship as well as what we should be thinking about as the United States-China relationship goes forward. But before we close, I do want to ask each of you: what are a few of the signposts that you will be watching in the coming weeks and months to have an indication of the direction of travel of US-China relationship going forward? Patty, maybe we'll start with you and then work over to Kyle and Jon. Sure. So what I would say is that while there's a lot of attention on this delayed summit—when is it going to happen, what's going to happen there—I'm going to be personally watching for what happens afterwards. And so less so on sort of the concrete deliverables that I think many of us could predict, like purchasing agreements, maybe investment, that would be sort of a new area that would be interesting. There's been talk about a potential board of investment and board of trade that the Trump administration has floated, so it'll be interesting to watch those deliverables. But what I will be looking at is how does the Chinese side articulate their demands, how do they describe the trajectory of the bilateral relationship, how do they frame the outcomes from the summit? Because this is going to tell us how Beijing is laying the steps to shape the terms of engagement going forward from this spring summit as the two sides look toward additional meetings, whether it's this year or beyond. Yeah, just building off of what Patty said, I think Beijing usually does not surprise people. They usually telegraph things pretty far in advance, and I think some of the asks that they might put on the table, they might signal pretty clearly from early on. And some things to look out for are what we've talked about so far: Taiwan, trade and investment, potentially export controls might be another topic. So, we'll see how the messaging is done by Beijing leading up to the summit. Yeah, I think from my perspective, I'm going to be more focused in the coming months on what Beijing does rather than what it says. I have very low expectations for what Beijing wants out of this and I think this is a dynamic across administrations where the US often wants a number of things from China, whether it's on the economic front or on the foreign policy front. And I think, you know, not to be glib about it, I think Beijing a lot of times just wants us to stay out of their way, for the most part. So I don't expect too many surprises from it. Part of the puzzle here is given where we're at in the bilateral relationship, what does Beijing do with that, right? Given that you have this surprisingly resilient stability. What I'm looking for is how they treat our allies and partners in the region in this moment, since Trump has put such an emphasis on the bilateral relationship and the relationship with Xi Jinping. Because I think we could start to see even over the course of last year as the administration was focused on mollifying Xi after the post-liberation day walk down. They were pushing pretty hard on some of our allies across the Indo-Pacific. And I think we kind of hit the high-water mark for that with what the Chinese did to Japan right after the meeting between the two leaders in Busan where they rolled out export controls again against the Japanese, and again without much pushback from the Trump administration. So I wonder if even if this meeting is delayed, if Beijing sees the kind of good vibes that we have in the relationship now as signaling that they have an opportunity to push our allies and partners in the region even harder in the coming year, especially if the US is distracted and primarily focused on the Middle East. Are there any domestic or elite political issues in China that you think we all should be following closely in the coming period? So big picture for domestic politics right now, I think the key motif so far in Xi's third term has been policy continuity. We've gotten a giant dose of that in the Five-Year Plan and then this kind of operatic turmoil in the personnel ranks. And you can see this most conspicuously in the PLA, but it's playing out across the broader party-state apparatus, right? But for the PLA alone, we did a paper a few weeks ago with CSIS where we think that it could be as many as over 100 generals who've been dismissed from their position during this most recent round of purges. And this is one of the things I've been really wondering about: how long is that tenable for in a Leninist system to have policy and politics operating on parallel tracks? At some point the politics is going to start infecting the policymaking process. We haven't seen that yet under Xi Jinping—he's been able to divide the two—but that strikes me as challenging. Right, so I think we'll continue to see the fallout from the purges in the coming year and then the political story is really going to become much more salient next year, right, as we go into 2027 and Xi really in earnest begins laying the groundwork for what will in all likelihood be his fourth term. Yeah, from my perspective, I think that everything that you've said makes perfect sense. The Chinese would like relief from American pressure so that they have more space and room to grow. But I think that they also would like the ability to make a subtle but significant signal to the rest of the region that the United States cares more about its relationship with China than it does with its allies and partners, and that President Trump recognizes that Xi Jinping is his peer and that China is a peer of the United States in the international system. That is, I think, what the Chinese are aiming for and that's why I think they will remain very interested in hosting President Trump in the coming period. The one asterisk I would add to the great list that you've put together of signposts is the possibility of President Putin traveling to Beijing. We know that he wants to travel in the first half of the year to China. We know that the Chinese are ready to host him and there is some room that has opened up on China's diplomatic calendar. And so if President Putin does go to China, what signal will that send, how will that trigger or not President Trump? And so that's a space that we should watch going forward as well. Thank you guys for a tremendous conversation. I learned a lot and I enjoyed it just as much. And thank you to everyone listening to us today to help unpack these complex issues. On behalf of the team at the John L. Thornton China Center, thank you for listening to The Beijing Brief. This podcast is produced by the Brookings Podcast Network. Our thanks to the production team including supervising producer Ike Blake, senior producer Fred Dews, producers Allie Matthias and Virginia Schaus, audio engineer Gastón Reboredo III, and video producers Daniel Morales and Teddy Wansink. Rachel Slattery designed the show's artwork. Also, thank you to our colleagues in the John L. Thornton China Center, Foreign Policy at Brookings, and Office of Communications at Brookings for their support. To learn more about our research, visit us at brookings.edu/chinacenter. To learn more about this podcast, go to brookings.edu/thebeijingbrief or wherever you like to get podcasts. Brookings.

(Grace Olmstead) Finding Place in an Era of Loneliness: Keynote Conversation

Pepperdine School of Public Policy(24.4K) ·27:41 ·4 views · 2026-03-31
Grace Olmstead (Author and Journalist)
> "Less than 1% of the United States population is still on a farm...
TL;DR: Author Grace Olmstead discusses the profound human need for "rootedness" and the social costs of mobility, arguing that addressing modern loneliness requires individuals to embrace a sense of "indebtedness" to their home communities. Agriculture as Identity: Grace...

TL;DR: Author Grace Olmstead discusses the profound human need for "rootedness" and the social costs of mobility, arguing that addressing modern loneliness requires individuals to embrace a sense of "indebtedness" to their home communities.

The Motivation Behind *Uprooted*

  • Agriculture as Identity: Grace Olmstead’s research began from a sense of "gut homesickness" for her farm town in Idaho while living in Washington, D.C.
      • Statistical Context: Her research highlighted that less than 1% of the U.S. population is still farming, leading to a concentrated agricultural system and a disconnect between rural and urban populations.
  • The Importance of "Place": The book serves as a microcosm for a national trend where specific, small-town stories represent a universal ache for connection.
      • Community Fabric: Olmstead argues that "place matters" because the people in overlooked communities are the primary weavers of a healthy social fabric.

The Universal Tension of Mobility vs. Stability

  • Cross-Demographic Resonance: Olmstead was surprised by the lack of pushback regarding the value of mobility. Instead, she received widespread outreach from diverse groups—ranging from UC Berkeley students to military families—struggling with whether to return to their roots.
  • Generational Concerns:
      • Younger Populations: Many feel a "tense question" regarding career prospects versus the desire for connectedness.
      • The Elderly: Older Americans increasingly face the dilemma of whether to move toward their children, as modern mobility means children rarely stay near their parents.
  • The Concept of "Debt": Olmstead posits that modern society fears the language of "indebtedness," yet recognizing what we owe to our past and our communities is a "huge gift" that fosters stability.

The Practical Reality of "Returning Home"

  • Starting from Scratch: Moving back to a hometown after a decade is more like moving to a new city than a simple "reset." Relationships and the physical landscape (such as the suburbanization of Idaho’s Treasure Valley) change significantly in the interim.
  • The "Perennial" Lifestyle: Olmstead introduces the idea of "living like a perennial." This means investing in a place as if your great-great-grandchildren will still hear your name spoken in the streets, regardless of how long you actually stay.
  • Familial Responsibility: Her personal decision to return to Idaho was driven by the practical reality of elderly care, noting that daughters statistically provide more care for aging parents than sons.

Policy Implications and Social "Meshwork"

  • Anthropological Perspective: Quoting Tim Ingold, Olmstead describes human life as a "meshwork" or web. Loneliness cannot be solved via a "quick fix" or a "grand unified theory" because it is intertwined with economic overwork, family decline, and political polarization.
  • Beyond Master Plans: The briefing concludes that while policy and government programs are important, responding to loneliness requires individual agency and a willingness to be "rooted" and responsible for one's neighbors.
  • Education as Preparation: As a teacher of high school seniors, Olmstead uses literature (such as T.S. Eliot) to help students frame their upcoming departures not just as "going far to do great things," but as a potential journey toward a future "return."

Notable Quotes

"Less than 1% of the United States population is still on a farm... that has all sorts of interesting implications... the peoples and communities in these tiny little places that nobody seems to care about are the people who make up a healthy, vibrant social fabric." — Grace Olmstead
"I think our society doesn't like to use the language of debt or indebtedness when it comes to human beings. But I think maybe that's part of our problem. If we think in this atomized way and try to distance ourselves from the language of indebtedness, we lose so much." — Grace Olmstead
"What does it mean to live there as if your great-great-grandchildren are going to hear your name talked about in the street after you're gone? That's something I think could maybe make a real difference." — Grace Olmstead
"Remember when you drink from the well, remember the well-digger." — Audience Member (quoted during the conversation)
I mentioned earlier that in reaching out to Grace as we were organizing this conference, it struck me as I was flipping through the first pages of Uprooted that we were somehow approaching at least the fifth year. I learned in speaking with you that we were really upon the fifth anniversary of the publication of the book, which I would not have noticed, for the record, if Pete hadn't pointed it out. He's like, "Your book is about to turn five." I was like, "Oh. I have a five-and-a-half-year-old. That's all I can think about right now." So it was kind of him to point that out. Well, I wanted to just as we kind of close up this, this time together, go back to that time when you wrote that book. First—and you discuss some of this in the book, but I think it's worth discussing here—what prompted the book, writing the book, A? And then B, what if anything were you surprised by in the response to it? So, I was living in Washington, D.C. at the time when I wrote Uprooted. And I was from a small farm town in Idaho that I had been away from for at least six to seven years by that point. I had met someone in the Air Force, got married, and settled in D.C. But I was homesick all the time. I missed the smells of Idaho when they harvest the mint. I missed the, the Boise River, and I missed going camping, and I missed the smell of the Ponderosa Pines. I just missed everything. I missed my grandparents. And I think it's because of that homesickness that I started researching agriculture. Because I was from a farm community—my grandpa was a farmer, my great-grandpa was a farmer—and it was so much a part of the fabric of my growing up years that then I, I sensed a very strange disconnect when I moved to Washington, D.C. I would be introduced at different happy hour events or things by coworkers as, "Oh, her grandfather's a farmer." And it was like, this is a strange person who knows strange people that the rest of us do not know. And, and I, because of course I had been living in a farm community, I, I started to do some research and I came to find out that less than 1% of the United States population is still on a farm, is still farming. That's where agriculture is at. It's become so concentrated. Yeah, less, less than 1%. And that has all sorts of interesting implications. And so I began to research and to write about farm policy and to look into, kind of especially, the decline of small to mid-sized farms. And I got a fellowship to work on that research. But when it came time to turn that research into a book, I had to address that gut homesickness that had drawn me to this in the first place. And so, every time I worked on this, I ended up just going home and talking to the people I knew and interviewing the farmers who grew up next to my grandpa and my great-grandpa. And it's funny, you'd call them on the phone and they, they would be like, "Who's this?" You'd be like, "Well, I'm a journalist." "Oh." And then I'd say, "I'm, I'm Wally's granddaughter, actually." "Oh! Oh, okay. You can come over if, if you're Wally's granddaughter." So, the book began to focus in from looking at national trends—what was happening on a national stage—to looking specifically at the farm community where my great-great-great-grandfather homesteaded. And I wanted to tell the stories of that place and how it had changed and grown and to look at it as a microcosm of what was happening on a larger scale. But my book editor at the time, who's wonderful, kept asking me, "Why should we care about Emmett, Idaho, and about the farmers there? Why should we care about this particular story of homesickness? You have to tell your readers why this all matters." And, ultimately, I, I just had to say, because place matters. And because the peoples and communities in these tiny little places that nobody seems to care about are the people who make up a healthy, vibrant social fabric. And if I have an Emmett, someone else does too. Someone else has a place they love, they care about, they've perhaps moved away and they want—something about them aches to be back there. And they have to decide what to do with that feeling, just like I did. So. So tell me or tell us—you can tell me, but they're here so might as well listen in—what were you, what kind of response did you expect to the book and what kind of response did you get to the book? I'm sorry, that was the second part of your first question that I forgot about. That's quite all right. I think I expected more pushback from people saying, "Well, we shouldn't be homesick. We should just live where we're supposed to live, move when we want to move." I, I was surprised by how many people emailed, wrote letters, called me, reached out at different places and said, "I feel the exact same way. And I'm trying to figure out what to do." It seemed to be quite a, a common feeling. I've gotten to speak to different groups of college students, from all walks of life, from all different universities, from UC Berkeley to the University of Louisville, to, different small Christian classical liberal arts colleges. And there is a constant theme of young people saying, "Do I move back or not?" And some of them are worried about how many people have left. Some of them are worried about how well they'll fit in. Some are worried about the job prospects that they might have there. But the, the sense of connectedness to a place was actually stronger than I ever expected it to be. And that desire to be in the place where you grew up seems to be just a universal human feeling, that of course can be ruined or broken by broken places, broken relationships, families coming apart. Some people are always on the move, and so I also talk to a lot of military families who are asking this question of, "How do I build a place now because I never had one growing up?" But that desire to have a place, at least, is quite a constant in, I think, the modern American experience. And when you said universal, does that mean that you were, you were getting emails and requests from all different, like, age demographics, you know, various other backgrounds? Or I guess one of the things that I, I sense is that you, you have a, a large following with, with younger people as well, and obviously this is the connection to the colleges. I'm just wondering to what degree you're sensing this among a younger population versus older populations. Right. Well, and I, I shouldn't say universal. That's not a good word to use. Because everyone's different, every community of and every demographic is going to have different, issues that they deal with. I think in older communities they've generally found their spot already, and they're not wanting to move anywhere else. Although you do, I think, have a lot of elderly Americans who are now asking, "Do we move for our kids because we're not sure they're going to move for us? They're not necessarily going to be close to us. So what does that mean for us as we age?" I think that's, a problem that's happening more and more often. But for, yes, for people probably from age 50 down, it seems to be more of a tense question, as they decide where they're at in their career, their family, what choices do they make as regards place. Yeah. And I, I think it is a problem in, in some countries more than others. I have, some cousins who recently visited Morocco and were invited into the home of a family who have literally lived in the same house for thousands of years. They can say that the place where they eat, the room where they eat, is the room where generations for millennia have sat. So we just have a different kind of context here, I think, in the United States as well. You know, in writing the book, what I think is fascinating about your life is that you are now back in Idaho. And I, I just wonder to what degree writing the book prompted that, and how has it been, your experience of moving back to Idaho, given that you've done such in-depth thinking and writing about being uprooted? Well, I think it would have been fine from a communal perspective if we had stayed in Virginia. We had worked really hard to build community there. We had a church, we had neighbors we loved. What it really came down to in my mind is quite practical. I, I wanted to be close to my parents as they aged. I did not want to make them move to me. And I knew that if I moved at the point when they might need me, my children would probably be in their teens or their twenties and they would not like me very much. But if we moved when we did, when they were two, five, and seven, we had this opportunity to grow community there and to embed them in that and it would not feel as terrible. And I think the process is hard because a lot of couples, I think especially those who meet via college or others, have family in different places. And we were really choosing my parents, in a sense, over my husband's parents. But he's the middle child and he's a boy—so boys generally—thanks for clarifying—thank you, yes. Boys don't generally provide elderly care for their parents. Just statistically speaking, it is usually daughters who do that. And my husband's parents are at a point where we know we're going to be cared for by multiple other family members. So, as one of the oldest daughters in the family, it was just like, this is probably what's going to happen. It might not. But now we're ready for it if, if it does happen. So that's not a very glamorous answer. I think the book really did cause me to think a lot about what sort of debt do I owe to the past, to place, and to the people in it. I don't think our society likes to use the language of debt or indebtedness when it comes to human beings. But I think maybe that's part of our problem. If we think in this atomized way and try to distance ourselves from the language of indebtedness, we lose so much. And thinking of myself as indebted to people who I grew up with in Idaho has actually been a huge gift to me. How, from when you left to when you returned, obviously you return to the place where you began, you know, married with a family. How different was your perspective on, on Idaho and moving back now that you were, you were coming back with, you know, obviously more mature, with a family? Did, did your perspective or—and did the place you return to change in the intervening years? What was it, what was it like coming back? Well, I think the first thing I learned is that moving back, especially when you've been gone for a long time, is a lot like moving to a new place. And I think you kind of have to treat it that way as if you are starting from scratch in a new community. Because when I left I was 17, and when I came back I was in my 30s and I had three children. So friendships were—some of the friendships could stay the same, some of the church community could stay the same—but then we had to build a lot from scratch. In terms of just getting back reintegrated, finding jobs, figuring out where we were going to live, all of that. And that is something that I was thankful I was able to kind of prepare for in advance by living in other places and learning that process of kind of growing into a community and looking for places to find that community. Boise, Idaho, the Treasure Valley has changed astronomically since I was a kid. It was once pretty agricultural. Now it is highly suburbanized, and there's a lot of loss that comes with that feeling. But I think part of the loss was experienced every time I went home and was like, "Well, and now that field's gone. And now that field's gone," you know? I, I was ready for a lot of that. But there were still, still things to kind of reckon with once we moved. You said that you're teaching. What about that experience? And is it with 12th graders? Yes, so seniors in high school. Seniors in high school. Is there anything generationally about the kids that you're, you're teaching that some of these concepts, whether it's teaching Tocqueville or some of these other issues around place, do you have a sense that they, they connect with these things in, in maybe some similar ways as you did? We've been, we've been memorizing "Little Gidding" by T.S. Eliot. That's the thing I make them do at the beginning of every class. And then we read together. And we're in their last quarter of high school and then they're going to go out into the larger world. And the words we've been reading every morning have been, "to make an end is to make a beginning." And, one of the greatest lessons Eliot tells us is that we will return to the place from which we started and know the place for the first time. And so we've talked a little bit about what it means for each of them to go out. And many of them are doing what I did. They're going to college on the other side of the country. They're going to be perhaps pursuing careers that might take them away from Idaho, at least for a time. But we've been asking this question of where are you now and what does it mean for this beginning, to be an end, but for it also then to foster this new beginning in your life. And what does it mean maybe for you to continue to think about this idea of returning as well, in Eliot's work. So yeah, they have lots of good questions, but I think they're at the beginning of this, this long journey I feel like I've been on where most adults in their lives are saying, "Oh, you're going to go far and do great things and don't worry about coming back to Idaho." And so it'll be interesting to see what they decide and, and what voices maybe can also call them back as well as forward. We, in our last panel, we were talking about, if you will, kind of the public policy of place and loneliness. I wonder if you had any advice for those in, political or policy leadership about, how we either think or communicate or frame issues with an understanding that that place is important, or, if you will, I mean, how do we, how do we make indebtedness cool again? Tim, do you want...? Hmm. Well, my first thought and I, I thought this was an important point that someone made in the last panel was, we're using multiple words to describe lots of different strands of connectedness. And when we're talking about political polarization, social atomization, the decline of the family, economic overwork, we're talking about all these different strands of, of things that have this overarching idea and language is important. But I think the reason we are floundering about with language is in fact very anthropological. It has much to do with what it actually means to be human. Tim Ingold is an anthropologist who's written a lot of work on questions of how we interact with the world around us. And he describes human life as a meshwork or a web. Even our skin is not actually a solid surface. It's, it's much more like a web and everything passes through it and it interacts with everything around it. And so I think one of the reasons loneliness draws this whole cluster of ideas to it is because it can never be an isolated thing. It must always be considered within that larger webwork of which it is a part. And I think it's perhaps easy whether it's in the realm of data or in the realm of ideas, of theory, of policy, to want to figure out the quick fix or the grand unified theory of, of being able to, make everything better in one fell swoop or the one policy that's going to make the biggest difference. And I do think communities feel respected when you account for their complexity. And so that's one thought as to how we make indebtedness cool again. We just all need to read more Wendell Berry, I guess. We've got a few minutes here before we close. Do we have some questions? Yes. You keep asking the questions. No, that's good. I've learned something from my husband's stepmother, who was an interesting person. But she said something once, she was giving a talk about my husband's father, actually. And she said, "Remember when you drink from the well, remember the well-digger." And, I'm someone who came from a small farming town. And, my father's goal for my life was to get me out of there. In fact, it had been his goal for his life. He was a railroader, and it was a railroad town as well as a farming town. So it was a place that was always controlled by forces that did not live there. And it still is. And it was a different—you know, my, I wish my best friend were here. I wish she'd been here all day. We grew up there together. We had railroad fathers. We were smart girls who left home and, and yet we went back. And for a dozen years, we, we renovated the hotel and the Carnegie Library in the town. And, they're still grateful to—frankly, they should be grateful to Howard's father, because it was his money. But anyway, and I gave that talk. In fact, I have a talk called "When You Drink from the Well, Remember the Well-Digger" and I gave it at several college commencement, addresses in Iowa. I'm from Iowa. And you know the thing I've been thinking about all day? I'm proud of being from there. And I'm very much shaped by it, even though to go—I was back last fall, it's not the same place, just as you said. And that made me think of T.S.—I'm a great Eliot fan. So when you said, you know, "Little Gidding" and "In my beginning is my end" and to go back—I mean, I actually found East Coker and found where he was buried. He did go home again, way home. On the other hand, there's Thomas Wolfe, "You can't go home again." And, and it works together. But I just that whole sense of, of the importance of—I mean, I wouldn't be who I am if I didn't grow up there. And it's why I've never felt at home in California. And I've been—I've lived here for 45 years and I'm married California, basically. But I feel more at home—I don't know, I don't really feel at home anywhere. And that may be a common American problem. So, maybe that, maybe my question is, how do you do that in a country that is made up of the people who came from people who came from somewhere? I mean, your great-great-grandfather left somewhere and homesteaded it in Idaho, which is a great heritage, actually. So what...? I don't know. We're asking a profound question about human migration and what it means to be human, frankly. Something I thought about a lot when I wrote the book is this idea of being transplanted, and that word already came up. I think being transplanted or, or having to transplant yourself can result in great good for a community because you bring, important and valuable lessons that might be a gift to that place. And so many people have to be transplants and I, I want to emphasize the fact that even while living in the Virginia area and D.C., we felt so blessed and lucky to live there. I learned a lot from my great-grandmother who, she was from, the Midwest and her family went to Idaho during the Dust Bowl. And it was really for a lot of economic reasons that they needed to move, they needed someplace else. And so, unlike my great-grandfather, who she married, she, she had to create something from scratch and she had to build a sense of identity. And her parents were from Sweden and Norway, so it wasn't like she left the Midwest with a lot of roots. She made food for every missionary family who came to town. She sang at every funeral and pretty much every wedding. She was constantly hosting families from out of town, inviting people over for Sunday dinner. She was very active with kind of the youth of the town. She had been a school teacher for a while, and so she worked a lot with troubled youth. She was on the board for the local hospital. When people, when I meet people from Emmett, they still talk about my great-grandparents. My great-grandmother passed away in the '80s. My great-grandfather died in 2007. People still talk about them. That is what it means to be rooted. And I don't think it has anything to do with how long they were there. I think it has to do with how they lived when they were there. And so I've talked a lot and in my book I talk a little bit about the idea of living like a perennial. You don't know how long you'll ever be in a place, but what does it mean to live there as if your great-great-grandchildren are going to hear your name talked about in the street after you're gone? That's something I think could maybe make a real difference. Do you have another question? I think that's a great point to end on. Well, I want to thank all of you for joining, and, Howard and Roberta, thank you again to both of you for helping to make this happen. We had a question about third spaces in our last panel and we are going to adjourn to one called Spruzzo's, which is about a 10-minute drive up the coast. For our panels and speakers, there will be a shuttle bus just outside the hotel taking everyone up there. But I hope you all can join and get a chance to speak further with what I thought has just been a great, great series of conversations. I'll just close by saying, that on this, on this point, this, this one about place and loneliness, I think it can get very easy as you were saying before to, and has been said a few times, to see what is the kind of the master strategic plan to address this or what is the so-called government program or what is the nonprofit program to address this. And I, I think what makes it so difficult to do this right—and we heard this from Mary, you know, talking about the work of Miracle Messages and and some others—is it really does require an, in your story too, it requires a lot of us individually if we're going to respond. And it's much easier to point to some plan or strategy or government agency or whatever to, to respond to it. At the same time, what I'm, what I leave here encouraged and inspired by is that there are some, there are wonderful people that are talking about this in ways that when we had that conference yea 14 years ago, it really did feel like we were just voices in the wilderness on this. And I think we are, and maybe it was exacerbated by COVID, a lot of other things happening in the world, but I, I what's evident just from the panels that we've had here is that there are a lot of people that are focused on this issue and talking about it and highlighting the challenges of it. And in that, I think there's at least hope for not solving loneliness but at least responding to it and moderating it. So with that, thank you.

President Trump Signs an Executive Order, Mar. 31, 2026

The White House(3.0M) ·41:37 ·226.9K views · 2026-03-31
Donald Trump (President of the United States)
President Trump signed an Executive Order aimed at eliminating mail-in ballot fraud through federal data sharing and a new USPS barcode tracking system, while also addressing ongoing White House construction, the "Save America Act," and pending Supre...
TL;DR President Trump signed an Executive Order aimed at eliminating mail-in ballot fraud through federal data sharing and a new USPS barcode tracking system, while also addressing ongoing White House construction, the "Save America Act," and pending Supreme Court arguments...

TL;DR

President Trump signed an Executive Order aimed at eliminating mail-in ballot fraud through federal data sharing and a new USPS barcode tracking system, while also addressing ongoing White House construction, the "Save America Act," and pending Supreme Court arguments regarding birthright citizenship.

Election Integrity and Mail-in Ballot Reform

  • Executive Order Mechanics: Will Scharf (Attorney) explained that the order addresses inaccurate voter rolls and mail-in ballot fraud.
      • Federal Data Sharing: The federal government will provide state election officials with comprehensive data to verify the legal eligibility of every voter in their jurisdiction.
      • USPS Verification: The Postmaster General is ordered to implement measures to verify that ballots are only sent to and returned by eligible voters.
  • Tracking Systems: Howard Lutnick described a new "one envelope per vote" system.
      • Barcode Coding: States using the US Mail for elections will receive specific barcodes from the USPS to be placed on envelopes.
      • Audit Trail: The system ensures a clear distinction for mail-in votes, allowing for an exact count of envelopes to match the number of citizens who voted.
  • Legislative Priorities: The President advocated for the "Save America Act," emphasizing the need for universal Voter ID and proof of citizenship.
      • Partisan Divide: Trump claimed Voter ID has 86–98% support among the public but is blocked by Democratic leadership to facilitate "cheating."
      • Policy Extensions: The administration seeks to ban "men in women's sports" and "transgender mutilation of children" as part of broader legislative efforts.

National Security and Crime Reduction

  • Urban Safety Claims: The President reported significant drops in crime across major cities following federal interventions.
      • Washington D.C.: Trump claimed the city is now "very safe" after the removal of approximately 4,000 individuals, noting that 2% of the population creates 90% of the crime.
      • New Orleans: Governor Jeff Landry reported the safest Mardi Gras in 58 years.
  • Border and Immigration: Trump asserted that 25 million people entered the country under the previous administration, including "criminals, drug dealers, and murderers" from countries like the Congo and various South American nations.
  • Iran and Military Capability: Trump stated that the "Iran war may be ending soon" because the Iranian military has been "decimated."
      • Nuclear Deterrence: He emphasized that the goal is to ensure Iran cannot produce a nuclear weapon for years, regardless of whether a formal deal is signed.
      • Military Tech: Mentioned the effectiveness of lasers in melting drones and noted that US planes are "roaming the skies over Tehran" without resistance.

White House Infrastructure and Legal Disputes

  • White House Ballroom: Trump addressed a judge's order to halt construction on a new ballroom, stating he will appeal.
      • Private Funding: The project costs between $300 million and $400 million and is funded entirely by private donations from "rich companies" and individuals, requiring no taxpayer money.
      • Security Features: The facility includes a "drone-proof roof," ballistic-proof glass 45 feet high, bio-defense systems, and bomb shelters.
      • Legal Argument: Trump argued that congressional approval is not required for White House grounds improvements, citing previous administrations.
  • Federal Reserve Criticism: Trump criticized the $4 billion cost of the new Federal Reserve building and called for the removal of Chairman Jerome Powell, labeling him "totally incompetent."

Judicial and Future Projects

  • Birthright Citizenship: President Trump announced he intends to personally attend Supreme Court arguments regarding his executive order to end birthright citizenship.
      • Historical Context: He argued the 14th Amendment was intended for the children of former slaves following the Civil War, not for "multi-millionaires and billionaires" from other countries.
  • Presidential Library: Trump confirmed plans for a Presidential Library in Miami, described as a skyscraper/hotel concept that would include a museum and a Boeing 747 Air Force One in the lobby.
