We're very good at physical things, and we're also very good at interacting with other people. And that makes sense, like, we have to be; that's a lot of our existence. So things that involve behaviors to interact with other people, where you have to actually help somebody, like helping somebody get out of bed or something like that—I think that's a lot harder than people appreciate. So I think like elderly care and taking care of small children, I think those things are going to be hard, and they're probably going to be harder than people think. And the stakes are very high. It's not just that; the stakes are high in many places. It's just that it's probably like the pinnacle of something that fools us into thinking that it's easier than it really is, right? Because we're so evolved for interacting with people and doing things physically. And, you know, if you're helping somebody get up the stairs or get out of bed, you don't have to think very carefully about how you're going to do that. You kind of know. So I think it's really the pinnacle of Moravec's paradox. If I think about an LLM as a brain and now it's effectively studied everything, I don't know how else to put it, and then I think about a robotics brain, a robotics model's brain, instead—what are like the "dark parts" of the brain? Like, what has it not been able to study or penetrate or learn? What are the areas that have just been really difficult that matter but have been hard for us to get into? One of the things that people are remarkably good at is using physical analogies to understand other situations. I don't know whether this is something that LLMs can or can't do, but it is something that people use a lot. They use it in everyday life, and they also use it for very sophisticated problems. So, for example, you could say that company has a lot of momentum. That's a physical analogy. You know exactly what it means; I don't have to explain that statement to you. But if you actually think about that, it is quite a complex thing. There's a lot riding on that word "momentum." There was an interview with Richard Feynman where he talks about analogies that he makes in regard to subatomic particles. And he says like, "Okay, we use the word spin." Okay, like the thing is not really spinning, like it's not like a spinning top. But all those kind of analogies really help us make sense of it, and not just in a way that allows explaining concepts, but it actually leads to conclusions. It actually leads to inferences, and those inferences actually make sense, right? So that's kind of remarkable: that we are so primed to interact with the physical world, so primed to have physical intelligence, that you can use it in everyday speech by saying that company has a lot of momentum, and you can use it when advancing fundamental theoretical physics. That's kind of remarkable. I don't know if LLMs can do that—maybe they can—but I think that really understanding physical interactions and causal structures, there is something kind of special about that, and it's clearly something that people get a lot of mileage out of. I'd love to talk about the role of researchers and the actual people doing the research. In the LLM world, it's fairly shocking how few people are at the global scale responsible for basically all the progress in LLMs—someone like Ilya as an example. What is that like in robotics? How many people in the world are truly impacting this trajectory, and what does good research mean? I think those kinds of questions are often very hard to answer about science because I think that we sometimes have a tendency, especially when we look at history, to underline particular milestones. Certainly in machine learning this is the case. You can say, "Okay, AlexNet was a big step for this, was a big step for that." That's true. But I think it's also important to remember that these advances happen because lots of people are trying lots of things, and even some of the failures are actually very instructive. Like, I complained before a little bit in a low-key way about the controversy around end-to-end robotic learning, but I don't know if robotic learning would have advanced the same way if it were not for the controversy, so to speak. It is true that you can kind of look through the list of successes and mark down that these folks have a history of repeatedly hitting home runs. But I think in reality in the scientific community, it's not just the home runs that are responsible for progress. Even some of the failures and even some of the bad ideas are very instructive in pushing towards the good ideas. Yeah, it's fascinating to think about. The example you gave before is so interesting where the research insight was just to give it some coaching and it gets better. It seems like that sort of insight can be very powerful and high leverage, which makes me wonder: what have you learned about what makes for a great researcher? Research is definitely different from engineering because in research the important thing is to get to an answer to a question, which often requires cutting some corners. And one of the most delicate decisions in research is: when do you try new things versus when do you stick with what you're already trying? And that's very, very delicate. It's very, very hard to figure that out. If you get it wrong, then you can miss something really remarkable. So if you get it wrong and you don't stick with something for long enough, you might be right there. You might be about to get to the answer and then you stop just short of it. That's terrible. Or you could get stuck just hammering against something that's never going to give way for years. So deciding when to sort of turn a little bit and look this way and that to open yourself up to more opportunities, versus when should you keep hammering on the thing because you're about to get the solution—that's often the most important decision, and some people have an instinct for getting that right and that counts for a lot. You've obviously been in and around and are great researchers. What are these people like as people? How do they tend to be distinctive from the average person? I think they're just the same. I'm thinking about the people that I deeply respect that are really good at this stuff. I have a very hard time thinking of a single set of personality traits. The one constant is that there is no constant, basically. There might be a commonality in that to do effective science you have to be very passionate about that, but even that passion can come from many different places. Like, I've worked with people that were remarkably effective that are just driven purely by the desire for novelty. They don't give a damn about what their technology does; they don't give a damn about whether it's useful. They just want cool new ideas. I've also worked with other people that just really want to solve a particular thing and they're just as happy building stuff as they are testing out experiments as they are just hammering away at things. Whatever it takes. And all those types can be very effective. You mentioned the difference between research and engineering which also makes me think of manufacturing. Like, Elon would be fond of saying that the factory is the product—that the hardest part of this whole equation is actually the scale up of whatever this thing ends up looking like, making 100 million of those. How do you think about that part of the equation, or is it too remote at this stage to spend too much of your time? No, I think it's an important part of the equation. I'm not sure it's the part of the equation that we most need to figure out right now, but it's certainly part of it. And as you might have guessed from my answers to the other questions, a lot of how I prefer to think about this is to figure out the hard part and then enable a lot of experimentation on the other parts, right? So yes, making a robot at scale is difficult. Making a robot at scale is even more difficult if you don't know what kind of software is going to run on it afterwards and you're not even sure whether it's the right kind of robot. So I think one of the really valuable things we can get out of general-purpose AI tools like robotic foundation models is the ability to get a lot of the other stuff figured out so that at least some of the uncertainty goes away. So that when you scale things up, you have some confidence that this is really going to work. A lot of people that listen to this are entrepreneurs, people that run companies. A very popular question has become: how should a traditional company begin to think about using LLMs or preparing itself for the ongoing improvement of these models? How would you answer the same question for robotics? It's a very good question; it's also a very difficult question because the technology is changing so rapidly. I want to illustrate why this question is difficult with an example. So here is a particular uncertainty about the tech. This is going to be a little bit specific, but it's an example. Will the robots rely more on demonstrations or on reinforcement learning from autonomous data? We're working on both of those things and they're clearly both important. But how somebody should prepare for the technology would be pretty different if they're expecting that they need lots of teleoperation to produce lots of demonstrations and a little bit of autonomous experience versus the opposite—like a tiny number of demonstrations and huge amounts of autonomous experience. Is it 90/10 or 10/90? And that's something we're hopefully going to learn about over the next few years, but it does change the correct approach pretty dramatically. So that's kind of a case study of how changes in technology will alter this dramatically. From a business standpoint, is the right way to think about it just like get really clear on the economics of the labor in your business or something? I'm curious how you think about that part of the equation—the way that this will change the nature of labor itself. I think coding tools are a really nice example to look at for a template of how this might work. It's not like coding tools came on the scene and suddenly we don't need software engineers anymore; it's that the coding tools increased the productivity of individual software engineers. There's some amount of work that needs to be done to make sure that people are able to use them, and there's some amount of technology development that needs to be done to make them useful for the appropriate use cases. These things are co-evolving. They're also still changing, like coding agents are different than code completion tools and so on. But I think it's a nice template for us to look at to see how AI tools combine with people doing a job to increase their productivity and also raise new challenges. I think we'll actually see something like that with robotics, too. A more realistic template is not like the humanoid goes in and the people just leave. I think it'll be more like there are some aspects of the job that can be done by a robot, some that can be done with a robot working together with a person, and some where the person needs to do something special to make the robot more productive. There will be cases where it's the other way around, where the robot does something that makes the human more productive, and it'll be this kind of dance that we've seen with coding tools. Do you have a favorite robot that's not part of what Physical Intelligence is doing? I do really like the Boston Dynamics robot, especially the new version of the Atlas, because it is in some ways very humanoid-like and in some ways very not humanoid-like, right? Like, they made some interesting decisions about how they want more range of motion on the joints, so it can do some pretty cool things. It's also a very agile robot, which is really cool. It makes for those awesome demos, so I'm a big fan of that. I'm generally a big fan of everything that Boston Dynamics has done. Should or could anything be read into the fact that Boston Dynamics has been doing very cool demos for a very long time and they don't actually do anything useful for people, for customers? Yeah, it's a fair question. I think it's also a fair question for lots of robotics companies, to be fair. I think that, what I'll say in general terms, is that I think there is a lot of value in demos that serve to illustrate challenges on the road to something useful and productive. Obviously, you can also do a demo without being on the road to something useful and productive, but I think that there is value in demos. I think that demos that are used correctly in service to a mission can provide people with an illustration of what to expect, and they also provide a challenge. You just have to be honest in setting up that challenge. How much do you think about the business endpoints? I think to this point Roomba's like the best-selling robot of all time in the consumer category, which is kind of surprising. And of course you might be on the edge of some sort of Cambrian explosion. How much of your cycles do you spend thinking about, like, "this is the shape of a product that might result from this that maybe is the way we bootstrap our way to all this data?" Yeah, I mean, I certainly spend some time thinking about it. I think it's just something that's very hard to reduce to like a very concrete answer right now, but it's not too bad to think about a space of possibilities. And a lot of what we're doing when we develop our models, when we experiment with different tasks, when we do demos like the robot Olympics—underneath, we're kind of prototyping what does it look like when we try to do something real with this. Obviously to different degrees of real, and what goes wrong. So it is something we think about a lot. It's not something that I have even close to like a concrete answer to, but there's a space of possibilities. And a lot of what we actually are planning to do in 2026 is also experiment with different things in that space. When you study the history of general purpose technologies, which certainly this would be a major one if it comes to fruition, you often find this constellation of things happening around that thing that enable it. Obviously, like LLMs are a direct complement to what you're doing. Are there any other surprising technology areas or trends that help you do what you do but are different? So, one interesting thing is that robotics hardware has become dramatically more affordable over the last few years. When I started working in robotics about a decade ago, I worked with a robot called a PR2 which I believe had a cost of about $400,000. When I started my lab at UC Berkeley, I used a robot that was in the ballpark of $30,000. Now each arm on this thing is maybe a tenth of that; we think they can be even less. And that's not due to any one single technology. It actually involves both hardware and software. The kind of low-cost arms that we have here—they wouldn't be useful in an industrial setting because traditional control methods that rely on a great deal of precision wouldn't be able to use them. So there's a wide range of different advances, a cluster, a "constellation" as you said, that have pushed down the price point of these things. And I think that does make it a lot more practical to think about general purpose robotics today. For people that would want to be fairly technical about following major milestones that are happening in this field, where does that information show up? A lot of it shows up in research papers. Research papers, unfortunately, are not a very accessible source of information because it takes a bit of care to sort through everything and figure out what is the signal and what does something really mean. Like, research results are sort of intended for an audience that already understands the starting point from all the past research results. But that's a big one. I think that robotics, and I think technology in general, is one of those things where the public-facing artifacts—like the demos and the videos that somebody might post on social media—are often actually not very good for providing a sense for the true underlying state of things. They're sort of meant more as a demonstration at the edge of capability. Grounding what the demo really means requires digging deeper. So, yeah, probably research papers are the way to go. Sometimes, even worse than that, you have to actually go talk to the individual people and find out what the inside story really is. Maybe that's not a great situation to be in, but that's kind of how science works. As we look forward to the future in your mission, what feels the most uncertain? I do think the timeline is uncertain. If anything, my sense of the timeline has gotten more optimistic since we started, but it's uncertain because of the nature of the technology. This is something where there's a bootstrap challenge: getting to a particular level of usefulness so that robots can be deployed, so they can do useful tasks, so they can start collecting data from open-world settings at scale. Because that's such a sudden kind of event with getting past the activation energy, I think there is a lot of uncertainty about the timing of that. And that's exacerbated by the fact that the timeline looks different depending on what kind of technology is deployed. Like the example I gave before, about whether it should be data collection through teleoperation or data collection with autonomous systems, or something in between—maybe shared autonomy, maybe like this coaching kind of thing. Those all sort of change the picture in terms of how deployments work and how in-the-wild data collection works. So because of that, I do think there's quite a bit of uncertainty about the timeline. You're in such an interesting position because you're at the center of research. Lots of different kinds of people are talking to you, asking you questions. What are questions that you're surprised people don't ask you? What are the things that people don't ask you about that they should? Well, I think the question you asked earlier, actually, about how somebody should prepare. I think there is a variant of that question which would be something like, "Okay, if I want to start using autonomous robots for a thing, what should I start setting up?" Should I set up teleoperation? Should I modify my task in some way so that it's more accessible? Should I design new hardware—like maybe I should design new hardware so that I can plug your software into it? And I think people make a lot of assumptions about that. For example, one assumption is, "Well, machine learning requires data, so let me just figure out something that will collect data." That's not often the best assumption because you need the right kind of data. Like, maybe some data is easy, like it's easy to get videos of people doing something, but that doesn't mean that's the right kind of data. It might be domain-dependent; it might be dependent on your thesis about the technology that will succeed. So I think that people do make a lot of assumptions about that. Not that I necessarily have a better answer for them even if they ask me, but it's something where there is a big space of possibilities. We've talked about these big, uncertain, long-term timelines. What is the very next thing you are trying to solve that's extremely visible? So, without giving too much away, what I can say is that a big focus for us right now is actually better understanding this kind of mid-level reasoning part of the problem. Because we think that we have a pretty good sense for how to acquire the low-level physical behaviors, but getting those low-level physical behaviors to generalize requires bringing to bear a lot of this kind of common sense knowledge. And the representation of that might be really important. LLMs make certain kinds of representations very convenient; they make it very convenient to basically turn text into other text. But that's not necessarily the best representation for what an embodied system needs to do. Like, sometimes it needs to think about things more spatially, sometimes semantically, sometimes other representations. Trying to figure out exactly how to structure that kind of internal thinking process might be a very important question, and the answer to that question might be different in the world of embodied foundation models than it is in the world of LLMs. So that's a concrete thing that we're working on now. Where do you think you fall, like if I could somehow get the 100 most informed and active robotics researchers in the room at once and poll them on how certain they are that things will have unlimited capabilities and how soon that might happen? Where do you fall in that distribution? I'm on the optimistic end when it comes to established robotics researchers, and on the pessimistic end relative to robotics entrepreneurs. Interesting. And I understand the entrepreneur part for sure—they're optimistic by nature. Why are you on the optimistic end of the researcher community? Robotics has a very long history which has precious few successes, let me put it this way, especially when it comes to robotic AI. So I think if we're being honest about it, most robots that are out there doing useful work are still running state-of-the-art technology from the 1980s. And that's because the robotics problem is hard; maybe not our fault, it's just a difficult problem. And because of that, I do think that there is good reason for caution, to say that, well, okay, maybe we've made a lot of headway on this part of the problem, but there's many other problems that still remain, right? Now, part of why I'm optimistic about this is that I kind of have a sense of what has proven tough for me before, and I can see a lot of the puzzle pieces that I'm imagining could be slotted in to address many of those things. But you know, as my co-founder Karol likes to say: "When you've climbed the mountain, only then do you see if there's another mountain after it," right? And in robotics, there's been a lot of experience of lots of mountains. Given that endurance is required, who or what most inspires you? I am actually quite inspired by Boston Dynamics. Again, I think there's a lot of things that we can debate on the technology side, but I think that there is a lot of value in repeatedly showing something that people wouldn't have thought possible. Even if there's all sorts of caveats and assumptions and so on—certainly in robotics, whatever we might say about demos and what not, I think it's very fair to say that people have revised their thoughts about what's possible from seeing some of that stuff. So I think that's definitely one. I think I'm also inspired by organizations that create an atmosphere for experimentation. And I think that there are some research labs that have done a very good job of this. I think actually OpenAI has historically done a great job of this—of creating an atmosphere where individual researchers can experiment with things and be empowered to see those things through. ChatGPT was basically John Schulman's pet experiment for a while. It wasn't a concerted corporate strategy with lots of spreadsheets and pie charts; it was a pet project. So I think there's something pretty inspiring about organizations that empower people to have pet projects turn into world-changing successes. And certainly one of the aspirations that I and my co-founders have here at Physical Intelligence is to provide some of that to the best of our ability. And it's hard to do. It's very hard to have an organization that has that kind of capability. I feel like Google used to have that "one day you can do whatever you want" thing. Is that the spirit of it? I was absolutely shocked when I started working at Google at the level of leverage that I felt I could have. One of the projects that I did with many of my colleagues there in 2015 was colloquially referred to as the "arm farm." We took a couple dozen robots, put them in a lab, and had them collect data. And that was a very bottom-up thing. It was just like: I found out from somebody that they had a warehouse full of robots that nobody was using, and I asked Jeff Dean and Vincent Vanhoucke if we could stick them in a lab. I was just thinking they're not going to take me seriously. I had just started; I was a level four research scientist. And Jeff was like, "Yeah, let's do it. What do you need?" And I just remember feeling like, "Wow, I had never in my life thought that I'd have that kind of leverage." I mean, obviously, I was very young at the time. And I think that's very special. Getting to a place where people can unlock their creativity and have that kind of agency can make for a very remarkable place. My friend Jesse has this great question which is: for companies that you're not involved with, which one do you most hope succeeds and why? People used to say Boom a lot because they want to fly places faster. Increasingly as I've asked this question, people have said "Pi," because the sheer impact that it might have if you're successful is massive on such a global scale. And it's been really fun just to hear about all the ins and outs of how you're thinking about the problem and attacking it. When I do these interviews I have the same traditional last question for everyone: what is the kindest thing that anyone's ever done for you? It's a tough question to answer because I do think that there are many moments in my career where I felt like I got a leg up on something. I think I have the kind of personality where I sometimes don't appreciate it in the moment and only reflect on it afterwards. I don't think I have a single answer to that, but probably the three moments in my career that stand out—actually one of them I had already mentioned to you, which was the arm farm thing. I'm especially grateful to Jeff and to Vincent for kind of being willing to take that bet on me and my colleagues. And there are a couple of other moments. Certainly, I think when I started my postdoc with Pieter Abbeel at Berkeley, I had zero robotics experience. I had done virtual character animation in computer graphics, right? And I felt like that was sort of a bet on my potential more so than my actual accomplishments. And there was another moment even earlier on, that maybe is even more minor, where when I was in college I got an internship at NVIDIA that really got me to experience some cool stuff when I was just a sophomore. The hiring manager for that also took a bet on me. I think that these kinds of things really matter in a person's career. And I think that maybe at the moment I should have been more grateful, but certainly in hindsight it's something that made a big difference, and hopefully I can make that difference in other people's careers as well. Well, I've learned so much from you and your co-founders and so much today. Thank you so much for your time. Thank you.

LIVE: Secretary Pete Hegseth and Gen. Dan Caine update on Iran 'Operation Epic Fury'

The Sun(6.4M) ·66:13 ·2.3K views · 2026-03-31
Operation Epic Fury has entered a decisive phase, with over 11,000 targets struck in 30 days and Iranian military morale collapsing amid desertions and command failures.
TL;DR Operation Epic Fury has entered a decisive phase, with over 11,000 targets struck in 30 days and Iranian military morale collapsing amid desertions and command failures. While the U.S. is actively negotiating with a "third" Iranian regime, military intensity will continue...

TL;DR

Operation Epic Fury has entered a decisive phase, with over 11,000 targets struck in 30 days and Iranian military morale collapsing amid desertions and command failures. While the U.S. is actively negotiating with a "third" Iranian regime, military intensity will continue to increase until Iran’s nuclear ambitions and power-projection capabilities are permanently neutralized.

Operational Status and Tactical Achievements

  • Scale of Strike Campaign: Gen. Dan Caine confirmed that over the past 30 days, the joint force has struck more than 11,000 targets.
      • Dynamic Targeting: In a single 24-hour period, U.S. forces conducted 200 "dynamic strikes"—engaging mobile launchers and troop formations identified in real-time by pilots.
      • B-52 Missions: Increased air superiority has allowed for the commencement of overland B-52 missions, enabling heavy bomber persistence over the battlespace.
  • Naval Dominance: The U.S. Navy has asserted total dominance over the Iranian Navy, sinking more than 150 ships to date.
      • Key Losses: All Jamaran-class frigates have been destroyed.
      • Expanded Tactics: Attack helicopters and close air support assets have now been integrated into naval domain operations to target mine-laying capabilities and depots.
  • Degradation of Defense: Secretary Hegseth reported that Iranian missile and drone fire reached its lowest level in 24 hours this weekend.
      • Recent strikes have targeted underground command bunkers, leaving leadership without power, water, or oxygen.

Strategic Objectives and Regime Negotiations

  • The "Negotiating with Bombs" Strategy: Secretary Hegseth emphasized that while the administration is open to a deal, military pressure is the primary tool to compel compliance.
      • Diplomatic Channels: Active negotiations are ongoing with a "third regime" that the administration views as more reasonable than its predecessors.
      • Non-Negotiables: The primary objectives remain the total destruction of Iran’s missile programs, naval power projection, and the guarantee that Iran never acquires a nuclear weapon.
  • Regime Morale: Intelligence reports indicate widespread desertions and key personnel shortages within the Iranian military as U.S. strikes destroy the defense industrial base.
  • The Strait of Hormuz: President Trump has signaled a demand for the waterway to be "open for business."
      • Hegseth noted that while the U.S. is prepared to secure the strait, international allies (specifically the Royal Navy) are expected to "step up" and share the burden of securing this international waterway.

Readiness and the Defense Industrial Base

  • The "Arsenal of Freedom": Gen. Caine and Secretary Hegseth highlighted the critical role of the U.S. domestic manufacturing base in sustaining "wartime speed."
      • Reconstitution Gap: While the U.S. defense industrial base is growing rapidly, Iran’s ability to rebuild its weapons factories and research labs has been reduced to "negligible" levels.
      • Technological Edge: Hegseth credited soldier ingenuity for new field-developed tactics, such as a young Army officer's new method for neutralizing maneuvering enemy missiles.
  • Troop Sentiment: Following a visit to CENTCOM, Hegseth reported high morale and a desire among the "unleashed" joint force to "finish the job" rather than prepare for standard rotations.
  • Allied Friction: Hegseth expressed frustration with certain NATO allies regarding roadblocks over basing and overflight permissions, suggesting that future alliance commitments may be influenced by support shown during this conflict.

Commemoration of Col. "Bud" Anderson

  • Honoring a Legend: Gen. Caine noted the burial of WWII Triple Ace Col. Clarence "Bud" Anderson at Arlington National Cemetery.
      • Anderson, who passed at age 102, was the highest-scoring P-51 ace. He was honored with a flyby of F-35s and P-51 Mustangs, with Gen. Caine personally piloting an F-16 in his honor.
      • The joint force cited Anderson’s legacy as the standard for the "Greatest Generation" spirit currently being applied in Operation Epic Fury.

***

"I met a junior airman... who, when asked what they needed, she simply looked up at me with a sly smile on her face and said, 'more bombs, sir, and bigger bombs.' We will happily oblige her." — Secretary Pete Hegseth
"Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that this new regime... is in a better place if they make that deal. And so we'll continue... in the meantime we'll negotiate with bombs." — Secretary Pete Hegseth
"The distance from that factory floor and that assembly line to the front line is incredibly short. ... To our adversaries out there, I remind you to beware of the American workforce." — Gen. Dan Caine
"Previous presidents were all talk. [President Trump] is all action. On the battlefield, because of the latitude the President has given us, American firepower is only increasing. Iran's? Decreasing." — Secretary Pete Hegseth
Well, over the weekend I had the opportunity to visit our troops fighting in Operation Epic Fury. We were on the ground in Centcom on Saturday for about half the day. For reasons of operational security, so those troops are not targeted, the places and bases will not be named. Suffice it to say, the trip was an honor. I had a chance to bear witness and I witnessed the best of America. I witnessed warriors, a brotherhood of men and women warriors all. Active duty, Guard and Reserve were united in their love for each other, their shared purpose, and their commitment to the mission. I witnessed sheer competency. I watched a Private First Class confidently calling out an enemy missile trajectory to a room full of officers. Everyone was focused and the room was locked in. Two enemy missiles were successfully shot down. I spoke to Air Force and Navy pilots on the flight line, who every day both deliver bombs deep into Iran but also shoot down drones defending their base. Many had just returned from the skies of Iran and Tehran. I put on a headset and spoke to one crew in the cockpit, locked and loaded, each and every day. I witnessed ingenuity—American ingenuity. I met the young Army officer who figured out how to neutralize maneuvering enemy missiles, saving countless lives. His commander confirmed that the whoops and cheers erupted in the tactical operation center when his new approach was first successful. I met the Air Force intel analyst who refines target packages faster than the enemy can adapt. I actually gave him my card and told him to keep me posted on the ground truth. I did the same with his boss, a colonel with a heart the size of Texas and a beautiful deployment mustache to match. I witnessed lethality. I met a junior airman as the sun was going down and a chill was setting on the tarmac. When asked what she needed, she simply looked up at me with a sly smile on her face and said, "More bombs, sir, and bigger bombs." We will happily oblige her. I met the Army targeting team who found and sunk the pride and joy of the Iranian Navy, their fighting position plastered with images of sunken enemy ships. And I witnessed urgency. Right when we landed, another C-17 landed just minutes after us. Within 30 seconds of the aircraft coming to a full stop, a team on the ground pulled up and the cargo was being uploaded. That is wartime speed. To a man and to a woman—on the ground, in the air, on the flight line, and in the TOC—I heard, "We want everything faster, higher op-tempo, wartime speed." The feeling was the exact opposite of the rotational units year after year in the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan that we're so familiar with. In those wars, it was always about the next rotation, never knowing when the mission would end or exactly what the mission was. Not with Epic Fury. I witnessed urgency to finish the job and urgency to achieve mission success. They are not looking at the next rotation, but only moving as fast as possible to win. I got a chance to troop the line and to witness firsthand what we already know is true. I spoke to all ranks and all services, none of whom knew we were coming. It was not rehearsed or scripted; sometimes we just wandered. What I witnessed was motivation. It was sheer mission focus. It was the American warrior unleashed. It was the kind of warfighting American spirit that comes with a clear mission against a determined enemy. A crew chief we flew with summed it up nicely. He said, "It's been a busy few weeks, sir. Tough stuff. But I'm so honored to be called up. This fight is long overdue. We need to address it for our kids. We cannot pass the buck. Please thank the President from us." I heard that time and time again. I asked each young American, "What do you need?" and nobody said better equipment. Nobody said more comfortable living conditions, and nobody said "send me home." Well, of course eventually we want all those things, and they do too. But what those Americans said to me—young and old, officer and NCO, male and female, black and white—was: "Let's finish the mission. Get us even more bombs, bigger bombs, more targets, let us finish this." In fact, Admiral Cooper noted this morning that the three Air Force captains shot down by friendly fire early in the fight weeks ago never left the theater. All of them dropped bombs over Tehran last night. These men and women live the Iranian threat every day, facing incoming missiles and drones. They know what the world would look like if Iran had the most dangerous weapon in the world, a nuclear weapon. As President Trump has said time and time again for years and in this administration, Iran cannot have a nuclear bomb and they won't. These troops want to finish this fight for their kids and their grandkids. This is about history; this is about legacy. Success matters, and because of this President and these Americans, we are closer than ever before to winning. President Trump is doing what no other president had the guts to do. Previous presidents were all talk, but he's all action. On the battlefield, because of the latitude the President has given us, American firepower is only increasing. Iran's is decreasing. We have more and more options and they have less. Just one month in, we set the terms. The upcoming days will be decisive. Iran knows that and there's almost nothing they can militarily do about it. Yes, they will still shoot some missiles, but we will shoot them down. Of note, the last 24 hours saw the lowest number of enemy missiles and drones fired by Iran. They will go underground, but we will find them. We recently destroyed another one of their command bunkers. Leaders were forced to flee with no water, no power, no oxygen, and no command and control. Their faith in their caves is diminishing. The latest intel is clear out of Centcom. Our strikes are damaging the morale of the Iranian military, leading to widespread desertions and key personnel shortages. This is causing frustrations amongst senior leaders. Just last night, we had 200 dynamic strikes alone. A dynamic strike is a strike where a pilot leaves and, during their flight, they get a new target set based on real-time intel given to them. This could be a new launcher, a new location, or a new troop formation. A dynamic target is one that changes while you're in the air because of improved intelligence. There were 200 dynamic strikes alone in addition to the pre-planned targets. The video the President posted last night of Esfahan showed an ammo depot struck by US bombers. You don't get to see many of those videos because, as a reminder, Iran has still shut off the internet to 99.9% of its population. But if Iran is wise, they will cut a deal. President Trump doesn't bluff and he does not back down. You can ask Khamenei about that. The new Iranian regime should know that by now. This new regime, because regime change has occurred, should be wiser than the last. President Trump will make a deal; he is willing. The terms of the deal are known to them. If Iran is not willing, then the United States War Department will continue with even more intensity. Standing here this morning in this briefing room, in my mind's eye I'm actually looking out at the groups I met this weekend. I see the pilots, the logisticians, the intel analysts, the targeters, the sustainers, the flight crews, the air defenders, and the base security. I see those maintainers that we walked up to at sunset with the chill in the air on the flight line. May God watch over all of them each day and each night. May his almighty and eternal arms of providence stretch over them, protect them, and bring them peace. In the name of Jesus Christ, amen. Mr. Chairman, over to you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Good morning everyone and thank you again for being here. As operations continue, I remain deeply grateful for the service and determination of 2.8 million members of our joint force, each of whom are serving something greater than themselves. Every day, those deployed and those at home who are deploying forward in bomber pulses put themselves in harm's way. We owe them a deep debt of gratitude. I remain especially grateful for our fallen who gave the ultimate sacrifice. We will never forget their valor and their determination to do something greater than themselves. Each day we continue to earn what they've given to us. This morning, I want to share that yesterday the joint force had the honor of participating in the burial of Colonel Clarence Emil "Bud" Anderson, who passed away in May of 2024 at the ripe old age of 102. He was laid to rest in Arlington Cemetery yesterday morning alongside his wife of 70 years, Eleanor, whom he married on February 23rd, 1945. She passed away in 2015 at the age of 92. Bud, a legend to our Air Force and fighter pilots everywhere, was honored with a combination flyby of F-35s and a four-ship of P-51 Mustangs. He was one of the most prolific flying aces of World War II and the highest scoring P-51 ace with 16 and a quarter kills. He served from 1942 to 1972, and his last combat tour was flying F-104 Thunderchiefs over North Vietnam. For anyone that had the chance to meet Colonel Anderson, you knew what an incredibly special man he was. And that's true for each and every one of our World War II vets, who become fewer and fewer with each passing day. They are the greatest generation and give us the gift of an incredible example as we execute Operation Epic Fury today. Colonel Anderson, we mourn for your loss and remember that smile on the right side as you went out to do our nation's business. I'd like to now share an operational update. Our joint force continues to focus on our military objectives as we systematically continue to degrade and destroy Iran's ability to project power and threaten stability beyond its borders. First, the joint force continues to destroy Iran's ballistic missile and UAS capabilities. We remain focused on interdicting and destroying the logistical and supply chains that feed these programs. This remains a truly joint effort prosecuted around the clock from air, land, sea, space, and cyberspace. Long-range bombers from US Strategic Command are coordinating with tactical fighter aircraft launched from bases around the region. Simultaneously, Navy fighters and sailors continue to project power from the sea, while Army and Marine artillery units execute long-range precision fires deep into enemy territory against high-value targets. Meanwhile, on the defense side, our Army and Air defenders and aviators remain vigilant, forming a shield to protect our forces and our partners. Together, we continue to deliver precision strikes against key manufacturing nodes, component storage sites, and research facilities deep within Iranian territory. Over the past 30 days, we've struck more than 11,000 targets. Given the increase in air superiority, we've successfully started to conduct the first overland B-52 missions. This allows us to continue to get on top of the enemy and switch towards more and more dynamic targets servicing mobile targets around the battlespace. We've continued to do the work against Iran's missile, drone, and naval production facilities through the multi-domain pressure that we've talked about. Second, on the Navy front, we continue to assert dominance over the Iranian Navy. We remain focused on targeting their mine-laying capability and their naval assets. We've now started to work attack helicopters and other close air support assets into the naval domain. Centcom continues to identify and work against naval depots and storage areas. We've taken out more than 150 ships, including all Jamaran-class frigates inside their navy. Third, we continue to prosecute our campaign against their defense industrial base at scale. This includes factories, warehouses, nuclear weapons research and development labs, and the associated infrastructure required for Iran to reconstitute its combat capability. I'd like to continue my theme of highlighting the incredible contributions of Americans to our joint force. Today, I want to talk a little bit about a different front line. It is a line that doesn't have bunkers or guard posts but is just as critical to our nation's security: our national assembly lines. Today I want to recognize a group of Americans who live at the beginning of our nation's combat power. The Americans who actually make our weapons, both inside our defense industrial base but even more broadly inside our national industrial base, are essential. In every military option, we could not do our jobs without the men and women who show up every day around the clock to a factory floor, a workshop, or a laboratory. These great Americans are the core of American combat power. They're the machinists running high-tech CNC machines cutting raw blocks of metal into incredibly precise parts. They're the assembly workers painstakingly turning a kit of parts into a complex guidance system, a precision munition, a rocket motor, a jet, or a submarine. They are the quality assurance technicians who ensure that when a warfighter pulls a trigger, the weapon works every time. This can be and is tough and gritty work. It's not a quiet office and a desk with paper, and there's nothing wrong with that, but this is exactly the way this group of Americans likes it. I know this; I've seen it myself and I remain deeply honored by that gift. It's often loud and dangerous work, requiring absolute focus for hours at a time with a deep commitment to get it right every single time. It's hands-on work where one uncaught mistake or deviation can put an American's life at risk. A single misplaced wire, a microscopic flaw in a weld, or an incorrectly calibrated sensor could mean the difference between mission success or mission failure. That difference is measured in the lives of our sons and daughters who we put on the field of battle. We rely on and trust in these great Americans. It's not just their manufacturing skill, it's their innovative minds and their entrepreneurial spirit. From those who build exquisite capabilities down to the mom-and-pop machine shops all over this country, they live at the core of America's combat capability. There are examples of this throughout history and current days, including building things like the F-117 stealth fighter and the B-2 stealth bomber. It's work like that in the shipyards of the East Coast that builds America's nuclear submarines, allowing us to patrol around the world at the time and place of our choosing. These innovators and workers don't get the same glory as a fighter pilot returning to a carrier deck at night, yet they show up every single day. Without them, we could not do the work that we are tasked to do. 24/7 they build the tools that we need to do our business. The skill, commitment, and patriotism poured into every piece of combat capability is seen and felt out at the edge of the force. You can see it, you can feel it, and it's real. And we're deeply grateful. So to the American workforce out there, thank you on behalf of the joint force. We carry the weapons that you build, and the distance from that factory floor to the front line is incredibly short. Thank you, keep it up. And to our adversaries out there, I remind you to beware of the American workforce. We continue to press forward in our military objectives. The Secretary and I remain deeply humbled by the spirit, tenacity, commitment, and grit of our 2.8 million member strong joint force. I want to as always thank the force and their families and as always remember our fallen. We carry them and their names every day. Sir, I'll turn it back over to you. Well, thank you Mr. Chairman. Two things: he didn't note it, but in memory of Colonel Bud Anderson, the Chairman himself flew his F-16 to honor him recently. And thank you for highlighting the defense industrial base, the arsenal of freedom. It's worth noting no one has rebuilt that defense industrial base faster and with more purpose than President Trump. Our defense industrial base is more vibrant today than it's been since World War II and growing rapidly. Contrast that with Iran, whose defense industrial base is nearly completely destroyed. Their ability to rebuild the weapons of war is negligible. I think that contrast is an enduring legacy of this as well. Right here. Thank you for your time. We've had some signaling that the US will retake control of the Strait of Hormuz. At that point, what military posture will be imposed to guarantee safe passage for our allies, deter our enemies, and demonstrate US dominance in the region? And also, can you speak to how much Russia, China, and North Korea are supporting Iran's war machine? Appreciate both questions. On the Strait of Hormuz, there are many more vessels flowing through today than there were, as the President has arranged. The President's been clear to Iran: open it for business or we have options, and we certainly do. When you look at what the Chairman laid out with the Navy, with coastal cruise missiles, and with counter-mine capabilities, we've been focused from the beginning on defeating those capabilities. There's lots we're doing as well, some of which is known, to set the conditions. I think the President was clear this morning that there are countries around the world who ought to be prepared to step up on this critical waterway as well. It's not just the United States Navy. Last time I checked, there was supposed to be a big, bad Royal Navy that could be prepared to do things like that. The world ought to pay attention and be prepared to stand up. President Trump's been willing to do the heavy lifting on behalf of the free world to address this threat. As far as Russia and China, we know exactly what they're doing. Where necessary, we're addressing it, we're mitigating it, and we are confronting it head-on. Do you have any timeline for operability in the strait? Thank you, Mr. Secretary. David Zier, Real America's Voice. I witnessed that enthusiasm on the lines and our submarine builders. But I wanted to ask, what's the status of the delivery of hardened bunkers for our troops and what measures are we taking to protect larger strategic aircraft? Well, it's a great question. I'll say what I witnessed was a completely locked-in discipline of bunker use and improvement. From the beginning, as we stated very clearly, the first thing we did was set up a defense to make sure our defensive capabilities were maxed out. That included fortifications as much as possible, but it also included dispersal. If all of our people are in one place, you could imagine why that's a big problem. Rapidly fielding and then improving those positions is a theater priority, as are the layered air defenses. It's not just Patriots and THAADs; it's fighters, defensive caps, kinetic defeat systems, and electronic warfare. The defense of our troops and our assets is maxed. Regarding some of those other assets, airwings and airframes, there are some things adversaries are doing to provide intel. We're aware of it, and ultimately we move things around. One of the biggest principles you learn in the military is to not set predictable patterns. Commanders are working hard to adjust in real time with those systems and make sure they're not easily targetable. Jillian. Thanks, Mr. Secretary. The US and Iran appear right now publicly to disagree about whether there are these negotiations ongoing. What impact does that have on military strategy? And General, the President wrote yesterday that the US might destroy Iran's electricity generating plants and oil wells. Is there a way to do either of those things without seriously harming civilians? So on the talks, I can tell you having been with Steve and Jared and the Vice President and many others yesterday, they are very real. They are ongoing, they're active, and I think gaining strength. We would much prefer to get a deal. If Iran was willing to relinquish material and ambitions they have and open the strait—great, that's the goal. We don't want to have to do more militarily than we have to. But I didn't mean it flippantly when I said in the meantime we'll negotiate with bombs. Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that this new regime is in a better place if they make that deal. We're working hand-in-hand, but the primary effort is a deal. If not, then we're prepared to continue. Thank you for the question. We're always thinking about those considerations and develop options to be able to mitigate those risks pursuant to the normal practices that we do in the military. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Is opening the strait an essential objective to Operation Epic Fury? And secondly, what is your message to Americans who love the President but are very worried about the notion of boots on the ground? Well, first on the Strait of Hormuz, our core objectives have been clear: the missile program, the defense industrial base, and navy power projection. Of course, wrapping it all is that Iran is never going to have a nuclear weapon. Defeating the navy is a core part of ensuring they can't project that kind of power. But ultimately, the President's truth this morning lays that out very well—this Strait of Hormuz issue is not just a United States problem set. President Trump's led the entire time, but other countries should pay attention when the President speaks. As far as boots on the ground, I don't understand why people wouldn't have faith in his ability to execute. Look at his track record of pursuing peace through strength and America First outcomes. He is simply saying we're not going to foreclose any option. You can't fight and win a war if you tell your adversary what you are willing to do or not do. Our adversary right now thinks there are 15 different ways we could come at them with boots on the ground. And guess what? There are. If we needed to, we could execute those options. But the point is to be unpredictable. If anybody has internalized the lessons of Iraq and Afghanistan, it is President Trump. He's not going to repeat those lessons. Mr. Secretary, the IC assessed for many years that Iran is protecting Al-Qaeda senior leaders. Does the US assess that Saif al-Adel is still in Iran? And Mr. Chairman, Iranian daily missile barrages have dropped dramatically, but they're not at zero. How do we get that closer to zero? I won't speak to a particular target; I'll just say that Al-Qaeda remains our enemy and there are a lot of people on our target list in Iran. If they were to be harboring Al-Qaeda, they would certainly fit that list. The mobile targets that they have, we continue to service. That's one component of continuing to lower that. The second impact is the strikes against their defense industrial base, which will not allow them to reload. They are still shooting, and we do continue to exert significant pressure. Thank you, Secretary Hegseth. You said we're a month into the operation. How long until the objectives are achieved? And for General Caine, there's been media coverage that suggests a ground invasion is imminent. What other purposes might those deployed soldiers and Marines serve? Just like the previous question, it's military 101. Don't tell your enemy when you're willing to stop, especially an enemy that likes to hide in bunkers. That's not a question I'm going to answer definitively. We have our own military objectives that we're moving toward. The Chairman and I look at this every single day. It will be the President's determination alone when those objectives are complete. I don't know if you have anything to add, Mr. Chairman. The range of military options those forces can offer are extensive. Not the least of which is that Iran should note that they're out there and that they are a pressure point. They should carefully consider what's in front of them at the diplomatic level. Do you still believe the Iranian leadership would surrender? And is the United States still committed to NATO's collective defense? And to Chairman Caine, what is your thinking about the legality of striking desalination plants? I would just say that any mission that ends on our terms... call it a surrender, call it a defeat, call it what you want. We remain committed to a conflict that ends on the President's terms. As far as NATO's concerned, that's a decision for the President. A lot has been shown to the world about what our allies would be willing to do for the United States when we undertake an effort of this scope. These are missiles that range allies, yet when we ask for assistance or basing, we get questions or roadblocks. The President is simply pointing out that you don't have much of an alliance if countries are not willing to stand with you. It'll be his decision what that looks like. I'll just say that the Joint Force always strikes lawful targets in accordance with our normal procedures. Chairman Caine, do you agree with no quarter or no mercy for enemy combatants? As Hegseth has said. For me? Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Thank you, sir. No problem. On Sunday, President Trump said that Iran's first regime was all destroyed and the third regime has been much more reasonable in negotiations. Do you agree with that assessment and why? I would defer to the negotiating team there. They're getting a back-and-forth on terms, which is a productive development. We stand right there next to our negotiating team, always willing to put them in an even better position. Thank you very much everyone. Thank you. Secretary, what happened at Esfahan? On the status of... yeah, exactly. Thank you.

Secretary Hegseth and Chairman Caine hold a press briefing on Operation Epic Fury - 03/31/2026

Department of War(310.0K) ·50:46 ·55.2K views · 2026-03-31
> "American firepower is only increasing.
TL;DR: One month into Operation Epic Fury, U.S. forces have struck over 11,000 targets, eliminated Iran’s frigate fleet, and shifted to high-intensity "dynamic targeting" to force a diplomatic resolution on American terms. Targeting Success: The joint force has struck more than...

TL;DR: One month into Operation Epic Fury, U.S. forces have struck over 11,000 targets, eliminated Iran’s frigate fleet, and shifted to high-intensity "dynamic targeting" to force a diplomatic resolution on American terms.

Operational Milestones and Strategic Impact

  • Targeting Success: The joint force has struck more than 11,000 targets in 30 days, focusing on ballistic missile sites, UAS capabilities, and the defense industrial base.
      • Dynamic Strikes: In the last 24 hours, pilots executed 200 "dynamic strikes"—engaging mobile targets identified in real-time—in addition to pre-planned sorties.
      • Aerial Dominance: Increased air superiority has allowed for the commencement of overland B-52 missions, facilitating deeper and more frequent strikes.
  • Naval Degradation: Chairman Caine confirmed the U.S. has asserted dominance over the Iranian Navy, sinking over 150 ships.
      • Key Losses: All Jamaran-class frigates within the Iranian Navy have been destroyed.
      • Strait of Hormuz: Secretary Hegseth emphasized that while the U.S. is setting conditions to keep the waterway open, international allies are expected to increase their participation in securing this critical route.
  • Degraded Enemy Morale: Intelligence reports indicate widespread desertions and critical personnel shortages within the Iranian military as command bunkers are neutralized.

Troop Readiness and Combat Focus

  • High Operational Tempo: Secretary Hegseth reported a "wartime speed" mentality among deployed forces, contrasting the current urgency with the rotational "status quo" of previous conflicts in Iraq and Afghanistan.
  • Mission Focus: Troops expressed a clear desire to "finish the mission" rather than rotate home, specifically requesting more and larger munitions to finalize the degradation of Iranian capabilities.
  • Resilience: Secretary Hegseth noted that three Air Force captains previously downed by friendly fire weeks ago have already returned to theater and participated in strikes over Tehran.

The "Arsenal of Freedom": Industrial Mobilization

  • Domestic Production: Chairman Caine highlighted the critical role of the national industrial base, crediting machinists and assembly workers for the precision and reliability of munitions used in theater.
  • Technological Edge: The briefing referenced the continued deployment and production of advanced platforms, including the B-21 Raider and the F-47.
  • Industrial Contrast: Secretary Hegseth noted a stark divergence in industrial capacity: while the U.S. defense industrial base is at its most vibrant state since World War II, Iran’s manufacturing capability is "nearly completely destroyed" and unable to reconstitute.

Diplomatic and Geopolitical Posture

  • Nuclear Red Line: The administration maintains a firm stance that Iran will never be permitted to possess a nuclear weapon.
  • Negotiation Status: A "third" Iranian regime is reportedly engaging in back-and-forth negotiations. Secretary Hegseth indicated that while President Trump is willing to make a deal, military intensity will increase if terms are not met.
  • Allied Expectations: Hegseth voiced frustration with certain NATO allies regarding access and basing, suggesting that future alliance commitments may depend on their willingness to support the U.S. during this conflict.
  • "Boots on the Ground": The Secretary refused to rule out a ground invasion, stating that maintaining "unpredictability" is essential to military success, though the current focus remains on air and sea dominance.

Notable Quotes

"American firepower is only increasing. Iran's decreasing. We have more and more options and they have less. Just one month in, we set the terms." — Secretary of War Pete Hegseth
"The distance from that factory floor and that assembly line to the front line is incredibly short. To our adversaries out there: beware of the American workforce." — Gen. Dan Caine, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff
"You can’t just have flags; you have to have formations. You can’t just have a few ships; you have to have enough to effect change. The world ought to pay attention and be prepared to stand up." — Secretary of War Pete Hegseth
Thank you, Mr. Secretary, and good morning everyone. Thank you again for being here. As operations continue, I remain deeply grateful for the service and determination of 2.8 million members of our joint force, each of whom are serving something greater than themselves. Every day, those deployed, and in many cases, those at home, who are deployed forward in bomber pulses and others, put themselves in harm's way, and we owe them a deep debt of gratitude. I remain especially grateful for our fallen, who gave the ultimate sacrifice. Well, over the weekend, I had the opportunity to visit our troops fighting in Operation Epic Fury. We were on the ground—excuse me—in CENTCOM on Saturday for about half the day. For reasons of operational security, so those troops are not targeted, the places and bases will not be named. Suffice it to say, the trip was an honor. I had a chance to bear witness, and I witnessed the best of America. I witnessed warriors, a brotherhood of men and women, warriors all: active duty, guard, and reserve. They are united in their love for each other, their shared purpose, and their commitment to the mission. I witnessed sheer competency. I watched a Private First Class confidently calling out an enemy missile trajectory to a room full of officers. Everyone was focused, and the room was locked in. Two enemy missiles were successfully shot down. I spoke to Air Force and Navy pilots on the flight line, who every day both deliver bombs deep into Iran but also shoot down drones defending their base. Many had just returned from the skies of Iran and Tehran. I put on a headset and spoke to one crew in the cockpit. They are locked and loaded, each and every day. I witnessed ingenuity, American ingenuity. I met the young Army officer who figured out how to neutralize maneuvering enemy missiles, saving countless lives. His commander confirmed that the whoops and cheers erupted in the Tactical Operations Center when his new approach was first successful. I met the Air Force Intel analyst who refined target packages faster than the enemy can adapt. I actually gave him my card and told him to keep me posted on the ground truth. I did the same with his boss, a Colonel with a heart the size of Texas and a beautiful deployment mustache to match. I witnessed lethality. I met a junior airman as the sun was going down and a chill was setting on the tarmac. When asked what they needed, she simply looked up at me with a sly smile on her face and said, "More bombs, sir. And bigger bombs." We will happily oblige her. I met the Army targeting team who found and sunk the pride and joy of the Iranian Navy. Their fighting position was plastered with images of sunken enemy ships. And I witnessed urgency. Right when we landed, another C-17 landed just minutes after us. Within 30 seconds of the aircraft coming to a full stop, a team on the ground pulled up and the cargo was being uploaded. That is wartime speed. To a man and to a woman, on the ground, in the air, on the flight line, and in the TOC, I heard: "We want everything faster. Higher op tempo. Wartime speed." The feeling was the exact opposite of the rotational units year after year in the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan that we're so familiar with. In those wars, it was always about the next rotation, never knowing when the mission would end or exactly what the mission was. Year after year. Not with Epic Fury. I witnessed urgency to finish the job, urgency to achieve mission success, not looking at the next rotation. They are only moving as fast as possible to win. I got a chance to troop the line, to witness firsthand what we already know is true. I spoke to all ranks and all services, none of whom knew we were coming. It was not rehearsed or scripted; sometimes we just wandered. What I witnessed was motivation. It was sheer mission focus. It was the American warrior unleashed. It was the kind of warfighting American spirit that comes with a clear mission against a determined enemy. A crew chief we flew with summed it up nicely. He said, "It's been a busy few weeks, sir. Tough stuff. But I'm so honored to be called up. This fight is long overdue. We need to address it for our kids. We cannot pass the buck. Please thank the President from us." I heard that time and time again. I asked each young American "What do you need?" and nobody said better equipment, nobody said more comfortable living conditions, and nobody said "Send me home." Well, of course, eventually we want all those things, and they do too. But what those Americans said to me—young and old, officer and NCO, male and female, black and white—was "Let's finish the mission. Get us even more bombs, bigger bombs, more targets. Let us finish this." In fact, Admiral Cooper noted this morning that the three Air Force captains shot down by Kuwaiti friendly fire early in the fight weeks ago, they never left the theater. All dropped bombs over Tehran last night. These men and women live the Iranian threat every day: incoming missiles and drones. They know what a world looks like, and what the world would look like if Iran had the most dangerous weapons in the world, a nuclear weapon. As President Trump has said time and time again for years and in this administration, Iran cannot have a nuclear bomb and they won't. These troops, they want to finish this fight for their kids and their grandkids. This is about history. This is about legacy. Success matters. And because of this President and these Americans, we are closer than ever before to winning. President Trump is doing what no other president had the guts to do. Previous presidents were all talk. He's all action. On the battlefield, because of the latitude the President has given us, American firepower is only increasing. Iran's is decreasing. We have more and more options and they have less. Just one month in, only one month, we set the terms. The upcoming days will be decisive. Iran knows that, and there's almost nothing they can militarily do about it. Yes, they will still shoot some missiles, but we will shoot them down. Of note, the last 24 hours saw the lowest number of enemy missiles and drones fired by Iran. They will go underground, but we will find them. We recently destroyed another one of their command bunkers. Leaders were forced to flee: no water, no power, no oxygen, no command and control. Their faith in their caves is diminishing. The latest intel is clear out of CENTCOM: our strikes are damaging the morale of the Iranian military. This is leading to widespread desertions, key personnel shortages, and causing frustrations amongst senior leaders. Just last night, we had 200 dynamic strikes alone. A dynamic strike is a strike where a pilot leaves and during their flight they get a new target set based on real-time intel given to them. This could be a new launcher, a new location, or a new troop formation. A dynamic target is one that changes while you're in the air because of improved intelligence. There were 200 dynamic strikes alone, in addition to the pre-planned targets. The video the President posted last night of Esfahan showed an ammo depot struck by U.S. bombers. You see, you don't get to see many of those videos because, as a reminder, Iran has still shut off the internet to 99.9% of its population. But if Iran is wise, they will cut a deal. President Trump does not bluff and he does not back down. You can ask Khamenei about that. The new Iranian regime should know that by now. This new regime—because regime change has occurred—should be wiser than the last. President Trump will make a deal; he is willing. And the terms of the deal are known to them. If Iran is not willing, then the United States War Department will continue with even more intensity. Standing here this morning in this briefing room, in my mind's eye, I'm actually looking out at the groups I met this weekend: the pilots, the logisticians, the intel analysts, the targeters, the sustainers, the flight crews, the air defenders, the base security, and those maintainers we walked up to at sunset. May God watch over all of them, each day and each night. May his almighty and eternal arms of providence stretch over them and protect them and bring them peace. In the name of Jesus Christ and amen. Mr. Chairman, over to you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, and good morning everyone. Thank you again for being here. As operations continue, I remain deeply grateful for the service and determination of 2.8 million members of our joint force, each of whom are serving something greater than themselves. Every day, those deployed, and in many cases, those at home, who are deployed forward in bomber pulses and others, put themselves in harm's way, and we owe them a deep debt of gratitude. I remain especially grateful for our fallen, who gave the ultimate sacrifice. We'll never forget their valor and their determination to do something greater than themselves. And each day, we continue to earn what they've given to us. This morning I want to share that yesterday the joint force had the honor of participating in the burial of Colonel Clarence "Bud" Anderson. He passed away in May of 2024 at the ripe old age of 102. He was laid to rest in Arlington Cemetery yesterday morning alongside his wife of 70 years, Eleanor, whom he married on February 23rd, 1945. She passed away in 2015 at the age of 92. Bud, a legend to our Air Force and fighter pilots everywhere, was honored yesterday with a combination flyby of F-35s and a four-ship of P-51 Mustangs. He was one of the most prolific flying aces of World War II and the highest-scoring P-51 ace with 16 and a quarter kills. He served from 1942 to 1972, and his last combat tour was flying F-105 Thunderchiefs over North Vietnam. For anyone that had the chance to meet Colonel Anderson, you knew what an incredibly special man he was. And that's true for each and every one of our World War II vets who become fewer and fewer with each passing day. They are the Greatest Generation and give us the gift of an incredible example as we execute Operation Epic Fury today. Colonel Anderson, we mourn for your loss and remember that smile on the right side as you went out to do our nation's business. I'd like now to share an operational update. Our joint force continues to focus on our military objectives. We systematically continue to degrade and destroy Iran's ability to project power and threaten stability beyond its borders. First, the joint force continues to destroy Iran's ballistic missile and UAS capabilities. We remain focused on interdicting and destroying the logistical and supply chains that feed these programs. And this remains a truly joint effort prosecuted around the clock from air, land, sea, space, and cyberspace. Long-range bombers from U.S. Strategic Command are coordinating with tactical fighter aircraft from our joint force, launched from bases around the region and the continental United States. Simultaneously, Navy fighters from the sea and sailors continue to project power, while Army and Marine artillery units continue to execute long-range precision fires deep into enemy territory against high-value targets. Meanwhile, on the defense side, our Army and Air defenders and aviators, as the Secretary talked about, remain vigilant, forming a shield to protect our forces and our partners, intercepting missiles and drones. Together we continue to deliver precision strikes against key manufacturing nodes, component storage sites, and research facilities deep within Iranian territory. Over the past 30 days, we've struck more than 11,000 targets. Given the increase in air superiority, we've successfully started to conduct the first overland B-52 missions. These allow us to continue to get on top of the enemy and switch towards more and more dynamic targets, servicing mobile targets around the battlespace. We've continued to do the work against Iran's missile, drone, and naval production facilities, and we continue the multi-domain pressure that we've talked about. Second, on the Navy front, we continue to assert dominance over the Iranian Navy. We remain focused on targeting their mine-laying capability and their naval assets. We've now started to work attack helicopters and other close air support assets into the naval domain. CENTCOM continues to work against naval depots and storage areas. We've taken out, again, more than 150 ships, including all Jamaran-class frigates inside their navy. Third, we continue to prosecute our campaign against their defense industrial base at scale. This includes factories, warehouses, and nuclear weapons research and development labs. I'd like to continue my theme of highlighting the incredible contributions of Americans to our joint force. Today I want to talk a little bit about a different front line, a line that doesn't have bunkers or guard posts, but is just as critical to our nation's security: our national assembly lines. Today I want to recognize a group of Americans who live at the beginning of our nation's combat power: the Americans who actually make our weapons, both inside our defense industrial base but even more broadly inside our national industrial base. In every military option, we could not and cannot do our jobs without the men and women across our country who show up every day around the clock to a factory floor, a workshop, or a laboratory. They build the weapons and capabilities we need. These great Americans are the core of American combat power. They're the machinists running high-tech CNC machines, cutting raw blocks of metal into incredibly precise parts. They're the assembly workers painstakingly turning a kit of parts into a complex guidance system. Or they're the quality assurance technicians who ensure that when a warfighter pulls a trigger, the weapon works every time. This can be and is tough and gritty work. It's not a quiet office and a desk with paper—and there's nothing wrong with that—but this is exactly the way this group likes it. I know this, I've seen it myself, and I remain deeply honored by that gift. It's often loud and dangerous work, demanding absolute focus for hours at a time with deep commitment to get it right every single time. It's hands-on work where one uncaught mistake can put an American's life at risk. A single misplaced wire, a microscopic flaw in a weld, or an incorrectly calibrated sensor could mean the difference between mission success or mission failure. The difference is measured in the lives of our sons and daughters who we put on the field of battle. We rely on and trust in these great Americans. It's not just their manufacturing skill; it's their innovative minds and their entrepreneurial spirit. From those who build exquisite capabilities down to the mom-and-pop machine shops all over this great country, they live at the core of America's combat capability. They are constantly adapting, constantly improving, and constantly learning. There are examples of this throughout history and current days, to include building things like the F-117 Stealth Fighter and the B-2 Stealth Bomber, making our combat capability undetectable over the enemy. It's work like in the shipyards of the East Coast and Northeast that build America's nuclear submarines that allow us to patrol around the world. These innovators, these workers, these incredible Americans, don't get the same glory as a fighter pilot returning to a carrier deck. And yet they show up every single day. Without them, we could not do the work that we are tasked to do. 24/7 they build the tools that we need to do our business. The skill, the commitment, the patriotism, and the dedication is seen and felt out at the edge of the force. You can see it, you can feel it, and it's real. And we're deeply grateful. So to the American workforce out there, both inside our defense industrial base and in our national industrial base: Thank you, on behalf of the joint force. We carry the weapons that you build, we rely on the systems that you create, and the distance from that factory floor and that assembly line to the front line is incredibly short. Thank you, keep it up. And to our adversaries out there, I remind you to beware of the American workforce. We continue to press forward in our military objectives. The Secretary and I remain deeply humbled by the spirit, tenacity, commitment, and grit of our 2.8 million member strong joint force. I want to, as always, thank the force and their families and, as always, remember our fallen. Sir, I'll turn it back over to you. Absolutely. Well, thank you, Mr. Chairman. Two things. He didn't note it, but in memory of Colonel Bud Anderson, the Chairman himself flew his F-16 to honor him recently. And thank you for highlighting the defense industrial base, the Arsenal of Freedom.
