I witnessed sheer competence. I watched a private first class confidently calling out an enemy missile trajectory to a room full of officers. Everyone focused, the room was locked in. Two enemy missiles were successfully shot down. I spoke to Air Force and Navy pilots on the flight line who every day both deliver bombs deep into Iran but also shoot down drones defending their base. Many had just returned from the skies of Iran and Tehran. I put on a headset and spoke to one crew in the cockpit—locked and loaded each and every day. I witnessed ingenuity, American ingenuity. I met the young Army officer who figured out how to neutralize maneuvering enemy missiles, saving countless lives. His commander confirmed that the whoops and cheers erupted in the tactical operation center when his new approach was first successful. I met the Air Force intel analyst who refines target packages faster than the enemy can adapt. I actually gave him my card and told him to keep me posted on the ground truth. I did the same with his boss, a colonel with a heart the size of Texas and a beautiful deployment mustache to match. I witnessed lethality. I met a junior airman as the sun was going down and a chill was setting on the tarmac who, when asked what they needed, she simply looked up at me with a sly smile on her face and said, "More bombs, sir, and bigger bombs." We will happily oblige her. I met the Army targeting team who found and sunk the pride and joy of the Iranian Navy. Their fighting position was plastered with images of sunken enemy ships. And I witnessed urgency. Right when we landed, another C-17 landed just minutes after us, and within 30 seconds of the aircraft coming to a full stop, a team on the ground pulled up and the cargo was being uploaded. This is wartime speed. To a man and to a woman, on the ground, in the air, on the flight line, and in the TOC, I heard, "We want everything faster, higher op-tempo, wartime speed." The feeling was the exact opposite of the rotational units year after year in the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan that we're so familiar with. In those wars, it was always about the next rotation, never knowing when the mission would end or exactly what the mission was, year after year. Not with Epic Fury. I witnessed urgency to finish the job, urgency to achieve mission success, not looking at the next rotation, only moving as fast as possible to win. I got a chance to troop the line, to witness firsthand what we already know is true. I spoke to all ranks and all services, none of whom knew we were coming. It was not rehearsed or scripted; sometimes we just wandered. What I witnessed was motivation. It was sheer mission focus. It was the American warrior unleashed. It was the kind of war-fighting American spirit that comes with a clear mission against a determined enemy. A crew chief we flew with summed it up nicely. He said, "It's been a busy few weeks, sir, tough stuff, but I'm so honored to be called up. This fight is long overdue. We need to address it for our kids. We cannot pass the buck. Please thank the president from us." I heard that time and time again. I asked each young American, "What do you need?" and nobody said better equipment, nobody said more comfortable living conditions, nobody said send me home. Well, of course, eventually we want all those things, they do too. But what those Americans said to me—young and old, officer and NCO, male and female, black and white—was, "Let's finish the mission. Get us even more bombs, bigger bombs, more targets, let us finish this." In fact, Admiral Cooper noted this morning that the three Air Force captains shot down by Kuwaiti friendly fire early in the fight weeks ago, they never left the theater. All dropped bombs over Tehran last night. These men and women live the Iranian threat every day—incoming missiles and drones—and know what a world looks like if Iran had the most dangerous weapons in the world, a nuclear weapon. As President Trump has said time and time again for years and in this administration, Iran cannot have a nuclear bomb and they won't. These troops, they want to finish this fight for their kids and their grandkids. This is about history, this is about legacy. Success matters, and because of this president and these Americans, we are closer than ever before to winning. President Trump is doing what no other president had the guts to do. Previous presidents were all talk; he's all action. On the battlefield, because of the latitude the president has given us, American firepower is only increasing. Iran's? Decreasing. We have more and more options and they have less. Just one month in, only one month, we set the terms. The upcoming days will be decisive. Iran knows that and there's almost nothing they can militarily do about it. Yes, they will still shoot some missiles but we will shoot them down. Of note, the last 24 hours saw the lowest number of enemy missiles and drones fired by Iran. They will go underground but we will find them. We recently destroyed another one of their command bunkers; leaders were forced to flee. No water, no power, no oxygen, no command and control. Their faith in their caves is diminishing. The latest intel is clear out of CENTCOM: our strikes are damaging the morale of the Iranian military leading to widespread desertions, key personnel shortages, and causing frustrations amongst senior leaders. Just last night we had 200 dynamic strikes alone. A dynamic strike is a strike where a pilot leaves and during their flight they get a new target set based on real-time intel given to them—a new launcher, a new location, a new troop formation. A dynamic target is one that changes while you're in the air because of improved intelligence. There were 200 dynamic strikes alone in addition to the pre-planned targets. The video the President posted last night of Esfahan, an ammo depot struck by U.S. bombers—you see, you don't get to see many of those videos because, as a reminder, Iran has still shut off the internet to 99.9 percent of its population. But if Iran is wise, they will cut a deal. President Trump doesn't bluff and he does not back down. You can ask Khamenei about that. The new Iranian regime should know that by now. This new regime, because regime change has occurred, should be wiser than the last. President Trump will make a deal; he is willing. And the terms of the deal are known to them. If Iran is not willing, then the United States War Department will continue with even more intensity. Standing here this morning in this briefing room, in my mind's eye I'm actually looking out at the groups I met this weekend—the pilots, the logisticians, the intel analysts, the targeters, the sustainers, the flight crews, the air defenders, the base security, those maintainers that we walked up to at sunset with the chill in the air on the flight line. May God watch over all of them each day and each night. May his almighty and eternal arms of providence stretch over them and protect them and bring them peace. In the name of Jesus Christ, amen. Mr. Chairman, over to you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, and good morning everyone, and thank you again for being here. As operations continue, I remain deeply grateful for the service and determination of 2.8 million members of our joint force, each of whom are serving something greater than themselves. Every day those deployed, and in many cases those at home who are deployed forward in bomber pulses and others, put themselves in harm's way and we owe them a deep debt of gratitude. I remain especially grateful for our fallen who gave the ultimate sacrifice. We'll never forget their valor and their determination to do something greater than themselves. And each day we continue to earn what they've given to us. This morning I want to share that yesterday the joint force had the honor of participating in the burial of Colonel Clarence Emil "Bud" Anderson, who passed away in May of 2024 at the ripe old age of 102. He was laid to rest in Arlington Cemetery yesterday morning alongside his wife of 70 years, Eleanor, who he married on February 23rd, 1945. She passed away in 2015 at the age of 92. Bud yesterday, a legend to our Air Force and fighter pilots everywhere, was honored with a combination flyby of F-35s and a four-ship of P-51 Mustangs. He was one of the most prolific flying aces of World War II and the highest scoring P-51 ace with 16 and a quarter kills in his squadron and in his group. He served from 1942 to 1972 and his last combat tour was flying F-104 Thunderchiefs over North Vietnam. For anyone that had the chance to meet Colonel Anderson, you knew what an incredibly special man he was, and that's true for each and every one of our World War II vets who become fewer and fewer with each passing day. They are the greatest generation and give us the gift of an incredible example as we execute Operation Epic Fury today. And Colonel Anderson, we mourn for your loss and remember that smile on the right side as you went out to do our nation's business. I'd like to now share an operational update. Our joint force continues to focus on our military objectives as we systematically continue to degrade and destroy Iran's ability to project power and threaten stability beyond its borders. First, the joint force continues to destroy Iran's ballistic missile and UAS capabilities. We remain focused on interdicting and destroying the logistical and supply chains that feed these programs. And this remains a truly joint effort, prosecuted around the clock from air, land, sea, space, and cyberspace. Long-range bombers from U.S. Strategic Command coordinating with tactical fighter aircraft from our joint force launched from bases around the region and the continental United States, while simultaneously Navy fighters from the sea and sailors continue to project power from the sea. Meanwhile, Army and Marine artillery units continue to execute long-range precision fires deep into enemy territory against high-value targets. Meanwhile, on the defense side, our Army and Air defenders and aviators, as the secretary talked about, remain vigilant, forming a shield to protect our forces and our partners, intercepting missiles and drones. Together we continue to deliver precision strikes against key manufacturing nodes, component storage sites, and research facilities deep within Iranian territory. Over the past 30 days we've struck more than 11,000 targets. Given the increase in air superiority, we've successfully started to conduct the first overland B-52 missions which allow us, as we've said before, to continue to get on top of the enemy. As the secretary talked about, we are switching towards more and more dynamic targets, servicing mobile targets around the battle space. We've continued to do the work against Iran's missile, drone, and naval production facilities and we continue the multi-domain pressure that we've talked about. Second, on the Navy front, we continue to assert dominance over the Iranian Navy. We remain focused on targeting their mine-laying capability, their naval assets, and we've now, as I mentioned briefly last time, started to work attack helicopters and other close air support assets into the naval domain. CENTCOM continues to identify and work against naval depots and storage areas, and we've taken out, again, more than 150 ships including all Jamaran class frigates inside their Navy. Third, we continue to prosecute our campaign against their defense industrial base at scale. This includes factories, warehouses, nuclear weapons research and development labs, and the associated infrastructure required for Iran to reconstitute its combat capability. I'd like to continue my theme of highlighting the incredible contributions of Americans to our joint force. Today I want to talk a little bit about a different front line, a line that doesn't have bunkers or guard posts but is just as critical to our nation's security: our national assembly lines. Today I want to recognize a group of Americans who live at the beginning of our nation's combat power, the Americans who actually make our weapons both inside our defense industrial base but even more broadly inside our national industrial base. In every military option, we could not and cannot do our jobs without the men and women across our country who show up every day around the clock to a factory floor, a workshop, or a laboratory. They build the weapons and capabilities we need to project American combat power at the time and place of our choosing. These great Americans, and I've had a chance to spend some time with them when I was in the private sector, are the core of American combat power. They're the machinists running high-tech CNC machines, cutting raw blocks of metal into incredibly precise parts. They're the assembly workers painstakingly taking a kit of parts and turning that into a complex guidance system or precision munition or a rocket motor or building a jet or submarine. Or they're quality assurance technicians who ensure that when a warfighter pulls a trigger, the weapon works every time. This can be and is tough and gritty work. It's not a quiet office and a desk with paper, and there's nothing wrong with that, but this is exactly the way this group of Americans likes it. I know this, I've seen it myself, and I remain deeply honored by that gift. It's often loud and dangerous work, demanding, and requires absolute focus for hours at a time with a deep commitment to get it right every single time. It's hands-on work where one uncaught mistake or deviation can put an American's life at risk. A single misplaced wire, a microscopic flaw in a weld, or an incorrectly calibrated sensor could mean the difference between mission success or mission failure. The difference is measured in the lives of our sons and daughters who we put around the field and on the field of battle. We rely on and trust in these great Americans, and it's not just their manufacturing skill; it's their innovative minds and their entrepreneurial spirit. From those who build exquisite capabilities down to the mom and pop machine shops all over this great country, they live at the beginning of and the core of America's combat capability, constantly adapting, constantly improving, constantly learning. And there are examples of this throughout history and current days, to include building things like the F-117 stealth fighter, the B-2 stealth bomber making our combat capability undetectable over the enemy, to today's B-21 and F-47. It's work like in the shipyards of the East Coast and northeast side of the United States that go out and build America's nuclear submarines that allow us to patrol around the world at the time and place of our choosing. These innovators, these workers, these incredible Americans don't get the same glory as a fighter pilot returning to a carrier deck at night or an artilleryman sending rounds downrange, and yet they show up every single day and without them we could not do the work that we are tasked to do. 24/7 they build the tools that we need to do our business. The skill, the commitment, the patriotism, and the dedication poured into every piece of combat capability and hardware is seen and felt out at the edge of the force, as the secretary talked about those young bomb builders out in the desert that he had the chance to see. You can see it, you can feel it, and it's real and we're deeply grateful. So to the American workforce out there, both inside our defense industrial base and in our national industrial base, thank you on behalf of the joint force. We carry the weapons that you build, we rely on the systems that you create, and the distance from that factory floor and that assembly line to the front line is incredibly short. Thank you, keep it up. And to our adversaries out there, I remind you to beware of the American workforce. We continue to press forward in our military objectives. The secretary and I remain deeply humbled by the spirit, tenacity, commitment and grit of our 2.8 million member strong joint force. I want to, as always, thank the force and their families and, as always, remember our fallen. We carry with them and their names every day. Sir, I'll turn it back over to you. Well, thank you Mr. Chairman. Two things: he didn't note it, but in memory of Colonel Bud Anderson, the chairman himself flew his F-16 to honor him recently. And thank you for highlighting the defense industrial base, the Arsenal of Freedom. It's worth noting no one has rebuilt that defense industrial base faster and with more purpose than President Trump in his first term, and now we're rebuilding the Arsenal of Freedom. Our defense industrial base is more vibrant today than it's been since World War II and growing rapidly. Contrast that with Iran, whose defense industrial base is nearly completely destroyed. Their ability to reconstitute the weapons of war they're using now—that they're hiding in underground facilities and attriting over time—their ability to rebuild that is negligible. And I think that contrast is an enduring legacy of this as well. Right here. Thank you for your time. We've had some signaling that the US will retake control of the Strait of Hormuz. At that point, what military posture will be imposed to guarantee safe passage for our allies, deter our enemies, and demonstrate US dominance in the region? And also can you speak to how much America's adversaries—Russia, China, North Korea—how much they're supporting Iran's war machine at this point with arms and intelligence and what we are militarily doing to punish the enemy coalition? Appreciate both questions. On the Strait of Hormuz, there are many more vessels flowing through today than there were, as the president has arranged. The president has been clear to Iran: open it for business or we have options, and we certainly do. And when you look at what the chairman laid out with the Navy, with the Navy industrial base, with coastal cruise missiles, with UAVs, and with counter-mine capabilities, we've been focused from the beginning on attriting and defeating those capabilities and limiting their options. There's lots we're doing as well, some of which is known, some of which is not known, to set the conditions. And I think the president was clear this morning in his Truth that there are countries around the world who ought to be prepared to step up on this critical waterway as well. It's not just the United States Navy. Last time I checked there was supposed to be a big bad Royal Navy that could be prepared to do things like that as well. So he's pointing out this is an international waterway that we use less than most, in fact dramatically less than most. And so the world ought to pay attention and be prepared to stand up. President Trump's been willing to do the heavy lifting on behalf of the free world to address this threat of Iran. It's not just our problem set going forward, even though we have done the lion's share of preparation to ensure that that strait will be open, which is an outcome the president's very clear on. As far as Russia and China, we know exactly what they're doing, what they are or are not doing. We don't have to air publicly what all of that is, but where necessary we're addressing it, we're mitigating it, and we're confronting it head on. I don't know if you have anything you want to add. No, sir, I think you covered it. Right here in the front. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. David Zere, Real America's Voice. And I witness that enthusiasm on the lines in our submarine builders and iron works. It's unbelievable. But I just wanted to ask you, what's the status of the delivery of any hardened bunkers for additional protection for our troops? And without getting too specific, what kind of measures are we taking to protect some of the larger and more strategic aircraft like the Sentry and others? Well, great question. I'll say what I witnessed where I went was a completely locked-in discipline of bunker use and bunker improvement. So from the beginning, as we stated very clearly, the first thing we did was set up a defense and make sure our defensive capabilities were maxed out before any of this even started. That included fortifications as much as possible but it also included dispersal. If all of our people are in one place, you can imagine why that's a big problem, so dispersing is part of that defeat. Alongside that dispersal is more and more bunkers, and I can tell you talking to base commanders, talking to our allies in Israel, talking to others, rapidly fielding that and then improving those positions is a theater priority. No doubt as are the air defenses and the layered air defenses. It's not just Patriots and THAADs, it's fighters in defensive caps, it's other kinetic defeat systems, it's electronic warfare. So the defense of our troops and our assets is maxed. I will say on some of those other assets you talked about—airwings, airframes—there's some things adversaries are doing to provide info and intel that they shouldn't. We're aware of it and ultimately we move things around and don't—one of the biggest principles you learn in the military is to not set patterns, predictable patterns. And so commanders are working hard to adjust in real-time with those systems and make sure they're in the right places and not easily targetable. Gillian. Thanks, Mr. Secretary. The U.S. and Iran appear right now publicly to disagree about whether there are these negotiations ongoing. What impact does that have on military strategy? Does it have any bearing at all? And General, the President wrote yesterday that the U.S. might destroy Iran's electricity generating plants and oil wells. Those are his words before ending this operation. Is there a way to do either of those things without seriously jeopardizing or seriously harming civilians? So on the talks, I can tell you having been with Steve and Jared and the vice president and Marco and many others yesterday, they are very real, they are ongoing, they're active, and I think gaining strength. And we appreciate that. As I said in my remarks, we would much prefer to get a deal. If Iran was willing to relinquish material they have and ambitions they have, open the strait—great, that's the goal. We don't want to have to do more militarily than we have to. But I didn't mean it flippantly when I said in the meantime we'll negotiate with bombs. Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that this new regime, this regime in charge, is in a better place if they make that deal. And so we'll continue. We're working hand in hand but the primary effort is a deal. We want that deal to be accomplished if at all possible. If not, then we're prepared to continue. Yeah, we're always thinking about those considerations and develop options to be able to mitigate those risks pursuant to the normal practices that we do in the military. So. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. I know you slightly addressed this already but just on the Strait of Hormuz, is opening the strait an essential objective to Operation Epic Fury or is that the job of those other countries? And then secondly, without asking you to comment on things that you can't talk about, what is your message to Americans who love the president and strongly believe in him but are very worried about this notion of boots on the ground? Well, first on the Strait of Hormuz, our core objectives from this podium from day one from me, from the chairman, from the president, from the vice president, from Secretary Rubio and others have been clear: missile production and missile programs—so their entire missile program—defense industrial base and production ability to build, and Navy and power projection. So those have been—and then of course wrapping it all is Iran's never going to have a nuclear weapon. So those have been very clear. Defeating the Navy is a core part of ensuring they can't project that kind of power. But ultimately I think the president's Truth this morning lays that out very well, that this Strait of Hormuz issue, which we've set the conditions for success and we will make sure Iran knows that very clearly, is not just a United States of America problem set, even though we have done the lion's share of preparation to ensure that that strait will be open, which is an outcome the president's very clear on. And ultimately I think other countries should pay attention when the president speaks. He's proven that when he speaks he means something, and he's pointing out, you know, might want to start learning how to fight for yourself. It's something some of us have been saying for quite some time. You can't just have flags; you have to have formations. You can't just have a few ships; you have enough to effect change. Those things matter in a dangerous world with ascendant adversaries. That's why the chairman's talking about our industrial base, that's why we're launching the Arsenal of Freedom tour to revive our defense industrial base. President Trump is doing it in a way that nobody else has. As far as President Trump and boots on the ground, I don't understand why the base, which they have already, wouldn't have faith in his ability to execute on this. Look at his track record of pursuing peace through strength, America First outcomes. And what he's simply saying is exactly true and I've said from this podium too: we're not going to foreclose any option. You can't fight and win a war if you tell your adversary what you are willing to do or what you are not willing to do, to include boots on the ground. Our adversary right now thinks there are 15 different ways we could come at them with boots on the ground. And guess what? There are. And so if we needed to, we could execute those options on behalf of the President of the United States and this department. Or maybe we don't have to use them at all. Maybe negotiations work or maybe there's a different approach. But the point is to be unpredictable in that, certainly not let anybody know what you're willing to do or not do. But if anybody has internalized the lessons of Iraq and Afghanistan, as the first one, President Trump, to call them out for what they are, he's not going to repeat those lessons. And I think I've been very clear about that from the podium. One last. Thank you Secretary Hegseth. Question for you and then a question for General Caine. You said we're a month into Operation Epic Fury. How long until the objectives are achieved and is there a scenario where a deal is struck before the objectives have been achieved? And then for General Caine, there's been lots of media coverage that suggested a ground invasion is imminent. What other purposes might the soldiers and the Marines who have been deployed over to the Middle East serve in this conflict? Well, just like the previous question, it's sort of military 101. Don't tell your enemy what you're willing to do or not do and don't tell your enemy when you're willing to stop, especially an enemy that likes to hide in bunkers and try to hoard their missiles and hope you'll wait out. So that's not a question I'm going to answer or the president has said definitively. We have our own goals and guidance and things we're—military objectives that we're moving toward and things that we look at. And as he has articulated, you know, he's said four to six weeks, six to eight weeks, three weeks—it could be any particular number. But we would never reveal precisely what it is because our goal is to finish those objectives and we're well on our way, and the chairman and I look at this every single day. It will be the president's determination, and the president's determination alone, when those objectives are complete and when it serves the interest of the American people to cut that deal to make sure that Iran doesn't have a nuclear capability and ultimately that our objectives, our interests are advanced. I don't know if you have anything to add, Mr. Chairman. No, just to answer your question, Reagan, you know the range of military options that those forces can offer are extensive, not just limited to what you mentioned in terms of forces on the ground. And I wouldn't want to take away the president's decision space but there are a multitude of things. Not the least of which is Iran should note that they're out there and that they are a pressure point, and so they should carefully consider—I think at the diplomatic level, not my job as the chairman—but at the diplomatic level to consider what's in front of them. Jerry. Okay, Mr. Secretary, the US government and IC assessed for many years that Iran is protecting Al-Qaeda senior leaders including the current emir Saif al-Adel. Does the US assess that he is still in Iran, has he fled to Afghanistan, is he on a target list? And Mr. Chairman, based on CENTCOM figures and open source, Iranian daily missile barrages and drone launches have indeed dropped dramatically since the start of the war, but they're not at zero. And they seem to have maintained some level of low but stable numbers, perhaps they've dropped a little bit more in recent days. How do we get that closer to zero and what impact is Iran's degraded but still very real capability impacting current and future operations? So I won't speak to a particular target; I'll just say that Al-Qaeda remains our enemy obviously and there are a lot of people on our target list in Iran and if they were to be harboring Al-Qaeda they would certainly fit that list. Just quickly, the mobile targets that they have that we continue to service, as the Secretary talked about the 200 hits that were done last night, that's one component of continuing to lower that. The second and more long-term impact that we're having is the strikes against their defense industrial base at scale which will not allow them to reload after that. So we do continue to see a trend down. They are still shooting and we do continue to exert significant pressure against both sides of that system. Secretary, thank you for your time. Do you still believe—you'd said previously that the Iranian leadership would surrender. Do you still believe that, and is the United States still committed to NATO's collective defense? And to Chairman Caine, could you give us a sense of your thinking about the legality of striking desalination plants if the president were to order that? I will just say that any mission that ends on our terms—call it a surrender, call it a defeat, call it what you want—we remain committed to a conflict that ends on our terms and the president's terms. There's no doubt about that. And as far as NATO's concerned, that's a decision that will be left to the president but I'll just say a lot has been laid bare. A lot has been shown to the world about what our allies would be willing to do for the United States of America when we undertake an effort of this scope on behalf of the free world. These are missiles that don't even range the United States of America; they range allies and others. And yet when we ask for additional assistance or simple access, basing, and overflight, we get questions or roadblocks or hesitations. And the president's pointing out you don't have much of an alliance if you have countries that are not willing to stand with you when you need them. He's simply pointing that out and ultimately it will be his decision of what that looks like after this is complete. I'll just—won't talk about any particular target; this really builds on Gillian's question too that I don't think I completely answered. But you know the joint force is the most professional force in the world and we have numerous processes and systems to carefully consider the whole range of considerations from civilian risk to legal considerations with any target. And as targets come before us, we run them through the same process that we always do and always strike lawful targets in accordance with the normal procedures that we use. Chairman Caine, do you believe in no quarter, no mercy for enemy combatants as Hegseth has said? For me? Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Thank you, sir. Right there. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, thank you. I appreciate on Sunday, President Trump said that Iran's first regime was all destroyed and the second one is mostly dead, that the third regime that we're on right now has been much more reasonable in negotiations. Do you agree with that assessment and why? I would defer to the negotiating team there. They're the ones talking to them but they're getting a back and forth on terms which is a productive development. We stand right there next to our negotiating team, always willing and prepared to put them in an even better position. Thank you very much. I appreciate it. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Admiral. Thank you.

Fmr Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger on chips, China, and lessons from Andy Grove

Summation (formerly World of DaaS)(93.4K) ·58:11 ·87 views · 2026-03-31
Pat Gelsinger (Former CEO, Intel) Auren Hoffman (Host, Summation)
The United States must commit to a decade-long industrial policy and technologist-led leadership to reclaim semiconductor dominance, mitigate extreme supply chain dependencies on Taiwan, and ensure AI is developed with human-centric values.
TL;DR The United States must commit to a decade-long industrial policy and technologist-led leadership to reclaim semiconductor dominance, mitigate extreme supply chain dependencies on Taiwan, and ensure AI is developed with human-centric values. The 30-Year Decline: The U.S....

TL;DR

The United States must commit to a decade-long industrial policy and technologist-led leadership to reclaim semiconductor dominance, mitigate extreme supply chain dependencies on Taiwan, and ensure AI is developed with human-centric values.

The State of U.S. Semiconductor Manufacturing

  • The 30-Year Decline: The U.S. share of global chip manufacturing dropped from 40% in 1990 to roughly 10% today.
      • Industrial Policy Gap: While Taiwan, China, and Korea implemented long-term industrial policies, the U.S. prioritized software margins and "Wall Street near-termism," avoiding capital-intensive fab projects.
      • The Return Timeline: Gelsinger estimates it will take at least a decade of consistent policy and capital to move the needle significantly.
  • The CHIPS Act & Beyond: While the CHIPS Act has spurred fab starts (Intel, TSMC, Samsung, Micron), Gelsinger argues it is only the first step.
      • Economic Incentives: To move fabless giants like Apple and Nvidia back to domestic wafers, the U.S. needs consistent tariff policies that create a clear economic advantage for domestic production.
  • U.S. Sovereign Wealth Fund: Gelsinger advocates for a national investment fund to counter China’s aggressive capitalization.
      • Strategic Focus: The fund should prioritize three foundational pillars: Semiconductors, Rare Earth Minerals, and Energy.
      • Investment Logic: Unlike debt-financing, a wealth fund targets long-term national security priorities while seeking a high internal rate of return (IRR).

Geopolitical Risks and the "Silicon Shield"

  • The Taiwan Paradox: The U.S. now imports more from Taiwan than from China—a "stunning" trade shift driven by AI chips and wafers.
      • Energy Vulnerability: Taiwan operates on less than three weeks of Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) reserves. A blockade exceeding 21 days would effectively shut down the island’s semiconductor production.
      • Economic Impact: A study by BCG suggests that losing Taiwan’s semiconductor supply chain would have twice the economic impact of the Great Depression.
  • China’s "Leap-Ahead" Strategy: Because China is blocked from purchasing ASML’s most advanced EUV (Extreme Ultraviolet) lithography machines, they are aggressively investing in "leap-ahead" technologies.
      • Free Electron Lasers: China is exploring new light sources to bypass current lithographic limits.
      • X-Light: Gelsinger is chairman of X-Light, a startup developing free electron lasers capable of reaching two nanometers, aiming to stay ahead of China's domestic innovation.

Leadership Lessons and Corporate Decline

  • The Andy Grove Mentality: Gelsinger describes his 35-year mentorship with Grove as "going to the dentist without novocaine."
      • Data-Driven Truth: Grove demanded that opinions be supported by data, famously stating, "Thank you for your opinion, but if you don't have data, I can get an opinion from the National Enquirer."
  • The Failure of Non-Technologist CEOs: Gelsinger attributes the decline of formerly great engineering firms (Boeing, GE, and Intel’s prior era) to leadership by "well-meaning" lawyers and finance professionals rather than technologists.
      • Intel’s Capital Mismanagement: In the five years before Gelsinger’s return as CEO, Intel gave $70 billion back to shareholders via buybacks/dividends instead of building new fabs or investing in EUV technology.
  • Apprenticeship vs. Academics: Gelsinger notes the U.S. "hollowed out" its middle class by outsourcing manufacturing and over-emphasizing formal schooling over the European-style apprenticeship model, which sustains deep technical trades.

AI and "Values-Aligned" Technology

  • Gloo and the Faith Ecosystem: Gelsinger serves as Chairman of Gloo, a platform modernizing the $1 trillion+ global faith and humanitarian market.
      • Powering Reach: Gloo uses AI to turn decades-old tech stacks into modern "agentic workflows" to increase the efficiency of charitable giving and donor engagement.
  • The "Gutenberg Moment": Gelsinger views AI as the most powerful technology in human history, comparing it to the invention of the printing press.
      • Flourishing AI: He argues the faith community must be active in shaping AI to ensure it reflects human values, specifically preventing social ills seen in the social media era (e.g., mental health decline and disengagement).
  • AI Research Agents: Personally, Gelsinger uses AI as a research agent (joking that he named his agent "Raghu" after a former colleague) to gain rapid expertise in complex deep-tech fields like cryogenics.
"Technology companies need to be run by technologists. Some of these end up being run by a lawyer, a business person, a finance person—all good people, but they weren't core to what the mission of that company was." — Pat Gelsinger
"Mentoring with Andy [Grove] was like going to the dentist without novocaine. Tough, hard, demanding, and an absolute pursuit of right. What's the data? Where is the truth among this?" — Pat Gelsinger
"We are now more dependent on Taiwan as an importer than China... If the supply chain of Taiwan to the semiconductor industry goes offline, it has twice the economic impact of the Great Depression." — Pat Gelsinger
Hello data nerds, my guest today is Pat Gelsinger. Pat is the chairman of Gloo, which builds tech products for faith-based communities. Pat previously served as the CEO of Intel, which is one of the world's most important companies and a company I truly admire. He also spent 30 years at Intel and became the first-ever CTO. He was also the CEO of VMware for nearly a decade, and he's a general partner at Playground Global and executive chairman at X-Light, a semiconductor startup developing free electron lasers. Pat, welcome to Summation. Thank you, Oren. Great to be with you today. In like 1990, which wasn't that long ago, the US made about 40% of the world's chips. Today it's like roughly 10%. Why did the US lose its edge during that time? Yeah, and you know, in some regards, there was never a vote of Congress to get rid of that terrible semiconductor industry. To say that there was ever a decision, you know, and to some degree, I'll say, you know, as I like to frame it, there were decisions in Taiwan, China, and Korea to get this industry. There were substantial and, you know, the chip war, you know, Chris Miller talks about this and all that, but there were long-term industrial policies put in place in other parts of the world. And the US thought, "Wow, that's capital intensive. We can make better margins on software." Why should I commit, you know, $10 billion to a fab project where you don't get cash flow for five years and you don't turn a positive profit for eight years? That's hard, right? Great. So all of a sudden you saw our supply chains starting to migrate to where the industrial policies were reinforcing the value of long-term capital-heavy manufacturing industries. And then three decades later, what happened? The entire industry has migrated to Asia. Then we wake up and then it's sort of like, "Oh my, we care about these things." Then COVID happens and the CHIPS Act happens; we gotta do something. Well, we weren't doing something for 30 years. And all of our Wall Street, you know, near-termism and, you know, focus on higher profits, you know, leveraging other people to do the hard manufacturing work... Okay, we woke up. If we want to go from like 10% back to 30 or 40%, like, is there some sort of realistic timeline? Is there some sort of like realistic plan for us to get there? Yeah, you know, I think it took three decades for our supply chains to set up in there. So, to think that they're going to come back quickly is just naive. It was always this, hey, you know, if we sort of take a decade-long view, can I make a difference in a decade? The answer to that in my mind is yes. But you have to take a decade-long view. You need meaningful pools of capital. You need consistent industrial capital and investments. I need big dollars going into this. I need real policy support, and ultimately I need to create economic advantage to doing this in the US. Do you think you need government capital or do you just need the government willingness to say you're allowed to build the plants, you're allowed to, you know, more like deregulation or kind of change regulations around it? Yeah, you know, and I think it is so hard to move supply chains, Oren. I mean, just so hard. That I think without the government being involved to say that the private industry is going to fix this, okay, to me that just seems naive. Private industry allowed this to migrate away, right? So why would you think that private industry can bring this back without substantial government involvement? I think that's just naive wishful thinking as black swans have shown. As people like Ray Dalio talk about it extensively, markets do not price in long-term risk. So the markets will not get us here. You need industrial policy with real economic policy to make this happen. So, when we started the CHIPS Act, it was clearly, "Hey, we need long-term R&D," check. "We need real shift in capital pools," check. The thing that we didn't do yet in my mind—and this administration has been emphasizing tariffs and others to create economic value and advantage to being in the US—but you need all of those to come together. You need to stay consistent on that for a decade or more because it takes you that long. And we're a few years now in like the CHIPS Act. Like, is it working? You know, it's having some benefit because clearly, you know, we've seen big expansions of TSMC in Arizona, Intel's investment, Samsung building, etc. So clearly we have more fab starts occurring in the US than we were before. Next would be some of the memory work. We've seen Micron with big expansion announcements and they're underway in New York. So I'll say, hmm, it seems to have some of the right characteristics, but we still have a long way to go. I'm still critical that we have no leading-edge TSMC in the US. No leading-edge TSMC R&D in the US. We still have way too much dependency, and many of the long-term R&D pieces are not in the US. So we still have a ways to go, but I'd say some of the early, early indications are more positive than they were in the prior decade. But we have a long way to go. I also argue, hey, I think tariffs are a factor to drive the economic value back to the US. But they need to be very consistent over a long period of time and big enough to make it valuable for the users. Most of the purchasers of fabs are the people who are buying wafers who are the fabless companies today: the NVIDIA, the Apple, the Qualcomm, the AMD, etc. They need to see real economic demand for them to be back in the US. Yeah, and the tariffs are tough too. It's hard to figure out because all these semiconductors are made up of all these other components. By definition, so many of those components are global. So if you have to figure out, "Okay, like we have to really target it very, very specifically," like we're going to put a tariff here, but we might not put this tariff on this other component because that's actually important to bring semiconductors to the US. Yeah, yeah. It's very intricate. Supply chains are very complicated. Where's the packaging, where's the testing, where's the substrate? That's why I say you need good industrial policy over a long period of time with real economic incentives. And you've also called for a US sovereign wealth fund, in some ways to counter China's tech investment. Walk us through that idea. Yeah, and obviously, hey, the sovereign wealth fund... we got Singapore, we got Australia, we got China, we got the Norwegians, etc. So the idea of a sovereign wealth fund, you know, I mean, for the most part, the US has chosen to debt finance many of the things where other countries are choosing to investment finance many of the things. So I just say in that regard, I think most people are starting to wake up and saying, "Boy, I'd prefer my retirement going into an investment fund as opposed to a debt fund." So the idea of a sovereign wealth fund is something that I think the time is long overdue in the US. We see what some of the things like the Superannuation Fund in Australia are doing; they now have trillions of dollars that they get to start pouring into their national agenda. So first I'd say the idea for the US is long overdue in that respect. But then you peel the second phase of it and you sort of say, "Well, what are we going to invest in?" If we have a sovereign wealth fund, how do we decide what to invest in? Obviously the risk of that is we are influenced by geopolitics and all that kind of stuff becomes too influential here. But I'd say, boy, if we were to look at it today, if you and I were to get the 10 most important academic and policy technological policy-minded folks in the US, we're going to agree pretty quickly. You think so? I mean, because I'd be a little worried if we do agree, really. Yeah, absolutely, because we're going to say, "Hey, I need long-term minerals in the US." Rare earth minerals and minerals to enable—okay, got it. Second is I need semiconductors. It's the underlayment of everything that we do. And third, I need energy. If you have a wealth fund, the goal is to make a good IRR on the wealth fund. And some of these things are like, "Okay, we need the government to do this," so maybe it's actually not a good IRR, but it's still good for the long-term economy or good term stability of the country or something, right? Yeah, and a sovereign wealth fund clearly overlays "I want good return," but I also want to be targeting those things that we as a sovereign entity care about. Because we could do that today without a sovereign wealth fund, like— But we're not. Those actions take extraordinary effort. Remember how much shoe leather was burned to get the CHIPS Act across the line in the bipartisan way? Super hard. A sovereign wealth fund could be a mechanism. I'm not saying it's the only one. I'm not saying Congress shouldn't do things like the CHIPS Act, but boy, I think a sovereign wealth fund with a good policy overlay on how we decide is needed. Today, if we pick those three areas and said, "Hey, we want a good IRR but we want to make sure that you're doing those three things: semiconductors, materials, and energy," those are the national priorities for the next decade. We can get consensus on that and let a good investment team go to work. I think it's going to enhance the power of the nation, not be detrimental in any way. Most sovereign wealth funds in the world take their capital and they invest outside their country. The Saudi sovereign wealth fund, the Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth fund, even like the Norway sovereign wealth fund... it's mostly taking that and putting it into America. We're getting that money from their sovereign wealth funds, right? And by the way, look at what was done in the tariff negotiations with Japan and Korea. We're essentially sucking their sovereign wealth fund to invest in the US. Yeah, yeah. And by the way, you know, hey, my opinion is I want to make those three industries great. Let's make semiconductors, energy, and minerals/rare earths foundational to the nation. Make them good. I think a sovereign wealth fund is one of the tools to do that. I think tariff policy is one of the tools to do that. I think acts of Congress with industrial policy is one of the tools to do that. And frankly, given where we are on those topics, I think we need all three. Now, China is reportedly building like an EUV prototype with former ASML engineers that they brought over and stuff. How do you assess their progress in advanced lithography? Because that seems to be one of the big things that's holding them back. Like they can't buy the ASML stuff, or they can't buy maybe some of the stuff that you're working on. Yeah. Well, first thing is, of course, remember we're not letting the good stuff get to China by international policy. So that means they've got to innovate on their side. So they have a huge gap. They have radically overinvested in their semiconductor industry. They have chosen to aggressively capitalize that industry. And I think if you look at some of the historical equipment purchase indexes, they've been buying equipment at more than 2X the rate that their market share in the world would justify. Essentially they are stockpiling deposition machines and etchers and wafer handling and all this kind of stuff. So they are overcapitalizing that industry as China has done in other industries, but they can't get the most advanced stuff. Every evidence is just that they're investing heavily to have their own EUV because the world is not letting ASML's EUV machines get there. But they're also looking at leap-aheads, like free electron lasers as new light sources. One of my companies, X-Light, that we're working heavily on... I do believe that they're saying, "Boy, catching up with what has been perfected is going to be hard. Are there leap-ahead opportunities?" So yeah, I think they're pouring enormous amounts of energy here because largely they're sort of stuck at about 10 nanometers. Intel proved you could do seven nanometers without EUV. It's just hard. But the rest of the world is going from three to two to one and a half. So that gap is about a decade of lithographic capacity and it's getting bigger because they don't have the EUV. To some degree, their motivation to get to the other side of EUV is increasing because the gap between their capabilities and where the rest of the world is is increasing in the absence of that. They're never going much below seven nanometers without something like EUV. So of course they're looking to leap ahead. And hey, there's lots of things that would say that free electron lasers is the next light source after LPP, which is used in the EUV machines of today. It is a big investment area for them. That is part of the reason I'm so motivated to move forward with X-Light. And when you think about X-Light, like why is it viable now as opposed to a decade ago? Like what has changed? Yeah, you know, and there were attempts to use a free electron laser a decade ago, or a decade and a half ago. ASML even looked at it. They tried it. And essentially there's been a couple of breakthroughs that X-Light has done to be able to create independent light sources of different wavelengths and energy levels in the free electron laser and the undulators associated with them and so on. That was the problem that led ASML to say, "We can't make this work." And if I was looking at it from their view, this is a little thing... if you're looking at it from "I'm getting a laser and now how do I carve it out," versus "I'm creating a laser that's optimized for this." It's a breakthrough that X-Light has now clearly shown we know how to make this work. And that's what we're off to do with X-Light. The fact that we made LPP work... we're dropping droplets of tin, hitting them with lasers, collecting light... it's just like an engineering marvel when you look at one of these things, Oren. I mean it is really amazing. But it's only ever a byproduct of the physics of tin. It's only ever going to be 13.5 nanometers. We believe we can take X-Light and free electron lasers to two nanometers raw. It really is pretty magical what we see as possible. If you go to Taiwan, like 100% of people will know what TSMC is. You could ask like a random cook and they'll know. It's the key company in Taiwan. Everyone talks about it. I was in Amsterdam recently getting a tour of the Rijksmuseum by a very smart PhD, a great tour guide. And then at some point there's like a room and it was sponsored by ASML. So I started telling my kids what ASML is and the tour guide had never heard of ASML. She was Dutch; she had never heard of it. And I think it just hasn't pervaded—it's not at the same level in Holland as TSMC is in Taiwan. Like, why has it not captured the imagination of the population there? Yeah. Well, one, I find that surprising. But remember TSMC in Taiwan, right, the whole situation with the Taiwan Straits and China... everybody in Taiwan knows what the silicon shield is. It's in the news every day. No one is invading the Netherlands right now, probably. So this is front of mind and the portion of the economy that TSMC represents in the little island of Taiwan is a larger percentage of the total economy than ASML is in the Netherlands. So you have the geopolitical importance of course. Everybody knows what the Taiwan Straits are and the situation is. At two years old they're already learning that. So I think that it is just such a central aspect of the entire economy and society of Taiwan that to me it's unprecedented. To think that although Taiwan is what, 26 million people? It's not that big of a population. And I'll give you a little... I was shocked by this fact that the US now imports more from Taiwan than we do from China. Yes. What? To me, this is insane. If that's true, if it's even like 20% of China, that seems crazy to me. Like you're saying more than 100% of China. You know, and just to give you the numbers, you can go do some research on this yourself. Because of tariffs, because of this administration and some of the actions they've taken, imports from China have decayed from 500 plus billion to less than 300 billion. And in that same period of time, the emphasis on supply chains, AI chips, and the acceleration of TSMC's imports... we have gone over the last decade from 20 plus million to over 300 billion from Taiwan. Taiwan is now a bigger importer to the US than China is as of the last quarter. Stunning. Holy mackerel. That is crazy. I was not aware of that actually. That's insane. Hey, everybody needs to see the Summation show because they don't realize how current, important, and factual we are together, Oren. Isn't this great? Yeah. This is crazy. This on its own is like the biggest stat that I'm going to start telling people. I had no idea. It's interesting because I have never heard this said before so I would have thought that this would have been more in the news. That's an insane stat. So does that basically mean that outside of like Canada and Mexico it's like the number one country we import from? I think it now sits in the top five. A nation of less than 30 million people, a small island. So it really shows you how important TSMC is, because a lot of that is probably TSMC, right? Now let me give you a next factoid that... our minds were just blown by realizing we import more from Taiwan than China. Now the next factoid is: Taiwan runs on imported LNG. The reserves of Taiwan are less than three weeks. So a blockade of the Taiwan Straits for more than three weeks out the island. If a fab goes down, it takes months to restart it. Very long supply chains. Literally, we've had multiple blockades over the Taiwan Straits over the last 10 years. The longest of which, if my memory is correct, was 13 days. So essentially, more than three weeks and you entirely undermine the semiconductor supply chains. A study by BCG last year said if the supply chain of Taiwan to the semiconductor industry goes offline, it has twice the economic impact of the Great Depression. And why don't they stock six weeks? Like why is it that hard? Why have they only done that? Hey, it's like just-in-time supply. You have to build new storage tanks and all those kind of things. To me, I'd say if you want to have a resilient Taiwan, the best thing that you could do is build more energy resilience. Politically they were building a new nuclear reactor; they stopped it. It's essentially built, but not activated. To me, I'm more interested in them buying energy infrastructure and stores than I am in them buying defense capacity. Anyway, those are two factoids of the day, Oren. That's super interesting. Now you're also like you're investing in deep tech and real deep tech, not just like someone writing some software. When you're doing that, how do you underwrite the technology risk? How do you actually make that investment? I joined Playground a year ago. Part of the reason I joined is because we're venture capitalists, we're out to make a return and give a good payday to our limited partners, but fundamentally all of our investment people at Playground are deep tech folk. We're PhDs, we're professors. Most of the venture capital firms have forgotten how to do hard. They're over here doing the next AI-driven dating app. And I'm sure that's important but, you know, hey, we do hard. The thing is, like if you're putting in hundreds of millions of dollars in more traditional venture capital, you're doing it at a point you already know it's working and now you're fueling the fire. In your case, how do you know it's the right bet? Is it different signals, or is it the science where you really have to dive in and understand it? Yeah, we are going deep on the science. It's almost more like biotech or something. You have to go super deep, right? Yeah, and we have energy projects, we have semiconductor projects, quantum computing, new thermal, superconducting... those are the things. And biotech and robotics efforts as well. Those are the areas that we're in because we think that our differentiator is, given that we're a bunch of geeks making investments, we're better able to assess the technology risk than we are the market risk. If I create a superconducting capability that's a hundred— Well, you know that's going to be—it's like biotech, this drug would be worth a gazillion dollars if it worked. The hard part is just getting it to work, right? Yeah, right. And we can make those judgments better than other venture firms because we go deep in the diligence early. If we make this quantum effect work in photonics, okay, we can do a lot of them. If we can make superconducting work, we can do— So you're not taking any market risk at all? You're taking 100% on the tech risk, right? Yep, that's what we do. And by the way, I've been a deep tech guy for my entire career. So at this phase of my career, Oren, it's sort of like, yeah, this fits me. And I get to leverage also the unique network that I've built. Imagine if we're creating a new power delivery technology. Everybody who consumes power delivery is in my Rolodex. I know those folks. We can accelerate and benefit from my decades of experience in the industry in pretty unique and powerful ways. Now, if I had to rank order the best managers of all time ever in history, I would almost certainly put Andy Grove in the top five and maybe even in the top one. You actually worked with him. What did you learn from him? Well, and just to, you know, maybe a fun story for your listeners here... I am in charge of the 80386 tape out. Okay? This is in 1983. We're assembling the 80386, putting the whole database together and so on. I'm the young engineer who's doing that work, getting the database assembled. And I'm giving an update to the executive staff of Intel: Gordon Moore, Robert Noyce, and Andy Grove. Noyce is still there in '83? He's still there. He's still very much there. These are like legends. You must have been shaking or something. So I'm this young, aggressive engineer. I'm barely past puberty, as I say, at this point as I'm meeting with them. And I am yelling at them because the computers aren't stable! They made some changes and I need them to fix the computers if I'm going to get my chip out the door. You need to go—right? I mean, like, what a precocious young rat I was with the gods of the industry in front of me. My phone rings, back when phones used to ring. I pick up the phone. It was about a few days after this meeting. And I say hello, and he says "Andy." Andy who? The voice comes back: "Andy Grove." I am just like— You didn't know it with his Hungarian accent or something? Oh hey, I'm just a young engineer trying to get the chip out the door. And then he starts shelling me with questions. "What do you read? What do you study? What's your next job? What do you want to do? What's your career—?" I'm just like, "How...?" I'm barely able to fumble out an answer and so shocked that I'm talking to one of the founders. And he says, "Those were lousy answers. Be in my office in a week with better ones." You either show up or you leave the country, right? And that began a mentoring relationship that lasted for 35 years, literally to the day Andy died. I joke that mentoring with Andy was like going to the dentist without novocaine. Tough, hard, demanding, and an absolute pursuit of right. "What's the data?" asking the hard questions. "Where is truth among this? Have you done your homework? Have you gotten the best opinions from the best people in the industry?" "Thank you very much for your opinion, Oren, but if you don't have data to support it, I can get an opinion from the National Enquirer. Show up with an opinion and data to support it and be ready to defend your perspectives. And we're going to have the meeting in the meeting." There were just so many things from Andy over the decades. Man, I remember when I started my first tech company at 21, the first book somebody gave me was High Output Management. And then later, Only the Paranoid Survive. And then I read his memoir—I think it was called Swimming Across—which was a beautifully written, very underrated book. He is just like a next-level thinker. Yeah, yeah. And I was in the meetings with him when he was arguing with Bill Gates. I was in the meetings with him with Steve Jobs. I was in the meetings with him with Michael Dell. So yeah, I mean just extraordinary experiences. Now we have these like formerly great engineering companies: Boeing, General Electric. They kind of seem to have lost their way, especially when it comes to R&D. How did that happen? Yeah. My perspective is that technology companies need to be run by technologists. Some of these end up being run by a well-meaning lawyer, a business person, or a finance person—all good people, but they weren't core to what the mission of that company was. It's just super hard to run a technology company because the winds of fortune move so dramatically based on technology. And Intel essentially had almost two decades that it did not have a technologist at the helm. How did that happen? I mean, I see how it happens because people leave and you promote the very competent salesperson or CFO, and they're great at that thing that they do. But then they're not pushing on innovating. They're not in the engineering system. In some ways, if you're a great finance person, you should run Citibank. That's the perfect place for you. But don't run the tech company. Yeah. You know, and as one example, when I came back to Intel and took over, Intel had given $70 billion back to shareholders in the five years before my return. What I wouldn't have done for $70 billion! Intel hadn't built a new fab for a decade. We had chosen not to invest in EUV. We gave up our position in ASML essentially for nothing at the time. We owned the supply chain for all the EUV machines and we gave it all away because it wasn't in the financial interests as driven by the CFO or salesmen. That was the state when I came back: rebuilding the technical bona fides of the company and rebuilding manufacturing. Those things are hard, heavy, expensive, and take a decade to realize the value of those investments. I also want to talk about Gloo. It is focused on the faith ecosystem in what you call "values-aligned AI." What does that mean in practice? Yeah, so first let's define what we're doing a little bit and then what it means to bring values-aligned AI to that. Imagine you have many charities; many of them are faith-driven. These might be campus ministries like Campus Crusade, humanitarian organizations like World Vision or Compassion, or church denominations like the Assemblies of God or Southern Baptist. It could also be things like the International Justice Mission for slavery and trafficking. When you think about them, this is enormous. Over $250 billion are given in church offering plates on a yearly basis. If you add up all of those humanitarian and charitable organizations, it is well over a trillion dollars. It's big, fragmented, venerable, and growing faster than the GDP. Even as we went through COVID, you can say less people were in church, but essentially the economics have continued to expand. We've become more philanthropic as a nation through periods like COVID, not less. It's quite stunning. And so you have all of those organizations, many of those are churches but these are faith-driven organizations. Christian universities: 900 faith universities in the US today. It is enormous. And their technology sucks because they're mission-driven organizations. Generally, they— By the way, that's probably true of most universities and most nonprofits. It's not just faith-based. Yeah, they just don't do a good job with tech. And what's happened is they've been trying to keep up, but then AI happened. It's sort of like "Oh, we already were a decade behind and now somebody turned the music up." Into that comes Gloo. We're saying we're going to do two things for that community. One is we're going to power their technology—power tech—and the other is we're going to power their reach. Make them more effective to donors and philanthropists as well. Power tech and power reach with the superpower of AI. That's what Gloo is doing. And for that, imagine, literally we are taking over and running the technology for many of these organizations now. Those become our people, we take over the full thing, and then we're turning what might be two or three-decade-old technology experiences into modern AI-driven agentic workflows that are far more efficient and far more modern. Today, if you would give a dollar to one of those organizations, because their tech sucks, you're probably getting 80 cents worth of value. Tomorrow the vision is you get a dollar 20 or more. This is an extraordinary mission and economic unleash for us. This year Gloo will be about 185 million in revenue, so we are building a real business. We took it public just before Thanksgiving of last year. We snuck out when the window was almost open to get it public and we're growing organically well over 40% year on year. We're also doing some acquisitions to grow and analysts have us at about 300 million in profit in the second half of this year. We want to influence not just the use of technology but the very essence of what AI means. The way I've described this, Oren, is the faith community did not show up for social media. Imagine the label that says "We're going to make you more disengaged, we're going to lower your academic performance, we're going to make you more susceptible to social ills." That is social media. That's what the facts have said. We would never do that. But the faith community didn't show up demanding that it was for good. So a lot of what we're working to do then also is saying we want to make AI flourishing. We want AI to be shaped for good because how we embed the values of humanity into LLMs is far more important. It's going to be far more leverageable. Does the automated vehicle have values? Does the robotic experience have human values? Does the online experience reflect human values? When I ask it "Tell me how to tie a noose," is it going to say, "No, we need to get you to a suicide clinic"? All of those things. We believe that this is the most powerful technology in the history of humanity. Let's make sure it's shaped and used for good. Now, as someone who practices faith yourself, how did that inform you as a leader, especially in the technology world? Yeah. Well, very early, when I was 18 years old, essentially I had my spiritual awakening. I became a Christian at that point, accepted Christ, and went through an experience where I felt like I was being called into ministry. I was like, "I don't want to be a minister, I love tech!" And then the answer from God was, "The workplace is your ministry." So I've always said I'm a full-time minister in the workplace. My wife and I, we've started a number of philanthropies. One of them has 32,000 kids in the schools that we've been helping to create in Africa. We started an organization called Transforming the Bay with Christ. We've always been involved with philanthropies and the faith community. How can we use the extraordinary platform that God has given us? The wealth that he has given us on a yearly basis... we're giving well over 50% of our yearly gross income to philanthropic work now. How do we use what we've been given, both in wealth but also in network and capacity? So I was always sort of doing it, but I worked hard. And now with Gloo, I get to put the two together. Yeah, which is really nice. When I used to meet engineers in San Francisco, where I spent most of my life, I would say a very high percentage of them, especially on the bleeding edge, were atheists. And then some time in the last five years, a very high percentage of them have become creationists. They believe they're in a simulation, which essentially believes there's some sort of intelligent design. They've kind of moved from atheist to creationist in some sort of way. They might not believe in the same God as a Christian, but they believe in some sort of technological type of thing. In some ways, they're much closer to a creationist. Have you seen a shift in people looking more for spirituality now? Yeah. I'll say probably two different things there. One, people that share a faith view like me include people like Gary Tan who runs Y Combinator, Tracy Stephens, Peter Thiel... we share our faith tradition on this. By the way, almost all of them have been guests on this podcast. And I'll say, with that, there's sort of like a lot of them weren't visible for their faith before. Certainly one of the journeys that I have is that all of a sudden people say, "Well hey, Pat's super public about his faith, I can too." I do think there's this—they were there, they were just okay showing up for work and doing their work. So I think one is you're seeing that. I think the second thing that you're seeing is there's almost a presumption of success now in Silicon Valley. My startup's going to be successful and I'm going to become a millionaire. But then it becomes the question of "Is it significant?" I do think this idea of moving from success to significance is in the water or in the air of Silicon Valley, where all of a sudden people are asking harder questions. We've clearly seen more churches and more attendance at churches in this place where people are showing up and they want to be in community. I had a very close individual in my life say, "I'm not sure if I believe like in church, but I believe I must be in community of church." So I think you're seeing some of that post-COVID. We lost some of that relational aspect. God created us to be in community and relationship. I go back to when I spoke at the Super Bowl breakfast. In Genesis it said God created and it was good. God created and it was good. We go through all of it. And then he gets to God created man and it was not good, until he created woman to put him in relationship. We as human beings want to be in relationship. COVID and so much of this view of science-only has driven us from relationship. I do think there is this idea of being in community again and much of those communities are spiritually-led. So I think all three of those factors—people becoming more visible for their faith, having role models they believe in, the move from success to significance, and the need to be in relationship—are at play. If you think of the evangelical side of Christianity specifically, it is very much like a startup. Every little church is its own startup in a way. Most of them started with nothing, then there's some story about it moving, and then of course they grow and then there's a lot of competition. They get killed, there's creative destruction... it mirrors a little bit more of the startup scene. Yeah, and it's quite interesting when you look at the statistics. Almost all of what are called the mainline denominations—Methodist, Presbyterian, Anglican, and Lutheran—their attendance is declining as the non-denominational, the Christian churches, and the evangelicals are climbing. You're seeing the sort of like... well, the old guard companies are dying and the new startup companies are emerging. And then there's also this vibrancy in the evangelical movement where somewhat like the founders of a startup company, they came out of another startup company. So many of those are planting new churches as well. But the difference is, I'm not necessarily trying to start my new business and send my founders out; I'm going to keep them here. In the church community, they actually do, and they're partnering to help those new churches get started as well. In fact, the organization that I started, TBC—Transforming the Bay with Christ—we have about 50 active church plants underway in the Bay Area. Now, if we go back to AI, how are you personally using it? Whether it's for business to make your business better or for your life, how are you diving into it? Yeah. Well, I'll say first I've got myself too busy between my two hats that I'm not using it personally as much as I want to. Yeah, by the way, I find myself canceling all these meetings just so I have time to play with it because things are changing so fast. They really are. But personally, I'm primarily using it as my research agent to just help me get smarter faster. I had this one person, Raghu Raghuram, who became my successor as VMware CEO. Super smart guy. On Fridays, I would always walk into his office and ask him a hard question because I knew over the weekend he'd work on the hard question and have an answer by Monday morning. So I named my first research agent "Raghu." Sort of like, "Okay, Raghu is going to go work on this and I'll go get myself a coffee." Right, while Raghu goes and works on this question. And trust me as a deep tech investor, I have a lot of questions. My brain— So you have like certain skills or whatever that you say "Okay, hey, go do these things or go look at these things." Yeah, like we're working on one the other day: "Tell me about the cryogenic industry, cryocoolers, and the different things," and so on like that. I don't know anything about cryogenics, but man, I need to get smarter quickly. So that's probably the primary personal use that I'm using. I'm now—if I go to a search engine versus a chat interface, I'm probably now about 20 to 1 in the chat interface. It's like every once in a while it's like, "Oh that's right, I got to go do a search engine," but it's just so much better with a user interface. And it really becomes... for 60 years humans had to adapt to computers. With AI, computers adapt to humans. Have you yet given access for an agent to your email, to your calendar, to those types of things yet? I've been playing with it. I've been playing with it. I'm actually sort of excited because OpenAI had so many security vulnerabilities that I played with it but turned most of them off for all the security folks who started to yell at me for what I shouldn't be doing with it. So anyway, I turned most of those off, but now that they're starting to become a part of the core engines, I think it's in better ways. Yeah, and you can use obviously Claude Co-work or Perplexity... Perplexity computer has a lot of those things kind of built in. Right? So it's going to get good enough and secure enough soon that essentially everybody will feel more comfortable with it. But like on the Gloo side, we're now—as part of our core Gloo offerings—asking how we turn them into agents as fast as possible. Because a lot of that stuff—marketing, whether it's to new members or for donors—is a perfect use case for all these AI agents. But even more meat and potatoes kind of stuff like code submissions having to go through all the security checks we have. We have a formal process so that portal process is now an agent process. It might prompt for some inputs a couple of points along the way, but we're able to do essentially a four or five-day security review process all through agentic flow today. Some of these things are just very meat and potatoes. Some of it is "Hey, let's explode your marketing pipeline with a much more refined database of potential target donors." So some of these things are just very quickly becoming "Okay, that's good, that's powerful." Every one of our coders, you know, we're now, you know, turning them into a, you know, agentic stack where, you know, we'll start measuring on how many tokens per coder they're using. And you know, you'll know those who are using the tools versus not and, you know, anyway, it's just an extraordinary period. Now, you started Intel, I think, like when you're 18 as like a quality control technician, right? With an associate's degree or, you know, nothing fancy, and then you kind of rose up to become the first CTO. That path seems somewhat very almost impossible today. I like your path. Everyone is blooming at different stages of their life; it's not everyone who scores a 1600 when they're on an SAT when they're 16 years old, right? What do we need to change to support more people like that? Yeah, and you know, I mean, my journey, I wouldn't say it's the right one for everybody, you know, but, you know, I am the American success story. You know, both of my parents had first through eighth-grade educations in one-room schoolhouses. Neither of them went to high school or college. Right? You know, growing up, "get your PhD, get your PhD." You know, part of the reason I started at Intel was I couldn't afford college. So Intel paid for my college. I lived in fear, Oren, that I wasn't going to get a passing grade because I needed a B or better to get tuition reimbursement from Intel, and I had no money to pay for tuition. Right?
