You know, so I put it on a credit card, the first debt of my life. I lived in fear of not getting a B or better, right? You know? You know, fear is a great motivator to study. Right? Yeah, it's fine. And you know obviously, you know, Intel just gave me extraordinary opportunities. You know, it was very much a meritocracy as Andy Grove, you know, going back to our Andy comments, you know as well, where hey, I worked. You know, I say you may be smarter than me, Oren, right? You may be more talented than me, Oren, but you're not gonna outwork me though, right? Yeah, you know, because I grew up as a farm kid, I knew how to work, right? You know for—and to me, you know, I mean that's part of, you know, like so many immigrants, right, you know, have that same sort of thing. You know, hey, just give me a chance. Right? And to me, you know, the magic of, you know, my career and I think of so many others, just give me a chance. And I think what we need to do, right, you know, is give that same "just give me a chance." You know, give me that first internship, you know, give me an opportunity to get my first associate's degree. Give me a chance. A long time ago or something, if you wanted to become a lawyer, you would go like apprentice at a law firm and you'd do like a lot of the grunt work. You'd be like sweeping the floors too, but you'd also get some books and they would help you, and they would help you study and eventually you would, you would, you would get to the point where you could take the bar and you could become a lawyer. And now it's like, it's like we've outsourced that to school. No, no, you have to go to law school. You have to go to law school to go do that and then you can come in. And same thing for everything. It's like you don't apprentice like—no, no, you have to—you don't apprentice to become an electrician, you have to go to electrician school or—it seems like we've kind of changed the model for some reason. Yeah. And in fact, you know, I mean there, you know, in many of the trades, you know, for instance today, you know, how many people do we have that are, you know, I mean skilled, you know, people doing civil engineering work? Right? You know, or civil building, welding? You know, we lost shipbuilding and, you know, nuclear reactors and so on like this. You know, the apprentices program of Europe, you know, has never stopped. Yeah, yeah, yeah. It's one of the things they do very well actually, yeah. Yeah. You know, and so, you know, I think that that is why they're stronger in some of those areas than the US because we stopped doing that. And I do think this power of apprenticeship, you know, across so many different aspects of the manufacturing in particular is so healthy, right, you know, for the nation. And, you know, to me that's one of the side effects of losing that manufacturing: "oh, we'll just outsource that to Asia." You know, we also hollowed out the entire middle of the nation and middle of the socioeconomic stack, and I think that's dreadful. Now, last question we ask all of our guests: what conventional wisdom or advice do you think is generally bad advice? Haha. Well, you know, I do think the, you know, I mean we just touched on one of them. Actually that's a good one, yeah. Yeah, super good. You know, and I would just say, hey, find your way, right? You know, and to me, I tell everybody your first job, the most important thing is who you're working for. You need the Andy Grove to me—someone who's going to see the potential in you, push you to become the best version of yourself. Absolutely. Right? You know, so if you gave me the choice of a great first job, right, with a first boss, right, even if it was a sub-tier university that I went to, a sub-tier, right, first job—but I had a great first boss who's going to beat the snot out of me and make me good? That is way more valuable than going to Harvard, you know, than going to Stanford. Right? You just need, right, that kind of influence, and they become a champion for you, right, you know, for the entirety, you know, of your life, you know. And I am such a believer in mentoring where, you know, all of us need, you know, what Andy Grove was to me. You know, I joke that people need, right, in Biblical terms, they need a Paul, a Barnabas, and a Timothy. Right? You need a Paul like Andy Grove breathing into you. You need a Barnabas, someone who's on the journey with you. And then finally, pay it forward. You know, you need a Timothy, somebody that you're pouring into their life and journey. I love that. This is amazing. This is a great place to end. Thank you, Pat Gelsinger, for joining us on Summation. I follow you at @PGelsinger on X. I encourage our listeners to engage with you there. This has been really enlightening and a ton of fun. Well, thank you so much, Oren. I appreciate the opportunity to be on the show. You know, to all of your, you know, listeners and viewers, right, you know, this is the greatest time in history to be a technologist. It just couldn't be better. And to be able to say the, you know, I say the enlivening of faith in the face of the greatest technological—I call it the AI moment. Right? And if I could, Oren, just as we finish up here, you know, this is one of my favorite gifts that I've ever given. That's a page of the original Gutenberg Bible. I have a page, right, you know, and, you know, Gutenberg, right, inventing printing changed humanity. Martin Luther made it good. Right? You know, he changed society in the most positive and meaningful way. You know, we live in the Gutenberg moment with AI today. Let's make it good. I love it. All right, thank you, Pat. This has been incredible. Thank you. Take care. One more thing before we go. I write a blog called Summation. It's the same as this podcast. The blog is about non-obvious idea sharing on business, talent, data, longevity, and random contrarian takes. If you like the conversations on this show, you'll probably like the blog. It's free. New content comes out twice a month. You can subscribe at oren.substack.com. That's oren.substack.com.

From Vision To Value: How AI Factories Ate Revolutionizing Enterprise Impact

Pascal BORNET(120.0K) ·35:57 ·7.5K views · 2026-03-31
Charlie Boyle (VP, DGX Systems at NVIDIA) DD Dasgupta (VP, NVIDIA) Pascal Bornet (AI Expert and Host)
AI factories represent a fundamental shift from data storage to revenue-generating production centers, necessitating a synergy between high-performance computing (NVIDIA DGX/Blackwell) and globally distributed, secure physical infrastructure (Equinix).
TL;DR AI factories represent a fundamental shift from data storage to revenue-generating production centers, necessitating a synergy between high-performance computing (NVIDIA DGX/Blackwell) and globally distributed, secure physical infrastructure (Equinix). From Retrieval to...

TL;DR

AI factories represent a fundamental shift from data storage to revenue-generating production centers, necessitating a synergy between high-performance computing (NVIDIA DGX/Blackwell) and globally distributed, secure physical infrastructure (Equinix).

The Evolution of the AI Factory

  • From Retrieval to Reasoning: AI is shifting from simple information retrieval (chatbots) to agentic models that reason, think, and execute complex tasks faster than human speed.
  • Production Focus: Charlie Boyle emphasizes that these are "factories" because they produce intelligence and revenue, rather than just "clusters" that store data.
  • Rapid Performance Gains:
      • The "35x" Factor: New generations like the Blackwell platform provide 35x to 100x speedups over previous iterations.
      • Software Value: NVIDIA’s software investment often doubles hardware performance within the first year, meaning the same hardware investment becomes more valuable over time.

Strategic Infrastructure: NVIDIA & Equinix Partnership

  • Physical Constraints: Modern AI hardware (like the Blackwell systems) requires massive power and cooling—often 20, 30, or 40 kilowatts per rack—which exceeds the capacity of traditional enterprise data centers.
  • Operational Readiness: Equinix provides the physical "home" for NVIDIA’s stack across 72 major metros and nearly 300 data centers, ensuring uptime, reliability, and network connectivity.
  • Co-Engineering: The companies work years in advance on mechanical and electrical specifications so that new NVIDIA products can be deployed in Equinix facilities as soon as they leave the factory line.

Distributed AI and Data Sovereignty

  • Processing Near Data: Because enterprise data is "heavy" and expensive to move, infrastructure must be distributed to where the data lives (clouds, edge, or sovereign zones).
  • The "Knobs" of Sovereignty: DD Dasgupta notes that customers need "knobs" to control different levels of privacy and compliance:
      • Data Residency: Ensuring data does not leave a specific firewall or national border.
      • Network Sovereignty: Controlling the transit path of data (e.g., preventing data from transiting through specific countries).
      • AI Privacy: Ensuring models are only trained on data belonging to a specific entity or nation.
  • Software Guardrails: NVIDIA integrates guardrails into its software stack to automate policy enforcement, preventing data from crossing international borders unless explicitly authorized.

Executive Action Plan for AI Adoption

  • Avoid the "Wait-and-See" Trap: With NVIDIA moving to a one-year product cycle, Boyle advises leaders to invest in what they need today. Waiting for the next generation creates a competitive disadvantage.
  • Strategic Ambition:
      • Move Beyond "Safe" Projects: Simple internal chatbots rarely impact the bottom line. Executives should target ambitious applications that touch revenue and strategic goals.
      • AI-Enabled Development: If software developers are not currently using agentic AI tools, the company is already behind. AI can make developers 10x more productive.
  • Data Strategy vs. Data Cleansing: Do not wait for "perfect" data. Modern AI can be used to clean, filter, and prepare data, replacing manual, human-led data cleansing processes.

The Future of AI Infrastructure

  • AI for AI: Future AI factories will use AI to manage themselves, particularly in power management and network operations.
  • Autonomous Operations: Using AI to monitor telemetry and adjust power consumption or network provisioning in real-time to optimize performance and sustainability.
"The AI factory builds things that are useful... It’s not just a data center; it's a factory because a factory builds things that are useful and generates revenue."

— Charlie Boyle

"Infrastructure needs to mirror the data patterns and the application patterns. Because data is distributed, that's why we're calling our solution Distributed AI."

— DD Dasgupta

"The wrong thing for your company is to not do anything. Be ambitious. Don't start with the safe stuff that doesn't deliver the impact."

— Charlie Boyle

Hello everyone and welcome. I'm here at GTC Nvidia 2024. It's an exciting event with 30,000 people this morning. We had Jensen Huang's visionary talk keynote, and I'm here with two guests: one from Nvidia and one from Equinix. We will talk about what everyone's talking about: AI factories. I want to talk about those AI factories with a certain angle that will interest you. How can AI factories help your company get to a better business impact and get to the vision that you expect? Let's get with the first of my guests. D.D., how are you, D.D.? "I'm good, Pascal, and a big hi to everyone." And then we have Charlie from Nvidia. "Hi, Pascal. Welcome everyone. I just ran over here from the keynote." "Cool. That was exciting." "Yes, so exciting. Yes. That was amazing. I loved the end as well." "Oh, the robots, yes. I really recommend that you watch this keynote. It's 2 hours long, but have a look at the end. That's really, really, that's the recap basically." Let's talk about AI factories and how both companies, Equinix and Nvidia—I had a look at the age of your companies, and each is about 30 years old. You both started in different fields. You, D.D., started in data centers. Of course, Nvidia is in the GPUs and AI stack now. It's really about the merging of those two worlds coming together. That's really what we're going to talk about. I want really, as well, to talk about how it's going to be useful for you as a business executive to know all this and how to get to the business impact that you're looking for. Let's start with you, D.D. Can you tell me about what you've been doing at Equinix? "Yeah, absolutely. So first off, I'm responsible for all of product marketing at Equinix. And as you said, both the companies have been around for almost 30 years—27 to be precise in the case of Equinix." "The way we got started was we became the place where the world interconnected. Let's see what was happening 27 years back. What was the biggest thing that was happening? It was the internet. But the internet traffic was being carried over different carriers. You had Verizon and AT&T and BT and, you know, all of these. But you needed a place for all of this traffic to come together. And when I say a place, I mean a physical location." "And that's how we got started. We targeted the big financial districts all over the world, and now 27 years later, we have data centers in all major metros—72 metros all over the world. But what's happened in the last 27 years beyond the internet, in fact, what is built on top of the internet?" "Let's see. There was the cloud, then there was social, there was mobile, and now this small little thing called AI. Guess where all the action is happening. It's happening in those physical locations. And so partnering with Nvidia for a very long time, we found it was the perfect place for the technology that's coming out of Nvidia. It needed a place, and that's where we have our partnership to promote the growth of AI factories." "That's beautiful. So thank you, D.D. So key message here: almost 300 data centers around the world in all the main cities around the world. An expertise that you've built over almost 30 years of data center management, governance, security, and all that we need to do when you have a data center. And this world is meeting with Nvidia. Charlie, can you tell us about you, about what excites you and what you've built?" "Similar background. I actually started working at an internet company in the late 90s when the internet was just kicking off. I was telling friends about some of the original exchanges in Northern Virginia, reminiscing about old times right before the keynote. At Nvidia, I've actually been here 10 years—this is my 10th anniversary—but it's also my product line DGX's 10th anniversary." "And 10 years ago when we were starting in AI, part of the reason that we came up with the DGX platform—and I can't take credit for that, I showed up as the smart engineers had thought about it and Jensen had invested in it—it was a very simple notion to take the greatest Nvidia technology that we had at the time, build that into a simple system, put a great software stack on it, and make it easy for customers to use." "That was 10 years ago when our customers' biggest questions back then was, 'How am I possibly going to use 8 GPUs?' I told that at my sales training earlier this weekend and got a laugh. 10 years ago, like, who needs 8 GPUs? Now people need 800,000; they need a million GPUs. And a big part of that journey has been just building on that same vision: make the latest technology accessible to people, make it accessible to every enterprise, every business, and meet them where they're at." "And that's a lot of where our partnership with Equinix has worked over the years, is helping businesses adopt AI where they need it when they need it, potentially without some expertise that the business didn't have in-house. They needed partners; they needed a great partner like Equinix to help them out." "Beautiful. So when we had D.D. and Equinix having a global presence of data centers, you are bringing the AI stack that we have inside that, and that grows with the new technologies, with the innovation, and brings new services and new capacities to companies. So we really see the complementarity of both companies coming together. Can you tell me more about your partnership and when did you start working together?" "So I think Charlie probably knows this better than I do in terms of exactly when we partnered, but I think we've been partnering for a very long time. And specifically for AI and around AI factories, I think it's been the last about three and a half years that we've really been partnering. And again, this is not a partnership in PowerPoint." "The systems are produced by Nvidia, and Equinix provides the physical space, power, cooling, and then the network connectivity pieces. And so that's a real partnership because all of our end customers, at the end of the day, they need the uptime, they need the reliability, they need the applications to run. And so in good times and bad times, we have partnered together to enable that outcome that the customer is looking for." "So yeah, I'd say in the whole AI factory distributed AI, it's been about three and a half years, but I think our partnership goes back even before that. Definitely goes back before that. As we started building the product line and customers started building larger and larger systems in our Ampere product generation, we announced a new product solution that we call the DGX SuperPOD." "Early AI systems—2016, 2017, 2018—all those people had a few systems and then they wanted more systems. And at about the same time was when we brought in the Mellanox acquisition. We'd always used the Mellanox networking technology as partners, but customers needed really much larger systems." "And those first A100 systems, the SuperPODs back then, could fit into a traditional enterprise data center. As they started to get bigger, they definitely needed a larger data center, something like an Equinix. But then once we got to our Hopper generation, we really saw people building much larger systems. Systems that had rack power that was starting at 20, 30, 40 kilowatts. Something much larger than you'd ever see in a traditional enterprise data center." "So it was very clear if a customer wanted to run one of these large SuperPOD configurations to drive new business, to drive AI technologies into the center of their business, they needed a partner to host that system somewhere, and that's where our work with Equinix comes in. It's not magic that we announce a new product and it's ready to run in Equinix." "We've been working with them years in advance so they know what our new power, cooling, security, networking, what all that looks like ahead of time. So that once we're shipping that next-generation product, the great Blackwell products that Jensen just talked about today, we'd already been talking to Equinix years in advance on the mechanical and electrical specifications on that. So as soon as those things roll off of the factory line, they're ready to land in an Equinix facility." "Beautiful. And as you said, this partnership is not new, but now we are getting to an environment that is new and needs that are new, and that's where it's a kind of new generation of AI factories that you're starting to build. So can you tell us about the new needs, the new environment, and distributed AI?" "A big part of it—and definitely if you haven't seen the keynote, watch the keynote—because a big part of what's changed is AI is now generating the information itself. It's no longer information retrieval. Yes, you have to retrieve information, but it's thinking, it's reasoning, it's building things, and it needs a lot of information from different places." "One of the big shifts that we're talking about is it's no longer just a human sitting in front of a chatbot asking it a question. You're giving it a task, asking it to do something, and then the AI is working faster than human speed. It used to be before, I only needed AI to be fast enough that I could type something or that I could ask it something with my voice and it would respond with the voice." "Well, now I want to give it something to do and I want it to work as fast as possible to give me that business result that I want. Ultra-low latency and access to a much larger wealth of data. When you look at the agentic models that there are today, it used to be if I had what we called a RAG—retrieval augmented generation model—you would look at that and say, 'Read this PDF and summarize it for me.'" "I just need to go find that PDF, read it, and do that work. Now with the reasoning model, it's going out there and it may read that PDF and it says, 'Oh, I need to go do research on these five different topics in order for me to answer the question that Pascal just asked me or to write a script that he needs to get his work done.'" "And so all of that requires not only access to data but access to data quickly and securely. Because inside your enterprise, you want to have the AI access the data that you want it to access, and at the same time, don't let it access the data that doesn't need to be in that part of the program." "100%. And I think that's where we are partnering. It's not just in the physical locations, but it's the connectivity piece that Charlie is talking about, because AI is fueled by data, but not fragments of data—all the data. And where is all this data? It's in the clouds, it's at the edge, behind sovereign zones." "That's crazy. When I talk to a CEO of a company, they always say, and we talk about data, it's always, 'My data is everywhere; it's in multi-formats, it's structured, unstructured, I don't even know where it is exactly.' And turns out it's heavy and expensive and really tough to move data. And so you want the processing, the models, the inference to come to wherever the data is." "And because data is distributed, that's why we're calling our solution distributed AI, because infrastructure needs to mirror the data patterns and the application patterns. And I think we want as well the inference from AI to happen as close as possible to where it's used. Is it right to say that for a latency problem, I think, and that depends highly on your AI application? It's processing the data as close to the data as possible." "Now when we were talking about a typical AI of maybe a couple of years ago, you asked it a question through probably your phone. It heard your voice, and to get a response back that seemed natural, you needed it sub-second for it to start talking to you. And so that was one form of AI, still a very valuable form of AI, but now in the agentic world, some AIs you're asking to write a computer program. That may take a week." "And so part of that optimization problem now for businesses isn't just where to do the AI, but it's where to do it at the most cost-effective state. Because if I ask you what the weather is outside, I don't want you to wait a week to tell me what the weather is; the weather's changed. If I ask you to write me a compiler, that might have taken somebody months before." "If it takes the AI a week, I'm super happy with that, but as a business you want to be able to do that very cost-effectively to get the work done at the price that you want it to hit the deadline that you want. There's another key trend that we see currently and key issue that I hear from those CEOs very frequently currently is about the data leakages, the data security basically, and this is a growing concern that we see in businesses. How is this solved in your partnership?" "So I think when it comes to security, privacy, sovereignty, these are all related topics, but I think it starts with data residency where data cannot leave the firewall of a company, the borders of a sovereign state. So it starts with data residency, but then on top of that you have to think about network sovereignty and network privacy. So data can move from, let's say, France to UK, but it can't transit through Germany, for instance." "And then customers are also looking at AI sovereignty now and AI privacy, which is a model in a country or in a company cannot be trained by data which is not belonging to that country. So I think there's different levels of security, privacy, and sovereignty, and that's where we're partnering with Nvidia to make sure each of those depends on if it's a regulated industry or not, if it's a government we're serving or not, if it's a cloud provider or not." "So I think there's so many different combinations, but what I feel good about in our partnership is the customer is doing this on their terms. We give the knob to the customer, like, what level of security, sovereignty, and privacy you're looking for, and then they have the knob to control how they want and how they want to implement it." "And that's the power of a large network as you have, 300 places around the world where you can have data close to you. And those knobs are something that are extremely important in the software itself, and that's a lot of the work that we do at Nvidia at the software layer is enabling that for customers to specify something that makes sense to them. Because like any complex system, we've all looked at a control panel or a complex thing and it's like, 'Well, if I've got too many knobs, like, which one do I turn?'" "And so we try to make that easy in software, and there's a lot of technologies, but the easy one to think about is what we call guardrails. And so that's built into every set of software, every library, every tool that we come out with. And whether the guardrail is built directly into the tool or whether it's making a call out to another tool that controls that, as an enterprise you want to think about those." "And we have great best practices out there, what other customers have done with their guardrails. Like, you can start with a blank sheet of paper and don't let it access anything, but then that kind of upsets your users—they gotta ask you for everything. And so you want to start out with a good secure posture and then ask it when it's going outside, like, 'Hey, the data I'm looking for isn't in this Equinix facility, but it is in an Equinix facility in the same country, just in a different city. Is that okay?'" "Well, I can probably set a policy that says that's generally okay, but if I've got to transit an international border, always ask me for that. But you want to build that into the program automatically so that you don't have those 'oh-no' moments like, 'Why did that happen?' You want to start from a secure posture and then trust the AI that you're building and then expand it from there." "Beautiful. What I would love to hear from you now is which companies are currently using these new types of AI factories and what are the impact, the benefits they get from these? Can you give a few examples, a few—you don't need to name the companies, but..." "I can start and I'm sure Charlie will chime in. So I think Alembic is one, which is a customer that they are in our SV11 data center here in the Bay Area. And it's the SuperPOD that they're using because their requirements are extremely intense and complicated. They're doing something called causal AI, they're ingesting huge amounts of data, and then it needs a facility like the data center we have in SV11." "Like the SuperPOD, the power, the cooling—we spent months together, Nvidia and Equinix spent months together just to make sure that once this system is up and running, it's deployed, it's performing like the way the customer is wanting. So we did a lot of proof of concept. So beyond that, just the sheer amount of power and cooling that was needed, it was only when both companies said, 'Yes, it is safe to use this; we're not going to cause a thermal event,' is when the customer kind of went." "So just basically goes to show, like, we were there through every step of the deployment with the customer together. And even in that same example, business value, we talked about it this morning. Every generation we see a huge speedup, and even that Alembic example, I know the guys there really well—actually, one of their early members and I worked together 30 years ago back at the internet company—but he shared with me some data points." "Like, they used to take 18 hours to 36 hours to run one of these jobs for their customers on their Hopper generation cluster they had that was working. When they moved to our Blackwell systems, they got a 50 to 100x speedup on that. And by doing that, when you think about that, you see it on a graph, and when we put up 35x, people are like, 'Well, is it really 35x?'" "I have customers all the time beat the numbers that we put up there, but the business value in that is they can now serve 50x, 100x more customers. They can lower their cost per customer per transaction by that same factor. So you can take that speedup in every generation in different business ways. And people often ask, 'Hey, should I wait for the next generation thing?' It's a classic IT question, 'Oh, something new is going to come out.'" "Well, Jensen set the pace for us a year ago. He said every year, absolutely every year, we're gonna have a whole new generation that comes out. And so my advice to any customer looking to get in is, like, invest what you need now. We're going to have something faster next year that's going to reduce your cost, but if you don't invest now, your competition's going to be there." "And you can always expect that even inside of a generation, this has been, I think, one of the best stories. And I've been at this for 10 years with Nvidia. The software investment we make every generation with the exact same hardware—didn't change the hardware, you didn't pay any more for it—generally gets twice as fast within the first year." "And so that investment that you're making now, you never see that in your phone. Your phone gets slower every year; that's why they want you to build and buy a new phone. But as we look at it, we constantly invest in software, in that CUDA ecosystem that Jensen talked about for 20 years. That constant investment in it, the same software that my customers ran in 2016 on their very first DGX-1 would run today on the brand new Blackwell platform because... and so that's how customers get value on it. Get on the train; it's going to be faster next year, but get on it today." "I love it. So every company, every CEO should think today of what is their strategy for leveraging AI factories and give them a benefit, a competitive advantage on their market. And they need, back to what Jensen said this morning, we all need to have an AI strategy, and the AI factory is the central concept." "One of the early stories that we had, probably back in 2016, Jensen gave all of our internal users 125 DGXs in a data center. We called it Saturn V because we looked around the company and everyone had a little thing underneath their desk. But you know in IT, if you want to get people off of using the thing that they own to use something central, you gotta give them something much better." "And so we piloted that and we now—it's hundreds of thousands of GPUs and systems around the world. You talked about billions in investment. But as a CEO, your AI strategy should always start with: where is my centralized AI resource? Is it in the cloud, is it an Equinix data center, where is that, and then I build out from there because you're always going to need something at the center and then you're going to need things that attach to that." "But having that investment, having that thing that you know your employees always have access to that you can depend on, is the center part of your AI strategy. And whether AI factory is small or whether it's a giga-scale factory that you see Jensen talking about, don't let giga-scale scare you. Don't let like, 'Oh, I could never afford to do that.' Your AI factory could fit in the room that we're all sitting in today. That could be the right thing for your company. The wrong thing for your company is to not do anything." "100%. And I think, you know, what we're also seeing is customers want to be on this AI journey on their terms. You know, said in a different way, they want the choice and flexibility. There is this hyper-specialization happening on a per industry vertical, like even within financial services, whether you're retail banking vs insurance vs mortgage vs high-frequency trading." "Customers want the choice of the best model, whether it's in a cloud, it's in an AI factory, it's behind their corporate firewall. They want the choice and flexibility of getting the best of breed. And that's why at Equinix, we have more than 3,000 partners and ecosystems, thereby giving the customers the choice and flexibility to go about on this journey on their terms. And that's the other thing we're bringing to the AI factories: that neutral partner ecosystem so the customer does not feel locked into any technology." "I think part of having an AI factory—and D.D. touched on it—in a centralized place in Equinix with all those connections, you never know what's going to change. You know that it's going to change. Five months ago, nobody heard about OpenCloth; now it's the hottest thing. Well, that same AI factory that you put in your Equinix data center today may have been running Llama before and now it's running OpenCloth today." "But because you built that infrastructure, because you have that solid operation, because you have the help from Equinix, that allows your software developers to do the great work they do. And as the software industry pivots, you don't have to redo the hardware; you don't have to redo the physical layer. Now you may have to call up Equinix and say, 'Hey, I need a new connection to this brand new AI company that didn't exist three months ago,' and sure they can do that for you. But having that centralized hub of your AI factory allows you that flexibility." "Is there any specific industry that benefits from those AI factories or is it really across industries? It's horizontal. People that you wouldn't think would have AI factories—some of you would consider the most conservative companies—D.D. touched on banking, healthcare. Healthcare's huge AI factories. Highly regulated industries, they can do it in a safe and secure way they partner with the Equinix." "But in classic IT, it's like, 'Oh, people won't move to a new database within 10 years.' Our phones are ringing off the hook for this stuff for industries that you would classically think of as slow, but they're like, 'I need it. I see the value. I've got employees that can really help my company grow.' And AI's all about growth. If I've got an AI factory, as Jensen said it best, the AI factory—what does a factory do? It produces things; it produces things that generate revenue." "That's why it's not called a cluster anymore; that's why it's not just a data center. It's a factory because a factory builds things that are useful. I'm a CEO watching us, watching you guys now. I'm thinking, 'That's what I need for my company because I want to differentiate, I want a competitive advantage, I want to take a step ahead from my competition.' What do I need to do? What prerequisite before going into an AI factory strategy? What is your view?" "Well, so I can start. Every data center or cloud on this planet was built to do one thing: it's to run applications. And so every business in the world irrespective of the industry, everybody is working on those applications whether they're internal facing or external facing, and both are equally important. So I would say most of the clients we're working with, we start with the applications that they have and look at the efficiency of those applications." "And where in almost all cases, how can AI improve the application efficiency, the application reliability, the application performance? Because that's what eventually will translate into how the business is successful or not. And then I'd say the next thing we work with clients is look at your data strategy. Data is everywhere; should it be? We talked about some regulated industries now the data cannot be in certain places." "So we typically start with profiling the application, profiling the data even before you get started with the infrastructure decisions. AI needs data—I mean, that is its lifeblood. But that doesn't mean you have to spend time to get perfect data because AI changes so quickly that oftentimes you can use AI to clean your data, to filter your data. So many of our customers just use AI in data preparation, looking at tons of data—what is actual signal, what is noise, what can I throw away, what do I need to move?" "And so it's not about, 'Do I need to clean up my data?' The days of human data cleansing, I believe, are largely gone. Now you still need an expert to look at things, look at these results, but from a data strategy, just get started. There are applications that are 100% safe that you could use any quality of data that will add business value to you today. But start with the applications, and far too often people want to start with something they think of as super safe, like, 'Oh, this can't hurt my company at all.'" "But you have to be bold. If your idea for AI is like, 'I'm going to have a simple chatbot,' well, sure you can do that, but that's not going to impact your company's bottom line; that's not going to build more revenue. Think of something that you maybe in your brain you can't quite achieve, but that's why you work with a great partner ecosystem. That's why you work with Equinix; that's why you work with us." "That's why not only does Equinix have thousands of partners, but we do too. I get to talk about and sell the nice gold shiny server; you don't have to buy that. All of our great partners, all of our great OEMs, all of our great system integrators help make AI real for customers every day that are starting from zero. Be ambitious. Don't start with the safe stuff that doesn't deliver the impact that you're looking for and then your board of directors is just thinking, 'We've tried that, we've seen the impact was not enough.'" "So we need something that delivers, that is visible, that touches the strategic goals of the company and that's going to make a difference. A lot of times I see CIOs starting with internal applications—HR, order management, internal systems—before they turn it on on some external. But like Charlie was saying, no risk, no gain. You have to be bold and don't try to wait to be perfectly ready. Nobody is perfectly ready, but you need to start." "You need to start somewhere and otherwise your competitors will do it. If you have a company and inside of your company you're doing software development and you have not given your software developers AI tools already, you're behind. Every single Nvidia software developer—we have more software developers inside of Nvidia than we do hardware developers—he was talking about in the keynote. Every software developer has multiple AI tools because not one tool is good for one thing." "If you can make your internal software developers 10x more productive, they're getting customer features out 10x faster, making your customers happier, your revenues grow. So start with software development that is agentic AI today. We've talked with Charlie, we've talked about evolutivity of those AI factories, the capacity to handle more and more of the work and also to be more and more intelligent and with higher capacities. What do you think is the future of AI factories? What is your vision?" "The vision has stayed the same from the beginning; the scale just changes. The tools change. In the beginning, there were people physically touching systems, loading software; those are becoming AI tools. We talked about the DSX blueprint in the keynote this morning. One of the biggest things on that is AI power management. There's so much traditional way of thinking about how much power I provision. Everyone's being very conservative but because humans couldn't react fast enough to turn a dial down, but AI can." "And so the future is using AI not only to build externally facing applications but it's using AI to power the tools that are running your AI factory and thinking about all the great telemetry and data and operational efficiencies you get from Equinix, piping all that into an AI to say, 'Should I turn this up a little bit, get a little more performance, get a little more revenue out of it?' or likewise with utilities." "Are utilities going to give Equinix a signal to say, 'If you turn it down a little bit, this is good for everyone,' and work on those things? So the future is very bright. The tools are just going to get better. You just got to get used to it, you got to adopt it, and it is the future. 100% agree; it's for AI by AI. And so we're using AI in another area where we're partnering very closely with Nvidia is management of the network operations." "If you think about the network, the internet was built for browsing, not to move like petabytes of data halfway across the world if needed. But so the network stability, the time for the network to recover, using AI to do those network provisions, even find where there is capacity short term, long term—so that's another area where we are using AI to deliver this for AI workloads for our customers. Beautiful. Thank you so much for this insight. So thank you D.D., thank you Charlie. That was really a lot of insight on AI factories. Leave us some comments, tell us where you are in this journey, and we'll see you next time. Thank you. Take care."

LIVE: Pentagon update on Operation Epic Fury

LiveNOW from FOX(5.5M) ·33:55 ·70.8K views · 2026-03-31
Pete Hegseth (Secretary of War)
Operation Epic Fury has entered its second month with over 11,000 targets struck, resulting in the systematic degradation of Iran’s military morale, naval fleet, and defense industrial base.
TL;DR Operation Epic Fury has entered its second month with over 11,000 targets struck, resulting in the systematic degradation of Iran’s military morale, naval fleet, and defense industrial base. While the administration remains open to a diplomatic deal, military operations...

TL;DR

Operation Epic Fury has entered its second month with over 11,000 targets struck, resulting in the systematic degradation of Iran’s military morale, naval fleet, and defense industrial base. While the administration remains open to a diplomatic deal, military operations are accelerating with "dynamic strikes" and B-52 overland missions to ensure Iran never acquires a nuclear weapon.

Operational Progress and Tactical Milestones

  • Scale of Attack: The Joint Force has struck more than 11,000 targets within the first 30 days of the operation.
      • Dynamic Targeting: In a single 24-hour period, U.S. pilots executed 200 "dynamic strikes"—targeting mobile assets like launchers and troop formations based on real-time intelligence gathered while aircraft were already airborne.
  • Naval Dominance: The U.S. Navy has asserted control over the region, destroying more than 150 Iranian ships, including the entirety of their Jamaran-class frigates.
  • Aerial Superiority: With air defense significantly weakened, the U.S. has transitioned to overland B-52 missions, allowing for heavy, sustained bombardment of the Iranian interior.
  • Infrastructure Destruction: Recent strikes successfully targeted command bunkers, leaving Iranian leadership without power, water, or command-and-control capabilities.

Iranian Military Degradation and Morale

  • Force Attrition: Central Command (CENTCOM) reports widespread desertions and critical personnel shortages within the Iranian military as strikes continue to damage morale.
  • Diminishing Capability: Enemy missile and drone launches have reached their lowest point in 24 hours since the conflict began; the U.S. assesses that Iran’s ability to reconstitute its "defense industrial base" is now negligible.
  • Information Blackout: The Iranian regime has cut off internet access to 99.9% of its population to hide the extent of the damage, including the recent destruction of a major ammo depot in Isfahan.

Strategic Objectives and "Negotiating with Bombs"

  • Primary Mission: Secretary Hegseth reaffirmed that the administration’s non-negotiable objective is ensuring Iran never obtains a nuclear weapon.
  • Diplomatic Status: Negotiations are "very real" and ongoing. Hegseth noted that the U.S. is currently "negotiating with bombs" to compel the "new regime" to realize they are better off making a deal.
  • Strait of Hormuz: The President has signaled that the U.S. may retake full control of the Strait. Hegseth emphasized that while the U.S. has the capability to keep the waterway open, international allies—specifically the Royal Navy—must "step up" and do their share of the "heavy lifting."
  • Ground Options: While emphasizing "peace through strength," the War Department refused to rule out "boots on the ground," citing the importance of unpredictability in military strategy.

The "Arsenal of Freedom" and National Industry

  • Defense Industrial Base: Hegseth and the Chairman highlighted a massive effort to rebuild the U.S. industrial base, describing it as more vibrant than at any time since World War II.
  • The "Frontline" Workforce: The Chairman credited American machinists, assembly workers, and technicians as the core of U.S. combat power, linking factory-floor precision directly to mission success in the theater.
  • Tribute to Legacy: The briefing opened with a tribute to the late Colonel Clarence Emil "Bud" Anderson, a WWII Triple Ace, framing Operation Epic Fury as a continuation of the "Greatest Generation’s" legacy of American air superiority.

Notable Quotes

"Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that this new regime... is in a better place if they make that deal. In the meantime, we'll negotiate with bombs." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of War
"We have more and more options and they have less. Just one month in, only one month, we set the terms. The upcoming days will be decisive." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of War
"To our adversaries out there, I remind you to beware of the American workforce... The distance from that factory floor and that assembly line to the frontline is incredibly short." — General Caine, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs
"You can’t just have flags, you have to have formations. You can’t just have a few ships, you have to have enough to effect change. Other countries should pay attention when the president speaks." — Pete Hegseth, Secretary of War
I witnessed urgency to finish the job, urgency to achieve mission success, not looking at the next rotation, only moving as fast as possible to win. I got a chance to troop the line, to witness first... unleashed. It was the kind of war-fighting American spirit that comes with a clear mission against a determined enemy. A crew chief we flew with summed it up nicely. He said, "It's been a busy few weeks, sir. Tough stuff. But I'm so honored to be called up. This fight is long overdue. We need to address it for our kids. We cannot pass the buck. Please thank the president from us." I heard that time and time again. I asked each young American, "What do you need?" And nobody said better equipment, nobody said more comfortable living conditions, nobody said "send me home." Well, of course, eventually we want all those things; they do too. But what those Americans said to me—young and old, officer and NCO, male and female, black and white—was "let's finish the mission." Get us even more bombs, bigger bombs, more targets; let us finish this. In fact, Admiral Cooper noted this morning that the three Air Force captains shot down by Kuwaiti friendly fire early in the fight weeks ago never left the theater. All dropped bombs over Tehran last night. These men and women live the Iranian threat every day, facing incoming missiles and drones. They know what a world looks like—what the world would look like—if Iran had the most dangerous weapons in the world, a nuclear weapon. As President Trump has said time and time again for years and in this administration, Iran cannot have a nuclear bomb and they won't. These troops want to finish this fight for their kids and their grandkids. This is about history. This is about legacy. Success matters. And because of this president and these Americans, we are closer than ever before to winning. President Trump is doing what no other president had the guts to do. Previous presidents were all talk. He's all action. On the battlefield, because of the latitude the president has given us, American firepower is only increasing. Iran's is decreasing. We have more and more options and they have less. Just one month in, only one month, we set the terms. The upcoming days will be decisive. Iran knows that, and there's almost nothing they can militarily do about it. Yes, they will still shoot some missiles, but we will shoot them down. Of note, the last 24 hours saw the lowest number of enemy missiles and drones fired by Iran. They will go underground, but we will find them. We recently destroyed another one of their command bunkers, leaders forced to flee, no water, no power, no oxygen, no command and control. Their faith in their caves is diminishing. The latest intel is clear out of CENTCOM: our strikes are damaging the morale of the Iranian military. This is leading to widespread desertions, key personnel shortages, and causing frustrations amongst senior leaders. Just last night, we had 200 dynamic strikes alone. A dynamic strike is a strike where a pilot leaves and during their flight, they get a new target set based on real-time intel given to them. This could be a new launcher, a new location, or a new troop formation. A dynamic target is one that changes while you're in the air because of improved intelligence. We had 200 dynamic strikes alone in addition to the pre-planned targets. The video the president posted last night of Isfahan shows an ammo depot struck by US bombers. You see, you don't get to see many of those videos because, as a reminder, Iran has still shut off the internet to 99.9% of its population. But if Iran is wise, they will cut a deal. President Trump doesn't bluff and he does not back down. You can ask Khamenei about that. The new Iranian regime should know that by now. This new regime—because regime change has occurred—should be wiser than the last. President Trump will make a deal; he is willing. And the terms of the deal are known to them. If Iran is not willing, then the United States War Department will continue with even more intensity. Standing here this morning in this briefing room, in my mind's eye, I'm actually looking out at the groups I met this weekend. I see the pilots, the logisticians, the intel analysts, the targeters, the sustainers, the flight crews, the air defenders, the base security, and those maintainers we walked up to at sunset with the chill in the air on the flight line. May God watch over all of them, each day and each night. May His almighty and eternal arms of providence stretch over them and protect them and bring them peace. In the name of Jesus Christ, and amen. Mr. Chairman, over to you. Thank you, Mr. Secretary, and good morning everyone, and thank you again for being here. As operations continue, I remain deeply grateful for the service and determination of 2.8 million members of our Joint Force, each of whom are serving something greater than themselves. Every day, those deployed and in many cases those at home who are deploying forward in bomber pulses and others put themselves in harm's way, and we owe them a deep debt of gratitude. I remain especially grateful for our fallen who gave the ultimate sacrifice. We'll never forget their valor and their determination to do something greater than themselves. And each day we continue to earn what they've given to us. This morning, I want to share that yesterday the Joint Force had the honor of participating in the burial of Colonel Clarence Emil "Bud" Anderson. He passed away in May of 2024 at the ripe old age of 102. He was laid to rest in Arlington Cemetery yesterday morning alongside his wife of 70 years, Eleanor, whom he married on February 23rd, 1945. She passed away in 2015 at the age of 92. Bud, yesterday a legend to our Air Force and fighter pilots everywhere, was honored with a combination flyby of F-35s and a four-ship of P-51 Mustangs. He was one of the most prolific flying aces of World War II and the highest-scoring P-51 ace with 16 and a quarter kills. He served from 1942 to 1972 and his last combat tour was flying F-104 Thunderchiefs over North Vietnam. For anyone that had the chance to meet Colonel Anderson, you knew what an incredibly special man he was. And that's true for each and every one of our World War II vets, who become fewer and fewer with each passing day. They are the greatest generation and give us the gift of an incredible example as we execute Operation Epic Fury today. And Colonel Anderson, we, we, we mourn for your loss and remember that smile on the right side as you went out to do our nation's business. I'd like to now share an operational update. Our Joint Force continues to focus on our military objectives as we systematically continue to degrade and destroy Iran's ability to project power and threaten stability beyond its borders. First, the Joint Force continues to destroy Iran's ballistic missile and UAS capabilities. We remain focused on interdicting and destroying the logistical and supply chains that feed these programs, and this remains a truly joint effort prosecuted around the clock from air, land, sea, space, and cyberspace. Long-range bombers from US Strategic Command coordinating with tactical fighter aircraft from our Joint Force launched from bases around the region and the continental United States. Simultaneously, Navy fighters and sailors continue to project power from the sea. Army and Marine artillery units continue to execute long-range precision fires deep into enemy territory against high-value targets. Meanwhile, on the defense side, our Army and Air defenders and aviators, as the Secretary talked about, remain vigilant, forming a shield to protect our forces. Together, we continue to deliver precision strikes against key manufacturing nodes, component storage sites, and research facilities deep within Iranian territory. Over the past 30 days, we've struck more than 11,000 targets. Given the increase in air superiority, we've successfully started to conduct the first overland B-52 missions. This allows us to continue to get on top of the enemy and, as the Secretary talked about, switch toward more and more dynamic targets servicing mobile targets. We've continued to do the work against Iran's missile, drone, and naval production facilities, and we continue the multi-domain pressure that we've talked about. Second, on the Navy front, we continue to assert dominance over the Iranian Navy. We remain focused on targeting their mine-laying capability and their naval assets. We've now, as I mentioned briefly last time, started to work attack helicopters and other close air support assets into the naval domain. CENTCOM continues to identify and work against naval depots and storage areas, and we've taken out again more than 150 ships, including all Jamaran-class frigates inside their Navy. Third, we continue to prosecute our campaign against their defense industrial base at scale. This includes factories, warehouses, nuclear weapons research and development labs, and the associated infrastructure required for Iran to reconstitute its combat capability. I'd like to continue my theme of highlighting the incredible contributions of Americans to our Joint Force. Today, I want to talk a little bit about a different frontline, a line that doesn't have bunkers or guard posts, but is just as critical to our nation's security: our national assembly lines. Today, I want to recognize a group of Americans who live at the beginning of our nation's combat power: the Americans who actually make our weapons, both inside our defense industrial base, but even more broadly inside our national industrial base. In every military option, we could not and cannot do our jobs without the men and women across our country who show up every day around the clock to a factory floor, a workshop, or a laboratory. They build the weapons and capabilities we need. These great Americans, and I've had a chance to spend some time with them when I was in the private sector, are the core of American combat power. They're the machinists running high-tech CNC machines cutting raw blocks of metal into incredibly precise parts. They're the assembly workers painstakingly taking a kit of parts and turning that into a complex guidance system or precision munition or a rocket motor or building a jet or submarine. Or they're the quality assurance technicians who ensure that the weapon works every time. This can be and is tough and gritty work. It's not a quiet office and a desk with paper, and there's nothing wrong with that, but this is exactly the way this group of Americans likes it. I know this, I've seen it myself, and I remain deeply honored by that gift. It's often loud and dangerous work, demanding, and requires absolute focus for hours at a time with deep commitment to get it right every single time. It's hands-on work where one uncaught mistake or deviation can put an American's life at risk. A single misplaced wire, a microscopic flaw in a weld, or an incorrectly calibrated sensor could mean the difference between mission success or mission failure. The difference is measured in the lives of our sons and daughters who we put around the field and on the field of battle. We rely on and trust in these great Americans, and it's not just their manufacturing skill, it's their innovative minds and their entrepreneurial spirit. From those who build exquisite capabilities down to the mom-and-pop machine shops all over this great country, they live at the beginning of and the core of America's combat capability, constantly adapting, constantly improving, constantly learning. There are examples of this throughout history and current days, to include building things like the F-117 stealth fighter or the B-2 stealth bomber. It includes today's B-21 and F-47 and work in the shipyards of the East Coast that go out and build America's nuclear submarines. These innovators, these workers, these incredible Americans don't get the same glory as a fighter pilot returning to a carrier deck at night or an artillerymen sending rounds downrange, and yet they show up every single day. And without them, we could not do the work that we are tasked to do. 24/7, they build the tools that we need to do our business. The skill, the commitment, the patriotism, and the dedication poured into every piece of combat hardware is seen and felt out at the edge of the force. As the Secretary talked about those young bomb builders out in the desert that he had the chance to see, you can see it, you can feel it, and it's real. And we're deeply grateful. So to the American workforce out there, both inside our defense industrial base and international industrial base, thank you on behalf of the Joint Force. We carry the weapons that you build, we rely on the systems that you create, and the distance from that factory floor to the frontline is incredibly short. Thank you, keep it up. And to our adversaries out there, I remind you to beware of the American workforce. We continue to press forward in our military objectives. The Secretary and I remain deeply humbled by the spirit, tenacity, commitment, and grit of our 2.8 million member strong Joint Force. I want to, as always, thank the force and their families, and as always, remember our fallen. We carry with them and their names every day. Sir, I'll turn it back over to you. Absolutely. Well, thank you, Mr. Chairman. Two things. He didn't note it, but in memory of Colonel Bud Anderson, the Chairman himself flew his F-16 to honor him recently. And thank you for highlighting the defense industrial base, the arsenal of freedom. It's worth noting, no one has rebuilt that defense industrial base faster and with more purpose than President Trump in his first term and now. We're rebuilding the arsenal of freedom. Our defense industrial base is more vibrant today than it's been since World War II and growing rapidly. Contrast that with Iran, whose defense industrial base is nearly completely destroyed. Their ability to reconstitute the weapons of war that they're using now, that they're hiding in underground facilities and attriting over time, their ability to rebuild that is negligible. And I think that contrast is an enduring legacy of this as well. Right here. Thank you for your time. We've had some signaling that the US will retake control of the Strait of Hormuz. At that point, what military posture will be imposed to guarantee safe passage for our allies, deter our enemies, and demonstrate US dominance in the region? And also, can you speak to how much America's adversaries—Russia, China, North Korea—how much they're supporting Iran's war machine at this point with arms and intelligence and what we are militarily doing to punish the enemy coalition? Appreciate both questions. On the Strait of Hormuz, there are many more vessels flowing through today than there were, as the president has arranged. The president's been clear to Iran: open it for business or we have options, and we certainly do. And when you look at what the Chairman laid out with the Navy, with the Navy industrial base, with coastal cruise missiles, with UAVs, and with counter-mine capabilities, we've been focused from the beginning on attriting and defeating those capabilities. There's lots we're doing as well, some of which is known, some of which is not known, to set the conditions. And I think the president was clear this morning in his Truth—that there are countries around the world who ought to be prepared to step up on this critical waterway as well. It's not just the United States Navy. Last time I checked, there was supposed to be a big bad Royal Navy that could be prepared to do things like that as well. So he's pointing out, this is an international waterway that we use less than most. In fact, dramatically less than most. And so the world ought to pay attention and be prepared to stand up. President Trump's been willing to do the heavy lifting on behalf of the free world to address this threat of Iran. It's not just our problem set going forward, even though we have done the lion's share of preparation to ensure that that strait will be open, which is an outcome the president's been very clear on. As far as Russia and China, we know exactly what they're doing—what they are or are not doing. We don't have to air publicly what all of that is, but where necessary, we're addressing it, we're mitigating it, and or we're confronting it head-on. I don't know if you have anything you want to add. No, sir. I think you covered it. Do you have any timeline for operability in the strait? Thank you, Mr. Secretary. David Zear, Real America's Voice. And I witnessed that enthusiasm on the lines and our submarine builders and the ironworks, it's unbelievable. But I just want to ask you, what's the status of the delivery of any hardened bunkers for additional protection for our troops? And without getting too specific, what kind of measures are we taking to protect some of the larger and more strategic aircraft, like the Sentry and others? Well, it's a great question. I'll say what I witnessed where I went was a completely locked-in discipline of bunker use and bunker improvement. So from the beginning, as we stated very clearly, the first thing we did was set up a defense. We made sure our defensive capabilities were maxed out before any of this even started. That included fortifications as much as possible. But it also included dispersal. If all of our people are in one place, you could imagine why that's a big problem. So dispersing is part of that defeat. Alongside that dispersal is more and more bunkers. And I can tell you, talking to base commanders, talking to our allies in Israel, talking to others, rapidly fielding that and then improving those positions is a theater priority. It is no doubt a priority, as are the air defenses and the layered air defenses. It's not just Patriots and THAADs, it's fighters in defensive caps, it's other kinetic defeat systems, and it's electronic warfare. So the defense of our troops and our assets is maxed. I will say on some of those other assets you talked about, airwings, airframes, there's some things adversaries are doing to provide info and intel that they shouldn't. We're aware of it, and ultimately we move things around. One of the biggest principles you learn in the military is to not set patterns, predictable patterns. And so commanders are working hard to adjust in real-time. Thanks, Mr. Secretary. The US and Iran appear right now publicly to disagree about whether there are these negotiations ongoing. What impact does that have on military strategy? Does it have any bearing at all? And General, the president wrote yesterday that the US might destroy Iran's electricity generating plants and oil wells. Is there a way to do either of those things without seriously jeopardizing or seriously harming civilians? So on the talks, I can tell you having been with Steve and Jared and the Vice President and Marco and many others yesterday, they are very real. They are ongoing. They're active. And I think gaining strength. And we appreciate that. As I said in my remarks, we would much prefer to get a deal. If Iran was willing to relinquish material they have and ambitions they have, and open the strait, great. That's the goal. We don't want to have to do more militarily than we have to. But I didn't mean it flippantly when I said in the meantime, we'll negotiate with bombs. Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that this new regime is in a better place if they make that deal. Thanks for the question. We're always thinking about those considerations and develop options to be able to mitigate those risks pursuant to the normal practices that we do in the military. So... Thank you, Mr. Secretary. I know you slightly addressed this already, but just on the Strait of Hormuz, is opening the strait an essential objective to Operation Epic Fury, or is that the job of those other countries? And then secondly, without asking you to comment on things that you can't talk about, what is your message to Americans who love the president and strongly believe in him, but are very worried about this notion of boots on the ground? Well, first on the Strait of Hormuz, our core objectives from this podium from day one have been clear: missile production and missile programs, the defense industrial base and production ability, and the Navy and power projection. So those have been, and then of course wrapping it all is that Iran's never going to have a nuclear weapon. Defeating the Navy is a core part of ensuring they can't project that kind of power. But ultimately, I think the president's Truth this morning lays that out very well, that this Strait of Hormuz issue is not just a United States of America problem set. We've been willing to lead, and President Trump's led the entire time, but it's not just us. So ultimately, I think other countries should pay attention when the president speaks. He's proven that when he speaks he means something, and he's pointing out, you might start learning how to fight for yourself. It's something some of us have been saying for quite some time. You can't just have flags, you have to have formations. You can't just have a few ships, you have to have enough to effect change. Those things matter in a dangerous world with ascendant adversaries. That's why the Chairman's talking about our industrial base and why we're launching the Arsenal of Freedom tour to revive our defense industrial base. As far as President Trump and boots on the ground, I don't understand why the base wouldn't have faith in his ability to execute on this. Look at his track record of pursuing peace through strength and America-first outcomes. And what he's simply saying is exactly true and I've said from this podium too: we're not going to foreclose any option. You can't fight and win a war if you tell your adversary what you are willing to do or what you are not willing to do. Our adversary right now thinks there are 15 different ways we could come at them with boots on the ground. And guess what? There are. And so if we needed to, we could execute those options on behalf of the President of the United States. Or maybe we don't have to use them at all. Maybe negotiations work, or maybe there's a different approach. But the point is to be unpredictable in that, and certainly not let anybody know what you're willing to do or not do. But if anybody has internalized the lessons of Iraq and Afghanistan, it is President Trump. He was the first one to call them out for what they are, and he's not going to repeat those lessons. I've been very clear about that from the podium. Follow up? Thank you, Secretary Hegseth. A question for you, and then a question for General Caine. You said we're a month into Operation Epic Fury. How long until the objectives are achieved and is there a scenario where a deal is struck before the objectives have been achieved? And for General Caine, there's been lots of media coverage that suggested a ground invasion is imminent. What other purposes might the soldiers and the Marines who've been deployed over to the Middle East serve in this conflict? Well, just like the previous question, it's sort of military 101. Don't tell your enemy what you're willing to do or not do, and don't tell your enemy when you're willing to stop, especially an enemy that likes to hide in bunkers. So that's not a question I'm going to answer. We have our own military objectives that we're moving toward and things that we look at. The President has articulated four to six weeks, or six to eight weeks... it could be any particular number. But we would never reveal precisely what it is, because our goal is to finish those objectives. And we're well on our way, and the Chairman and I look at this every single day. It will be the president's determination alone when those objectives are complete. No, just to answer your question, Reagan, the range of military options that those forces can offer are extensive, not just limited to what you mentioned in terms of forces on the ground. I wouldn't want to take away the president's decision space. But there are a multitude of things. Not the least of which is that Iran should note that they're out there and that they are a pressure point. And so they should carefully consider what's in front of them at the diplomatic level. Secretary, how long will those... stay open? Okay, Mr. Secretary, the US government and IC assessed for many years that Iran is protecting Al-Qaeda senior leaders including the current emir Saif al-Adel. Does the US assess that he's still in Iran? Has he fled to Afghanistan? Is he on a target list? And Mr. Chairman, Iranian daily missile barrages and drone launches have indeed dropped dramatically since the start of the war, but they're not at zero. How do we get that closer to zero, and what impact is Iran's degraded but still very real capability impacting current and future operations? I won't speak to a particular target, I'll just say that Al-Qaeda remains our enemy, obviously. There are a lot of people on our target list in Iran, and if they were to be harboring Al-Qaeda they would certainly fit that list. Just quickly, the mobile targets that we continue to service are one component of continuing to lower that. The second and more long-term impact that we're having is the strikes against their defense industrial base at scale, which will not allow them to reload after that. So we do continue to see a trend down. They are still shooting and we do continue to exert significant pressure against both sides of that system. Mr. Secretary, do you still believe—you said previously that the Iranian leadership would surrender. Do you still believe that? And is the United States still committed to NATO's collective defense? And to Chairman Caine, could you give us a sense of your thinking about the legality of striking desalinization plants if the President were to order that? I will just say that any mission that ends on our terms—you want to call it a surrender, call it a defeat, call it what you want—we remain committed to a conflict that ends on our terms and the President's terms. And as far as NATO's concerned, that's a decision that'll be left to the President. But I'll just say a lot has been laid bare. A lot has been shown to the world about what our allies would be willing to do for the United States. These are missiles that don't even range the United States, they range allies and others, and yet when we ask for additional assistance or basing and overflight, we get questions or roadblocks. The President's pointing out, you don't have much of an alliance if countries are not willing to stand with you. Ultimately it'll be his decision of what that looks like after this is complete. I won't talk about any particular target, but the Joint Force is the most professional force in the world. We have numerous processes and systems to carefully consider the whole range of considerations, from civilian risk to legal considerations with any target. We always strike lawful targets in accordance with the normal procedures that we use. Chairman Caine, do you agree with no quarter or no mercy for enemy combatants as Hegseth has said? Thank you, Mr. Secretary. On Sunday, President Trump said that Iran's first regime was all destroyed and the second one is mostly dead. The third regime that we're on right now has been much more reasonable in negotiations. Do you agree with that assessment and why? I would defer to the negotiating team there. They're the ones talking to them, but they're getting a back-and-forth on terms, which is a productive development. We stand right there next to our negotiating team, always willing and prepared to put them in an even better position. Thank you very much, sir.

US IRAN WAR LIVE: PENTAGON PRESS BRIEFING – Hegseth & Gen. Caine on US Military Strategy

MIRROR NOW(2.2M) ·140:54 ·2.4K views · 2026-03-31
Pete Hegseth (United States Secretary of War) Dan Caine (Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff)
Operation Epic Fury has entered a decisive phase, with U.S.
TL;DR Operation Epic Fury has entered a decisive phase, with U.S. forces successfully striking over 11,000 targets in 30 days to systematically dismantle Iran’s nuclear ambitions, defense industrial base, and naval capabilities while maintaining a 90% reduction in enemy missile...

TL;DR

Operation Epic Fury has entered a decisive phase, with U.S. forces successfully striking over 11,000 targets in 30 days to systematically dismantle Iran’s nuclear ambitions, defense industrial base, and naval capabilities while maintaining a 90% reduction in enemy missile effectiveness.

Strategic Objectives and Operational Progress

  • Core Mission Goals: Secretary Pete Hegseth reaffirmed the administration’s "America First" objectives: the total destruction of Iran’s missile programs, the elimination of its naval threat, and ensuring Iran never acquires a nuclear weapon.
  • Scale of Engagement:
      • Target Volume: Over 11,000 targets struck in the first 30 days, with 7,000 specific military infrastructure hits.
      • Dynamic Strikes: Pilots executed 200 "dynamic strikes" in a single night—real-time targeting based on shifting battlefield intelligence rather than pre-planned coordinates.
  • Degradation Metrics:
      • Missile/UAV Suppression: Iranian ballistic missile and "kamikaze" drone attacks against U.S. forces have dropped by 90% since the conflict began.
      • Leadership Impact: U.S. strikes have neutralized numerous command-and-control bunkers; CENTCOM reports widespread desertions and critical personnel shortages within the Iranian military.

Naval Dominance and Air Superiority

  • Naval Attrition: General Dan Caine reported the destruction of over 150 Iranian vessels, including the entirety of their Jamaran-class frigates.
      • The "Bottom Half" Strategy: Hegseth noted the U.S. has "shared the ocean" with Iran by sending their surface fleet and all 11 of their submarines to the bottom.
  • Advanced Air Assets:
      • B-52 Operations: For the first time, B-52s are conducting overland missions to service mobile targets.
      • Specialized Munitions: The Joint Force is utilizing 5,000-pound "penetrator" weapons to destroy weapons caches buried deep under rock and concrete.
      • Tactical Support: A-10 Warthogs and AH-64 Apaches are now active on the southern flank, specifically hunting fast-attack watercraft and protecting Gulf partners.
  • Strait of Hormuz: The U.S. maintains the capability to keep the waterway open but emphasized that international allies (specifically the Royal Navy) must step up to protect this global commerce route.

The "Arsenal of Freedom" and Domestic Support

  • Industrial Base Recognition: General Caine highlighted the "national assembly lines" as the beginning of American combat power.
      • Workforce Criticality: Success depends on machinists, quality assurance technicians, and assembly workers whose precision prevents mission failure.
  • Defense Rebuilding: Secretary Hegseth credited the current administration with rebuilding the defense industrial base to levels not seen since World War II, contrasting this with Iran’s nearly destroyed manufacturing capability.
  • Strategic Flexibility: Leadership refused to rule out "boots on the ground," noting that unpredictability is a core military necessity. Hegseth stated that while negotiations are ongoing, the U.S. is currently "negotiating with bombs" to compel a favorable deal.

Diplomatic Relations and Allied Support

  • Regional Partners: Israel and Gulf States (UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia) were praised for their active cooperation and defense integration.
  • European Criticism: Hegseth criticized certain "ungrateful allies" in Europe for hesitating on basing and overflight rights while U.S. forces neutralize threats that primarily range European territories.
  • Counter-Disinformation: The Pentagon is actively bypassing Iran’s 99.9% internet blackout to deliver messages to the Iranian population and countering "AI-generated" propaganda designed to show false Iranian successes.

Notable Quotes

"Our job is to ensure that we compel Iran to realize that this new regime... is in a better place if they make that deal. In the meantime, we’ll negotiate with bombs." — Secretary Pete Hegseth
"We’ve decided to share the ocean with Iran. We’ve given them the bottom half." — Secretary Pete Hegseth
"They represent the best of our nation... They died for you so that your generation doesn't have to deal with a nuclear Iran." — Secretary Pete Hegseth (on the fallen service members at Dover)
